title: Week of Intellectual Adventure
text: I got nominated and eventually selected for the NUS Young Fellowship Program, but I did not think that this opportunity would become one of the best experiences of my academic life so far. As one of the five students picked from India, alongside some brilliant minds from IIT Bombay and IIT Madras, I was nervously excited. My essay titled “Guardrails” (check it out on under the Madhav_Speaks) had managed to impress the selection committee and opened the door to this incredible journey. This program brought together nearly 90 students from 20+ countries. We were from India, China, USA, UK, Hungary, Japan, South Korea, Malaysia, Australia, Singapore, Saudi Arabia, Philippines, Canada, Vietnam, Thailand, and more. When I packed my bags for this week-long adventure in learning, I knew I was in for something special. My dad came with me till Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport Mumbai as it was first time I was travelling international.

title: Week of Intellectual Adventure
text: I got nominated and eventually selected for the NUS Young Fellowship Program, but I did not think that this opportunity would become one of the best experiences of my academic life so far. As one of the five students picked from India, alongside some brilliant minds from IIT Bombay and IIT Madras, I was nervously excited. My essay titled “Guardrails” (check it out on under the Madhav_Speaks) had managed to impress the selection committee and opened the door to this incredible journey. This program brought together nearly 90 students from 20+ countries. We were from India, China, USA, UK, Hungary, Japan, South Korea, Malaysia, Australia, Singapore, Saudi Arabia, Philippines, Canada, Vietnam, Thailand, and more. When I packed my bags for this week-long adventure in learning, I knew I was in for something special. My dad came with me till Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport Mumbai as it was first time I was travelling international.

title: Week of Intellectual Adventure
section: Day 0: Touchdown in the Lion City
text:
Day 0: Touchdown in the Lion City



Changi Airport on arrival


Stepping off the plane in Singapore, I was hit by two the humidity (whew!) and the efficiency of Changi Airport. This place runs like a well-oiled machine! Although NUS had arranged transportation, I decided to grab(yes) a taxi. As we zipped past skyscrapers and lush greenery, I was excited for the week ahead. The NUS campus welcomed me with open arms as campus is part of city and not a isolated place (and comfy accommodation). As I settled in, I decided to take a stroll around University town with other fellows and had intros and handshakes with a lot of people.



Arrival of students

title: Week of Intellectual Adventure
section: Day 1: Diving into the Deep End
text:
Day 1: Diving into the Deep End



Prof Jessical Pan in inaugural address



Morning: Breaking the Ice and Blowing Our Minds
The program kicked off with some ice-breakers. It was here that I met my squad: Rena, Yi Xin, Xinming, and Swastika. We were dubbed Group 12. Our first lecture was by Professor Chai Kah Hin, he kept pushing us to ask “So what?” – making us really think about the impact and relevance of our research ideas. He introduced us to this cool concept called the “ghost deck” which basically is about creating a storyboard of your presentation before getting into the nitty-gritty. Apparently, it’s a trick used by big shots like McKinsey to keep their presentations tight and logical. (Mental note: must try this for my next class project!)



Inaugural session




Afternoon: Problem-Solving Like a Boss
Prof. Chai wasn’t done with us yet. His mantra was “Rough, Rapid, Right”– the idea that making quick mistakes is actually a good thing. It’s all about learning fast and adapting faster. He also talked about horizontal and vertical logic in presentations, emphasizing the need for a clear, newspaper-like summary to hook your audience from the start.

Pro tip: If you ever find yourself in Singapore, don’t miss out on trying the local kopi (coffee).

We rounded off the afternoon with a quick but eye-opening lecture by Dr. Amirhassan Monajemi on Generative AI. While a lot of it was familiar territory for us computer science geeks, seeing how it’s being applied across different fields at NUS was fascinating. It’s one thing to code an AI model, but seeing it potentially reshape entire industries? Although I found myself yawning during this lecture, I went out and took a Kopi to keep myself fresh. Actually Luo helped me to get to know about Kopi. I am attaching one guide here for your reference that I used over remaining week.






Kopi guide







First Lunch







Evening: The Ethics of AI
The highlight of the day was hands down the session on AI and ethics by Simon Chesterman, Dean of the NUS Faculty of Law. He didn’t just lecture; he made us question everything we thought we knew about education and technology. He painted a vivid (and slightly unsettling) picture of the challenges universities face in the AI era. He threw some real curveballs at us: Are students customers or products? How do we evaluate people when AI can basically be a performance-enhancing drug for the mind? He started by addressing the fear of AI turning into Skynet and taking over the world. While he didn’t completely rule out the possibility (gulp!), he was more concerned about how we humans might misuse this insanely powerful tech.



Address of Dean Simon Chesterman


The role of universities in this brave new AI world was a big talking point. He told us in clear terms that the age-old notion of professors knowing more than students is becoming outdated and with all the world’s knowledge at our fingertips, universities need to focus on teaching us how to think, not just what to think. We also dove into the ethical minefield of AI in various fields. In research, for instance, AI is doing some pretty cool stuff like speeding up drug discovery. But it also raises some tricky questions about plagiarism and the temptation to take shortcuts. He also stressed the importance of separating the hype from reality when it comes to AI’s capabilities. One of the most thought-provoking parts of the discussion was about responsibility in the AI era. Who’s to blame when an AI system messes up? The developers? The users? The data it was trained on?
After the session, some graduate students took us for a guided campus tour where I got to know about a lot of folks and had some interesting conversations with friends from Japan and South Korea.



Campus Tour later that day


Over dinner at a local hawker center near Clementi mall which is very close to University Town, our group, along with a couple of others, started with debate about the future of creativity and AI’s role in the arts. The diversity of viewpoints, influenced by our varied cultural backgrounds, made for a conversation which later ended up into a conversation of stories, our stories to each other. I walked nearly 18km that day!! (eventually, I breached 30km a day mark).

title: Week of Intellectual Adventure
section: Day 2: From PhD Dreams to Workplace Scenarios
text:
Day 2: From PhD Dreams to Workplace Scenarios

Morning: PhD Tales That’ll Make You Laugh, Cry, and Think
The day started with a panel discussion featuring PhD candidates and recent graduates. I was particularly struck by Dr. Boyd Anderson from New Zealand. This guy went from tinkering with embedded systems to revolutionizing how we teach. Choosing the right supervisor and institution is like picking a life partner, it can make or break your academic journey. As one panelist put it, “The institution matters, but within it, your supervisor matters most.” (Note to self: choose wisely!)


Afternoon: The Dark Side of Office Life
Professor Sandy Lim’s session was on Workplace Incivility. Using Structured Equation Modeling, she told us about how rudeness can wreck a workplace. 1. Workplace incivility is more common than bad coffee in offices, 91% of employees in Asia reported experiencing it. 2. Your boss being rude affects you more than your annoying cubicle mate when it comes to wanting to quit. 3. Rudeness can spread through an office faster than gossip if left unchecked.
In the evening, we visited robotics lab and nano-materials lab. I got to handle an electron microscope and also play with a robotic car. That day, I along with other vegeterian friends, visited Anand Bhawan (A2B) in Little India and had very home like dinner whereas our non-vegeterian friends enjoyed their meals in a restaurant inside NUS. We also went to clark quay for a walk.






In the nanomaterials lab







Robotics lab









Incubation center for converting ideas to products







Robotics lab

title: Week of Intellectual Adventure
section: Day 3: From Nano-Wonders to Urban Jungles
text:
Day 3: From Nano-Wonders to Urban Jungles

Morning: The Invisible Tech Running Our World
Professor Aaron Thean’s lecture was on semiconductors He started with : “If you don’t have semiconductors, you don’t have technology.”
I was listening very mindfully that I was able to note these points only.

Moore’s Law isn’t just about cramming more transistors onto a chip; it’s a testament to human ingenuity and stubbornness.
Current AI, with far fewer parameters than our brains, guzzles huge energy.
His research on rewritable chips, mimicking our brain’s plasticity, hinted at a future where our gadgets might think more like us.

I thought about future devices that might blur the line between silicon and neurons.


Morning: Harnessing Nature’s Power (Without Angering the Weather Gods)
Dr. Ghim Wei Ho’s session was on solar technologies for tapping into low-grade heat fluctuations.



Dr. Ghim Wei Ho addressing us


She stated that roof gardens can actually make solar panels work better and last longer? It’s like giving your panels a cool, green hat. Niladri, a undergrad from UMich asked a ton of questions in this session.


Afternoon: Transforming Concrete Jungles into Digital Paradises
Ervine Lin’s lecture on digitizing landscapes. He challenged us to see beyond trees and buildings to the designed environments shaping our cities and lives.



Very interesting session by Ervine Lin


Some “cool” takeaways:

Singapore’s urban planning philosophy evolved from “Garden City” to “City in Gardens” a subtle difference with mindset shift!
3D scanning and reality capture tech are revolutionizing landscape architecture.
Understanding topography is crucial in urban planning, especially for flood prevention.




Asking question


After the lectures, we hopped on NUS buses for a tour of Singapore’s highlights like Gardens by the Bay, cloud forest, flower dome(nature on steroids!), the iconic Merlion (half-lion, half-fish, 100% Instagram-worthy although I don’t use instagram yet), and some architectural marvels that made my neck hurt from looking up so much.



Visit to Gardens by the bay


The evening was a culinary delight through Little India and Chinatown. As we stuffed ourselves with everything we liked, I loved how this tiny island nation had become a melting pot of cultures and cuisines.






Near Merlion







Marine Bay sands









In front of Merlion







Inside flower dome









Inside Marine Bay Sands Casino

title: Week of Intellectual Adventure
section: Day 4: Health, Wealth, and the Art of Not Boring People to Death
text:
Day 4: Health, Wealth, and the Art of Not Boring People to Death

Morning: AI: Your New Healthcare Bestie?
Professor Dean Ho’s session on N-of-1 Digital Health felt like peeping into the future of medicine. He explained us WisDM and CURATE platform, which uses AI to personalise treatments to the level of dosage quantity to individual patients based on their individual history.

The old “one-size-fits-all” approach to medicine is outdated.
AI could be the key to cracking the code on personalized cancer treatments. Take that, cancer!
In healthcare data, “stories over snapshots”




After talking with Prof. Ho


Prof. Ho also dove into the gamification of healthcare. Imagine earning points for taking your meds or hitting your step goal, being healthy becomes as addictive as your favorite mobile game! I bombarded him with questions after the session (sorry, not sorry!), and to his credit, he answered every single one with patience and enthusiasm. It’s not every day you get to pick the brain of someone like him!


Afternoon: Economics in the Age of AI
The lecture on economic strategies for the Industrial revolution 5.0. The key takeaways were:

We need to rewire our brains when it comes to economic development. The old rules are irrelevant.
It’s time to question everything we thought we knew about economics.
Crafting economic strategies that play nice with AI and automation is the new holy grail.

As I sat there, furiously scribbling notes, I was thinking: in this absolute new world, would my future job/business even exist? Or would I be competing with a hyper-intelligent AI? (Note to self: start being nicer to Claude, just in case.)


Afternoon: The Art of Not Boring People (In Three Minutes or Less)
The 3-Minute Thesis (3MT) workshop by Rafi Rasheed and Jason Banta was a crash course in not putting people to sleep while talking about your research. Their golden rules:

Start with a bang! Your opening line should grab attention faster than a cat video on social media.
Use stories that people can relate to.
Problem, solution, impact are the essentials of any good pitch. If you can’t explain it to your grandparentts, you’re doing it wrong.

Later before evening Prof Johannes Widodo took us to architectural tour of academic building of department of architecture.



During the tour with Prof Johannes


We spent the evening practicing our pitches, giving each other feedback, and trying not to panic at the thought presenting the poster in front of everyone. It was challenging, hilarious, and oddly exhilarating. Since next day was presentation day, we did not go for any outdoor adventures. We visited the merchandise store in NUS, bought few things, had some snacks and started working on presentations. Although our group did not make it to finals (we missed it with very small margin becasue of too much text on poster), we enjoyed the work we did and got to learn a lot from posters of other groups.






Preparing Poster Presentation







After all nighter work

title: Week of Intellectual Adventure
section: Day 5: Battling Urban Heat and Jamming with AI
text:
Day 5: Battling Urban Heat and Jamming with AI

Morning: Saving the World, One Cool Roof at a Time
Professor Wong Nyuk Hien’s lecture was on the CoolNUS Project on urban climate challenges. Cool facts (pun intended):

Tall buildings have a urban heat island effect.
In Singapore, 1°C in temperature can spike cooling demand by 7-10%.
Urban greenery is more than just for beauty.



Afternoon: When AI Drops the Beat
The program finale with Francis Tan exploring AI and Music was fun. We played around with tools like SUNO and UDIO. Authenticity, Connection and Catchiness are key ingredients even for AI generated music. As we experimented with AI-generated tunes, everyone debated about the future of creativity. Can a machine really capture the soul of music? Or are we on the brink of a new era of human-AI collaboration in art? The jury’s still out, but one thing’s for sure is that the content industry is in for one wild ride!



Poster Presentation on final day

title: Week of Intellectual Adventure
section: The Grand Finale: Posters, Presentations, and Goodbyes
text:
The Grand Finale: Posters, Presentations, and Goodbyes
As the program neared its end, our group pulled an all-nighter on second last night to put the finishing touches on our poster and presentation. Fueled by a mix of excitement, caffeine, and the fear of embarrassing ourselves in front of some of the brightest minds in academia, we worked through the night. It was exhausting, exhilarating, and oddly fun ; a perfect microcosm of the entire week.






Evaluation of Poster Presentation







After the last session greeting to everyone and wishing them good luck!









With Dean Jessica Pan and Prof Kah-Hin Chai







Sentosa Islands





The next day, everyone presented their projects, I loved the creativity and innovation on display. From AI-powered solutions for sustainable agriculture to novel approaches in quantum computing, the range of ideas was staggering. As the program officially came to a close, a bittersweet feeling settled over us all. We’d come as strangers from different universities but were bonded so close during such short period. That evening we went to an extra mile to squeeze out joy from every moment. We visited so many tourist attractions. We also went to Casino as entry was free for foreginers (just to see it from inside!). This is the day which made me breach 30km mark. Also, after coming back to U Town around 2AM, we wandered inside U Town till 4:30 AM. Next day, half of us came together to the aiport, bid farewell to each other. Everyone became so emotional at this point that some among us were actually sobbing. I said goodbye to everyone. But the jounery together was not over, we will still spend some time together remotely, to prepare our 3MTs and will present them to the jury.
To anyone considering applying to the NUS Young Fellowship Program : do it! It’s an unparalleled opportunity to immerse yourself in NUS research environment, engage with leading academics, and connect with exceptional peers from around the globe.



Roaming around airport


Update : 3MT is done. It was really really good to listen to knowledge-dense pitches.



Leaving for the airport together


After farewell, I roamed around the airport for an hour or two with few friends who were still to board their flights. Then I sat at starbucks for 2 hours since my flight was delayed beause of bad weather. It was here that I finetuned my digital journal. I boarded my flight to Mumbai. I thought about this week and interactions and as I started seeing Mumbai from Window, I played Swades (Yeh Jo Des Hei Tera) song. When I deboarded, I was overwhelmed with emotions and filled eyes. The NUS Young Fellowship Program showed me cutting edge of research and innovation and a journey that can be taken by cities and even entire nation together.



Swastika, Yi, Lili (Luo Xinming), Rena and Me


Note : My notes were very scattered, I have taken help from Claude Sonnet 3.5 on 30 Sept 2024 to make them coherent.

title: Sayak Paul from Hugging Face
text:
What Sayak talked about??

Diffusers library is his recent work. He loves wasting GPU hours and he thinks Vision Transformers are very Robust Learners. Take everything with grain of salt since he is highly optiniated. When he learned the stuff, No Fast.ai then, Andrew Ng course was start. How school maths meant the things in ML was initial hook for him. He didn’t dabble in other subjects.
Andrej Karpathy Andrej Karpathy says:- Spend 10000 hours quote, He wasn’t dubious in his choice, he was 100 percent sure that he wanted to just go into ML. Surface level knowledge just leads to suffering in which case if you are even able to build models, Models might fail silently and you won’t be able to understand why if you are not learning in deep. Spend time with subject you like, Maths is necessary but not university level, Calculus Linear Alg Prob, CS231n, Andrew NG, FastAI, Deep Learning Book Ean Goodfellow, Deep Learning with python, Grokking Machine Learning.
Implementation is crux of most of the things, (Which paper did he implement?) Implementing the papers that align/ some part of those paper, gives a lot of learning, Balancing off understanding, intution, and how things work in practice, grokking/cramming alone never help, deep learning is often alchemy than science, Curious mind is necessary.
He didn’t start with a ML heavy job but it was clear to him totally that he will eventually. Having public record of our understanding is important so he started to write technical blogs, one way to write blog is write self reference, It was public record that got attention of employees (Where did he write ?) Forced himself on engineering side of ML, like deploying the mode, Docker/Kubernetes, Mentor at GSoC at TensorFlow, Publishing the tier-1 paper is really crazy, Got into hugging face because of public work, Open-Source,
He was not interested more education, He didn’t want to be research scientist, non-trivial public portfolio is really a thing that always helps, Matured Work Experience >>> academic affliaition, Someone who have tensorflow contributions will always get more pref over stanford grad, Difficult Stuff => learning opportunities, You will always feel overwhelmed but carry on with those moments, Do not settle, Don’t be complecent, Sharpening the Sword - Have the same mindset as the one you had at the beginning. Reach out to other collaborators,
A very nice doc of faqs he compiled, can be found at bit.ly/sayak-faqs
Questions :

He was working with Roberta and Bert sized model, He got comfortable then with codebase so he contributed to open issues, Connext took it him inside and after 6 months they reached out to him>
Doing things for open source is crux of HF
You have to build your case for yourself, make something that you can show to tell that I have expertise, Interview is not the way to go.
On his personal site he used to post blogs, it helps your own understanding and articulate it properly.
Inception you need to go deeper, regularization, SAM, 2018, High threshold for patience,
Project should have enough thickness to show your expertize,
Ex: Finetuning is really easy but you can show maturity if you can tell why did you finetune> Like prompt engineering didn’t work, RAG didn’t suffice then You curated custom dataset, then finetuned/
Kaggling is good, but focus on learning aspect rather than winning aspects.
How to Flash-Attention 2, Why not in C? How to find papers to be implemented. Youwill learn with time . During intital time randomly kicling a paper alinging with your field.

No cracked failure, its a Back and forth process, Focus on representativeness, no corruptions, check and check again. Tuning CV models with task rewards, RAG is beneficial for factuality, Do a Blog on YOLO, D2L.ai. Detection is far from saturation, contribute detection model to transformer.

title: वारकरी संप्रदाय:एक समृद्ध जीवनधारा
text:
भारतीय संस्कृति के उद्यान में अनेक पंथ और संप्रदाय फूलों की तरह खिले हुए हैं। इन मनोहारी पुष्पों में से एक है वारकरी संप्रदाय, जिसकी सुगंध बारहवीं शताब्दी से दक्कन की धरती को महका रही है। यह संप्रदाय भगवान विठ्ठल की भक्ति का अमृत पान कराता है, जिनकी दिव्य मूर्ति पंढरपुर में विराजमान है। “वारकरी” शब्द स्वयं में एक कहानी छिपाए है - ‘वारी करने वाला’। वारी एक ऐसी तीर्थयात्रा है जो विभिन्न गाँवों से प्रारंभ होकर पंढरपुर की पावन भूमि पर समाप्त होती है। यह यात्रा केवल पैरों की नहीं, बल्कि आत्मा की भी यात्रा है। इस आध्यात्मिक उद्यान के मुख्य माली रहे हैं - ज्ञानेश्वर, तुकाराम, नामदेव और एकनाथ जैसे महान संत। इन दिव्य आत्माओं ने भक्ति के बीज बोए, जिन्होंने सत्य और निष्ठा की जड़ों को गहरा किया। वारकरी इसी पथ पर चलते हुए अपने जीवन रूपी वृक्ष को पल्लवित करते हैं।
इस संप्रदाय की विशेषता है इसकी समानता की भावना। यहाँ छोटे-बड़े का भेद मिट जाता है, जैसे समुद्र में सभी नदियाँ एकाकार हो जाती हैं। यहाँ मान्यता है कि ईश्वर के नाम के जप से ही पुण्य का फल प्राप्त होता है। वारकरी परंपरा की समृद्धि इसके साहित्य, सिद्धांतों और इतिहास में निहित है, जैसे किसी कलश में अमृत भरा हो। साहित्य में सदियों की ऐसी कविताओं का भंडार है, जो सरल और मधुर भाषा में वह ज्ञान संप्रेषित कर पाती हैं, जिसे सैकड़ों महाकाव्य भी शायद नहीं व्यक्त कर पाते। उन कविताओं में जीवन का सत्य और अनुभवों की गहराई इस सहजता से रची है कि वे मन को छू जाती हैं, जैसे शीतल हवा आत्मा को शांति प्रदान करती है। यह संप्रदाय आत्मा और ईश्वर के अस्तित्व को स्वीकार करता है, मानो दो चिरंतन सत्य हों। इस आध्यात्मिक इमारत के निर्माण में अनेक संतों ने अपना योगदान दिया है। इसका सुंदर चित्रण इन पंक्तियों में किया गया है:

“भागवतधर्मे कृपा झाली इमारत फळा आली ।
ज्ञानदेवे रचिला पाया उभारिले देवालया ।
नामा तयाचा किंकर तेणे केला हा विस्तार ।
जनार्दन एकनाथ खांब दिला भागवत ।
तुका झालासे कळस भजन करा सावकाश ।
बहिणी फडकती ध्वजा निरूपण आले ओजा ।”

इन पंक्तियों में बताया गया है कि भागवत धर्म की कृपा से इस संप्रदाय की इमारत खड़ी हुई। ज्ञानदेव ने इसकी नींव रखी, नामदेव ने इसका विस्तार किया, एकनाथ ने इसे मजबूती प्रदान की, तुकाराम इसके शिखर बने, और बहिणाबाई ने इस पर ध्वज फहराया। संत ज्ञानेश्वर ने मानवता के महत्व को पसायदान के इन अमर शब्दों में व्यक्त किया:

“जे खळांची व्यंकटी सांडो । तया सत्कर्मी रती वाढो ।
भूतां परस्परे जडो । मैत्र जीवांचे ।।”

अर्थात, दुष्टों की दुष्टता समाप्त हो और सत्कर्म में रुचि बढ़े, और सभी प्राणियों के बीच मैत्री का भाव जागे। वारकरी परंपरा में एक प्रसिद्ध दंतकथा है जिसमें कहा जाता है कि भगवान विठ्ठल भक्त पुंडलिक के लिए ईंट पर खड़े हो गए। यह कथा माता-पिता की सेवा के महत्व को उजागर करती है, मानो यह कह रही हो कि मनुष्य की सेवा ही ईश्वर की सेवा है। प्रकृति के प्रति प्रेम और सम्मान की भावना इन पंक्तियों में झलकती है:

“वृक्षवल्ली आम्हां सोयरे वनचरे । पक्षीही सुस्वरे आळविती।”

इसका अर्थ है कि वृक्ष और लताएँ हमारे रिश्तेदार हैं, वन के जीव हमारे मित्र हैं, और पक्षी भी मधुर स्वर में इसी बात को गाते हैं। यह पंक्ति पर्यावरण संतुलन के महत्व को रेखांकित करती है। गुरु का महत्व इस संप्रदाय में सर्वोपरि है। संत ज्ञानेश्वर के शब्दों में:

“भावेविण देव न कळे निःसंदेह गुरूविण अनुभव कैसा कळे।”

अर्थात, बिना भाव के ईश्वर का ज्ञान नहीं होता, और बिना गुरु के अनुभव कैसे प्राप्त हो सकता है। जीवन की पवित्रता पर बल देते हुए एक अभंग कहता है:

“नाही निर्मळ जीवन काय करील साबण”

इसका अर्थ है कि यदि जीवन निर्मल (पवित्र) नहीं है, तो साबुन क्या करेगा। यह पंक्ति हमें याद दिलाती है कि बाहरी शुद्धता से अधिक महत्वपूर्ण है आंतरिक शुद्धता।
वारकरी संप्रदाय की शिक्षाएं सरल और व्यावहारिक हैं, जैसे किसी माँ का स्नेहिल मार्गदर्शन। ये शिक्षाएँ कहती हैं - अपने कर्तव्यों का पालन करते हुए भगवान को न भूलें। प्रातःकाल जल्दी उठें, स्नान के बाद गोपीचंदन का तिलक लगाएं, नियमित रूप से हरिपाठ का पाठ करें, संतों के ग्रंथों का अध्ययन करें, भगवान के दर्शन करें, पंढरपुर की यात्रा करें। सात्विक भोजन करें, हिंसा न करें, मांसाहार न करें। अच्छा आचरण रखें, परोपकारी बनें, परमार्थ की साधना करें। जीवन के बंधनों से धीरे-धीरे मुक्त होकर ईश्वर के साथ एकरूप होने का प्रयास करें।
वारकरी संप्रदाय की एक उल्लेखनीय विशेषता यह है कि इसमें भागवतधर्म का अनुयायी बनना जन्मजात अधिकार नहीं है, अपितु यह व्यक्ति की स्वेच्छा पर निर्भर करता है। यह दृष्टिकोण इस संप्रदाय को अधिक समावेशी और उदार बनाता है, जहाँ किसी भी पृष्ठभूमि का व्यक्ति अपनी आध्यात्मिक यात्रा शुरू कर सकता है।
इस संप्रदाय के सार को इन पंक्तियों में व्यक्त किया जा सकता है:

“अध्यात्माने तृप्त जीवन । नाही कुठली खंत
करील मानवतेचे रक्षण । हा वारकरीपंथ ।।”

अर्थात, वारकरी संप्रदाय एक ऐसा मार्ग है जो अध्यात्म से जीवन को तृप्त करता है और जो मानवता की रक्षा करता है। इस प्रकार, वारकरी संप्रदाय केवल एक धार्मिक आंदोलन नहीं, बल्कि एक समृद्ध जीवनधारा है जो सदियों से महाराष्ट्र की संस्कृति और समाज को प्रभावित कर रही है। यह संप्रदाय आध्यात्मिकता, मानवता और सामाजिक समरसता का एक अनूठा संगम है, जो आज के समय में भी उतना ही प्रासंगिक है जितना कि सदियों पहले था। वारकरी संप्रदाय की समृद्धि उसके अध्यात्म में है, और उसका अध्यात्म उसकी समृद्धि में। यह एक ऐसा पथ है जो अपने अनुयायियों के वैचारिक स्तर को उन्नत करने की अद्भुत क्षमता रखता है। वारकरी संप्रदाय एक ऐसा आध्यात्मिक पथ है जो हमें सिखाता है कि कैसे जीवन के प्रत्येक क्षण को एक तीर्थयात्रा में बदला जा सकता है। यह हमें याद दिलाता है कि सच्चा मंदिर हमारे हृदय में है, और सच्ची पूजा मानवता की सेवा में। यह संप्रदाय हमें आमंत्रित करता है कि हम भी इस आध्यात्मिक यात्रा में शामिल हों, जहाँ प्रत्येक कदम हमें ईश्वर के और करीब ले जाता है।


This article was originally written in Marathi without help from any GenAI models. Claude is used for converting it to Hindi.

title: India@100
text:
We have already burned through a quarter of this century. India stood at its start with a promise to own it. I remember when we were all charged up as kids that she would be a superpower by 2020, a goal set by Dr. APJ Abdul Kalam. It was ambitious and inspiring, and it gave us something to strive for collectively as a nation. We indeed got many things right and must be proud of those achievements, but we also missed many opportunities. Now, we cannot rest on partial victories. The mission is Viksit Bharat @2047. We must at least become a upper-middle-income nation, but that is not the end goal. The life of citizens must be the north star. I propose a framework with a three-prong heuristic through which most policy and directives can take cue. First, prevent unnatural death. Second, increase life expectancy. Third, enrich that long and healthy life.

The First Prong: Prevent Unnatural Death
The most immediate duty of governance is the preservation of life. We must aggressively arrest any and all deaths. This encompasses everything from maternal mortality to road accidents, deaths from malnutrition and preventable diseases, from farmer suicides and industrial accidents. Every single unnatural death is in some way a failure of governance.
When accidents claim lives that could have been saved, or disasters strike without adequate response systems, we fail at the basics (We need automated SOS alarm systems at scale that can respond to emergencies swiftly and effectively). We extend this concept to economic survival as well. The surge in regional inequality that has forced millions to migrate must be reversed through townification. More of our population has to shift out from agriculture, and we must modernize our farming with machines and automation to reduce the burden on farmers struggling under economic distress.
National security is equally vital to preventing the loss of life and sovereignty. Digging a well when thirsty cannot be the national strategy. Two things India heavily imports are defense equipment and energy. We cannot afford to be highly dependent on others for our security or our energy needs. We must be competent in our strengths for a free-market world, and simultaneously, we must build self-reliance in every sector. A bit far fetched under this prong, we also need to ramp up space exploration.


The Second Prong: Extending Life Span
Once survival is secured, we must focus on the span of existence. Increasing life expectancy requires a holistic approach to health and environment. It begins before birth. For a nation with demography as one of its strongest assets, every child born must have access to proper nutrition in the childhood and befor birth as well. This is when the foundation for a healthy, productive life is laid.
The reach of healthcare services must expand through targeted interventions. We need more primary health centers, more trained medical professionals in rural areas, and better health insurance coverage. Preventive healthcare should be prioritized over curative healthcare.
However, medicine alone cannot guarantee a long life if our environment is toxic. Our cities need to be livable, breathable, and beautiful. No more should our cities be designed for cars. They should be designed for people, for walking, cycling, and using public transit, with parks, lush greenery, lakes, and playgrounds. Clean air and green spaces directly impact how long and how well we live. The transition to renewable or clean energy is also about securing our future in a world increasingly constrained by climate change.
We must also guard against the forces that shorten life from within. Addictions of different kinds are giving today’s youth a slow death. Whether substance abuse, screen addiction, or the more subtle addictions to instant gratification, we are losing a generation to these forces.


The Third Prong: Enriching the Life
Yet, what is the point of living long if that life lacks meaning, opportunity, beauty, and dignity? Education, cultural enrichment, economic opportunity, social harmony, and environmental aesthetics make life worth living.
This enrichment begins with education. The promise of education as the great equalizer remained largely unfulfilled. We need great public schooling, and institutions like Navodaya need big upgrades. The school curriculum needs to be more explorative but also harder. We can make it accessible without making it easy or dumbing it down. Higher education needs an absolute revolution. The way to improve education in India is to put the best national and international minds as faculties. They won’t do it for living, they will do it because they care. Teaching in native languages and Ancient Indian Knowledge are good initiatives but are least impactful when fundamentals like basic infrastructure, quality teaching, student-teacher ratios, and learning outcomes are not fixed. Primary and secondary education must sensitise young adults on essential topics. We must arrest vulgarity in society spreading through mass media. Scientific temperament needs to reclaim front seat again.
We need to build an ecosystem where talent thrives. We need cities or even remote locations where foreign top talent can come, stay, and work. Brain drain has worsened because our best minds perceive limited pathways at home. When our environment fails to retain talent or attract global expertise, we compromise our ability to extend both the length and quality of life for all citizens.
Prosperity is the engine of a rich life (Aim is to have at least three spots out of the top 10 valued companies in the world). The means to prosperity is moving low-skilled labor to high-skilled jobs, which requires rethinking vocational training and labor laws. We must look beyond IT and services to green technology, biotechnology, and advanced manufacturing. Artificial Intelligence and robotics hold immense promise. We must aggressively push for space exploration. Furthermore, as a key data source in the modern world, we should monetize our data or control it with strong laws. Our demographic dividend can be our data dividend.
Cultural and aesthetic richness is equally important. Indian tourism is woefully underexploited. We have culture, history, and diversity that almost no country can match. Promoting tourism and our soft power through cinema, music, food, spirituality can open doors that hard power cannot. At the same time, we have to start travelling more internationally and exposing ourselves more to other cultures. The world is full of beauty and we should embrace it in our lifetimes. We want efficiency, but we also crave beauty. Jumbled electric wires are aesthetically ugly. Our cities need to be pleasing to the eye, as aesthetics impact our mental well-being.
Finally, a rich life requires a just society. Making sure that half of India gets representation in parliament is important, the number of women MPs has to increase rapidly. We need to strive for a society where individuals vote as citizens first, as Indians, not as members of narrower identities. The communalism offered to aspiring youthful India must be ended. Civil servants should have the courage and integrity to suggest what is best for the people. We need technologies like blockchain and ZKPs to make governance more efficient, transparent, and accountable.


Compounding
Compounding has immense power. To allow compounding of national progress, policies by and large should outlive the governments. Dialogue and broad consensus, decentralisation of power with clearer responsibilities helps ensure compounding continues by avoiding a single point of failure. Also, to not discourage thriving states, we need a better fund distribution algorithm which rewards good governance but also helps lagging states to catch up in other than financial means. Freebies, especially on backdrop of elections are extremely harmful as they take away the funds otherwise available for improving economic health. Radical technological innovation catalyses compounding manifold and IP protection, easier access to capital, no unnecessary bureaucratic red tape, culture of celebrating risk-taking and forgiving failures will help this happen.


The Pledge of 2047
We should ask, as a net result, does something help improve life? If the answer to all three prongs is no, we should reconsider. We don’t need premature juvenile pompous declarations of victory, What India truly needs is a silent redemption that is patient, persistent work where all arms of democracy fulfill their duties diligently. We need scientists in their labs, teachers in their classrooms, doctors in their clinics, engineers at their drawing boards, farmers in their fields, civil servants at their desks, and everyone and all working towards making India live better. The clock is ticking, and our destination 2047 is not as far away as it seems. Let us make sure we enjoy the journey. This is a road we must take because India deserves better, and her children deserve better. Humanity deserves better. Let us Make India Great Again, a country that is prosperous, just, free, and beautiful. By the end of 2047, when India celebrates a century of independence, we should be able to say that we indeed redeemed the pledge our forefathers made. The ambition of the greatest beings of our generation now, must be to wipe every tear from every eye and then bring a smile to every lip. That is the north star. That is the ultimate filter.

title: विटी
text:
एकदा मी बसलो होतो पिंपळाच्या झाडाखाली
तेवढ्यात पोरे गावची माझ्याभोवती गोळा झाली

मला म्हणाली दादा विटी आमची झाडावर अडकली
विजा गेलाय काढायला त्याला एक फांदी थडकली

मी म्हणालो बोला आहे काय रे इच्छा तुमची
पोरे म्हणाली दादा द्या विटी हो काढून आमची

ठीक म्हणून पॅडल मारून मी गेलो झाडापाशी
तेव्हा मला दिसली तिथे एक मधुमाशी

मुलांनी का बोलावलं तेव्हा मला समजलं
आपलं काही खरं नाही तेव्हाच मला उमजलं

दगड अडकवून गलोलीला विटीला मी मारला
तेव्हा मात्र आमचा सगळा घोळपा गंजीत लपला

नशीब आमचं पोळ्याला दगड लागला नाही
विटी मिळाली डंक बरा त्यांचा मिळाला नाही

एक तात्पर्य सांगतो वेळ काळ बघून घ्यावा
परिस्थिती जाणून मगच होकार तो द्यावा

-अविक्षित नचिकेत

title: विशाद
text:
भोवताली घडणाऱ्या आणि रोजच्या वाटणाऱ्या घटना, भोवतालच विश्व,
यावर विश्वासून, जीवनाच, आकलन आणि आरेखन करताना,
एक दिवस, सबंध भोवताल तुमच्यावर आक्रमून येतो,
ज्या वाटखुणांवर तुम्ही विश्वास टाकला, त्या एका दिवशी वादळामध्ये विरून जातात,
पाऊलखुणांची पायवाट, त्या दिवशी एकाएकी अदृश्य होते,
डोळे भरून जातात, गळा दाटून येतो,
कोणी माझे नाही, मी कोणाचा नाही, विशाद पेटून येतो
-अविक्षित नचिकेत

title: धर्मवीर
text:
धर्मासाठी जगला
आणि धर्मासाठी झिजला
शिवरायांचा पुत्रचि तो
एक राजा होऊन गेला
शिवरायांसोबत त्यांनी तलवार फिरवली
जिजाऊंसोबत त्यांनी अक्षरे गिरवली
हिंदवी स्वराज्य रक्षण करण्या
एक विद्वान जन्मला
मुघलांना जेरीस आणले त्यांच्या मुलखात जाऊन
दोन महिने रूप बदलले मथुरेत राहून
आजीच्या देखरेखी खाली
एक मुत्सद्दी वाढला
औरंगजेब चालून आला त्याला ज्यांनी चिरडला
केवळ स्वकियांच्या गद्दारीने अटकेत जो गेला
मर्द मराठी मातीचा
जणू गंधच हरपला
इस्लाम स्वीकार सरदार करतो औरंगजेब बोलला
सह्याद्रीचा वाघ तो त्या अतिशहाण्यावर गरजला
डोळे काढले जीभ कापली उपाशी मारले
त्वचा सोलली तरी मस्तक ताठ ठेवले
दृढ मनाने धर्माभिमानी
इतिहास रचला
-अविक्षित नचिकेत

title: महाराष्ट्रभूमी
text:
माझी महाराष्ट्र भूमी आहे थोरचि अपार
शौर्य वीरांचे घर समृद्धीचे खुले द्वार
लढले येथे छत्रपती रक्ते वीरांची सांडली
माझ्या महाराष्ट्र भूमीत एकता नांदली
माझी महाराष्ट्र भूमी सदा दुर्जनांशी झुंजली
शाहिरांचे पोवाडे नि कविता येथे गुंजली
मराठी भाषेवर केले सर्व रयतेने प्रेम
अतिथि देवो भव हाचि आमुचा नेम
कांदा भाकर पुरणपोळी तोंडी वाळके नि मुळा
जुनी संस्कृती आमुची अहो आहे नाद खुळा
राष्ट्राच्या विकासासाठी रयत तयार मरणाला
माझे अनंत प्रणाम मायभूमीच्या चरणाला
आहेत ध्यास नवे आहे संकल्प नवा
श्वासाश्वासामधून वाहे नाविन्याची हवा
-अविक्षित नचिकेत

title: गणेशोत्सव
text:
टिळकांनी सुरू केले जयाला तो खरा गणेशोत्सव चाललाय लयाला
ढोल ताशे गायब झाले तेथे डॉल्बी साहेब आले
मुल्ये गेली बाराच्या भावात 100 मंडळी एका गावात
विधायक कामे मागे पडली देणगीच्या जीवावर पार्टी झडली
एकतेचे धोरण गेले हरपुण नेते गेले तूप वरपून
उत्सवाची सर्वांनी पहावी स्थिती गणेशाची आराधना होतेच किती
नतमस्तक व्हा एकदंताच्या चरणी करा प्रार्थना भक्ती ठेवा मनी
-अविक्षित नचिकेत

title: आषाढ
text:
हा ऊन पावसाचा खेळ
चहूकडे पसरली हिरवळ
फुलून गेले बोडके माळ
पेरले गेले ज्वारी नि साळ
आषाढाचा खेळ नवा
वासुदेव वाजवी पवा
चालती वाजवत टाळ
वारकरी लेवूनी तुळशीची माळ
हे पिवळे पिवळे ऊन कोवळे
हे ढग आता काळे काळे
तेव्हा सदरा कधी न वाळे
ऊन पावसाचा खेळच चाले
अचानक आला हलकासा पाऊस
ऊन पडले कोवळे तांबूस
तेव्हा आई मला ओरडे
पहिले कर तुझे अंग कोरडे
झाली पृथ्वी हिरवीगार
वाहू लागलं वारं गार
आता हिवाळ्याची ओढ असे
लिहावे तेवढे थोडं असे
— अविक्षित नचिकेत

title: Bapu
text:
Though there is dark but as you are lying in our minds the bark of dogs will show us the way
Though there is extremism but as you are lying in our minds the shots of gun will teach us tolerance
Though many in Indian villages sleep without dinner but as you are lying in our mind we will be winner against hunger
Though we have reached the Mars the world is still under the threat of wars but as you are lying in our minds the kind and sensible people will come together
Bapu though many of us are blind but as you are lying in our mind the kind god will blow the wind and we will find our way
-Avikskhit Nachiketa

title: मोहन
text:
वर्षों तक जग में घूम-घूम
वियोग विघ्नों को चूम चूम
सह अब शब्द अपमान प्रखर
मोहन आया कुछ और निखर 
सौहार्द की राह बताने को
सबको एक साथ लाने को
अंग्रेजों को समझाने को
और भारत से हटवाने को
गांधीजी वापस भारत आए
सत्याग्रह की महारथ लाए

संभाले संग्राम की डोर चले
पकड़े अहिंसा की छोर चले
तिलक गोखले साथ थे
सर पर भारत मां के हाथ थे 
अंग्रेज चाहे नील उगाना
और असंगत कर भराना
चंपारण में पहुंचे गांधी
उठाव की जो लाई आंधी
अंग्रेजों को रुकना पड़ा
निवासियों सामने झुकना पड़ा

खिलाफत की रमजान मनाई
रामराज्य की याद दिलाई
डायर ने जब जीवन जलाया
गांधी ने असहयोग चलाया
अपनों को भी बहुत सुनाया
स्वराज का सपना आगे बढ़ाया
उपोषण किया स्वदेशी चलाया
प्रेम एवं सत्य का रास्ता दिखाया 
गांधी ने हुंकार किया
सविनय अवज्ञा का प्रचार किया
दांडी मार्च पर चले गांधी
संपूर्ण स्वराज बोले गांधी

गोलमेज का मेल चला
तोड़ो हुकूमत करो का खेल चला
जेल में उपवास चलाया
मंदिरों में छुआछूत जलाया

छोड़ो भारत की साख दिखाई
करो या मरो की आंख दिखाई
अंग्रेजों और अब सह ना सके
अखंड भारत की राह ले ना सके
सांप्रदायिकता की आग लगा के
भाई को भाई से भीड़ा के
किसी का सपना साकार हुआ
गांधी का मन निराकार हुआ

सब कुछ छोड़ने चले गांधी
समाज को जोड़ने चले गांधी
कट्टर पंथियों को अब जाग आई
गांधी के निषेध की मांग आई
हितवचन इन्हें न समझ आया
राम के उपासक को रावण बताया
गांधी के विचार को मारने चले
जो असाध्य था साधने चले
युवा हत्यारे 78 साल के बूढ़े से डर गए
राम का नाम लेते-लेते गांधी मारे नहीं अमर हो गए

-अविक्षित नचिकेत

title: औकात
text:
हम खुशियां बांटने निकले थे एक सौगात बताई गई
जानके हो या अनजाने में हमे हमारी औकात बताई गई
लेकिन औकात तो कोई नियतांक नही वक्त का फलन है
ताकत के वर्ग का दौलत से गुणन है
किंतु जब इस समीकरण पे शिक्षा और सादगी का घात छा जाएगा
हम बताएंगे नही तब पर सबको अपने आप समझ आ जायेगा
की ज्ञान दौलत सत्ता भगवान देता बांटने हेतु
रिश्ते नाते समाजों में बांधने सौहार्द के सेतु
यही सत्ता और शौहरत तों बहार का तौर है, आज नही तो कल झड़ जायेगा
कौशल और ज्ञान तो अशोक की वे जड़ें है, जिनसे इंसान किसीभी हालात से लड़ जायेगा
-अविक्षित नचिकेत

title: Operating Systems
text:
Operating Systems
A program that acts as an intermediary between a user and a computer and its hardware. Computer Hardware → Operating System → System and application programs → Users. Operating system is a resource allocator and a control program. Kernel is the one program that runs at all the times on a computer. A bootstrap program which is stored in ROM/EPROM [Erasable Programmable Read Only Memory]/EEPROM [Electrically EPROM] is loaded by kernel at power-up or reboot. CMOS [Complimentary metal oxide semiconductor] is a small memory in motherboard that stores BIOS [basic Input/output System] settings.
Now, I/O and CPU can execute concurrently. Direct memory access (DMA) [DMA is direct memory access] There are device controllers which are in-charge of particular device type and each of such device controller have its own local buffer. Each device controller has a device driver to manage I/O. I/O is from device to local buffer of controller where as CPU works between main memory and local buffers. Completion of I/O is informed to CPU causing interrupt. Interrupt transfer control to ISR [Interrupt Service Routine] using interrupt vector [Contains addresses of all the service routines]. A trap/exception [Exceptions are interrupts triggered by the CPU itself wich can further be clsassified as Aborts and Traps. Aborts are things that prevent the interrupted code from continuing. These are things that indicate a major problem - e.g. division by zero, hardware failures, etc. Whereas Traps are things that don’t prevent the interrupted code from continuing. These can be used for debugging, for virtual memory management, etc.] is software generated interrupt. Operating system is said to be interrupt driven. After an interrupt, OS preserves state of CPU by storing registers and Program counter. I/O subsystem is responsible for spooling [Overlapping og output of one job with input of other jobs], buffering and caching. There are two possible I/O structures : After I/O starts control returns to user program can be either be given only upon completion or can be returned without waiting for I/O completion. In the later case, OS uses 1) System calls are basically request to OS to allow user to wait for I/O completion and 2) Device status tables contains entry for each I/O device indicating its type, address and state.
Storage Structure : 1) RAM : Volatile, random access, storage media that CPU can access directly 2) Secondary storage is non-volatile extension of Main memory. Caching is copying information into a faster storage system temporarily. Magnetic Tapes → Optical Disks → Hard disks → Solid-state disks → Main memory → cache → registers. Cache is smaller than storage that is being cached and its management is a important design problem.



Von neumann Architecture


Von neumann Architecture
Multiprocessor systems are also known as parallel or tightly coupled systems. These have higher throughput, economy of scale and increase reliability because of graceful degradation and fault tolerance. In Asymmetric Multiprocessing each processor performs specific task whereas in symmetric each processor performs all the tasks. There are clustered systems which share storage via a SAN [Storage Area Network]. Asymmetric clustering is when one machine stays in hot stand-by mode whereas in symmetric all machines look after each other. Clustering can be used for HPC [High Performance Computing] using parallelisation. There is DLM [Distributed Lock Manager] mechanism to avoid conflicting operations. Cache coherency [all the CPUs shall have the most recent values in their cache] is required.
Multiprogramming and Multitasking : Multiprogramming is batch system where job scheduling is done so that single user cannot keep CPU and I/O busy at all the times and ensures that CPU always has one to execute. For ex, when I/O happens, we have to wait, so for that time OS switches to another Job that is kept in memory. Batch interfaces thus are non-interactive where user specifies all the details in advance for a batch job and receives output when all the processing is done. Multitasking on other hand is timesharing which is logical extension in which CPU switches job so frequently that users can interact with each job while it is running creating pretence of interactive computing. Swapping, virtual memory, CPU scheduling, managing response time are all part of multitasking.
Operating systems do have dual mode - 1) User Mode 2) Kernel Mode. Mode bit is provided by hardware. Now a days more and more CPUs are also supporting multi-mode operations.
A process is a program in execution. It is a unit of work within system. Each thread has a program counter specifying the location of next instruction to execute.
Kernals have singly linked list, doubly, circular linked list, binary search tree or even hash maps and bit maps. Computing environments can be traditional, mobile (android), distributed (Network Operating System), Client-Server, Peer-to-peer (Gnutella, Voice over IP, Napster), Virtualized (Citrix XenServer, VMware Elastic Sky X), cloud computing [Loda balancing is essential along with firwalls and cloud custome interface.] (Logical extension of virtualization ex, Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) : Now, these can be SaaS (software), PaaS (platform), IaaS (infrastructure) ), Real time embedded systems. There are various open source OSes which counter to copy protection and Digital Rights Management (DRM) systems.
Operating System Services include a) User interface b) Program execution c) I/O operations d) File system manipulations e) Communications f) Error detection g) Resource allocation h) Accounting i) Protection and Security



image.png


System call is a programming interface to service provided by OS which are typically written in C/C++ and accessed by programs via a high-level API calls rather than direct use. Typically a number is associated with each system call, system call interface maintain a table indexed according to this number.



API working of system calls


API working of system calls
Some systems have a program known as registry which is used to store and retrieve configuration information. Policy is what to do and mechanism is how to do?



Types of System Calls


Types of System Calls



Android Architecture


Android Architecture
Debugging in OS is finding and fixing errors/bugs. Log files are generated containing error info. Core dump [When Application fails] and Crash dump [When OS fails/crashes] and trace listing. Profiling is periodic sampling of instruction pointer to look for statstical trends. Kernighan s Law: Debugging is twice as hard as writing the code in the first place. Therefore, if you write the code as cleverly as possible, you are, by definition, not smart enough to debug it. Dtrace tool allows live instrumentation on production systems by firing probes within a provider and captures state data and sends it to consumers of these probes. SYSGEN program obtains information concerning configs of hardware and compiles system-tuned kernal which is more efficient for that particular set of hardware. GRUB [GNU GRand Unified Bootloader] allows selection of kernal from multiple disks, versions and options.

Process
In batch systems, processes are usually called jobs and in time-shared systems tasks. A process has multiple parts, viz. text section which is program code, program counter, process registers, stack for temporary data and data section with global variables and heap which is dynamically allocated memory during runtime. Thanks to virtualisation, each process has its own address space. Information associated with each process is stored in PCB (Process control block).



States of a process


States of a process
PCB also contains CPU scheduling info, memory management info, accounting info, I/O status information. Process number (PID)



image.png


Context of a process is stored by virtue of registers and PCB. So here one process have a single program counter i.e single thread. task_struct is used for representing a process in C. Process scheduler have Job Queue, ready queue, device queue and processes migrate among these queues.



image.png


Short term scheduler is frequently invoked to select next process for CPU allocation. Long term scheduler (Job scheduler) selects which processes shall be brought into ready queue in turn controlling degree of multiprogramming. The long term scheduler strives to maintain a good mix of I/O bound and CPU bound processes. If degree of multiprogramming needs to be decreased, a medium term scheduler can be added which swaps processes in and out of memory.
Parent processes can create children, forming a tree of processes. A child duplicates parent’s address space. System calls associated are,fork()→creation of child process, exec()→replace the process memory with new program exit()→a process calls after execution of last statement wait()→provides the status information and return pid, abort()→can terminate child process. Zombie: Whenever a child process is started an entry is added in process table and that entry is removed only after the child exits and parent gets this status of exit via wait system call which is called reaping, but if that wait call is not invoked, then this entry stays there called as zombie (even if wait is invoked small time period between exit and removing entry, that process is a zombie). Often parent is yet to invoke wait. Zombies are usually transferred to init for dealing. Orphan: If parent terminates without invoking wait the child is called orphan.
Processes within a system may be indpendent or cooperating. IPC [Interprocess communication] required. Shared memory and message passing are two models for IPC. Producer-Consumer problem deals shared memory model. Bounded buffer only Buffersize-1 item shared. In message passing send() and recieve() are used on a communication link which can be physical like bus, network, shared memory or logical (direct, indirect, synchronous/asyn, automatic/explicit buffering). Indirect uses mailboxes (ports). Blocking is used in synchronous IPC. If both sender and reciever are blocking we have a rendezvous. Buffering is not required in case of rendezvous. Buffering can be boudned or unbounded capacity. In windows message passing uses advanced local procedure calls (LPC).
In client server case, RPCs( remote procedure calls), pipes, sockets and remote method invocation (java) are used. Socket is defined as endpoints of communication. Concat IP and port to get socket. RPC abstracts procedure calls between processes on networked systems using ports for service differentiation. Stubs are used. Client-side stub: locates server, marshals parameters and Server-side stub: receives message, unpacks parameters, performs procedure. Windows: stub code compiled from MIDL (Msft Interface defn lang) specification and Data representation: XDL (external data representation) format to handle different architectures (big-endian, little-endian). OS provides rendezvous (matchmaker) service to connect client and server. Pipes are conduit. They can be ordinary where parent child relation required (unidirectional also called anonymous pipes), or named where it is not required (bidirectional).


Threads
A thread is a lightweight process that runs within the context of a parent process. Threads share the same memory space as the parent process. Threads are lightweight, requiring fewer resources to create and manage. Communication between threads is simpler and more efficient, as they share the same memory space. Ex, A web browser with multiple tabs, where each tab runs in a separate thread.
Amdahl’s law : \(speedup\) ≤ \(\frac{1}{S+\frac{(1-s)}{N}}\) where S is non-parallelizable portion.
Threads are of two types, user threads and kernel threads. Many user level threads are mapped to single kernel threads which might result in blocking. One to one mapping provides more concurrency but number of processes per thread is restricted because of overhead. Many to many model allows OS to create a sufficient number of kernel threads. Two level Many to many models allows to bound a user thread to particular kernel thread. POSIX is a library that provides API for managing and creating threads. Runnable in java allows to create threads. Thread pool can be created where threads await for work. Threads can be terminated before it finishes called thread cancellation which can be asynchronous (immediate) or deferred (periodic check for cancellation). A thread can be created with cancellation option disabled as well. Signals are used to notify a process that a event has occurred. Each thread can have TLS (Thread Local Storage) to have own copy of data.
Lightweight Process (LWP): An intermediate data structure between user and kernel threads. LWPs appear as virtual processors, allowing processes to schedule user threads to run.Each LWP is attached to a kernel thread. Scheduler activations provide a communication mechanism from the kernel to the thread library. This mechanism, called an upcall, allows the application to maintain the correct number of kernel threads.


Process Synchronisation
counter++ and counter-- concurrent execution enters in a race condition. Such part of the code where we change common variables, update tables, write files, etc are critical sections. General structure of process do { entry section → critical section → exit section → remainder section} while(true); . Solution of critical section problem requires 4 things, 1. Mutual exclusion 2. Progress and 3. Bounded waiting. OS handles CSP [Critical Section Problem] depending on if kernal is preemptive or not.
Peterson’s Solution : For two processes



image.png


Hardware support for CSP comes with locking and atomic (non-interruptable) hardware instruction.

Test and Set approach : Shared boolean variable lock is initialised to false.




image.png





image.png





Bounded wait in test and set


Bounded wait in test and set

Compare and Swap Approach :




image.png





image.png



Mutex Locks : software solution implemented using hardware atomic instructions acquire() and release() . It requires busy waiting and thus called spinlock.
Semaphore : S is an integer variable. Only 2 wait() and signal() atomic operations can use it. Wait call waits till S becomes positive and decrements it. Signal just increments it. Semaphore can be binary or counting. Semaphore with no busy waiting have a value and list or queue. It also has two operations block() and wakeup().



CPU scheduling
Dispatcher module gives control of the CPU to the process selected by short-term scheduler. It switches context, switches to user mode and jumps to proper location to restart that program. The time taken to do this is called dispatch latency. Gantt Chart is useful depiction of processes.
Scheduling Criteria : 1. CPU Utilisation : Percentage of time the CPU is busy executing processes. 2. Throughput : Number of processes completed per unit of time. 3. Turnaround Time : Time taken to execute a process from start to finish. 4. Waiting Time : Time spent by a process in the ready queue waiting for CPU access. 5. Response Time : Time taken to produce the first response after a request is submitted (for time-sharing environments).

FCFS : Convoy effect : Short processes behind long processes increase waiting time. (Like when a convoy has to pass, traffic stops)
Shortest Job First (SJF) : Processes with shortest length of next CPU burst executed. minimum average waiting time is achieved. Length of next CPU burst can be estimated using exponential averaging with \(\alpha\) set to 0.5 (usually).
Shortest Remaining time first : Preemptive version of SJF.
Priority Scheduling : An integer associated with each process, smaller is given higher priority. Starvation might be a problem and aging is a solution for the same.
Round Robin : Time quantum q is given. q should be large enough to respect context switching time. Higher turnaround but lower response time.
Multilevel Queue : foreground (interactive -RR) and background (batch - fcfs). Time slicing is done for each queue.

Threads are scheduled within PCS (process contention space). Kernel threads are scheduled in SCS (system-contention space).
In case of multiple processors, asymmetric scheduling ( one processor does for all) or symmetric (every processor does its own scheduling). Process has a affinity for processor on which it is currently running. Soft affinity comes from baggage of previous instance already in queue. Hard affinity comes when process joins only one specific queue. NUMA is non-uniform memory access. Little’s law : n = λ × W where n is the average queue length λ is the average arrival rate into the queue W is the average waiting time in the queue. In steady state, the number of processes leaving the queue must equal the number of processes arriving.
For additional resources and reference :
https://os-book.com/
https://os-book.com/OS10/study-guide/Study-Guide.pdf
Disclaimer : All the figures I added to my notes are from the “Operating System Concepts by Avi Silberschatz, Peter Baer Galvin, Greg Gagne”. They hold the copyrights.

title: Madhav Kadam
text:
https://www.janestreet.com/puzzles/subtiles-2-index/
Refer this puzzle. I want you to write its detailed solution step by step with brevity. Use the image (download it and refer the cells with their position (row, column) to write the solution.)
How I found the solution is
First of all grid contains 169 cells, assuming somehow we can fill all the cells, we get n(n+1)<=1692 where we fill numbers unto n. This gives us value that n has to be 17 since n must be integer. Thus we know that all the equations in the grid should resolve to a number between 1 to 17. Now we need to solve them for the same.
Few equations that I primaraily take cue from are (You find and refer them with their cells, in fact I want you to use markdown the create the grid and code your equations in a way they get rendered correctly in quarto qmd.) square roots, log and powers. Since b^2 is present b has to an integer among +-1,+-2,+-3, +-4. but (b-1)^2 removes -4 from consideration.
Because a-c is denominator a!=c. c-1 is denominator so c!=1. Now there is a cell with square root of c-a which means c>a and in another cell where value is b/(a-c), a-c is denominator which is negative, so to make overall number positive, we need to eliminate positive possibilities of b. So b is now -1/-2/-3.
Now there is a element c^b and b is negative integer which suggests c has to be of form 1/d where d is integer >1. and since we have a cell with log(base c) a gives away that a=c^k where k is an integer among 2 to 17 beacuse c>a. There is a cell in which neumerator is postive as it is square root and denominator is a which means a is positive implying c is also positive.
There is a cell with a^b - 12/a where if b=-1, we have -11/a which won’t work. So remove b=-1. Now there is also b/a-1. Which means if we put a as c^k = (1/d)^k we get -bdk/(dk-1). Now, let’s take b=-2 so 2dk/(dk-1) has to be an integer –> d^k has to be 2 or 3 and with current constraints on d and k we cannot have it. So b=-2 has to be ruled out. Only b=-3 is left. So we have here 3*dk/(dk-1) so d^k should be either 2 or 4. d^k=2 won’t work so d^k=4 and deterministicly we can conclude that d=2 and k=2. Thus c = 1/2 and a = 1/4.
a= 1/4, b=-3, c=1/2. We can now fill labeled cells.
//table
Now we have values at 37 cells. R4C11 has to be 12. R11C6 has to 14. Now just to connect exisiting 12 numbers we must exhaust all remaining 12. There are only 2 ways to do this. (In 3rd way we cut off 6s). But we can surely confirm R5,R6,R7 in C10 to be 12.

title: VLM Deployment
text:
Once a VLM is trained, the hard part begins: training it at scale across many GPUs and many nodes, then squeezing it into a phone, a smart-TV, or a kiosk while keeping latency low and accuracy intact. This document covers the full lifecycle: distributed training, communication tuning, edge-export formats, Tizen / mobile deployment, monitoring, and forensic troubleshooting.




The chapters are ordered the way a deployment engineer actually encounters them.

Configuring Multi-GPU Training
Managing Multi-Node Training
Optimizing Communication Overhead
Converting Models to Edge-optimized Formats
Optimizing for Tizen Platform
Implementing Mobile Inference
Monitoring Deployment Performance
Troubleshooting Optimized Models
Reading List & References

Every section gives mental model + PyTorch code + war-story-tier tips. The references at the end are the canonical sources you should cite in interviews and design reviews.







Before any code, you must know what you are sharding.








Parallelism
What is split across devices?
Best for




Data Parallel (DP / DDP)
Mini-batch
Model fits on one GPU


ZeRO-1
Optimizer states
DP + memory savings


ZeRO-2
Optimizer states + grads
More memory savings


ZeRO-3 / FSDP
Optimizer + grads + params
Models too big for one GPU


Tensor Parallel (TP)
Single layer’s matrices
Layers too big for one GPU


Pipeline Parallel (PP)
Whole stages of layers
Very deep models


Sequence / Context Parallel (SP/CP)
The sequence dimension
Very long contexts


Expert Parallel (EP)
MoE experts
MoE models



Modern VLMs use a combination — typically FSDP + TP, with SP for long-video models.



DDP replicates the full model on every GPU and averages gradients via NCCL all-reduce after each backward.
# launch with:  torchrun --nproc_per_node=8 train.py
import os, torch, torch.distributed as dist
from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, DistributedSampler

def setup():
    dist.init_process_group(backend="nccl")
    local_rank = int(os.environ["LOCAL_RANK"])
    torch.cuda.set_device(local_rank)
    return local_rank

local_rank = setup()
model = build_vlm().to(local_rank)
model = DDP(model, device_ids=[local_rank], find_unused_parameters=False,
            gradient_as_bucket_view=True, broadcast_buffers=False)

sampler = DistributedSampler(dataset, shuffle=True, drop_last=True)
loader  = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=8, sampler=sampler,
                     num_workers=4, pin_memory=True, persistent_workers=True)

opt    = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4)
scaler = torch.amp.GradScaler("cuda")

for epoch in range(EPOCHS):
    sampler.set_epoch(epoch)                     # different shuffle every epoch
    for batch in loader:
        with torch.autocast("cuda", dtype=torch.bfloat16):
            loss = model(**batch).loss
        loss.backward()                          # all-reduce happens here
        opt.step(); opt.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
Five DDP details that always come up:

device_ids must contain a single device — the local rank.
find_unused_parameters=True is required if any parameter is conditionally skipped in forward (e.g. a frozen LoRA branch). It costs CPU time; flip it off whenever possible.
gradient_as_bucket_view=True saves memory by giving you a view into the all-reduce bucket buffer instead of allocating new gradient tensors.
broadcast_buffers=False turns off the all-reduce of BatchNorm running stats — useless for ViTs/LLMs which use LayerNorm only.
set_to_none=True in zero_grad() keeps the gradient pointer null instead of a zero tensor, slightly faster.




To simulate a larger batch when memory is tight:
accum = 4
opt.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
for step, batch in enumerate(loader):
    is_last = (step + 1) % accum == 0
    ctx = model.no_sync() if not is_last else nullcontext()
    with ctx:
        with torch.autocast("cuda", dtype=torch.bfloat16):
            loss = model(**batch).loss / accum
        loss.backward()
    if is_last:
        opt.step(); opt.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
model.no_sync() skips the all-reduce during the inner accumulation steps — communication cost goes down by accum. Forget this and you will all-reduce on every micro-batch, paying the full network cost while gaining no math.



When the model does not fit on a single GPU, shard everything: parameters, gradients, optimizer states.
import functools
from torch.distributed.fsdp import (
    FullyShardedDataParallel as FSDP,
    MixedPrecision, ShardingStrategy, BackwardPrefetch,
)
from torch.distributed.fsdp.wrap import transformer_auto_wrap_policy
from transformers.models.qwen2.modeling_qwen2 import Qwen2DecoderLayer

mp = MixedPrecision(
    param_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    reduce_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    buffer_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
)

wrap_policy = functools.partial(
    transformer_auto_wrap_policy,
    transformer_layer_cls={Qwen2DecoderLayer},   # one shard per LLM block
)

model = FSDP(
    model,
    sharding_strategy=ShardingStrategy.FULL_SHARD,         # ZeRO-3
    auto_wrap_policy=wrap_policy,
    mixed_precision=mp,
    backward_prefetch=BackwardPrefetch.BACKWARD_PRE,       # overlap comm
    limit_all_gathers=True,
    device_id=torch.cuda.current_device(),
    use_orig_params=True,                                  # required for ParamGroups
)
Sharding strategies:

FULL_SHARD — ZeRO-3 (params + grads + opt states sharded).
SHARD_GRAD_OP — ZeRO-2 (grads + opt states only).
HYBRID_SHARD — full-shard inside a node, replicate across nodes (best for 2-node setups with fat intra-node NVLink and thin inter-node Ethernet).
NO_SHARD — equivalent to DDP.

FSDP2 (PyTorch ≥ 2.4) introduces fully_shard() as the preferred API — per-parameter sharding, cleaner ergonomics, full DTensor-native. Same conceptual model, nicer code.












Format
Range
Precision
Where to use




fp16
±65 504
~3 decimal
Inference, V100


bf16
±\(3.4\times 10^{38}\)
~2 decimal
Default training on A100/H100


fp8 (e4m3 / e5m2)
±448 / ±57 344
~1 decimal
H100 only, large speedup



bf16 is the de facto training dtype because it has the same exponent range as fp32 — no GradScaler required, no overflow surprises. fp16 only stays alive on V100s that have no bf16 hardware. fp8 needs the Transformer Engine library (pip install transformer_engine) and explicit scaling factors.



Recompute activations during backward instead of storing them — trades FLOPs for memory.
from torch.distributed.algorithms._checkpoint.checkpoint_wrapper import (
    apply_activation_checkpointing,
    CheckpointWrapper,
    CheckpointImpl,
)

def check_fn(submodule):
    return isinstance(submodule, Qwen2DecoderLayer)

apply_activation_checkpointing(
    model,
    checkpoint_wrapper_fn=functools.partial(
        CheckpointWrapper,
        checkpoint_impl=CheckpointImpl.NO_REENTRANT,
    ),
    check_fn=check_fn,
)
NO_REENTRANT is the modern variant and integrates cleanly with FSDP. Saves ~\(O(L)\) memory at the cost of one extra forward.



When a single transformer block does not fit on a GPU (e.g. 70 B+), split the matrices themselves:

Column-wise split on W_q, W_k, W_v and W_up, W_gate of MLP.
Row-wise split on W_o and W_down.
All-gather along the head dimension before the row-parallel layer.

In PyTorch (≥ 2.3):
from torch.distributed.tensor.parallel import (
    parallelize_module, ColwiseParallel, RowwiseParallel, SequenceParallel,
)
from torch.distributed.device_mesh import init_device_mesh

mesh = init_device_mesh("cuda", (DP, TP), mesh_dim_names=("dp","tp"))
tp_mesh = mesh["tp"]

plan = {
    "self_attn.q_proj": ColwiseParallel(),
    "self_attn.k_proj": ColwiseParallel(),
    "self_attn.v_proj": ColwiseParallel(),
    "self_attn.o_proj": RowwiseParallel(),
    "mlp.gate_proj":  ColwiseParallel(),
    "mlp.up_proj":    ColwiseParallel(),
    "mlp.down_proj":  RowwiseParallel(),
}
for blk in model.model.layers:
    parallelize_module(blk, tp_mesh, plan)
Sequence parallel additionally splits LayerNorm activations along the sequence axis between TP collective calls — relevant when sequences are very long (video, long-context VLMs).



Pipeline parallel (PP) splits the model into stages and pipelines micro-batches through them. PyTorch’s torch.distributed.pipelining (PiPPy) does this cleanly. PP shines when:

Model is deep (≥ 80 layers).
Inter-node bandwidth is the bottleneck (PP communicates only activations at stage boundaries, not gradients across all params).

For 7-13 B VLMs, PP is overkill; FSDP + TP is enough.











Size
Single node 8×H100 80 GB
Multi-node




1–3 B
DDP + bf16
seldom needed


7–13 B
FSDP FULL_SHARD + activation ckpt
HYBRID_SHARD across 2 nodes


30–70 B
FSDP + TP=2 + activation ckpt
FSDP + TP=4 across N nodes


100 B+
FSDP + TP=8 + PP=2/4
scale TP first, then PP











Inside a node, GPUs talk over NVLink/NVSwitch — 600–900 GB/s on A100/H100 nodes.
Across nodes, they go over Ethernet (25–100 Gbit/s) or InfiniBand (200–400 Gbit/s, ~5× faster). The bandwidth difference is 6–30×; the latency difference is even larger. Every architectural decision in multi-node training is a workaround for this fact.



torchrun (PyTorch’s own launcher, supersedes torch.distributed.launch):
# Node 0 (master):
torchrun \
  --nnodes=4 --nproc_per_node=8 \
  --node_rank=0 --rdzv_id=vlm_run01 --rdzv_backend=c10d \
  --rdzv_endpoint=node0.cluster:29500 train.py

# Node 1..3: same command with --node_rank=1, 2, 3
torchrun is elastic by default — workers can join and leave thanks to the c10d rendezvous backend.
SLURM (srun) — the dominant academic / national-lab scheduler:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=vlm
#SBATCH --nodes=4
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node=8
#SBATCH --gpus-per-task=1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=16
#SBATCH --time=24:00:00
#SBATCH --partition=h100

export MASTER_ADDR=$(scontrol show hostnames "$SLURM_JOB_NODELIST" | head -n1)
export MASTER_PORT=29500
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=8
export NCCL_DEBUG=WARN

srun python -u train.py
Inside train.py you read RANK, LOCAL_RANK, WORLD_SIZE from environment variables that SLURM/torchrun set automatically.



import os, torch, torch.distributed as dist

def setup():
    if "RANK" in os.environ and "WORLD_SIZE" in os.environ:
        dist.init_process_group(backend="nccl")
    else:
        # SLURM path
        rank   = int(os.environ["SLURM_PROCID"])
        world  = int(os.environ["SLURM_NTASKS"])
        local  = int(os.environ["SLURM_LOCALID"])
        dist.init_process_group(backend="nccl", rank=rank, world_size=world,
                                init_method=f"tcp://{os.environ['MASTER_ADDR']}:{os.environ['MASTER_PORT']}")
        os.environ["LOCAL_RANK"] = str(local)
    torch.cuda.set_device(int(os.environ["LOCAL_RANK"]))



NCCL is NVIDIA’s collective communication library; PyTorch’s nccl backend is a thin wrapper. The collectives you need to know:

all_reduce — every rank ends up with the sum (or other op) of every input. Used after loss.backward() to average gradients.
all_gather — every rank collects all inputs concatenated. Used by FSDP to materialise full parameters before forward.
reduce_scatter — sum, then scatter chunks back. Used by FSDP for gradients.
broadcast — one rank’s tensor goes to all others. Used to seed initial weights and to share the schedule.
barrier — synchronisation point.

NCCL chooses among several algorithms:









Algorithm
Bandwidth-optimal?
Latency
Notes




Ring
Yes for large messages
\(O(P)\) steps
classic


Tree
Better latency
\(O(\log P)\)
newer default for small msgs


CollNet (SHARP)
Hardware-accelerated on Mellanox switches
very low
InfiniBand only


NVLS (NVLink SHARP)
Intra-node H100 NVSwitch
very low
DGX H100 only



You can profile NCCL with NCCL_DEBUG=INFO; it prints the algorithm chosen for each collective.



A simple mental picture: an 8×H100 DGX node looks like
    GPU0—NVLink—GPU1—NVLink—GPU2—…
       \        /
        NVSwitch (intra-node, 900 GB/s pairwise)
       /        \
   GPU4    …   GPU7
   |                  |
   NIC0  NIC1  NIC2  NIC3  (InfiniBand HCAs, 1:1 with GPUs)
       \    |    |    /
        switch fabric (200–400 Gbit/s per port)
For best inter-node performance you must:

Use GPUDirect RDMA (NICs read GPU memory directly).
Pin each GPU process to the NUMA node owning its HCA via numactl.
Enable NCCL_IB_HCA to use the right HCA.
Set NCCL_IB_GID_INDEX=3 for RoCE v2, NCCL_IB_GID_INDEX=0 for IB.




export NCCL_DEBUG=WARN                  # INFO for first run, WARN afterwards
export NCCL_ASYNC_ERROR_HANDLING=1      # crash on collective error
export NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME=^lo,docker0   # which NIC for bootstrap
export NCCL_IB_HCA=mlx5_0,mlx5_2        # which HCAs to use
export NCCL_IB_TIMEOUT=22               # higher for flaky fabrics
export NCCL_IB_RETRY_CNT=7
export NCCL_NET_GDR_LEVEL=5             # max GPUDirect RDMA
export NCCL_P2P_LEVEL=NVL               # force NVLink intra-node
export NCCL_NVLS_ENABLE=1               # H100 NVSwitch SHARP
export NCCL_ALGO=Tree,Ring              # let NCCL choose
export NCCL_PROTO=Simple,LL,LL128       # protocols, lowest latency to highest BW
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=8                # avoid CPU oversubscription



A 64-GPU job runs for a week and hits a memory error. What do you do?

Checkpoint frequently (every 250–1000 steps) but asynchronously (next chapter).
torchrun elastic restarts crashed workers automatically.
torch.distributed.elastic monitors and signals workers.
TorchSnapshot / Distributed Checkpoint (DCP) writes sharded checkpoints in parallel — orders of magnitude faster than gathering everything to rank 0.

import torch.distributed.checkpoint as dcp

state = {"model": model.state_dict(), "opt": opt.state_dict(), "step": step}
dcp.save(state, checkpoint_id=f"/ckpt/step_{step}")
Each rank writes its own shard; loading reverses the process. DCP is the default in PyTorch 2.4+.



A 64-GPU job demands ~64× the data throughput of a single GPU. Best practices:

Sharded data: WebDataset or MosaicML Streaming format — many tar shards on object store, each rank streams a disjoint set.
num_workers ≈ 4 × CPU/GPU with pin_memory=True.
persistent_workers=True to avoid re-spawning every epoch.
prefetch_factor=2 to overlap CPU and GPU.
Image decoding on GPU with NVIDIA DALI for VLMs — JPEG → tensor is often the bottleneck.









For a parameter \(P\), an all-reduce takes time
\[
T_{\text{ar}}(P) \approx \alpha + \beta \cdot 2 \frac{N-1}{N} \cdot P
\]
where \(\alpha\) is latency, \(\beta = 1/\text{bandwidth}\), and the factor \(\frac{2(N-1)}{N}\) comes from the ring-all-reduce algorithm. For \(N\) large, this becomes \(2P/B\) — twice the parameter bytes over the network.
A single bf16 7 B model has \(2 \cdot 7\text{B} = 14\) GB. On a 100 Gbit/s NIC = 12.5 GB/s, one all-reduce takes 1.1 seconds. Compare to ~0.4 seconds of compute for the backward pass → communication dominates unless we hide it.



PyTorch DDP automatically buckets gradients and starts all-reducing the bucket as soon as it is filled — overlap with the rest of the backward pass. Two knobs:

gradient_as_bucket_view=True — saves memory.
bucket_cap_mb (default 25 MB) — larger buckets = fewer collectives but less overlap. For 7-13 B models, 50-100 MB is often optimal.

For FSDP, set backward_prefetch=BackwardPrefetch.BACKWARD_PRE so the next shard’s parameters are all-gathered while you compute the current backward. The matching forward_prefetch=True does the analogous thing in forward.



Trade communication for accuracy:

fp16 all-reduce with MixedPrecision(reduce_dtype=torch.float16) — halves bytes.
PowerSGD (Vogels et al., 2019) — rank-r low-rank approximation. Built into PyTorch DDP.

import torch.distributed.algorithms.ddp_comm_hooks.powerSGD_hook as ps
state = ps.PowerSGDState(process_group=None, matrix_approximation_rank=4,
                         start_powerSGD_iter=1_000)
model.register_comm_hook(state, ps.powerSGD_hook)
PowerSGD is essentially free for many models after a warm-up period.



DDP reduces buckets in the order parameters are registered. To maximise overlap, register parameters in forward order (default if you wrap a nn.Module directly).
For FSDP, set the auto-wrap policy at the right granularity — each FSDP unit is a single all-gather. Too many small units = collective overhead; too few large units = no overlap.



ZeRO-3 / FSDP FULL_SHARD has the lowest memory but the most communication: every parameter is gathered and re-discarded once per forward + once per backward. ZeRO-2 (SHARD_GRAD_OP) gathers parameters once (they are kept) but still scatters gradients. Choose:

Memory-bound? → ZeRO-3.
Communication-bound? → ZeRO-2 or HYBRID_SHARD.

HYBRID_SHARD is the sweet spot for 2–4 node setups: full-shard inside the fast NVLink island, replicate across the slow Ethernet/IB.



When you use TP and SP, you must overlap the all-gather of activations before each row-parallel matmul with the previous compute. PyTorch’s parallelize_module does this automatically if you pass use_local_output=False.



A few real-world tunes:

For a 2-node A100-40 GB setup with 200 Gbit/s IB, NCCL_ALGO=Ring NCCL_PROTO=LL128 gives best throughput for 1 GB messages.
