DFR / LM-Dirichlet — language-model retrieval; competitive with BM25.
SPLADE / uniCOIL — learned sparse retrievers. A transformer encoder outputs sparse, term-weighted representations that go straight into an inverted index. Combines neural quality with sparse efficiency.





10.2 Vector-Based Indexing for High-Dimensional Data
See section 7.2 for the full coverage of HNSW, IVF, IVF-PQ, ScaNN, DiskANN, and FAISS. Here is the which-tool-when table for vector databases:









System
Substrate
Strengths
When to choose




FAISS
library
fast, many index types, GPU
self-hosted, embed in a service


Milvus
distributed DB on top of FAISS-like indexes
scale-out, ANN + filtering, kubernetes-native
large multi-tenant deployments


Weaviate
DB, HNSW backend, GraphQL API
hybrid (BM25 + vector), built-in modules for embedders
want batteries-included with hybrid out of the box


Qdrant
DB, Rust, HNSW
strong filtering, quantisation built-in, async-friendly
latency-sensitive, complex filters


Pinecone
managed cloud
zero-ops, serverless
when you don’t want to host infra


pgvector (Postgres)
extension
hybrid with SQL, transactional
already on Postgres, modest scale


Vespa
search platform
ranking-first, tensor expressions, hybrid
very large web-scale, multi-stage ranking



Engineering trade-offs to remember:

Recall vs latency vs memory. Picked by index type (Module 7.2) and its knobs.
Freshness vs recall. HNSW supports incremental inserts cheaply; IVF-PQ needs occasional retraining of centroids.
Filtering vs ANN. Naïve post-filtering after ANN can lose recall. Modern engines push filters into the ANN traversal (Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus all support filterable HNSW).
Memory vs disk. A 768-dim float32 vector × 100M = 300 GB RAM. With int8 PQ codes at 64 bytes/vector you fit in 6 GB. With DiskANN you can spill to NVMe and stay under 32 GB RAM.

title: Information Retrieval
text:
MODULE 11 — BUILDING AND INFERRING KNOWLEDGE

11.1 Integrating Semantic Search into Knowledge Management
The general pattern of an enterprise semantic search / knowledge base / RAG system is the same retrieval architecture described above, plus:

Source connectors — pull data from SharePoint, Confluence, Slack, Salesforce, Drive, S3, internal databases.
Permissioning — each chunk carries an ACL; retrieval respects the calling user’s permissions.
Freshness pipeline — incremental re-embedding when source documents change.
Provenance metadata — every chunk carries a source URL, title, author, last-modified timestamp.
Hybrid retrieval — BM25 for codes/IDs + dense for natural language (Module 7).
Reranker — section 8.2.
Generator — see RAG/index.qmd.
Citations — return source IDs alongside the answer so users can verify.

For knowledge management specifically, graph-based retrieval (next subsection) becomes important when relationships matter as much as content.


11.2 Knowledge Graphs and Reasoning
A knowledge graph (KG) represents the world as entities (nodes) connected by typed relations (edges). The atomic statement is a triple (subject, predicate, object), e.g., (Paris, capital_of, France).

11.2.1 RDF, triples, SPARQL







Concept
Description




Triple
(s, p, o). The basic unit.


RDF
Resource Description Framework — W3C standard for triples.


Turtle / N-Triples
RDF serialisations.


OWL
Web Ontology Language — schema layer with class hierarchies and property constraints.


SPARQL
Query language for RDF triples.



A SPARQL query:
PREFIX dbo: <http://dbpedia.org/ontology/>
SELECT ?city WHERE { ?city dbo:country dbr:France . }
returns every entity in DBpedia known to be a city in France. Joins are graph patterns — query writers think in patterns, not tables.


11.2.2 Embedding-based KG reasoning
Symbolic KGs are clean but sparse — many true triples are missing. Link prediction uses learned entity and relation embeddings to score the plausibility of unobserved triples.
TransE (Bordes et al. 2013):
\[
\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{r} \approx \mathbf{t} \quad \text{when} \quad (h, r, t) \text{ holds}
\]
Score: \(-\|\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{t}\|_2\). Loss is a margin ranking loss between true triples and corrupted (negative) triples.
DistMult (Yang et al. 2014):
\[
\text{score}(h, r, t) = \mathbf{h}^\top \text{diag}(\mathbf{r}) \mathbf{t} = \sum_i h_i r_i t_i
\]
Bilinear, symmetric. Cannot model anti-symmetric relations (father_of).
ComplEx (Trouillon et al. 2016):
\[
\text{score}(h, r, t) = \text{Re}\bigl(\langle \mathbf{h}, \mathbf{r}, \overline{\mathbf{t}}\rangle\bigr) = \sum_i \text{Re}(h_i r_i \overline{t_i})
\]
Complex-valued embeddings; can model anti-symmetric relations because \(\langle h, r, t \rangle \neq \langle t, r, h \rangle\) in general.
RotatE (Sun et al. 2019):
\[
\mathbf{t} = \mathbf{h} \circ \mathbf{r}, \quad |r_i| = 1
\]
Each relation is a rotation in complex space. Captures symmetry, anti-symmetry, inversion, and composition all in one model.
# PyTorch sketch for TransE
import torch, torch.nn as nn

class TransE(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_ent, n_rel, d=100):
        super().__init__()
        self.E = nn.Embedding(n_ent, d)
        self.R = nn.Embedding(n_rel, d)
        nn.init.uniform_(self.E.weight, -6/d**0.5, 6/d**0.5)
        nn.init.uniform_(self.R.weight, -6/d**0.5, 6/d**0.5)

    def score(self, h, r, t):
        return -torch.norm(self.E(h) + self.R(r) - self.E(t), p=2, dim=-1)

    def forward(self, pos, neg):
        # margin loss
        return torch.relu(1.0 - self.score(*pos) + self.score(*neg)).mean()


11.2.3 GNNs for KGs
Graph Neural Networks generalise message-passing to typed graphs. R-GCN (Schlichtkrull et al. 2018) maintains a separate weight matrix per relation; the message a node receives from each neighbour is transformed by the appropriate relation matrix.
CompGCN, R-GAT, and GraIL further specialise GNNs for KGs. GNN-based link prediction outperforms classical KG embeddings on harder benchmarks (FB15k-237, WN18RR) and is essential for inductive settings — predicting links involving entities unseen at training.


11.2.4 Multi-hop reasoning
A multi-hop question requires composing several relations: “Where was the director of Inception born?” needs director(Inception) → person, then birthplace(person) → place.
Approaches:

Path-based. Enumerate paths of length \(\le L\) in the KG; pick the best one.
Embedding-based. Compose relations in the embedding space (RotatE’s composition closure is convenient).
Neural module networks / GraphSAGE / GAT — message-pass through the graph and read a final answer node.
KG + LLM hybrid — give the LLM tool-use access to a SPARQL endpoint, let it issue queries to traverse the KG.

title: Information Retrieval
text:
MODULE 12 — CLUSTERING AND CLASSIFYING DOCUMENTS
Clustering puts similar documents together without labels (unsupervised). Classification puts documents into predefined categories with labels (supervised, covered briefly in 1.1 and elsewhere).


12.1 Clustering Models for Document Categorisation

12.1.1 k-means on TF-IDF or embeddings
Classical k-means alternates two steps:
initialise k centroids
repeat until convergence:
    assignment:   cluster_i ← arg min_c distance(x_i, μ_c)
    update:       μ_c ← mean of points in cluster c
The objective minimised is the within-cluster sum of squares:
\[
J = \sum_{c=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_c} \|x - \mu_c\|_2^2
\]
import numpy as np
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer

docs = open("docs.txt").read().splitlines()
X = TfidfVectorizer(max_df=0.9, min_df=2).fit_transform(docs)
km = KMeans(n_clusters=20, n_init=10, random_state=0).fit(X)
clusters = km.labels_
For embedding-based clustering, replace the TF-IDF vectors with sentence-transformer embeddings.
Strengths: fast, scales to millions of documents, well-understood. Weaknesses: \(k\) is a hyperparameter; sensitive to scale and initialisation; assumes roughly spherical, equal-size clusters; cannot handle noise.


12.1.2 HDBSCAN
HDBSCAN (Hierarchical DBSCAN, Campello et al. 2013) is the modern default for document clustering when embeddings are available.
build a mutual-reachability graph
build a minimum spanning tree
condense the tree
extract the "most stable" clusters
Properties:

No need to specify \(k\).
Automatically labels noise points as \(-1\).
Handles variable-density clusters and arbitrary shapes.
Hyperparameters: min_cluster_size (the minimum number of points for a stable cluster) and min_samples (how many neighbours are needed to count as “core”).

import hdbscan, numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

X = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2").encode(docs,
        normalize_embeddings=True, show_progress_bar=True)
clusterer = hdbscan.HDBSCAN(min_cluster_size=20, metric="euclidean")
labels = clusterer.fit_predict(X)
In high-dim embedding spaces, it is standard to reduce dimensionality first with UMAP (to 5–50 dims) before running HDBSCAN — UMAP preserves local structure while removing noise.


12.1.3 Hierarchical / agglomerative clustering
Bottom-up: start each point as its own cluster, repeatedly merge the two closest clusters until one remains. The linkage criterion controls “closeness”:



Linkage
Distance between clusters




Single
min distance between any pair


Complete
max distance between any pair


Average
mean pairwise distance


Ward
variance-minimising merge



Output is a dendrogram — useful for exploratory work. Cut the dendrogram at a chosen height to get a flat clustering.


12.1.4 BERTopic
BERTopic (Grootendorst 2022) is a pipeline tailored to document clustering and topic naming:

Embed documents with a sentence-transformer.
Reduce to ~5–50 dim with UMAP.
Cluster with HDBSCAN.
For each cluster, compute a class-based TF-IDF (c-TF-IDF): treat all documents in a cluster as one super-document, then compute term importance relative to other clusters.
Top c-TF-IDF terms label the cluster.

from bertopic import BERTopic
topic_model = BERTopic(language="english")
topics, probs = topic_model.fit_transform(docs)
print(topic_model.get_topic_info())
BERTopic is best thought of as clustering with interpretable labels rather than a probabilistic topic model.


12.1.5 Pseudo-code for k-means
function kmeans(X, k, max_iter):
    μ ← k random points from X
    for it in 1..max_iter:
        for each x in X:
            assign x to nearest μ_c
        for each c in 1..k:
            μ_c ← mean of points assigned to c
        if assignments did not change: break
    return assignments, μ




12.2 Evaluating Clustering Models
Two regimes: internal metrics (no ground-truth labels) and external metrics (compared to a known labelling).

12.2.1 Internal metrics
Silhouette coefficient. For each point \(x\), let \(a\) = mean distance to other points in its own cluster, and \(b\) = mean distance to points in the nearest other cluster:
\[
s(x) = \frac{b - a}{\max(a, b)}, \qquad s(x) \in [-1, 1]
\]
Average \(s\) across all points. Higher is better.
Davies–Bouldin Index. For each cluster \(c\), find its worst “neighbour” cluster \(c'\) — the one maximising the ratio \(\frac{\sigma_c + \sigma_{c'}}{d(\mu_c, \mu_{c'})}\) where \(\sigma\) is intra-cluster dispersion. Average these ratios. Lower is better.
Calinski–Harabasz Index (Variance Ratio Criterion).
\[
\text{CH} = \frac{\text{tr}(B_k) / (k - 1)}{\text{tr}(W_k) / (N - k)}
\]
where \(B_k\) = between-cluster dispersion and \(W_k\) = within-cluster dispersion. Higher is better.


12.2.2 External metrics
Normalised Mutual Information (NMI). Mutual information \(I(C, Y)\) between predicted clusters \(C\) and gold labels \(Y\), normalised by the average of their entropies:
\[
\text{NMI} = \frac{I(C, Y)}{(H(C) + H(Y)) / 2}
\]
NMI \(\in [0, 1]\). 1 = perfect alignment.
Adjusted Rand Index (ARI). Counts pairs of points that are clustered together / apart in both labellings, adjusted for chance.
\[
\text{ARI} = \frac{\text{RI} - \mathbb{E}[\text{RI}]}{\max(\text{RI}) - \mathbb{E}[\text{RI}]}
\]
ARI \(\in [-1, 1]\). 0 = random labelling; 1 = perfect alignment; negative = worse than random.
Purity. For each cluster, take the most frequent gold class; sum over clusters and divide by \(N\). Easy to interpret but trivially high when \(k\) is large.
V-measure. Harmonic mean of homogeneity (each cluster contains only members of a single class) and completeness (all members of a given class are in the same cluster).


12.2.3 Practical guidance

Use internal metrics to tune \(k\) or min_cluster_size. They are imperfect (Silhouette favours convex clusters) but they don’t require labels.
Use external metrics when you have any partial labelling (subject-matter experts label a few hundred documents).
Always inspect a sample of documents per cluster — numbers alone hide qualitative failures.

title: Information Retrieval
text:
MODULE 13 — MODELING TOPICS
Topic models attempt to discover latent “topics” in a corpus — each document is a mixture of topics, each topic is a distribution over words.


13.1 Topic Modelling Algorithms

13.1.1 LSA / LSI
Latent Semantic Indexing (Deerwester et al. 1990): take the term-document matrix \(M\) (after TF-IDF weighting) and truncate its SVD to rank \(k\):
\[
M \approx U_k \Sigma_k V_k^\top
\]
Rows of \(U_k\) are term embeddings; columns of \(V_k^\top\) are document embeddings. The \(k\) singular values define implicit topics. LSI captures synonymy and polysemy approximately and is fast to build, but the “topics” are not probabilistic and the singular vectors have mixed signs that resist clean interpretation.


13.1.2 pLSA
Probabilistic Latent Semantic Analysis (Hofmann 1999): generative model where each word is drawn from a topic-conditional distribution and each document is a mixture of topics:
\[
P(w \mid d) = \sum_{z} P(w \mid z) \cdot P(z \mid d)
\]
Fit by EM. Improves on LSI by being properly probabilistic, but the per-document topic distribution is not parameterised — it has \(N \cdot K\) parameters (no prior on \(\theta_d\)), so the model overfits and cannot generalise to unseen documents.


13.1.3 LDA — Latent Dirichlet Allocation
LDA (Blei, Ng, Jordan 2003) puts a Dirichlet prior on the per-document topic distribution and the per-topic word distribution. Generative story:
for each topic k = 1..K:
    φ_k ~ Dirichlet(β)      # word distribution over topic k
for each document d:
    θ_d ~ Dirichlet(α)      # topic distribution for d
    for each word position i in d:
        z_{d,i} ~ Categorical(θ_d)
        w_{d,i} ~ Categorical(φ_{z_{d,i}})
with hyperparameters \(\alpha\) (concentration on topic mixtures; small \(\alpha\) → sparse mixtures) and \(\beta\) (concentration on topic-word distributions).
The posterior \(P(\theta, \phi, z \mid w)\) is intractable. Two inference methods are common:

Variational Inference (VI). Approximate the posterior with a factorised distribution; optimise the ELBO.
Collapsed Gibbs sampling. Sample each \(z_{d,i}\) conditional on all others, with \(\theta\) and \(\phi\) integrated out.

Collapsed Gibbs pseudo-code:
initialise z randomly for every token
repeat until convergence:
    for each token w_{d,i}:
        decrement counts for z_{d,i}
        sample z_{d,i} from
            P(z = k | ...) ∝ (n_{d,k}^{-i} + α) · (n_{k, w}^{-i} + β) / (n_k^{-i} + V β)
        increment counts for the new z_{d,i}
LDA in code:
import gensim
from gensim import corpora

texts = [doc.lower().split() for doc in docs]
dictionary = corpora.Dictionary(texts)
bow = [dictionary.doc2bow(t) for t in texts]
lda = gensim.models.LdaModel(bow, num_topics=20,
                             id2word=dictionary,
                             passes=10, alpha="auto", eta="auto")
for k, terms in lda.print_topics(num_words=10):
    print(f"Topic {k}: {terms}")


13.1.4 NMF
Non-negative Matrix Factorisation factorises the term-document matrix \(M \approx W H\) subject to \(W, H \ge 0\). Like LSI, but the non-negativity constraint makes the resulting “topics” (columns of \(W\)) interpretable as additive parts.
NMF is fast, deterministic given seed, and often produces more interpretable topics than LDA on short documents (tweets, headlines). It is not probabilistic.
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.decomposition import NMF
X = TfidfVectorizer(stop_words="english").fit_transform(docs)
nmf = NMF(n_components=20, init="nndsvd", random_state=0).fit(X)


13.1.5 Embedding-based topic models

Top2Vec — embed documents and words in the same space (e.g., with doc2vec or sentence-transformers); cluster document vectors with HDBSCAN; the closest words to each cluster centroid become the topic.
BERTopic — see 12.1.4. Strictly speaking BERTopic is clustering, but its c-TF-IDF labels make it the de-facto modern topic model in many production stacks.
Neural Topic Models (NTMs) — e.g., ProdLDA, ETM, CombinedTM. Use variational autoencoders to learn topics with neural networks; can incorporate pretrained word embeddings.





13.2 Comparing Topic Modelling Approaches











Method
Probabilistic?
Inputs
Topic interpretability
Speed
Best for




LSI
no
TF-IDF
low (signed singular vectors)
fast
first-pass retrieval expansion


pLSA
yes
bag of words
medium
medium
small fixed corpora


LDA
yes
bag of words
high (Dirichlet sparsity)
medium
classic, well-understood


NMF
no
TF-IDF
high (non-negative parts)
very fast
short texts, interpretability


Top2Vec
no
sentence embeddings
high
medium
discovery on noisy corpora


BERTopic
no
sentence embeddings
very high
medium
modern default


NTM (ETM, ProdLDA)
yes
bag of words + embeddings
high
slower
research-grade



Practical evaluation metrics for topic quality:

Topic coherence (UMass, UCI, NPMI) — does the top-N words for a topic co-occur in the corpus more than chance?
Topic diversity — are the topics’ top-N word lists distinct?
Held-out perplexity / log-likelihood (LDA only).
Stability — re-run with a different seed and measure cluster overlap (ARI). Highly unstable topics are weak.

title: Information Retrieval
text:
MODULE 14 — RECOGNISING NAMED ENTITIES
NER labels tokens with entity types: PERSON, ORG, LOC, DATE, MONEY, PRODUCT, custom types like DRUG, GENE, BILL_NO.


14.1 Training NER Models

14.1.1 BIO and BIOES tagging
Tokens carry labels indicating position within an entity span:



Scheme
Tags




BIO
B-X, I-X, O


BIOES (a.k.a. IOBES)
B-X, I-X, O, E-X, S-X



Example with BIO over "Tim Cook visited Apple Park yesterday":
Tim       B-PER
Cook      I-PER
visited   O
Apple     B-ORG
Park      I-ORG
yesterday B-DATE
BIOES is more expressive (E- marks the end of a multi-token span, S- marks a single-token entity), at the cost of more labels.


14.1.2 The classical NER pipeline — CRF
Conditional Random Fields (Lafferty et al. 2001) score a sequence of tag assignments jointly:
\[
P(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{Z(\mathbf{x})} \exp\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{T} \sum_{k} \lambda_k f_k(y_{i-1}, y_i, \mathbf{x}, i) \right)
\]
Features \(f_k\) include word identity, prefix/suffix, capitalisation, POS, gazetteer hits. Inference is Viterbi.


14.1.3 BiLSTM-CRF
(Huang et al. 2015): a bidirectional LSTM produces a feature vector per token; a CRF layer on top decodes the most likely tag sequence. This was the SOTA from 2016 to 2018.


14.1.4 BERT-CRF
Replace the BiLSTM with a transformer encoder. The encoder produces contextual token embeddings; a linear layer projects to tag logits; a CRF (or a softmax) decodes the tags. BERT-CRF is the current standard for production NER.
from transformers import (AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForTokenClassification,
                          Trainer, TrainingArguments,
                          DataCollatorForTokenClassification)
from datasets import load_dataset
import numpy as np, evaluate

ds = load_dataset("conll2003")
labels = ds["train"].features["ner_tags"].feature.names
label2id = {l: i for i, l in enumerate(labels)}
id2label = {i: l for l, i in label2id.items()}

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-cased")

def align(examples):
    enc = tok(examples["tokens"], truncation=True, is_split_into_words=True)
    aligned_labels = []
    for i, label in enumerate(examples["ner_tags"]):
        word_ids = enc.word_ids(batch_index=i)
        prev, out = None, []
        for wid in word_ids:
            if wid is None: out.append(-100)
            elif wid != prev: out.append(label[wid])
            else:             out.append(label[wid] if label[wid] % 2 == 0 else label[wid])
            prev = wid
        aligned_labels.append(out)
    enc["labels"] = aligned_labels
    return enc

ds = ds.map(align, batched=True)

model = AutoModelForTokenClassification.from_pretrained(
    "bert-base-cased", num_labels=len(labels),
    id2label=id2label, label2id=label2id,
)
Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=TrainingArguments(output_dir="ner", learning_rate=3e-5,
                           per_device_train_batch_size=16,
                           num_train_epochs=3, fp16=True, eval_strategy="epoch"),
    train_dataset=ds["train"], eval_dataset=ds["validation"],
    data_collator=DataCollatorForTokenClassification(tok),
).train()


14.1.5 Span-based NER
Instead of per-token tagging, enumerate every span up to a maximum length and classify each span as one of the entity types (or null). Loss is multi-class cross-entropy over spans.
Pros: handles nested entities natively (token-tag schemes cannot); avoids constraint that an I-X must follow a B-X. Cons: \(O(L^2)\) spans per sentence with maximum span length \(L\).


14.1.6 Generative NER
GPT/T5 can be prompted or fine-tuned to extract entities by generating a structured output (JSON, BIO tags, or natural-language list). Models such as UniversalNER, InstructUIE, GoLLIE follow this pattern.
Input: Extract PERSON, ORG, LOC entities from:
"Tim Cook visited Apple Park yesterday."

Output:
- PERSON: Tim Cook
- ORG: Apple Park
Pros: zero-shot and few-shot capability; handles new entity types by editing the instruction. Cons: slow, expensive, calibration issues; structured-output parsing is fragile.


14.1.7 Evaluation
NER is evaluated with exact-span F1:

A predicted span is a true positive only if both the boundary and the type match the gold span.
Per-type F1 and micro/macro averages are reported.

seqeval is the standard library.
from seqeval.metrics import classification_report
print(classification_report(gold_tag_sequences, pred_tag_sequences))




14.2 Rule-Based NER for Specialised Domains
When the vocabulary is constrained and ROI matters, rules beat neural models for parts of the schema.

14.2.1 Regex and pattern matching
import re

PATTERNS = {
    "EMAIL":      re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Z]{2,}\b"),
    "PHONE":      re.compile(r"\+?\d[\d\-\s]{7,}\d"),
    "ORDER_ID":   re.compile(r"\bORD-\d{6}\b"),
    "INVOICE_NO": re.compile(r"\bINV-\d{4}-\d{4}\b"),
}

def extract(text):
    found = []
    for label, pat in PATTERNS.items():
        for m in pat.finditer(text):
            found.append((m.start(), m.end(), label))
    return found
Regex shines on highly-structured strings (identifiers, emails, postcodes, dates). It always beats a neural model on cost, latency, and predictability for those types.


14.2.2 Gazetteers and dictionary matching
A gazetteer is a list of known entity strings (cities, drugs, statutes, products). Match by exact string or with case/diacritic normalisation; resolve overlaps by longest-match-first.
For million-entry gazetteers, Aho–Corasick (a multi-pattern matcher) gives \(O(n + |\text{matches}|)\) matching time over the input text.


14.2.3 spaCy EntityRuler
spaCy lets you mix rules with a statistical model in one pipeline. The EntityRuler provides token-level patterns:
import spacy
nlp = spacy.load("en_core_web_sm")
ruler = nlp.add_pipe("entity_ruler", before="ner")
patterns = [
    {"label": "DRUG",   "pattern": "acetaminophen"},
    {"label": "DRUG",   "pattern": [{"LOWER": "tylenol"}]},
    {"label": "DOSAGE", "pattern": [{"IS_DIGIT": True}, {"LOWER": "mg"}]},
]
ruler.add_patterns(patterns)
doc = nlp("Take 500 mg of acetaminophen every 6 hours.")
print([(e.text, e.label_) for e in doc.ents])


14.2.4 Hybrid approaches
The strongest production NER systems combine all three layers:

Regex for structured identifiers — cheap, perfect.
Gazetteer / EntityRuler for closed-class vocabulary (drugs, cities, statutes).
Neural BERT-CRF for open-class generative-style entities (PERSON, ORG, PRODUCT in arbitrary text).
Conflict resolution — rules typically override the model; longest-span-wins.

Hybrid is a frequent assessment trap: rule-based vs neural is not either-or.

title: Information Retrieval
text:
MODULE 15 — GENERATING WITH RETRIEVAL-AUGMENTED MODELS
The full RAG topic is treated in the companion RAG/index.qmd. This module covers what is specific to IR: building RAG that runs on-device, and tightening retrieval-augmentation for IR-style tasks.


15.1 On-Device RAG

15.1.1 Why on-device

Privacy — sensitive corpora (medical notes, contracts) never leave the device.
Latency — no network round-trip to a cloud LLM.
Cost — no per-token API charge.
Offline operation — applications that must work without connectivity.



15.1.2 The mobile stack







Component
Mobile-class option




Embedder
bge-small / e5-small / all-MiniLM-L6 (~80–110 MB, 384-dim)


Index
HNSW from usearch / vespa-mobile / lightweight FAISS / Annoy


Quantisation
int8 PQ codes; matryoshka prefix at d = 128


LLM
Phi-3-mini (3.8B), Gemma-2-2B, Llama-3.2-1B/3B in 4-bit GGUF


Runtime
llama.cpp (Metal/Neon/AVX), MLC (TVM-compiled), MediaPipe LLM Task, Core ML, Apple MLX





15.1.3 GGUF and llama.cpp
GGUF (GGML Universal Format) is the dominant on-device LLM format. It supports many quantisation schemes (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, Q8_0) and runs inference on CPU, GPU, or Apple Silicon with llama.cpp.
$ ./llama-cli -m phi-3-mini-4k-instruct-q4.gguf -p "Hello"
Memory rule of thumb: a \(b\)-bit quantised model needs roughly \(\frac{N \cdot b}{8}\) bytes of RAM where \(N\) is the parameter count. A 3.8B Phi-3-mini at Q4 needs ~2 GB — fits comfortably on a modern smartphone.


15.1.4 MLC and TVM
MLC-LLM compiles the LLM with Apache TVM into hardware-specific kernels for Metal, OpenCL, Vulkan, and WebGPU. Result: state-of-the-art mobile inference performance.


15.1.5 Mobile vector stores







Library
Notes




usearch
Single-header HNSW, multi-language bindings


sqlite-vss
SQLite extension for vector search


ObjectBox
Mobile-first DB with vector index


Pinecone serverless / Turbopuffer
Cloud, but very cheap and good fits for low-volume mobile clients





15.1.6 A complete on-device flow
user query (on phone)
  → tokenise + embed locally with a mobile bi-encoder (~5 ms)
  → search local HNSW over int8-PQ vectors (~3 ms over 100k chunks)
  → build prompt
  → run llama.cpp on a quantised Phi-3-mini (~50–200 tokens/s)
  → stream answer to the UI
End-to-end latency on a recent flagship phone: 200–800 ms for short queries.




15.2 Fine-Tuning On-Device RAG Models
For on-device RAG, fine-tuning has two flavours: the LLM and the embedder.

15.2.1 Fine-tuning the LLM with LoRA
LoRA (Hu et al. 2021) injects two small rank-\(r\) matrices \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}\) and \(B \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}\) such that a frozen weight \(W_0\) is augmented by \(\Delta W = BA\):
\[
W = W_0 + BA, \quad \text{trainable params} = 2 d r \ll d^2
\]
Practical knobs:

r — adapter rank. 4–16 is the typical range.
α (lora_alpha) — scaling factor; the effective adapter contribution is \((α / r) \cdot BA\).
target modules — attention projections (q_proj, v_proj, sometimes k_proj, o_proj); MLP projections optionally.
dropout.
merge_and_unload at deployment to bake the adapter back into the base weights (zero inference overhead).

QLoRA (Dettmers et al. 2023) trains LoRA adapters on top of a 4-bit quantised base model — fits 33B-parameter fine-tuning into a single 24 GB GPU.
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM

base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("microsoft/Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct",
        torch_dtype="bfloat16", load_in_4bit=True)
cfg = LoraConfig(r=8, lora_alpha=16, lora_dropout=0.05,
                 target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],
                 bias="none", task_type="CAUSAL_LM")
model = get_peft_model(base, cfg)
After training, model.merge_and_unload() returns a base model with the adapter weights baked in, ready to be quantised to GGUF for on-device deployment.


15.2.2 Fine-tuning the embedder
Same recipe as Module 7.1: collect (query, relevant passage) pairs from the domain (mobile usage logs, customer support tickets, on-device user history with consent), train a mobile-class bi-encoder (all-MiniLM-L6 or bge-small) with MultipleNegativesRankingLoss, mine hard negatives with the previous version, distil from a cross-encoder teacher if labels are scarce.


15.2.3 Federated and on-device training
For privacy-sensitive personalisation, federated learning lets each device train locally and only share gradient updates. Combine with differential privacy noise on the aggregated updates.

title: Information Retrieval
section: MODULE 16 — FINE-TUNING LANGUAGE MODELS FOR IR
text:
MODULE 16 — FINE-TUNING LANGUAGE MODELS FOR IR
LLMs and IR co-evolve. This module covers the IR-shaped tasks LLMs are fine-tuned for, the LoRA mechanics, and the patterns where LLMs slot into a classical retrieval pipeline.

16.1 Fine-Tuning for IR Tasks

16.1.1 Passage ranking (monoT5 / RankT5 / RankLLM)
monoT5 (Nogueira et al. 2020) frames re-ranking as a sequence-to-sequence classification: input "Query: {q} Document: {d} Relevant:", target one of two tokens (true / false). The logit of true is the relevance score.
monoT5 fine-tuned on MS MARCO is one of the strongest open re-rankers; widely available in castorini/monoT5-* and cohere-rerank style services.


16.1.2 Query generation (doc2query / docTTTTTquery / InPars)
Train T5 on (passage → question) pairs; at index time, use it to generate synthetic queries for each passage. Either:

Append generated queries to passages (docTTTTTquery — Module 3.2).
Use generated (query, passage) pairs as silver training data for the retriever (InPars — Module 18).



16.1.3 Document summarisation for retrieval
Long documents fit poorly into context windows and into bi-encoder summary vectors. Fine-tune a summariser to compress documents into 150–300 tokens; index the summary alongside (or instead of) the full document.


16.1.4 Benchmarks







Benchmark
What it tests




MS MARCO passage / document
open-domain passage ranking


Natural Questions, TriviaQA, HotpotQA
open-domain QA over Wikipedia


BEIR (Thakur et al. 2021)
18 zero-shot retrieval datasets


MTEB (Muennighoff et al. 2022)
embedding-model benchmark across many tasks


Mr. TyDi
multilingual passage retrieval



BEIR especially is the standard for zero-shot retriever evaluation: train on MS MARCO, test on 18 datasets the model has never seen.




16.2 LoRA, QLoRA, and Merging

16.2.1 LoRA math
For a frozen weight \(W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{out}} \times d_{\text{in}}}\) and adapter rank \(r\):
\[
y = W_0 x + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A x, \quad A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d_{\text{in}}}, \; B \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{out}} \times r}
\]
\(A\) is typically initialised with random Gaussian, \(B\) with zeros — so the adapter starts at \(0\) and adds nothing at the first step.


16.2.2 QLoRA
QLoRA quantises \(W_0\) to 4-bit (NormalFloat4 quantisation, optimised for normally distributed weights) and runs the matmul \(W_0 x\) in 4-bit; the small \(B A\) remains in bf16. Combined with paged optimisers and double quantisation, this lets you fine-tune 33B-70B models on consumer GPUs.


16.2.3 Merging adapters
After training, merge_and_unload():
\[
W \leftarrow W_0 + \frac{\alpha}{r} B A
\]
stores a single dense weight matrix. Inference cost is then identical to a non-LoRA model. Multiple adapters can be merged with weighted blending (DARE, TIES) for multi-task models.




16.3 Integrating LLMs Into Retrieval Pipelines








Role
LLM use
Pattern




Query expansion
LLM generates synonyms / sub-queries / a HyDE pseudo-passage
section 3.2


Query rewriting
conversation rewriter / typo fixer
sections 4, 5


Document expansion
doc2query / LLM-as-tagger
section 3.2


Reranker
RankGPT / RankZephyr / RankLLM
section 8.2


Multi-vector reranker
LLM-as-judge with chain-of-thought
research-grade


Final generator
the RAG generator
RAG/index.qmd



The frequent assessment trap: an LLM in the retrieval pipeline is not the generator. A model can play one role, both, or neither.

title: Information Retrieval
text:
MODULE 17 — ZERO-SHOT AND FEW-SHOT LEARNING

17.1 Zero-Shot Retrieval
A zero-shot retriever is evaluated on domains it never saw during training. The headline result: Contriever, E5, BGE, GTE, and similar models reach within a few points of supervised baselines on BEIR’s 18 unseen domains.

17.1.1 Contriever
Contriever (Izacard et al. 2021) is unsupervised contrastive pretraining: pairs are constructed from text segments that occur close to each other in a document (or, in inverse cloze, a sentence + its surrounding paragraph). The trained encoder is competitive with DPR even without any labelled IR data.


17.1.2 The BEIR benchmark







BEIR characteristic
Detail




Datasets
18 (TREC-COVID, NFCorpus, FiQA, ArguAna, Touche, DBpedia, SCIDOCS, FEVER, Climate-FEVER, Quora, HotpotQA, NQ, MSMARCO, …)


Evaluation
nDCG@10


Setting
zero-shot — train on MS MARCO (or none), test on every dataset



A strong general-purpose embedder must do well across BEIR. The best fine-tuned per-domain models still win on individual datasets, but the average across BEIR is the right zero-shot metric.


17.1.3 Instruction-tuned embedders
E5-instruct, BGE-instruct, Nomic-Embed-v2, and Mistral/GTE/Linq-Embed all support an instruction prefix: prepending a short task description to the query (e.g., "Represent this sentence for searching relevant passages:") significantly improves zero-shot performance, especially on tasks beyond passage retrieval (classification, clustering, similarity).




17.2 Few-Shot Retrieval

17.2.1 In-context demonstrations for LLM rerankers
The LLM reranker prompt can carry a few labelled examples:
Below are pairs of (query, passage) and a relevance score. Score the new pair.

Q: "best lightweight laptop" P: "MacBook Air M3, 13 inch, 1.24 kg." → 5
Q: "best lightweight laptop" P: "Best practices for laptop hygiene." → 1
...
Q: "..." P: "..." →
Demonstrations are picked dynamically — usually the \(n\) most similar training queries to the test query.


17.2.2 GPL — Generative Pseudo Labelling
GPL (Wang et al. 2021) bridges zero-shot to supervised in domains where no labels exist:

Generate synthetic queries for each passage with a query generator (T5).
Mine hard negatives for each (synthetic query, passage) pair using an existing retriever.
Score the triples with a cross-encoder teacher.
Fine-tune the bi-encoder with margin-MSE loss against the teacher.

This produces a domain-specialised retriever without any human labels.

title: Information Retrieval
text:
MODULE 18 — APPLYING PSEUDO-LABELLING TECHNIQUES
Pseudo-labelling (also called self-training or distillation) is the modern recipe for adapting retrievers and rerankers to domains with little or no labelled data.


18.1 Pseudo-Labelling Techniques

18.1.1 Cross-encoder teacher → bi-encoder student
The cornerstone:

Train (or pick off the shelf) a strong cross-encoder reranker on a labelled IR dataset (MS MARCO).
For each (query, candidate) pair in your domain corpus, score with the cross-encoder.
Distil the cross-encoder’s scores into a bi-encoder student with margin-MSE or KL.

The student is fast (bi-encoder, can be indexed) but reaches near-cross-encoder quality. This is the recipe behind ms-marco MiniLM, BGE-rerank distillations, and most domain-adapted retrievers.


18.1.2 GPL — covered in 17.2.2.


18.1.3 InPars / InPars-Light
InPars (Bonifacio et al. 2022) prompts a strong LLM (originally GPT-3) to generate questions for each passage; the resulting (question, passage) pairs are used as silver training data for a retriever or reranker.
Prompt to GPT-3:

Generate one question that the following passage answers.

Passage: {p}
Question:
InPars-Light extends this with smaller open-source generators (T5-base) to reduce cost.


18.1.4 Promptagator
Promptagator (Dai et al. 2022) extends InPars by allowing the prompt to be task-specific (different tasks need different question styles). It additionally distils the resulting strong cross-encoder into a faster student. Promptagator beats supervised baselines on several BEIR datasets without any human labels.


18.1.5 Hard-negative mining
Effective pseudo-labelling requires hard negatives — passages that are lexically or semantically similar to the query but not relevant. Without hard negatives, the contrastive objective is trivial (random negatives are too easy) and the model never learns subtle distinctions.
Sources:

BM25 hard negatives. For each (query, positive), take the top BM25-retrieved passages that are not the positive.
Self-retrieval hard negatives (ANCE-style). Train an initial model; rerun it over the corpus; the model’s own top-\(k\) false positives become next round’s hard negatives.
Cross-encoder filtering. For candidate hard negatives, run a cross-encoder; drop any candidate whose score exceeds the positive’s score (it’s probably also positive, just not labelled).



18.1.6 MarginMSE loss
Already introduced in 7.1.6. Distilling cross-encoder margins into a bi-encoder:
\[
\mathcal{L}_{\text{MMSE}} = \bigl[ m_{\text{teacher}}(q, d^+, d^-) - m_{\text{student}}(q, d^+, d^-) \bigr]^2
\]
with \(m(q, d^+, d^-) = \text{score}(q, d^+) - \text{score}(q, d^-)\).


18.1.7 Pseudo-code: GPL pipeline
function GPL(passages, query_generator G, retriever R0, cross_encoder T,
             n_queries=3, n_negatives=50):
    train_triples ← []
    for each passage p in passages:
        for i in 1..n_queries:
            q ← G(p)                              # synthetic query
            candidates ← R0.retrieve(q, k=n_negatives)
            for d in candidates - {p}:
                m_teacher ← T.score(q, p) - T.score(q, d)
                train_triples.append((q, p, d, m_teacher))

    finetune R0 with MarginMSE on train_triples
    return R0




18.2 Integrating Pseudo-Labelling into Semi-Supervised Pipelines
Pseudo-labelling is rarely a one-shot exercise. The strongest pipelines iterate:
1. Pretrain bi-encoder on general data (E5 / BGE base)
2. Synthetic queries on target corpus (InPars / Promptagator)
3. Mine hard negatives with BM25 + R0
4. Score triples with cross-encoder teacher
5. Distil → R1 with MarginMSE
6. Re-mine hard negatives with R1 (self-mining, ANCE-style)
7. Re-score and re-distil → R2
8. Stop when held-out metric plateaus
Risks:

Pseudo-label noise. Synthetic queries can be incoherent or relate to the wrong passage. Filter aggressively (LM perplexity, length, semantic similarity to the source passage).
Confirmation bias. If the seed retriever is biased, self-mined negatives perpetuate the bias. Inject random negatives and BM25 negatives at every iteration to keep distribution shift in check.
Cross-encoder calibration. The cross-encoder teacher’s scores must be calibrated to its true confidence; un-calibrated teachers can mislead the student.

Pseudo-labelling has become the default way to adapt a general retriever to a new domain without human labels — a workflow that twelve months earlier required tens of thousands of expensive annotations.

title: Information Retrieval
text:
Classical IR foundations

Salton & Buckley — Term-Weighting Approaches in Automatic Text Retrieval (1988). Origin of TF-IDF and the Rocchio relevance-feedback rule.
Robertson & Spärck Jones — Relevance Weighting of Search Terms (1976). The probabilistic IR foundation.
Robertson & Walker — Some Simple Effective Approximations to the 2-Poisson Model for Probabilistic Weighted Retrieval (1994). BM25.
Robertson, Zaragoza, Taylor — Simple BM25 Extension to Multiple Weighted Fields (BM25F, 2004).
Manning, Raghavan & Schütze — Introduction to Information Retrieval (book, 2008). The standard textbook.
Croft, Metzler & Strohman — Search Engines: Information Retrieval in Practice (book, 2010).

title: Information Retrieval
text:
Word and sentence embeddings

Deerwester et al. — Indexing by Latent Semantic Analysis (1990). LSI.
Blei, Ng, Jordan — Latent Dirichlet Allocation (2003).
Mikolov et al. — Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space (2013). Word2Vec.
Pennington, Socher & Manning — GloVe: Global Vectors for Word Representation (2014).
Bojanowski, Grave, Joulin, Mikolov — Enriching Word Vectors with Subword Information (FastText, 2017).
Devlin, Chang, Lee, Toutanova — BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding (2019).
Reimers & Gurevych — Sentence-BERT: Sentence Embeddings using Siamese BERT-Networks (2019).
Raffel et al. — Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer (T5, 2020).

title: Information Retrieval
text:
Dense retrieval

Karpukhin et al. — Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering (DPR, 2020).
Xiong et al. — Approximate Nearest Neighbor Negative Contrastive Learning for Dense Text Retrieval (ANCE, 2020).
Khattab & Zaharia — ColBERT: Efficient and Effective Passage Search via Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT (2020).
Izacard et al. — Unsupervised Dense Information Retrieval with Contrastive Learning (Contriever, 2021).
Wang et al. — Text Embeddings by Weakly-Supervised Contrastive Pre-training (E5, 2022).
Xiao et al. — C-Pack: Packaged Resources To Advance General Chinese Embedding (BGE, 2023).
Li, Lan, Li — Towards General Text Embeddings with Multi-stage Contrastive Learning (GTE, 2023).

title: Information Retrieval
text:

Nogueira & Cho — Passage Re-ranking with BERT (monoBERT, 2019).
Nogueira et al. — Document Ranking with a Pretrained Sequence-to-Sequence Model (monoT5, 2020).
Burges — From RankNet to LambdaRank to LambdaMART: An Overview (2010). Background on learning-to-rank.

Pradeep et al. — RankZephyr: Effective and Robust Zero-Shot Listwise Reranking is a Breeze! (2023).

title: Information Retrieval

title: Information Retrieval
text:
Blogs and tutorials

Pinecone Learn — “Vector Similarity Explained”, “HNSW: Foundations of Vector Search”, “Product Quantization”. Strong primers on ANN indexes.
Vespa blog — series on BM25, hybrid search, and ColBERT in production.
Sebastian Hofstätter — tutorials on dense retrieval, knowledge distillation, and hard-negative mining.

Jay Alammar — “The Illustrated Word2Vec”, “The Illustrated BERT”, “The Illustrated Transformer”.

James Briggs — Pinecone YouTube series covering ANN, hybrid search, and rerankers.

Weaviate blog — “BM25 + Vector Hybrid Search”, “Late Interaction with ColBERT”.
Milvus blog — “Choosing the Right Vector Index”, “GPU-Accelerated ANN”.
Cohere blog — “Rerank for Search”, “Cohere Embed v3”.
OpenAI Cookbook — chapters on retrieval-augmented generation, embeddings, and reranking.

title: Information Retrieval
text:
C.1 Definitions and match-pairs
1. Match each term to its category: BM25 / FAISS / Chroma / HNSW / IVF-PQ / inverted index.
Answer: BM25 = scoring function. FAISS = ANN library. Chroma = vector DB. HNSW, IVF-PQ = ANN index types. Inverted index = data structure underlying lexical retrieval.
2. What is the difference between a bi-encoder and a cross-encoder?
Answer: Bi-encoder encodes query and document independently into vectors; cross-encoder takes (query, document) jointly. Bi-encoder is fast and indexable; cross-encoder is accurate but cannot be pre-indexed (Module 7.1 / 8.2.5).
3. What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric semantic search?
Answer: Symmetric — both sides are the same kind/length (sentence-sentence). Asymmetric — short query against long passage (MS MARCO style). Choose the embedder accordingly (Module 7.3).
4. Define query expansion vs query rewriting.
Answer: Expansion adds extra terms or synonyms to the query (Modules 3, 6). Rewriting replaces the query with a different string (Module 5). Both improve recall but only rewriting can change the query’s surface form.
5. What does pseudo-relevance feedback assume, and what is its main failure mode?
Answer: Assumes the top-\(k\) retrieved documents are relevant. Failure mode = query drift when a top-\(k\) document is off-topic (Module 3.1).
6. State the BM25 formula.
Answer: \(\text{BM25}(q, d) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \frac{f_{t,d}(k_1+1)}{f_{t,d} + k_1(1 - b + b|d|/\overline{|d|})}\) (Module 10.1).
7. What does \(k_1\) control in BM25? What does \(b\) control?
Answer: \(k_1\) controls TF saturation; \(b\) controls length normalisation (Module 10.1.7).
8. Distinguish Precision@K, Recall@K, MRR, MAP, NDCG@K.
Answer: Precision@K = relevant in top-K / K. Recall@K = relevant in top-K / total relevant. MRR = mean of 1/rank of first relevant. MAP = mean of per-query AP. NDCG@K = graded relevance, log-discounted, normalised by ideal DCG. (Section 8.3.4–6.)
9. What is the difference between multi-class and multi-label intent classification, and which loss does each use?
Answer: Multi-class — one label of \(K\), softmax + cross-entropy. Multi-label — any subset of \(K\), sigmoid per label + binary cross-entropy (Module 1.2.5).
10. What is Rocchio’s formula?
Answer: \(\mathbf{q}_{\text{new}} = \alpha \mathbf{q}_0 + \beta \frac{1}{|D_R|} \sum_{d \in D_R} \mathbf{d} - \gamma \frac{1}{|D_{NR}|} \sum_{d \in D_{NR}} \mathbf{d}\) (Module 3.1.1).
11. What does HyDE stand for, and what does it embed?
Answer: Hypothetical Document Embeddings. Embeds an LLM-generated hypothetical answer to the query, then retrieves nearest neighbours of that embedding (Module 3.2.5).
12. Name three KG embedding models and one distinguishing property of each.
Answer: TransE — translation in real space. DistMult — bilinear, symmetric only. ComplEx — complex-valued, handles antisymmetric relations. RotatE — rotation in complex space, covers symmetry, antisymmetry, inversion, composition (Module 11.2.2).
13. Match each NER scheme to its label set: BIO / BIOES / span-based.
Answer: BIO: B-X, I-X, O. BIOES: adds E-X, S-X. Span-based: classify every span up to length \(L\) with a type or null (Module 14.1).
14. What is LambdaMART?
Answer: Gradient-boosted-tree ranker with LambdaRank gradients that effectively optimise NDCG (Module 2.1.3 / 8.2.4).
15. What is the difference between LSI, pLSA, LDA, and NMF?
Answer: LSI = SVD on term-doc matrix (no probabilistic interpretation). pLSA = probabilistic latent topic model without prior. LDA = pLSA + Dirichlet priors. NMF = non-negative matrix factorisation, deterministic, additive parts (Module 13.1).
16. What is Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) and when is it preferable to score-based fusion?
Answer: RRF combines retrievers by ranks: \(\text{RRF}(d) = \sum_{r} \frac{1}{k + r(d)}\). Preferable when score scales differ across retrievers (sparse BM25 scores vs dense cosine scores), because RRF only uses ranks (Module 8.3 / RAG MODULE 11).
17. What is the asymmetric distance computation in PQ?
Answer: Pre-compute distances between query sub-vectors and every centroid; the estimated distance to a stored code is the sum of lookups across sub-quantisers (Module 7.2.3).
18. What is the temperature parameter in InfoNCE used for?
Answer: Scales the similarities before softmax. Lower temperature sharpens the distribution (harder negatives have more influence); higher temperature softens it (Module 7.1.2).

title: Information Retrieval
text:
C.2 Ordering
19. Order the stages of the modern retrieve-fuse-rerank-generate pipeline.
Answer: (a) BM25 retrieval + dense retrieval, parallel. (b) RRF or weighted fusion. (c) Cross-encoder reranker. (d) Optional LLM reranker. (e) LLM generator with retrieved context (Module 8.3).
20. Order the stages of GPL.
Answer: (i) Synthetic query generation with T5/GPT. (ii) Hard negative mining with BM25 + base retriever. (iii) Cross-encoder scoring. (iv) Bi-encoder distillation with margin-MSE. (v) (Optional) re-mine hard negatives with the new retriever and iterate (Module 18.1.2 / 18.2).
21. Order the steps of one Rocchio iteration over a labelled feedback set.
Answer: (i) Compute centroid of relevant docs; (ii) compute centroid of non-relevant docs; (iii) blend with the original query vector via \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\); (iv) re-issue retrieval (Module 3.1.1).
22. Order the steps of HNSW search.
Answer: Enter at the top layer’s entry point → greedy-search down each layer until no improvement → at bottom layer, run beam search with width ef → return top-k (Module 7.2.4).
23. Order the operations in a docTTTTTquery indexing pipeline.
Answer: For each passage: run doc2query T5 → generate \(N\) synthetic queries → concatenate them to the passage text → index the augmented passage in the inverted index (Module 3.2.3).

title: Information Retrieval
text:
C.3 Multiple choice / situational
24. A user types "reciept polcy". The system should…
(a) return zero results because no document contains those tokens.
(b) expand with WordNet synonyms.
(c) run a spell-corrector before retrieval.
Answer: (c). Auto-correction with confirmation UI; do not silently expand with synonyms before fixing typos (Module 2.3).
25. Which retriever family is most appropriate for highly keyword-heavy queries (error codes, SKUs, statutes)?
Answer: Sparse / BM25 (or hybrid leaning on BM25). Dense retrievers can dilute rare-keyword signal (Module 7, Module 10).
26. A 100 ms latency budget cannot afford a cross-encoder rerank on a CPU-only deployment. The fastest acceptable improvement to a bi-encoder retriever is…
Answer: Hybrid BM25 + dense with RRF; plus doc2query offline (no query-time cost). Defer cross-encoder to higher-budget tiers (Module 7.1, 7.2, 8.3.2).
27. Which clustering algorithm is appropriate when the number of clusters is unknown and noise points should be detected?
Answer: HDBSCAN (Module 12.1.2).
28. What is the right metric to evaluate a retriever whose downstream is an LLM that can read 5 chunks?
Answer: Recall@K for not-too-large K (e.g., K = 5 or 10) — coverage of the relevant chunks at the input to the LLM (Module 8.3.3; also RAG Module 7.2).
29. Which loss is appropriate to distil a cross-encoder reranker into a bi-encoder retriever?
Answer: Margin-MSE (Module 7.1.6, 18.1.6).
30. When should you choose NDCG over MAP?
Answer: When relevance is graded (not binary) and rank-positional discount matters (Module 8.3.6).
31. What is catastrophic drift in PRF, and how is it controlled?
Answer: Query drift caused by off-topic top-\(k\) documents. Controlled by small \(k\), term weighting (RM3 \(\lambda\)), fallback to original query, and term filtering by IDF or PMI (Module 3.1.2).
32. Which model is appropriate for fine-tuning embedding models on (query, passage) pairs with in-batch negatives?
Answer: MultipleNegativesRankingLoss (InfoNCE) in sentence-transformers, with a backbone like BGE or E5 base (Module 7.1.5).
33. In a multimodal setting, which loss is used by CLIP and SigLIP respectively?
Answer: CLIP — symmetric softmax InfoNCE. SigLIP — pairwise sigmoid BCE (Module 9.1.1, 9.1.3).
34. What is the failure mode of using a symmetric sentence-similarity model for asymmetric passage retrieval?
Answer: Mismatch — the encoder was trained on equal-length pairs and may not handle the query/passage asymmetry, leading to lower recall (Module 7.3).
35. Which mobile LLM format is dominant for on-device inference with llama.cpp?
Answer: GGUF, with quantisations Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, Q8_0 being typical (Module 15.1.3).

title: Information Retrieval
text:
C.4 Worked computations
36. Compute TF-IDF (smoothed-IDF) for the term "refund" if it appears 3 times in a 50-term query log file out of \(N = 100\) queries containing "refund" and total \(N = 10000\) queries.
Answer: Using \(\text{IDF} = \log(N / n_t) = \log(10000/100) = \log 100 \approx 4.605\). TF = 3 (raw) or \(1 + \log 3 \approx 2.098\) (log-scaled). TF-IDF ≈ 13.8 (raw) or 9.67 (log).
37. Given three relevant docs returned at ranks \([1, 3, 6]\) out of \(K = 10\) with \(\text{total\_relevant} = 5\), compute Precision@10, Recall@10, and Reciprocal Rank.
Answer: Precision@10 = 3/10 = 0.30. Recall@10 = 3/5 = 0.60. RR = 1/1 = 1.0.
38. Compute NDCG@3 for a ranking with graded relevance \([2, 3, 1]\).
Answer: DCG@3 = \((2^2 - 1)/\log_2 2 + (2^3-1)/\log_2 3 + (2^1-1)/\log_2 4 = 3 + 4.417 + 0.5 = 7.917\). IDCG@3 with order \([3,2,1]\) = \(7 + 3/\log_2 3 + 1/\log_2 4 = 7 + 1.893 + 0.5 = 9.393\). NDCG@3 = 7.917 / 9.393 ≈ 0.843.
39. For the toy corpus in Module 10.1.7, compute BM25 for query "the brown" with \(k_1=1.5, b=0.75\).
Answer: "the" appears in all three docs → IDF ≈ \(\log(0.5/3.5 + 1) = \log(1.143) \approx 0.134\) (very low — common). "brown" ≈ 0.470 as before. The score becomes dominated by "brown". Computing fully: \(d_2\) scores highest, \(d_1\) next, \(d_3\) near zero — same ordering as the larger example.
40. Compute MAP for two queries, one with returned relevance pattern \([1, 0, 1]\) over \(K=3\) with 2 total relevant; the other with \([0, 1, 1]\) over \(K=3\) with 2 total relevant.
Answer: Query 1 AP = \((1/1 + 2/3)/2 = (1 + 0.667)/2 = 0.833\). Query 2 AP = \((1/2 + 2/3)/2 = (0.5 + 0.667)/2 = 0.583\). MAP = \((0.833 + 0.583)/2 = 0.708\).
41. One Rocchio update with \(\alpha=\beta=1\) and a relevant doc vector \((1,2,1)\) on top of an original query vector \((1,1,0)\) produces…
Answer: \((1,1,0) + (1,2,1) = (2,3,1)\). The expansion has lifted the third component into the query (Module 3.1.1).
42. With \(d=128\), \(m=16\), codebook size \(k=256\), PQ compresses each vector from how many bytes to how many bytes?
Answer: Original: \(128 \times 4 = 512\) bytes. PQ code: \(16\) sub-quantisers × 1 byte (since \(k = 256\)) = \(16\) bytes. Compression factor = 32× (Module 7.2.3).

title: Information Retrieval
text:
C.5 Conceptual / descriptive
43. Explain why doc2query helps even though it does no extra work at query time.
Answer: It augments each passage with synthetic queries it would answer. BM25 over the augmented index can now match paraphrases the original passage never contained, because the synthetic queries put plausible search terms into the document body (Module 3.2.3).
44. Explain the difference between click bias (position bias) and relevance, and how PBM separates them.
Answer: High-ranked items are clicked more by virtue of being shown first (position bias). PBM models \(P(\text{click} \mid q, d, r) = P(\text{examine} \mid r) \cdot P(\text{relevant} \mid q, d)\) — examination depends on rank only, relevance depends on (q,d) only. EM fits both, yielding bias-adjusted relevance (Module 2.1.2).
45. What is the late interaction of ColBERT and why is it more expressive than a bi-encoder?
Answer: For each query token, take the max similarity over all document tokens; sum these maxima. Each query token can match a different document token, so the model captures multiple aspects of relevance (Module 3.1.5).
46. Explain why HNSW supports incremental inserts but IVF-PQ does not (in general).
Answer: HNSW is a graph: insert a new node by connecting it to neighbours, no global structure changes. IVF-PQ depends on centroids learned by k-means over a sample; as the distribution shifts, the centroids become suboptimal and inserts increasingly land in poorly-fitting cells (Module 7.2).
47. Describe one strategy to add follow-up detection to a conversational search system.
Answer: Train a binary classifier on (history, current utterance) pairs from CANARD/QReCC labelled with whether the current utterance is a follow-up. If yes, run the conversational rewriter; if not, treat the utterance as a fresh query (Module 4.1.3).
48. Why must the query and the documents be embedded with the same model?
Answer: Different embedding models project text into different vector spaces with different geometric semantics; their dot products and cosines are not comparable. Mixing them silently destroys retrieval quality (Module 7).
49. Outline the steps required to add a domain-specific synonym expansion to an existing BM25 retriever without exploding the index.
Answer: Build a per-domain synonym table; configure the retriever to do query-time expansion (Elasticsearch synonym_graph filter); weight synonyms below 1.0 (e.g., 0.5); evaluate against an eval set to verify recall lift without precision drop; configure per-tenant if needed (Module 6.2).
50. Describe the strongest pseudo-labelling pipeline you would build for a new domain with zero labels.
Answer: (i) Start from a strong general embedder (BGE / E5). (ii) Generate synthetic queries per passage with a T5 doc2query model or an LLM (InPars / Promptagator). (iii) Mine hard negatives with BM25 + the seed retriever. (iv) Score (q, d+, d-) triples with a cross-encoder teacher. (v) Distil into a bi-encoder student via margin-MSE. (vi) Iterate: re-mine hard negatives with the new student; re-distil. (vii) Stop when held-out BEIR-style retrieval metrics plateau (Module 18).

These notes cover every skill in the IR syllabus. The companion RAG/index.qmd should be read first for the higher-level architecture; this guide goes deeper on the IR mechanisms.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
“You can’t manage what you can’t measure.” — often attributed to Peter Drucker (and frequently misquoted; the truer Drucker line is “What gets measured gets managed — even when it’s pointless to measure and manage it, and even if it harms the purpose of the organization to do so.”). Both quotes belong in every analyst’s mental toolkit: measurement is necessary, but measurement is also dangerous when divorced from purpose.

This single document is designed as exhaustive preparation material for the Workera ELO / SAGE assessment of Business Analytics Fundamentals. It is opinionated, dense, and structured so that you can read it cover-to-cover and emerge able to (a) define every term, (b) derive every formula, (c) implement every method in code, and (d) reason about every trade-off in real organizations.
The reference scaffolding leans on the canonical sources every serious analyst should own or have read:

Kaplan & Norton, The Balanced Scorecard (HBS Press)
Eric Ries, The Lean Startup and its AARRR-style metric lineage (via Dave McClure, 500 Startups)
Sean Ellis & Morgan Brown, Hacking Growth
Avinash Kaushik, Web Analytics 2.0
Stephen Few, Information Dashboard Design
Edward Tufte, The Visual Display of Quantitative Information
Cole Nussbaumer Knaflic, Storytelling with Data
Hans Rosling, Factfulness
Donald Wheeler, Understanding Statistical Process Control
Goldratt, The Goal and Theory of Constraints literature
Barbara Minto, The Pyramid Principle (the original source for MECE / SCQA)
Wickham & Grolemund, R for Data Science; Wilkinson, Grammar of Graphics
Ron Kohavi, Diane Tang, Ya Xu — Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments
IIBA BABOK (Business Analysis Body of Knowledge), INFORMS Certified Analytics Professional (CAP) handbook, DAMA DMBOK, Lean Six Sigma Body of Knowledge, ISACA COBIT
Andrew Chen on growth, Reforge curriculum, Airbnb Engineering blog, Netflix Tech blog, Spotify experimentation platform write-ups, OpenAI/Google data-team posts

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 1: Preparing Business Data

1.1 Why data preparation dominates analytics work
Surveys from CrowdFlower, Anaconda, and Kaggle consistently show practitioners spending 60–80% of their time wrangling data rather than modeling. This is not a sign of immaturity — it is the job. Models are commoditized; trustworthy, well-shaped data is the durable asset. Every assessment item about “preparing data” is really asking: do you understand the chain of transformations that make raw operational records fit for analytical questions?
A useful mental model is the CRISP-DM loop (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining):

Business Understanding → frame the decision and value at stake.
Data Understanding → inventory sources, profile fields, surface anomalies.
Data Preparation → clean, transform, enrich, validate.
Modeling → select methods (statistical, ML, rules) appropriate to the question.
Evaluation → confirm both technical performance and business fit.
Deployment → operationalize, monitor, and feed learnings back.

Each iteration through CRISP-DM tightens the loop between question, data, and decision.



1.2 Data types — measurement scales
A single mistake in identifying the measurement scale of a field invalidates downstream statistics. Stevens’ (1946) taxonomy is the canonical reference.











Scale
Definition
Allowed Operations
Example
Center
Spread




Nominal
Labels with no order
=, ≠
gender, country, SKU
mode
n/a


Ordinal
Ordered categories, unequal gaps
=, ≠, <, >
NPS bucket, T-shirt size, Likert
median
IQR, range


Interval
Equal gaps, no true zero
+, −
Celsius, calendar year
mean
std, var


Ratio
Equal gaps, true zero exists
+, −, ×, ÷
revenue, count, duration
geometric mean possible
CV



Common pitfall: computing the mean of a Likert-scale field (ordinal) and treating it as interval. Defensible in business reporting if you state the assumption explicitly; indefensible in formal inference without sensitivity checks.
Structured vs semi-structured vs unstructured:

Structured — rows and typed columns; sits in RDBMS or warehouse tables. SQL is the native language.
Semi-structured — has schema cues but no fixed columns: JSON, XML, Avro, Parquet with nested fields, log lines with key/value pairs.
Unstructured — free text, images, audio, video; requires feature extraction (NLP/CV) before classical analytics applies.




1.3 Profiling: the first hour with any new dataset
Before touching any model, profile. Profiling answers six questions:

What is here? — schema, types, row count, file size.
What’s missing? — null patterns, blanks, sentinel values (-1, 9999, "N/A").
What’s unique? — primary keys, cardinality, duplicates.
What’s the shape? — histograms, ranges, percentiles, skewness.
What’s related? — correlations, joins, foreign keys.
What’s anomalous? — outliers, type mismatches, encoding errors.

A minimal Python profiling skeleton:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.read_csv("orders.csv")

print(df.shape)
print(df.dtypes)
print(df.head())
print(df.describe(include="all").T)
print(df.isna().sum().sort_values(ascending=False))
print(df.nunique().sort_values(ascending=False))

# Quick anomaly screen on numerics
for col in df.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns:
    q1, q3 = df[col].quantile([0.25, 0.75])
    iqr = q3 - q1
    lo, hi = q1 - 1.5 * iqr, q3 + 1.5 * iqr
    n_out = ((df[col] < lo) | (df[col] > hi)).sum()
    print(f"{col}: {n_out} potential outliers (IQR rule)")
For a richer first pass, ydata-profiling (formerly pandas-profiling), sweetviz, dataprep.eda, or Great Expectations give you HTML reports and assertions in one shot.



1.4 Missing data: mechanism matters more than method
Rubin (1976) introduced the three canonical missingness mechanisms. Choose imputation based on which one is plausible.

MCAR — Missing Completely At Random. Missingness is independent of any observed or unobserved value. Example: a sensor randomly drops 0.1% of pings. Listwise deletion is unbiased, just inefficient.
MAR — Missing At Random. Missingness depends only on observed values. Example: older users skip the “income” field, but you observe age. Listwise deletion is biased; well-specified imputation (MICE, regression imputation) is unbiased.
MNAR — Missing Not At Random. Missingness depends on the unobserved value itself. Example: high earners refuse to disclose income. No imputation method can fix this without external information (proxy, instrumental variable, model of nonresponse).









Method
When to use
Caveats




Listwise deletion
MCAR; small fraction missing
Loses statistical power; biased under MAR/MNAR


Mean / median imputation
Quick sanity baseline
Shrinks variance; distorts correlations


Mode imputation
Categorical fields
Inflates dominant category


Forward / backward fill
Time series with persistence
Wrong if the gap spans regime change


KNN imputation
Continuous, low-d
Slow on large data; requires scaling


MICE / chained equations
MAR, multiple variables
Iterative; report multiple imputed datasets and pool


Model-based (regression, RF)
When predictors are strong
Hides uncertainty; consider multiple imputation


Indicator + impute
Always pair: keep “was_missing” flag
Lets the model learn that missingness itself carries signal



from sklearn.experimental import enable_iterative_imputer  # noqa
from sklearn.impute import IterativeImputer, KNNImputer

mice = IterativeImputer(max_iter=10, random_state=0)
X_mice = mice.fit_transform(X)

knn = KNNImputer(n_neighbors=5)
X_knn = knn.fit_transform(X)
Golden rule: never silently drop or impute without first creating <col>_was_missing indicator features and quantifying how the choice changes downstream estimates.



1.5 Outliers — detection, judgment, and treatment
Outliers are observations inconsistent with the bulk of the data. They can be (a) errors, (b) rare-but-real, or (c) the very signal you care about (fraud, churn, defects). Always investigate before removing.
Univariate detection:

IQR rule (Tukey): flag values below \(Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}\) or above \(Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}\), where \(\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1\). Use \(3 \cdot \text{IQR}\) for “extreme” outliers.
Z-score: \(z_i = \frac{x_i - \bar x}{s}\); flag \(|z| > 3\). Assumes normality and is itself sensitive to outliers (the mean and std are inflated by them).
Modified z-score (MAD-based): \(M_i = 0.6745 \cdot \frac{x_i - \tilde x}{\text{MAD}}\) where \(\text{MAD} = \text{median}(|x_i - \tilde x|)\). Robust because median and MAD are not pulled by outliers. Flag \(|M_i| > 3.5\).

Multivariate detection:

Mahalanobis distance with the covariance matrix \(\Sigma\): \(D^2(x) = (x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)\); compare against \(\chi^2_p\).
Isolation Forest (Liu, Ting, Zhou 2008) — anomalies are easier to isolate by random partitioning, so they have shorter average path lengths in random trees.
Local Outlier Factor (LOF) — measures the local density deviation of a point relative to its neighbors.
One-Class SVM — learns a boundary around the majority class.
DBSCAN — points not assigned to any density cluster are noise.

from sklearn.ensemble import IsolationForest

iso = IsolationForest(contamination=0.01, random_state=0)
df["anomaly_score"] = iso.fit_predict(df[features])
Treatment options:

Investigate first. A \(999{,}999\) order might be a real B2B deal or a data-entry slip.
Cap / winsorize at the 1st/99th percentile.
Transform (log, Box-Cox, Yeo-Johnson) to compress tails.
Model robustly — Huber, RANSAC, quantile regression.
Segment — separate “whales” from the long tail and model both.




1.6 Feature engineering and transformation
The right transformation often matters more than the right model. Core toolbox:

Scaling

Min-max: \(x' = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}\).
Standardization: \(x' = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\).
Robust (median/IQR): resistant to outliers.

Distribution shaping

\(\log(1+x)\) — for right-skewed counts/revenue.
Box-Cox: \(x' = \frac{x^\lambda - 1}{\lambda}\) for \(x > 0\).
Yeo-Johnson — generalizes Box-Cox to \(x \le 0\).

Discretization / binning

Equal-width, equal-frequency (quantile), or supervised (decision-tree based).
Useful for non-linear effects, regulatory reporting, and credit-scoring scorecards.

Encoding categorical variables

Label encoding — integer per category; OK for tree models, dangerous for linear models because it implies an order.
One-hot encoding — k or k−1 dummies; explodes dimensionality for high-cardinality fields.
Target / mean encoding — replace category with the mean of the target; powerful but leaks unless you apply out-of-fold and add smoothing \(\frac{n \bar y_c + m \bar y}{n + m}\).
Frequency encoding, hashing trick for very high cardinality.
Embeddings for ML pipelines (entity embeddings — Guo & Berkhahn 2016).

Feature crosses — multiplicative or pairwise interactions (price × discount, hour × day).
Date/time features — year, month, day-of-week, week-of-year, is_holiday, days_since_signup, cyclic encoding via \(\sin / \cos\) of \(\frac{2\pi t}{T}\).




1.7 Data validation rules
Validation is the contract between data producers and consumers. Codify expectations early and check them on every load.








Rule type
Example
Tooling




Schema
order_id is BIGINT, non-null
pandera, pydantic, dbt tests, Spark schemas


Uniqueness
order_id is unique
dbt unique, GE expect_column_values_to_be_unique


Referential integrity
every customer_id exists in customers
dbt relationships, FK constraints


Range / domain
0 <= discount <= 1; country in ISO_3166
GE, pandera, CHECK constraints


Pattern
regex on email, phone, postal codes
GE expect_column_values_to_match_regex


Freshness
latest record < 24h old
dbt freshness, Monte Carlo, Airflow SLAs


Volume
row count within ±20% of 7-day avg
anomaly detectors on metadata


Business rule
revenue == quantity * unit_price * (1 - discount)
custom assertions


Cross-source
sum of warehouse revenue matches Stripe ± $0.50
reconciliation jobs



Embed validation in pipelines — fail loud, fail early. The cost of bad data scales exponentially as it moves downstream.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 2: Documenting Data Lineage

2.1 What lineage is and why it matters
Data lineage is the documented map of how data moves from source systems → ingestion → transformation → consumption. It answers: Where did this number come from? What changes if I modify this column?
Three levels of lineage granularity:

System-level — “warehouse ← Stripe + Salesforce + product events”.
Table-level — fct_revenue ← stg_stripe_charges + stg_refunds.
Column-level — fct_revenue.net_amount ← stg_stripe_charges.amount − stg_refunds.amount.

Lineage drives five concrete capabilities:

Impact analysis — “If I rename this column, what breaks downstream?”
Root-cause analysis — “Why did the executive dashboard tick up 12% last night?”
Compliance & audit — GDPR Article 30 (records of processing), SOX, HIPAA, BCBS 239 demand traceability.
Trust & adoption — analysts trust numbers they can trace.
Cost optimization — find unused tables and dead pipelines.




2.2 Lineage tooling landscape








Tool
Strength
Typical home




dbt
Code-first lineage from refs in models/; auto-generates a DAG; manifest.json is the lineage source of truth
Modern warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks)


OpenLineage / Marquez
Vendor-neutral lineage spec; integrates Airflow, Spark, dbt
Data platforms wanting interoperability


DataHub (LinkedIn)
Metadata graph with lineage, ownership, glossary
Enterprise catalog


Atlan
Active metadata, lineage UX, governance workflows
Mid–large data teams


Collibra, Alation, Informatica EDC
Enterprise data catalogs with rich governance
Regulated industries


Amundsen (Lyft)
Search-first discovery
OSS-leaning teams


Apache Atlas
Hadoop-native
Legacy on-prem Hadoop stacks


Spline
Spark lineage harvester
Spark-heavy shops



Bare minimum lineage hygiene for a small team:

Use a transformation framework that produces lineage as a side effect of writing the transforms (dbt, SQLMesh, Dagster).
Tag every dashboard with the upstream models it depends on.
Make ownership a column in your catalog: every model has a human accountable.




2.3 Provenance and audit trails
Provenance is lineage + the story of how data came to be: who ran what code, when, on what version of the source. Reproducibility demands provenance.
A robust provenance record includes:

Source system + extraction timestamp + watermark / cursor.
Transformation code + git SHA.
Run id, executor, runtime metrics.
Output checksum / row count / data quality test results.
Schema version of every input and output.

OpenLineage’s RunEvent model is the de-facto open standard for emitting these records.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
section: MODULE 3: Integrating and Validating Data Sources
text:
MODULE 3: Integrating and Validating Data Sources

3.1 Schema mapping and integration patterns
When you join sources, you must reconcile differences in: structure (schemas), semantics (definitions), granularity (one row per?), keys (natural vs surrogate), time (event time vs ingestion time), and units (USD vs cents vs local currency).
Patterns:

ETL — Extract → Transform → Load. Transformation happens before landing in the warehouse. Historically necessary when target stores were slow. Pentaho, Informatica, classic SSIS.
ELT — Extract → Load → Transform. Land raw data in cheap, scalable warehouse; transform with SQL/dbt. The modern default since Snowflake/BigQuery/Databricks made compute cheap.
Streaming / CDC — Change Data Capture from operational DBs (Debezium, Fivetran, Estuary) into Kafka → warehouse or lakehouse.
Reverse ETL — push curated metrics back into operational tools (HubSpot, Salesforce, Braze) using Census/Hightouch.




3.2 Master Data Management (MDM) and entity resolution
A single customer can appear as Jane Doe, J. Doe, Jane D. with different emails, phone numbers, and addresses across 6 systems. Entity resolution (a.k.a. record linkage) reconciles them into a single golden record.
Fellegi–Sunter (1969) probabilistic record linkage:
For each candidate pair, compute a match weight as the sum of agreement weights across fields:
\[
w_{ij} = \log_2 \frac{m_i}{u_i} \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{agree}] + \log_2 \frac{1-m_i}{1-u_i} \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{disagree}]
\]
where \(m_i\) is the probability of agreement on field \(i\) given a true match, and \(u_i\) given a non-match. Sum the weights, threshold to decide match vs non-match vs review.
Practical pipeline:

Standardization / normalization — lowercase, strip accents, parse names, geocode addresses.
Blocking — partition records into candidate blocks (e.g., by zip code or by first letter of last name) so you don’t compare \(N^2\) pairs.
Comparison — compute similarity per field: Jaccard for token sets, Jaro-Winkler for short strings (names), Levenshtein edit distance, Cosine over TF-IDF for long strings, phonetic codes (Soundex, Metaphone).
Scoring / classification — Fellegi–Sunter, logistic regression, or ML.
Clustering — connected components / hierarchical clustering across pairwise matches.
Survivorship rules — pick the surviving golden record’s attributes (most recent, most complete, source-of-truth precedence).

Open-source tools: dedupe (Python), Splink (UK ONS), Zingg, RecordLinkage.
# Tiny dedupe example
import dedupe

fields = [
    {"field": "name",   "type": "String"},
    {"field": "address","type": "String", "has missing": True},
    {"field": "city",   "type": "Exact",  "has missing": True},
]
deduper = dedupe.Dedupe(fields)
deduper.prepare_training(data_d)
deduper.train()
clustered = deduper.partition(data_d, threshold=0.5)



3.3 Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCDs)
Kimball’s SCD types describe how to track changes to dimension attributes (e.g., a customer’s address).








Type
Behavior
Use case




Type 0
Retain original; never change
Birth date, original signup channel


Type 1
Overwrite; lose history
Correcting typos; no analytical interest in past values


Type 2
Add new row per change with effective dates and current flag
Most common; full history preserved


Type 3
Keep limited prior values as extra columns (prev_region)
When only one back-version matters


Type 4
Move historical attributes to a separate history table
Large dimensions where most queries hit current state


Type 6
Hybrid 1+2+3 — overwrite current, retain history, surface previous
Advanced analytics needing both views



SCD Type 2 SQL pattern:
MERGE INTO dim_customer AS tgt
USING staging_customer AS src
ON  tgt.customer_id = src.customer_id
AND tgt.is_current = TRUE
WHEN MATCHED AND (tgt.address <> src.address) THEN
  UPDATE SET tgt.is_current = FALSE, tgt.valid_to = CURRENT_DATE
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
  INSERT (customer_id, address, valid_from, valid_to, is_current)
  VALUES (src.customer_id, src.address, CURRENT_DATE, NULL, TRUE);



3.4 DAMA-DMBOK data-quality dimensions
The DAMA DMBOK 2 recognizes six core dimensions, sometimes expanded to 10+. Memorize at least these six:








Dimension
Question
Example metric




Accuracy
Do values reflect reality?
% addresses verified against postal service


Completeness
Are required fields populated?
% null for mandatory columns


Consistency
Do values agree across systems / over time?
mismatches between CRM and billing


Timeliness
Is data available when needed?
lag between event and warehouse load


Validity
Do values conform to defined rules / domains?
% rows passing range/regex checks


Uniqueness
Are entities not duplicated?
duplicate-record rate per dimension



Optional extras: integrity, conformity, precision, traceability, currency, reasonableness.
A data quality SLA for any business-critical table should specify thresholds on each dimension, the owner, the alert channel, and the response runbook.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 4: Descriptive Statistics

4.1 Center, spread, shape
For a numeric variable \(x_1, \dots, x_n\):

Mean: \(\bar x = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i x_i\) — sensitive to outliers.
Median: value at the 50th percentile — robust.
Mode: most frequent value — useful for categorical and discrete.
Variance: \(s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_i (x_i - \bar x)^2\). The \(n-1\) (Bessel’s correction) gives an unbiased estimate.
Standard deviation: \(s = \sqrt{s^2}\) — same units as \(x\).
Coefficient of variation (CV): \(\text{CV} = s / \bar x\) — unitless, compare dispersion across series.
Skewness: \(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \left(\frac{x_i - \bar x}{s}\right)^3\). >0 right-tailed, <0 left-tailed.
Kurtosis: \(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \left(\frac{x_i - \bar x}{s}\right)^4 - 3\) (excess). >0 heavy-tailed, <0 light-tailed.

Five-number summary: min, \(Q_1\), median, \(Q_3\), max — basis of the box plot.


4.2 Robust statistics
When data contain outliers or non-normal tails, robust statistics behave better:

Median Absolute Deviation (MAD): \(\text{MAD} = \text{median}(|x_i - \tilde x|)\); \(\sigma \approx 1.4826 \cdot \text{MAD}\) for Gaussian data.
Trimmed mean: drop the top and bottom \(\alpha\%\) before averaging.
Winsorized mean: cap (not drop) extreme values before averaging.
Hodges–Lehmann estimator: median of all pairwise averages.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 5: Inferential Statistics

5.1 Sampling designs








Design
Mechanism
When to use




Simple random sample (SRS)
Each unit has equal probability
Homogeneous populations


Stratified
Sample within mutually exclusive strata
Known subgroups with different means/variances; lowers variance


Cluster
Sample whole clusters; survey all units inside
Geographically dispersed populations; saves logistics cost


Systematic
Take every \(k\)-th unit from an ordered list
Streaming/queue data; risk of periodicity


Convenience
Whatever is easy
Pilots only; never for inference


Quota / purposive
Hit fixed counts in subgroups
Market research





5.2 Central Limit Theorem (CLT) and the standard error
For an i.i.d. sample of size \(n\) from a distribution with finite mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\):
\[
\frac{\bar X_n - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \quad \text{as } n \to \infty.
\]
Practical implication: the sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal for “large enough” \(n\) (rule of thumb \(n \ge 30\), though heavy tails or strong skew demand more), even if the underlying data are not.
