The standard error of the mean is \(\text{SE} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}\) (estimated as \(s / \sqrt{n}\)).


5.3 Confidence intervals
A 95% confidence interval for the mean (with known \(\sigma\), large \(n\)):
\[
\bar x \pm z_{0.975} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}, \quad z_{0.975} \approx 1.96.
\]
With unknown \(\sigma\), swap in the Student-\(t\) critical value \(t_{n-1, \,1-\alpha/2}\) and the sample SD. Interpretation: if we were to repeat the experiment many times, 95% of constructed intervals would contain the true mean. It is not “a 95% probability that \(\mu\) lies in this interval” (frequentist vs Bayesian distinction).


5.4 Hypothesis testing framework
Five-step framework:

State \(H_0\) (null) and \(H_1\) (alternative).
Choose significance level \(\alpha\) (commonly 0.05).
Pick the test statistic appropriate to the data and design.
Compute the p-value: \(P(\text{statistic at least as extreme} \mid H_0)\).
Decide: reject \(H_0\) if \(p < \alpha\).

Errors:

Type I (false positive) — reject true \(H_0\). Probability \(\alpha\).
Type II (false negative) — fail to reject false \(H_0\). Probability \(\beta\).
Power \(= 1 - \beta\) — probability of detecting a true effect.

P-value cautions: a p-value is not the probability the null is true, not the probability the result happened by chance, and not a measure of effect size (ASA Statement on p-values, 2016). Always report effect sizes and confidence intervals alongside.


5.5 The test zoo









Test
Question
Statistic
Assumptions




One-sample t-test
Is \(\mu = \mu_0\)?
\(t = \frac{\bar x - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt n}\)
Normal-ish; \(n \ge 30\) for CLT


Two-sample t-test (Welch)
Are two group means equal?
\(t = \frac{\bar x_1 - \bar x_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2}}\)
Independent groups; unequal variances OK with Welch


Paired t-test
Difference within pairs ≠ 0?
\(t = \bar d / (s_d / \sqrt n)\)
Pairs (before/after)


One-way ANOVA
≥3 group means equal?
\(F = \text{MSB}/\text{MSW}\)
Independent, equal variance (Levene check)


Two-way ANOVA
Effects of two factors + interaction
\(F\) per factor
As above


Repeated-measures ANOVA
Within-subject across conditions
\(F\)
Sphericity (Mauchly)


Chi-square goodness-of-fit
Observed vs expected counts
\(\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O-E)^2}{E}\)
Expected counts ≥ 5


Chi-square independence
Are two categoricals independent?
as above on contingency table
as above


Fisher’s exact
Small contingency tables
exact
Small \(n\)


Mann–Whitney U
Non-parametric two-sample
rank-based \(U\)
Independent, similar shapes


Wilcoxon signed-rank
Non-parametric paired
rank-based
Symmetric differences


Kruskal–Wallis
Non-parametric ≥3 groups
\(H\)
Independent, similar shapes


Z-test for proportions
Two conversion rates equal?
\(z = (\hat p_1 - \hat p_2)/\text{SE}\)
Large \(n\), both \(np\) and \(n(1-p) > 10\)



Multiple comparisons. Running 20 tests at \(\alpha = 0.05\) yields ~64% chance of at least one false positive. Adjust:

Bonferroni: \(\alpha' = \alpha / k\) — conservative.
Holm–Bonferroni: sequentially less conservative.
Benjamini–Hochberg (BH-FDR): control the false discovery rate instead of FWER — preferred when many hypotheses (genomics, marketing test suites).

from statsmodels.stats.multitest import multipletests
reject, p_adj, _, _ = multipletests(pvals, alpha=0.05, method="fdr_bh")


5.6 Power and sample size
For a two-sample mean comparison with significance \(\alpha\), power \(1-\beta\), common SD \(\sigma\), and minimum detectable effect \(\delta\):
\[
n \approx \frac{2 (z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta})^2 \sigma^2}{\delta^2}.
\]
For two proportions:
\[
n \approx \frac{(z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta})^2 \cdot [p_1(1-p_1) + p_2(1-p_2)]}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}.
\]
Power analysis is the single most important tool to prevent both wasted experiments (underpowered) and wasted traffic (overpowered).
from statsmodels.stats.power import NormalIndPower
analysis = NormalIndPower()
n = analysis.solve_power(effect_size=0.1, alpha=0.05, power=0.8)

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 6: Correlation, Regression, GLMs

6.1 Correlation

Pearson \(r\) — linear association between two continuous variables, \(-1 \le r \le 1\). \[
r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar x)(y_i - \bar y)}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar x)^2 \sum (y_i - \bar y)^2}}.
\]
Spearman \(\rho\) — Pearson on ranks; captures monotonic, not necessarily linear.
Kendall \(\tau\) — based on concordant vs discordant pairs; robust to ties and outliers.

Correlation ≠ causation. Anscombe’s quartet (1973) and the Datasaurus Dozen (Matejka & Fitzmaurice 2017) drive this home: very different datasets can share the same \(r\), \(\mu\), \(\sigma\).


6.2 Ordinary Least Squares regression
For \(y = X\beta + \varepsilon\), the OLS estimator minimizes \(\|y - X\beta\|_2^2\) and has closed form:
\[
\hat \beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y.
\]
Gauss–Markov: under linearity, exogeneity (\(E[\varepsilon | X] = 0\)), no perfect multicollinearity, homoscedasticity, and no autocorrelation, \(\hat\beta\) is BLUE (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator).
LINE assumptions (mnemonic):

Linearity — relationship between \(X\) and \(E[y]\) is linear in parameters.
Independence — residuals are independent (no autocorrelation).
Normality — residuals are normally distributed (matters mostly for small-sample inference).
Equal variance — homoscedasticity.

Diagnostics:

Residual vs fitted plot — checks linearity and homoscedasticity.
Q-Q plot — residual normality.
Scale–location — variance pattern.
Cook’s distance, leverage — influential observations.
Variance Inflation Factor: \(\text{VIF}_j = 1/(1 - R_j^2)\), where \(R_j^2\) is the \(R^2\) of regressing \(x_j\) on the rest. \(\text{VIF} > 5\) (some say 10) → multicollinearity concern.
Durbin–Watson for autocorrelation in time series residuals.

Goodness-of-fit:

\(R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}}\) — share of variance explained.
Adjusted \(R^2 = 1 - (1 - R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-p-1}\) — penalizes added predictors.
\(F\)-test for overall significance; \(t\)-tests for individual coefficients.

import statsmodels.api as sm
X = sm.add_constant(X)
model = sm.OLS(y, X).fit()
print(model.summary())
print("VIFs:")
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor
for i, name in enumerate(X.columns):
    print(name, variance_inflation_factor(X.values, i))


6.3 Logistic regression and GLMs
For binary outcomes, model the log-odds linearly:
\[
\log \frac{p}{1-p} = X\beta, \quad p = \sigma(X\beta) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-X\beta}}.
\]
Coefficients are interpreted as log-odds-ratios; \(e^{\beta_j}\) is the multiplicative odds change for a unit change in \(x_j\), holding others fixed. Parameters fit via Maximum Likelihood.
Generalized Linear Models (Nelder & Wedderburn 1972) generalize the OLS framework with three components:

Random component — distribution from the exponential family (Normal, Binomial, Poisson, Negative Binomial, Gamma).
Systematic component — linear predictor \(\eta = X\beta\).
Link function — \(g(\mu) = \eta\).




Model
Distribution
Link
Use case




OLS
Normal
identity
continuous targets


Logistic
Binomial
logit
binary outcomes


Poisson
Poisson
log
count data


Negative Binomial
NB
log
over-dispersed counts


Gamma
Gamma
log/inverse
positive continuous (claim severity)





6.4 Regularization
When \(p\) is large or predictors are correlated, regularize:

Ridge (L2): \(\min \|y - X\beta\|^2 + \lambda \|\beta\|^2\) — shrinks coefficients, keeps all.
Lasso (L1): \(\min \|y - X\beta\|^2 + \lambda \|\beta\|_1\) — produces sparse solutions; performs feature selection.
Elastic Net: mixture; useful when groups of correlated features.

Choose \(\lambda\) by cross-validation. Always scale features before applying.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 7: Forecasting

7.1 Time-series components
A classical decomposition:

Additive: \(y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t\) — trend + seasonal + cycle + irregular. Use when amplitude does not grow with level.
Multiplicative: \(y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot I_t\). Use when seasonal swings scale with level. Equivalent to additive on \(\log y_t\).
STL (Seasonal-Trend decomposition using Loess) — robust, handles multiple seasonalities.



7.2 Stationarity and differencing
Stationarity means moments do not depend on time. Most ARIMA-family models require weak stationarity (constant mean, constant variance, autocovariance depending only on lag).

Augmented Dickey–Fuller (ADF): \(H_0\) = unit root (non-stationary).
KPSS: \(H_0\) = stationary.

Apply differencing \(\nabla y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}\) until stationary; seasonal differencing \(\nabla_s y_t = y_t - y_{t-s}\) removes seasonality.


7.3 ARIMA / SARIMA
\(ARIMA(p, d, q)\):
\[
\phi(B) (1-B)^d y_t = \theta(B) \varepsilon_t,
\]
where \(B\) is the lag operator, \(\phi(B)\) the AR polynomial of order \(p\), \(\theta(B)\) the MA polynomial of order \(q\), and \(d\) the differencing order. SARIMA adds seasonal terms \((P, D, Q)_s\).
Box–Jenkins method:

Plot, transform (log), difference.
Inspect ACF/PACF to choose \(p, q\).
Fit and check residual diagnostics (Ljung–Box).
Forecast and assess on holdout.



7.4 Exponential smoothing

SES (Simple Exponential Smoothing): \(\hat y_{t+1} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha) \hat y_t\).
Holt’s linear: adds a trend term.
Holt–Winters: adds seasonality (additive or multiplicative).
ETS state-space — unifies them with maximum likelihood.



7.5 Modern tools

Facebook Prophet — decomposable model robust to missing data and outliers; great default for business forecasts.
statsmodels — ARIMA, ETS, SARIMAX.
sktime / Darts / Nixtla statsforecast — modern unified APIs.
Croston / TSB / SBA — intermittent demand (e.g., spare parts).
DeepAR, N-BEATS, Temporal Fusion Transformer, TimesFM — global deep models for many series.



7.6 Evaluation








Metric
Formula
Notes




MAE
\(\frac1n \sum |y - \hat y|\)
intuitive, same units


RMSE
\(\sqrt{\frac1n \sum (y - \hat y)^2}\)
penalizes large errors


MAPE
\(\frac1n \sum \frac{|y - \hat y|}{|y|}\)
breaks when \(y \to 0\)


sMAPE
\(\frac1n \sum \frac{|y - \hat y|}{(|y| + |\hat y|)/2}\)
symmetric


WAPE
\(\frac{\sum |y - \hat y|}{\sum |y|}\)
weighted, robust


MASE
scale-free
benchmarks vs naïve



Use walk-forward / time-series cross-validation, not random k-fold — leakage will inflate accuracy.
from sklearn.model_selection import TimeSeriesSplit
tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5)
for tr, te in tscv.split(y):
    fit_on(y[tr]); score_on(y[te])

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 8: Root Cause Analysis (RCA)

8.1 RCA as a discipline
RCA is the structured pursuit of the underlying causes of a problem, not just its symptoms. The Toyota Production System (TPS) elevated RCA from craft to method. Three meta-principles:

Symptoms vs causes — the headache is not the brain tumor.
Necessary vs sufficient — distinguish causes that had to be present from those that triggered.
Stop at actionable — drill until the next “why” yields a controllable change.



8.2 5 Whys
Originated at Toyota (Sakichi Toyoda). Ask “why” until you reach a root cause you can act on. Example:

Why did revenue drop? — fewer checkouts completed.
Why? — checkout button errors on mobile.
Why? — payment API returned 500s.
Why? — TLS certificate rotation broke a downstream service.
Why? — renewal automation lacked monitoring.

Action: monitor cert expiry; do not “fix the button”.
Limit: linear; misses multi-cause problems.


8.3 Ishikawa (fishbone, cause-and-effect)
Categorize potential causes along branches. The classic 6 Ms for manufacturing:
Manpower, Machines, Methods, Materials, Measurement, Mother Nature.
For services, the 4 Ps: People, Process, Policies, Plant.
Build the diagram with the team — it generates hypotheses; combine with data to test them.


8.4 Pareto analysis (80/20)
Vilfredo Pareto observed 80% of land was owned by 20% of Italians. In quality, ~80% of defects come from ~20% of causes. Plot causes by frequency or impact; act on the vital few. Pair with cost-weighted analysis (impact × cost-to-fix).


8.5 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)
Top-down deductive analysis used in safety-critical industries. The undesired top event decomposes via AND/OR gates into basic events. Quantify with reliability probabilities.


8.6 Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)
Forward-looking, exhaustive enumeration of failure modes for each step. For each: Severity (S), Occurrence (O), Detection (D), each 1–10. Compute Risk Priority Number \(\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D\). Prioritize highest RPN for mitigation.


8.7 A3 problem-solving
One-page report (A3 paper) capturing the entire problem-solving cycle: background, current condition, goals, root-cause analysis, countermeasures, plan, follow-up. Used at Toyota to enforce both rigor and brevity.


8.8 Kepner–Tregoe and Apollo RCA

Kepner–Tregoe — separates Problem Analysis (what, where, when, extent; what is and is not), Decision Analysis, and Potential Problem Analysis.
Apollo RCA — every effect has at least two causes (action + condition); chart them recursively.



8.9 Causal diagrams and counterfactuals
Modern RCA borrows from causal inference (Pearl). A Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) encodes assumed cause–effect relationships. Counterfactual reasoning asks: what would \(Y\) have been if \(X\) had been different, holding \(Z\) fixed? When randomized experiments are impossible, lean on:

Difference-in-Differences (DiD) — compare pre/post trends in treated vs control.
Regression Discontinuity (RDD) — exploit a threshold.
Instrumental Variables (IV) — find variation in \(X\) unrelated to confounders.
Propensity Score Matching / IPW — re-weight to balance covariates.
Synthetic Control — construct a weighted “synthetic” control unit from non-treated peers.



8.10 End-to-end RCA worked example: a 12% conversion drop

Frame. Conversion fell from 4.5% to 4.0% week-over-week on the signup flow.
Triangulate. Decompose: visitors steady, signups down → conversion really fell. Check freshness of data: yes, ETL ran on time.
Segment. By device: mobile −18%, desktop flat. By geo: US only. By browser: iOS Safari.
Hypothesize. Recent release shipped a new TLS lib affecting iOS 17.
Test. Sentry shows spike of TLS errors on iOS 17 builds; logs confirm.
Counterfactual. Roll back the lib in a canary cell; conversion returns to 4.5%.
Permanent fix. Pin compatible lib; add iOS-version smoke test; alert on conversion + error correlation.
Document. A3 + postmortem; share to broader team.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
section: MODULE 9: Translating Business Goals to Metrics
text:
MODULE 9: Translating Business Goals to Metrics

9.1 Strategy maps and MECE decomposition
Kaplan & Norton’s strategy map visualizes how learning & growth investments enable internal processes, which deliver customer outcomes, which produce financial results. Each link is a testable hypothesis. Translating strategy to measurement means turning each box into a metric.
Barbara Minto’s MECE principle (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive) ensures decompositions cover the space without overlap. A revenue decomposition might look like:
\[
\text{Revenue} = \text{Users} \times \text{Conversion} \times \text{ARPU}
\]
then ARPU \(= \text{Sessions per User} \times \text{Conversion per Session} \times \text{AOV}\), and so on. Each leaf is owned, measured, and movable.


9.2 Jobs-to-be-done (JTBD)
Clayton Christensen’s lens: customers “hire” products to make progress on a job. JTBD reframes metrics around progress rather than features. Example: a CRM’s “job” is “close the deal by Friday”; the metric should track that, not “logins per week”.


9.3 Hypothesis-driven analytics
Frame every analysis as: “We believe X will move Y because Z, and we will know we are right when we see W.” This forces falsifiability and avoids unfocused dashboards.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 10: KPI Frameworks

10.1 SMART criteria
A KPI worth its name is:

Specific — unambiguous numerator and denominator.
Measurable — instrumented end-to-end.
Achievable — within reach of plausible interventions.
Relevant — ties to a strategic outcome.
Time-bound — has a target date or cadence.



10.2 Lead vs lag metrics

Lagging indicators measure outcomes (revenue, churn, NPS). They confirm whether strategy worked.
Leading indicators measure inputs and intermediate behaviors (sign-ups, activations, support contact rate). They predict outcomes and provide steering capability.

Pair them: every lagging KPI should have one or more leading KPIs that move first.


10.3 Inputs → Outputs → Outcomes → Impact
A four-tier KPI hierarchy from logic-model program evaluation:

Inputs — resources committed (dollars, headcount).
Outputs — what we produce (features shipped, emails sent).
Outcomes — behavior change (activation, retention).
Impact — long-term, often multi-causal (revenue, market share, NPS).

Vanity metrics live at the Output layer. Real impact lives further down.


10.4 North Star Metric (NSM)
Coined by Sean Ellis and popularized by Reforge/Andrew Chen. The NSM:

Captures the core value customers get.
Reflects product-market fit and growth simultaneously.
Is leading — predictive of long-term revenue.

Examples:



Company
Plausible NSM




Airbnb
nights booked


Spotify
time spent listening


Slack
weekly active teams sending ≥ 2000 messages


Duolingo
DAU


Facebook (early)
7 friends in 10 days (activation proxy)



NSM should be one number, with a small set of input metrics under it (the “constellation”).


10.5 AARRR / Pirate metrics
Dave McClure’s funnel for product analytics:








Stage
Question
Sample metric




Acquisition
How do users find us?
sessions, signups, CPA


Activation
Do they get value fast?
% completing onboarding, time-to-value


Retention
Do they come back?
D1/D7/D30 retention, churn


Referral
Do they invite others?
viral coefficient, NPS


Revenue
Do they pay?
ARPU, LTV, MRR





10.6 Google’s HEART framework
For user experience: Happiness (sat surveys), Engagement (DAU/session length), Adoption (new users), Retention (returning), Task success (completion, error rates). Pair each with Goals, Signals, Metrics (GSM).


10.7 OKRs (Doerr, Measure What Matters)
Andy Grove → John Doerr → Google. An OKR:

Objective — qualitative, ambitious, time-bound.
Key Results — 3–5 quantitative, measurable outcomes.

Cadence: quarterly; review weekly; score 0–1; aim for ~0.7 on stretch.
Pitfalls:

Confusing KRs with tasks (“launch onboarding redesign” — that’s an initiative, not a KR).
Cascading rigidly instead of aligning.
Tying directly to compensation — breeds sandbagging.



10.8 Balanced Scorecard (Kaplan & Norton)
Four perspectives, each with objectives, measures, targets, initiatives:

Financial — revenue, margin, ROI.
Customer — satisfaction, retention, share of wallet.
Internal Processes — cycle time, defect rate, throughput.
Learning & Growth — skills, culture, information capital.

The BSC pioneered the idea that financials alone are lagging and insufficient. Strategy maps link the four perspectives causally.


10.9 KPI / Driver trees
Decompose a top-level KPI multiplicatively/additively. Example:
Revenue
├── New customer revenue
│   ├── Traffic
│   ├── Conversion rate
│   └── First order value
└── Existing customer revenue
    ├── Active customers
    ├── Orders per active customer
    └── AOV
Tools like Causal, Mosaic, Pigment, Anaplan, and Causal Lens specialize in driver trees with scenario modeling.


10.10 Vanity vs actionable metrics
Eric Ries’s distinction. Vanity metrics (cumulative signups, page views) only go up and tell you nothing. Actionable metrics are:

Comparable (across cohorts, channels, time)
Tied to behavior change
Per-capita rather than total



10.11 Goodhart’s and Campbell’s law

“When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.” — Marilyn Strathern’s restatement of Goodhart.


“The more any quantitative social indicator is used for social decision-making, the more apt it will be to distort and corrupt the social processes it is intended to monitor.” — Donald T. Campbell.

Implications:

Metric gaming is the default response, not the exception.
Pair every “drive-up” metric with a “do-no-harm” guardrail (e.g., DAU paired with retention; revenue paired with refunds).
Rotate or refresh metrics when gaming behavior emerges.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 11: Performance Trends

11.1 Period-over-period framing

YoY (Year-over-Year): \(\frac{V_t - V_{t-12}}{V_{t-12}}\) — controls for seasonality.
MoM, WoW, QoQ: sensitive to seasonality; pair with YoY.
Trailing-Twelve-Months (TTM) or Last-Twelve-Months (LTM): rolling 12-month sum; smooths noise.
Year-To-Date (YTD) vs Prior-Year-To-Date (PYTD).



11.2 CAGR — Compound Annual Growth Rate
\[
\text{CAGR} = \left( \frac{V_f}{V_0} \right)^{1/n} - 1.
\]
CAGR assumes smooth compounding — it does not describe the path, only endpoints. Pair with a variance-aware view (e.g., volatility).


11.3 Moving averages and smoothing

SMA (Simple Moving Average) — equal weight over \(k\) periods.
WMA (Weighted Moving Average) — declining weights.
EWMA / EMA (Exponential) — \(\hat y_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha) \hat y_{t-1}\); controls memory via \(\alpha\).
LOESS / LOWESS — local regression smoothing for exploratory plots.



11.4 Indexing and base periods
Convert raw series into indices (Base 100) to compare differently scaled metrics on one chart. Use rebased views to compare since-launch performance regardless of absolute scale.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 12: Driver Analysis

12.1 Decompositional drivers
Use identities to attribute change:
\[
\Delta \text{Revenue} \approx \frac{\partial R}{\partial U} \Delta U + \frac{\partial R}{\partial \text{ARPU}} \Delta \text{ARPU}.
\]
The price–volume–mix bridge widely used in CFO analytics:
\[
\Delta R = \underbrace{(P_1 - P_0) Q_1}_{\text{price}} + \underbrace{P_0 (Q_1 - Q_0)}_{\text{volume}} + \underbrace{\text{mix effect}}_{\text{interaction}}.
\]


12.2 Statistical / regression-based drivers
Fit a model to identify which inputs drive the KPI:

Linear regression with standardized coefficients (β weights).
Random Forest / Gradient Boosting feature importances (with care — correlated features distort).
SHAP values for both global and local driver attribution.
Partial Dependence Plots for shape of effect.



12.3 Causal vs correlational drivers
Driver dashboards often mislabel correlation as cause. Reduce risk by:

Holding everything else constant via segmentation.
Using lagged predictors to enforce temporal ordering.
Backing key drivers with experiments.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
section: MODULE 13: Practical forecasting in a business setting
text:
MODULE 13: Practical forecasting in a business setting

Always present a point forecast + interval — wider intervals signal uncertainty honestly.
Build at the most granular level you can forecast accurately, then aggregate; or build hierarchically with reconciliation (MinT, bottom-up, top-down).
Reserve a holdout window that mimics how the forecast will be used in production.
Track forecast bias (mean error) and accuracy separately. Persistent bias indicates a systemic miss; high variance indicates noise.
Document every assumption, especially levers vs exogenous drivers.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
section: MODULE 14: SIPOC and Value Stream Mapping
text:
MODULE 14: SIPOC and Value Stream Mapping
SIPOC — Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers. The first deliverable of any improvement project: a one-page agreement on scope and stakeholders.
Value Stream Map (VSM) — visualizes material and information flow, value-add vs non-value-add time. The classic metric: Process Cycle Efficiency = value-add time / total lead time. Typical office processes start at 5–15%.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 15: Process mining
Process mining (Wil van der Aalst’s discipline) discovers process models from event logs. An event log has at minimum: case ID, activity, timestamp; often resource and additional attributes.
Three families of techniques:

Discovery — learn a process model from logs (Alpha miner, Heuristic miner, Inductive miner).
Conformance — compare observed traces against a normative model; flag deviations (token replay, alignments).
Enhancement — extend the model with performance, frequency, or organizational overlays.

Tools: Celonis, UiPath Process Mining, Disco, ProM, PM4Py (Python).
import pm4py
log = pm4py.read_xes("loan_process.xes")
net, im, fm = pm4py.discover_petri_net_inductive(log)
pm4py.view_petri_net(net, im, fm)

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
section: MODULE 16: Lean and the Seven Wastes (TIMWOODS)
text:
MODULE 16: Lean and the Seven Wastes (TIMWOODS)
The eight wastes of Lean (the original seven plus the modern “Skills” / non-utilized talent):

Transport — moving things unnecessarily.
Inventory — work-in-progress or stock piling up.
Motion — people moving unnecessarily.
Waiting — idle time.
Overproduction — making more than demanded.
Over-processing — doing more than the customer needs.
Defects — rework.
Skills — under-utilizing people’s capability.

Lean’s mantra: flow, pull, perfection. Reduce batch size, expose problems, improve relentlessly.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 17: Six Sigma — DMAIC
Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control. Each phase has classic tools:



Phase
Tools




Define
Project charter, SIPOC, VOC, CTQs


Measure
Data collection plan, Gauge R&R, baseline capability


Analyze
Pareto, fishbone, hypothesis tests, regression, FMEA


Improve
DOE (design of experiments), pilot tests, Poka-Yoke


Control
Control charts, SOPs, control plans, training



Belts: Yellow (basic), Green (project lead), Black (full time), Master Black (program leader).

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 18: Statistical Process Control (SPC)
Walter Shewhart (Bell Labs, 1924) introduced control charts. Donald Wheeler’s Understanding Statistical Process Control remains the practitioner bible.
Why control charts? Distinguish common-cause variation (inherent in the process) from special-cause (assignable, requiring action). Reacting to common-cause variation as if it were special — “tampering” — increases variation.

18.1 Control chart families








Chart
Data type
Use case




X-bar / R
continuous; subgroups
Manufacturing measurements


X-bar / s
continuous; large subgroups
as above


I-MR (Individuals, Moving Range)
continuous; n=1
Service / process metrics


p-chart
proportion defective
call abandonment rate, error rate


np-chart
count defective with fixed n
as above when n fixed


c-chart
count of defects per unit
typos per page


u-chart
defects per unit, variable area
defects per kilometer of cable





18.2 Control limits
For an X-bar chart (subgroups of size \(n\)):
\[
\text{UCL} = \bar{\bar X} + A_2 \bar R, \quad \text{LCL} = \bar{\bar X} - A_2 \bar R,
\]
where \(A_2\) depends on \(n\). More generally, the three-sigma rule:
\[
\text{UCL/LCL} = \mu \pm 3\sigma.
\]


18.3 Western Electric / Nelson rules
Detect special-cause patterns:

One point beyond 3σ.
Nine points in a row on one side of the centerline.
Six points trending up or down.
Two of three consecutive points beyond 2σ on the same side.
Four of five beyond 1σ on the same side.
Fifteen points in a row within 1σ (over-control suspicion).
Eight points in a row outside 1σ.



18.4 Process capability — Cp, Cpk
For a centered, normal process with spec limits LSL and USL:
\[
C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}, \quad C_{pk} = \min\!\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right).
\]
Rule of thumb: \(C_{pk} \ge 1.33\) is “capable”; \(\ge 1.67\) is excellent; \(\ge 2.0\) implies Six Sigma quality (3.4 DPMO).
Pp / Ppk use long-term sigma instead of within-subgroup sigma; usually \(C_{pk} \ge P_{pk}\).

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 19: Theory of Constraints (ToC)
Eliyahu Goldratt, The Goal (1984). The throughput of a system is determined by its slowest step. The Five Focusing Steps:

Identify the constraint.
Exploit it (make sure it never starves or stalls).
Subordinate everything else to the constraint.
Elevate the constraint (invest to expand its capacity).
Repeat — the constraint moves.

ToC introduces Throughput Accounting (Throughput = Sales − truly variable cost) and Drum-Buffer-Rope scheduling. In knowledge work, ToC manifests as WIP limits (Kanban) and bottleneck-aware sequencing.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 20: BPMN and process simulation
BPMN 2.0 (OMG standard) is the lingua franca of process modeling: pools, swim lanes, tasks, events, gateways, message flows.
Discrete-event simulation (SimPy in Python, AnyLogic, Arena) lets you model arrival distributions, service times, and resource constraints, then experiment with policy changes (more staff, smaller batch sizes) before committing to them.
import simpy, random

def customer(env, name, server):
    yield env.timeout(random.expovariate(1/2))   # inter-arrival
    with server.request() as req:
        yield req
        yield env.timeout(random.expovariate(1/3))  # service

env = simpy.Environment()
server = simpy.Resource(env, capacity=2)
for i in range(100):
    env.process(customer(env, i, server))
env.run(until=200)

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 21: Defining the Prediction Need
A predictive analytics request should fit on one page:

Decision to be made (concrete, recurring).
Action taken once a prediction is in hand (who acts, how, when).
Target to be predicted (definition, label-source).
Granularity (per user, per session, per SKU, per minute).
Horizon (how far ahead).
Latency budget (real-time vs daily batch).
Value of correctness and cost of errors (asymmetric for fraud, churn).
Baselines (rule-based, naïve, current human accuracy).

If you cannot fill in any of these, you have a research project, not a predictive analytics project.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 22: Data Readiness
Borrow the Data Readiness Levels (Lawrence 2017) framework — a maturity ladder from raw bytes to model-ready, decision-ready data:



Level
State




D-4 Conceived
the dataset is hypothesized but not collected


D-3 Available
dataset exists somewhere


D-2 Loadable
machine-readable, accessible


D-1 Faithful
known to represent the phenomenon


C-3 Cleaned
clean of obvious errors


C-2 Quality-assured
passes QA checks


C-1 Modeling-ready
features engineered


B-3 Task-relevant
matches the prediction task


B-2 Contextualized
situated in problem domain


B-1 Decision-ready
tied to the decision/action



Move along this ladder before fitting a model. Skipping causes the all-too-common “we built a model but it doesn’t move the metric”.

22.1 Label readiness
Models inherit the quality of their labels.

Where do labels come from? Direct measurement, human annotation, weak supervision, business outcomes, proxies.
How noisy? Quantify inter-annotator agreement (Cohen’s kappa, Krippendorff’s alpha).
How fresh? Are labels available within the latency budget?
How biased? Do labels reflect past decisions (selection bias) rather than ground truth?



22.2 Feasibility checklist

Volume sufficient for the chosen model class (rule of thumb: 10× as many examples as parameters).
Signal-to-noise ratio plausible (talk to domain experts; pilot a small predictive study).
Labels stable over time (concept drift).
Regulatory and ethical clearance (GDPR, EEOC, HIPAA, etc.).
Tech stack ready: feature store, model registry, monitoring.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 23: Scoping Analytics Solutions
Scope artifacts:

Analytics charter — purpose, sponsor, scope in/out, deliverables, success metrics, decision rights.
Stakeholder map — sponsor, owner, users, contributors, governance.
RACI — Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed.
Risk register — top 10 risks with likelihood × impact and mitigation.
Definition of Done for each phase.

Build vs buy vs partner decision tree:

Is the capability core to competitive advantage? If yes → build.
Is a mature off-the-shelf solution available, well-aligned? If yes → buy.
Do we need rapid time-to-value and external expertise? → partner.
Hybrid is the norm: buy the platform, build the domain layer.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
section: MODULE 24: Plan and Define Usage Scenario
text:
MODULE 24: Plan and Define Usage Scenario
A usage scenario is a concrete narrative of how a real user will interact with the analytics output.
Example template:

“At 8:05 AM every Monday, the regional sales director opens the executive dashboard, reviews the YoY revenue, and inspects the top three negative drivers. If any driver exceeds a 5% deviation, the dashboard surfaces a drill-down link to the operational report. The director either reassigns quota, flags to the COO, or marks the variance as accepted with a comment.”

Each verb in the scenario implies a feature: refresh frequency, calculation, threshold, surfacing, drill-through, annotation, audit trail.

24.1 Project plan structure
Modern analytics projects often follow a hybrid waterfall/agile cadence:

Discovery (1–2 weeks): scoping, data readiness, alignment.
Design (1–2 weeks): metric definitions, mockups, data contracts.
Build sprints (2-week cycles): data pipeline, modeling, UI.
Pilot / UAT.
Launch.
Hypercare (first 2 weeks post-launch).
Steady-state operation with monthly reviews.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 25: Designing Business Rules
Not every decision should be ML. A business rule beats a model when:

The logic is stable, transparent, and regulated.
The data needed for ML is missing.
The cost of an opaque error is unacceptable.
The volume is low (rules are cheap; models need scale).


25.1 Decision tables
A compact form:



Condition: Customer Tier
Order Value
Action




Platinum
any
Free shipping + handwritten note


Gold
≥ $200
Free shipping


Gold
< $200
Standard shipping


Silver
≥ $500
Free shipping


Silver
< $500
Standard shipping


Bronze
any
Standard shipping



Validate that the table is complete (every input combination handled) and consistent (no contradictions). Modern decisioning platforms: Drools, Camunda DMN, IBM ODM, FICO Blaze.


25.2 DMN — Decision Model and Notation
OMG standard for decision models, complementing BPMN. Decision Requirements Diagrams (DRD) plus DMN tables make business rules first-class.


25.3 Hybrid rule + model systems
Common patterns:

Champion / challenger — rule is champion; model is challenger gated by an experiment.
Rules as guardrails — ML predicts; rules override at boundaries (e.g., regulatory minimums).
Model triages, rule decides — ML flags risky cases; rules determine action.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 26: Driving Adoption
A model that nobody uses is a write-off. Anchor adoption from day 1.

26.1 Stakeholder mapping
Map stakeholders on influence × interest. Treat each cell differently:




Low interest
High interest




Low influence
Monitor
Keep informed


High influence
Keep satisfied
Manage closely





26.2 Change management — Kotter’s 8 steps
John Kotter, Leading Change (1996):

Create urgency.
Form a guiding coalition.
Develop the vision and strategy.
Communicate the vision.
Empower broad-based action (remove obstacles).
Generate short-term wins.
Sustain acceleration (build on wins).
Anchor change in culture.



26.3 ADKAR (Prosci)
Personal-level change model — every individual must traverse:

Awareness — that change is needed.
Desire — to support and participate.
Knowledge — of how to change.
Ability — to apply new behaviors.
Reinforcement — to make change stick.

Diagnose where individuals are stuck and intervene at that step (a comms problem at “Awareness” is very different from a training problem at “Ability”).


26.4 Adoption anti-patterns

Dashboard graveyard — hundreds of unused dashboards.
Data hoarding — analysts keep numbers in private spreadsheets.
Tribal definitions — same metric, different numbers across teams.
Build-and-pray — no onboarding plan; expect users to “discover” the tool.
Vanity sponsorship — exec signs the charter but never uses it.

Mitigations: clear ownership, executive dogfooding, named “data champions” in each team, telemetry on dashboard usage, sunset policies for stale assets.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 27: Validating Business Impact — Experimentation

27.1 A/B test fundamentals
The gold standard for causal claims. Randomly assign units to control and treatment, measure the difference in the target metric.
Sample size formula for two means with significance \(\alpha\), power \(1-\beta\), SD \(\sigma\), minimum detectable effect \(\delta\):
\[
n \approx \frac{(z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta})^2 \cdot 2 \sigma^2}{\delta^2}.
\]
For two proportions \(p_1, p_2\) with mean \(\bar p\):
\[
n \approx \frac{(z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta})^2 \cdot [p_1(1-p_1) + p_2(1-p_2)]}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}.
\]
Always plan with a minimum detectable effect (MDE) that is meaningful to the business — not just “statistically detectable”.


27.2 Common pitfalls

Peeking / sequential testing — stopping when significant inflates Type I rates. Use sequential testing methods (mSPRT, Bayesian alpha-spending, group sequential).
Sample Ratio Mismatch (SRM) — if your 50/50 split delivers 49/51, your randomization is broken; do not trust results. Use a chi-square SRM check on assignment counts.
Novelty / primacy effects — short tests overestimate new feature impact.
Network / spillover effects — users in marketplaces influence each other; consider cluster randomization.
Heterogeneous treatment effects — overall null may hide strong subgroup effects; pre-register cuts.
Multiple comparisons — testing many metrics or many cells inflates Type I.
p-hacking — choosing the test, segmentation, or metric post-hoc.
Underpowered tests — drawing strong null conclusions from a 70% power study is a Type II in waiting.
Drifting populations — pause during marketing pushes that change traffic mix.



27.3 Variance reduction — CUPED
Deng, Xu, Kohavi, Walker (2013). Use a covariate \(X\) (typically pre-experiment value of the metric) to reduce variance:
\[
Y^{\text{cuped}} = Y - \theta (X - \bar X), \quad \theta = \frac{\text{Cov}(Y, X)}{\text{Var}(X)}.
\]
Variance reduction \(\approx \rho^2_{Y,X}\). Effective sample size can grow 30–60% without recruiting more users.


27.4 Beyond A/B — quasi-experiments
When randomization is impossible:

Difference-in-Differences (DiD) — compare changes in treated vs control over the same period.
Regression Discontinuity Design (RDD) — exploit a sharp cutoff (e.g., scholarship at GPA = 3.5).
Interrupted Time Series (ITS) — model a single series, look for a level/slope shift after intervention.
Synthetic Control (Abadie et al.) — weighted average of donors mimics treated unit’s pre-period.



27.5 Trustworthy experimentation maturity
Kohavi/Tang/Xu identify maturity levels: ad-hoc → standard process → platform → integrated decisions → cultural default. At maturity, every shipped change has a hypothesis, a guardrail set, a sample size, and a registered analysis plan.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 28: Monitoring Business Value

28.1 ROI and friends

ROI = \(\frac{\text{Gain} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}}\). Use a defined time horizon.
Payback period = months until cumulative cash flow ≥ initial investment.
Discounted Payback — same, with discounted cash flows.
NPV = \(\sum_t \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}\) — positive NPV → value-creating.
IRR — discount rate that makes NPV = 0. Beware of multiple IRRs with sign changes.
EVA (Economic Value Added) = NOPAT − WACC × Invested Capital.
Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) — includes capex, opex, training, change-management, opportunity cost.



28.2 Customer Lifetime Value (CLV / LTV)
Subscription model (simple):
\[
\text{LTV} = \frac{\text{ARPU} \times \text{Gross Margin}}{\text{Churn Rate}}.
\]
Discounted version:
\[
\text{LTV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{\text{ARPU}_t \cdot \text{Margin}_t \cdot \text{Retention}_t}{(1+r)^t}.
\]
Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) = total sales & marketing / new customers acquired in a period.
LTV/CAC rule of 3: <1 unsustainable, 1–3 weak, ≥3 healthy SaaS. Pair with CAC payback in months — under 12 is great, under 18 is normal.


28.3 Cohort retention curves
Track retention by acquisition cohort over tenure:
\[
\text{Retention}_{c, t} = \frac{\text{Active}_{c, t}}{\text{Cohort size}_c}.
\]
Curves typically follow a steep early drop then flatten (the “retention smile” in healthy products). Look for:

The asymptote (plateau) — does the curve stabilize above 0? That fraction is your long-term loyal base.
Cohort-over-cohort improvement — newer cohorts should retain better as you iterate.
Seasonality — Q4 cohorts may behave differently.

import pandas as pd
df["cohort"] = df.groupby("user_id")["signup_dt"].transform("min").dt.to_period("M")
df["age"] = (df["event_dt"].dt.to_period("M") - df["cohort"]).apply(lambda r: r.n)
retention = (
    df.groupby(["cohort", "age"])["user_id"]
      .nunique()
      .unstack()
      .pipe(lambda d: d.divide(d[0], axis=0))
)


28.4 Continuous business value monitoring
Set up:

A value dashboard that tracks the originally promised business outcome alongside model/process metrics.
Tripwires that alert if value falls below threshold or if assumptions invalidate.
Quarterly value review with the executive sponsor — what we promised, what we delivered, what we learned, what we’ll do next.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 29: Communications, training, dogfooding

Communications cadence — kickoff, midpoint, launch, post-launch. Match channel to audience (Slack for daily, email for milestones, exec readouts for sponsorship).
Training programs — three layers: power users (deep), end users (workflow), executives (interpretation).
Dogfooding — leaders use the tool in real decisions; make it visible.
Data champions network — embedded analysts in each business unit who escalate issues and evangelize good practice.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 30: Governance & ethics in analytics

Data privacy & anonymization — k-anonymity, l-diversity, differential privacy.
Role-based access (RBAC), attribute-based access (ABAC) — least privilege.
PII handling — masking, tokenization, encryption at rest.
Fairness & proxies — Simpson’s paradox warning: aggregated metrics can hide subgroup disparities. Always check segments for protected attributes (where legally allowed). Beware proxy bias (zip code ↔︎ race).
Regulatory landscape — GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, SOX, BCBS 239, EEOC; emerging EU AI Act; sector-specific guidance (Model Risk Management SR 11-7 in banking).

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 31: Chart selection by intent
The intent of the chart drives the form. Misalign these and the audience misreads. Andrew Abela’s “chart chooser” and Few/Tufte’s principles converge on:







Intent
Best chart




Comparison across categories
Horizontal bar (sortable), grouped bar (≤4 categories)


Composition (parts of whole), single time point
100% stacked bar; avoid pie when many categories


Composition over time
Stacked area (relative) or 100% stacked column


Trend over time
Line; sparklines for compact context


Distribution
Histogram, density plot, box plot, violin


Distribution comparison
Box plot side-by-side, ridgeline plot


Relationship between two variables
Scatter plot; add regression line


Three-variable relationship
Bubble (size = third variable)


Correlation matrix
Heatmap with diverging palette


Hierarchy
Treemap, sunburst, icicle


Flow
Sankey, alluvial


Geography
Choropleth (rates), proportional symbol (counts), cartogram


Rank
Ordered bar, dumbbell, slope chart for “before/after”


Process
Funnel, swim lane, BPMN


Variance to target
Bullet chart (Stephen Few)



Pie charts — defensible for two slices or single-emphasis (~“X% vs everything else”); otherwise prefer a bar.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
section: MODULE 32: Tufte, Few, and the data-ink ratio
text:
MODULE 32: Tufte, Few, and the data-ink ratio
Tufte’s principles:

Maximize data-ink ratio — every drop of ink should encode data.
Reduce chartjunk — gridlines, gradients, 3D effects, decorations.
Small multiples — repeat the same chart across subsets to enable comparison.
Sparklines — word-sized data graphics; great in tables and exec reports.
Layering and separation — use weight, color, and position to create hierarchy.
Integrate text, table, graph — show the number and the picture together.

Few adds:

Pre-attentive attributes — properties that the visual system perceives in <250 ms: position, length, color, orientation, size, shape. Use position and length for accurate quantitative comparison; reserve color for categorical or qualitative encoding.
Bullet charts for KPI vs target on dashboards.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 33: Color, type, accessibility

Sequential palettes — single-hue gradient (e.g., light to dark blue) for ordered data.
Diverging palettes — two hues with neutral midpoint for data with a meaningful zero (e.g., RdBu).
Qualitative palettes — distinct hues for unordered categories (≤7).
Colorblind safety — Okabe-Ito palette; check with simulators (Sim Daltonism, Colorbrewer’s CB-safe option).
Type hierarchy — title > subtitle > axis > annotation > footnote. Use weight and size, not five different fonts.
Accessibility — WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ≥ 4.5:1 for text; provide non-color cues (icons, labels); enable keyboard navigation in interactive dashboards.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 34: Dashboards — strategic, tactical, operational
Three dashboard tiers (Few):









Tier
Audience
Refresh
Focus




Strategic
execs, board
daily/weekly
outcomes, period-over-period, forecasts


Tactical
managers, PMs
hourly/daily
drivers, segments, comparisons


Operational
front-line, NOC
real-time
live status, alerts, queues




34.1 Shneiderman’s mantra

“Overview first, zoom and filter, then details-on-demand.”

A good dashboard layout follows this top-to-bottom: headline KPIs → driver breakouts → segment tables → drill-throughs.


34.2 Cross-filtering, parameters, row-level security

Cross-filtering — clicking a bar filters the rest of the page. Essential for exploratory dashboards.
Parameters — user-supplied inputs (date range, region, currency) cascade through visuals.
Row-level security (RLS) — same dashboard, different users see different rows (e.g., regional managers see only their region). Implement in semantic layer (LookML, Tableau RLS, Power BI RLS, Cube.js, Snowflake row access policies).



34.3 Tooling landscape







Tool
Sweet spot




Tableau
rich ad-hoc viz; enterprise-friendly


Power BI
Microsoft estate; DAX modeling


Looker / Looker Studio
semantic layer (LookML); governed self-service


Mode, Hex
analyst-friendly notebook + SQL + dashboard


Sigma
spreadsheet-native cloud BI


Apache Superset
open-source, SQL-native


Metabase
simple self-serve open source


Grafana
ops dashboards on time-series databases


Streamlit / Dash / Panel
custom data apps for analysts

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 35: Executive dashboards
For executives, outcome > activity, narrative > data dump, fewer numbers > many. Practices:

One screen, no scrolling. Fits on the laptop and the phone.
Five to ten KPIs, each with target, actual, variance, trend sparkline.
Period context — YoY, MoM, vs target.
Forecast cone — best/base/worst case for the rest of the period.
Narrative annotation — “Q2 dip driven by APAC FX swing; remediation in flight (link)”.
Single source of truth — one number, one definition; link to underlying analysis.
Mobile-friendly — many execs review on phones.

Example layout:
+----------------------------+---------------------------+
| Revenue (YoY +12%) ▲       | Margin (YoY -150 bps) ▼   |
| spark + target band        | spark + benchmark         |
+----------------------------+---------------------------+
| Net Retention 118%         | Activation Rate 64%       |
| spark + cohort heatmap     | funnel mini-chart         |
+----------------------------+---------------------------+
| Top movers this week:                                  |
|  + Enterprise APAC pipeline +$2.4M                    |
|  - SMB churn EU +1.1 ppt                              |
| (commentary, owner, ETA)                              |
+--------------------------------------------------------+

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 36: Performance scorecards
A performance scorecard is the operational artifact of the Balanced Scorecard. Each KPI row:
