Visual conventions: traffic lights (R/A/G), arrows (▲/▼), bullet charts for target attainment.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 37: Business case structure
A complete business case answers, in order:

Executive summary (1 page) — the pyramid principle: situation, complication, question, answer.
Background & problem statement — what is the pain, with quantified evidence.
Strategic alignment — which OKRs / BSC objectives this serves.
Options analysis — at least three options, including the “do nothing” baseline. Evaluate on cost, benefit, risk, time, dependencies.
Recommended option — with rationale.
Quantified benefits — incremental revenue, cost savings, risk reduction; show methodology and assumptions.
Costs — capex, opex, change costs, opportunity cost.
Financial summary — NPV, IRR, payback, ROI, sensitivity tornado.
Risks & mitigations — top 5 with owners.
Timeline & milestones — gantt or roadmap.
Decision asked of the audience — approve, reject, send back.

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
section: MODULE 38: NPV / IRR / Payback in detail
text:
MODULE 38: NPV / IRR / Payback in detail

38.1 NPV
\[
\text{NPV} = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}.
\]

\(C_0\) — initial investment (today).
\(CF_t\) — net cash flow in year \(t\).
\(r\) — discount rate (often WACC).

Positive NPV → invest; comparing mutually exclusive projects, pick the highest NPV.


38.2 IRR
IRR is the \(r\) that makes \(\text{NPV}(r) = 0\). Pitfalls:

Multiple IRRs when sign of cash flows changes more than once.
Reinvestment assumption (cash flows reinvested at IRR) is often unrealistic — prefer MIRR.



38.3 Payback and discounted payback

Payback = years until cumulative \(CF\) ≥ \(C_0\). Ignores time value and post-payback flows.
Discounted payback — same with discounted flows.
Useful for liquidity-constrained executives, but never the sole decision criterion.



38.4 Sensitivity and scenario analysis

One-way sensitivity (tornado) — vary each input ±X% while holding others fixed; rank by NPV impact.
Scenario analysis — combine inputs into best / base / worst cases.
Monte Carlo — sample inputs from distributions; report NPV distribution and probability of negative NPV.

import numpy as np
n = 100_000
growth = np.random.normal(0.06, 0.02, n)
margin = np.random.normal(0.30, 0.05, n)
spend  = 1_000_000
revenue = spend * (1 + growth)
npv = (revenue * margin - spend) / 1.10
print(np.mean(npv), np.percentile(npv, [5, 50, 95]))

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
section: MODULE 39: Pyramid Principle and SCQA storytelling
text:
MODULE 39: Pyramid Principle and SCQA storytelling
Barbara Minto (McKinsey, The Pyramid Principle). Build communication top-down:

Answer first — start with the main message.
Group supporting arguments under it, MECE.
Order the arguments by importance, time, or structure.
Drill for details only as needed.

SCQA is the executive opener:

Situation — agreed-upon context.
Complication — what changed or is going wrong.
Question — what should we do?
Answer — the recommended path.

Pair with MECE and so-what tests (“if my reader stopped reading here, did they get the message?”).

title: Business Analytics Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 40: Resource & Performance Allocation

40.1 Capital budgeting and portfolio prioritization
When demand for investment > supply:

Rank projects by NPV / capital required when capital is the binding constraint.
Use profitability index (NPV / cost) when comparing similarly sized projects.
Apply Cost of Delay for time-sensitive opportunities.
WSJF (Weighted Shortest Job First) from SAFe: \(\text{WSJF} = \frac{\text{Cost of Delay}}{\text{Job Size}}\).



40.2 Prioritization frameworks for product/analytics work



Framework
Inputs
Output




MoSCoW
Must / Should / Could / Won’t
Buckets


RICE
Reach × Impact × Confidence / Effort
Score


ICE
Impact × Confidence × Ease
Score


Kano
Basic / Performance / Delight
Map


Value vs Effort
2×2
Quadrants


Eisenhower
Urgent × Important
Quadrants


Cost of Delay × WSJF
CoD / Duration
Score



import pandas as pd
backlog = pd.DataFrame({
    "name": ["A", "B", "C", "D"],
    "reach": [10000, 5000, 2000, 8000],
    "impact": [3, 2, 3, 1],
    "confidence": [0.8, 0.6, 0.9, 0.5],
    "effort_weeks": [4, 2, 1, 3],
})
backlog["RICE"] = (backlog.reach * backlog.impact * backlog.confidence) / backlog.effort_weeks
print(backlog.sort_values("RICE", ascending=False))


40.3 Capacity planning and throughput

Little’s Law: \(L = \lambda W\) — average WIP = throughput × cycle time. Powerful identity for queues and pipelines.
Theory of Constraints — invest in the constraint, not the non-constraints.
Queueing intuition: as utilization \(\rho \to 1\), wait time explodes — keep critical resources < ~80% utilized.



40.4 Goal cascading and alignment
Cascade NSM/OKRs from company → function → team → individual. At each level, ensure:

Higher-level KR appears as the line-of-sight target.
Local OKRs sum (or otherwise compose) to the parent.
Trade-offs are explicit; conflicts surfaced.

title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
text:
This single document is designed to take you from zero to absolute expert in the Computer Vision Fundamentals domain of the Workera ELO / SAGE assessment. It mirrors the depth, math, code, intuition and pedagogy of a classical curriculum (Szeliski’s Computer Vision: Algorithms and Applications — see the freely available PDF at https://szeliski.org/Book/), the Stanford CS231n notes (https://cs231n.github.io/), Andrej Karpathy’s blog (https://karpathy.github.io/), Distill.pub essays such as Feature Visualization and The Building Blocks of Interpretability, Lilian Weng’s Lil’Log posts on detection and the Vision Transformer, and Jay Alammar’s visual explanations.
Recommended primary reading list, all referenced inline:

Szeliski R., Computer Vision: Algorithms and Applications, 2nd ed. (free PDF).
Stanford CS231n: Convolutional Neural Networks for Visual Recognition.
LeCun Y. et al., “Gradient-Based Learning Applied to Document Recognition” (1998).
Krizhevsky A. et al., “ImageNet Classification with Deep CNNs” — AlexNet (2012).
Simonyan K., Zisserman A., “Very Deep CNNs for Large-Scale Image Recognition” — VGG (2014).
Szegedy C. et al., “Going Deeper with Convolutions” — Inception/GoogLeNet (2014); Inception v2/v3 (2015); Inception-v4 / Inception-ResNet (2016).
He K. et al., “Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition” — ResNet (2015).
Huang G. et al., “Densely Connected Convolutional Networks” — DenseNet (2016).
Howard A. et al., “MobileNets” (2017); Sandler M. et al., “MobileNetV2” (2018); Howard A. et al., “Searching for MobileNetV3” (2019).
Tan M., Le Q., “EfficientNet” (2019); “EfficientNetV2” (2021).
Dosovitskiy A. et al., “An Image is Worth 16×16 Words” — ViT (2020).
Liu Z. et al., “Swin Transformer: Hierarchical ViT with Shifted Windows” (2021).
Girshick R. et al., R-CNN / Fast R-CNN / Faster R-CNN; Redmon J. et al., YOLO; Lin T.-Y. et al., “Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection” — RetinaNet; He K. et al., Mask R-CNN; Carion N. et al., DETR.
Ronneberger O. et al., U-Net (2015); Goodfellow I. et al., GAN (2014); Ho J. et al., DDPM (2020); Rombach R. et al., Latent Diffusion / Stable Diffusion (2022).

title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
section: MODULE 1: WHAT IS A DIGITAL IMAGE?
text:
MODULE 1: WHAT IS A DIGITAL IMAGE?

1.1 Pixels: discrete samples of a continuous signal
A digital image is a 2-D (or 2-D plus channels) discrete sampling of a continuous light field \(L(x, y, \lambda, t)\) that strikes a sensor. The sensor integrates radiance over a small area and over the wavelengths of each spectral filter, producing a single number per pixel (picture element) per channel:
\[
I[i, j, c] = \int_{x_i}^{x_i + \Delta x} \int_{y_j}^{y_j + \Delta y} \int_{\lambda_{\min}}^{\lambda_{\max}} L(x, y, \lambda)\, S_c(\lambda)\, dx\, dy\, d\lambda
\]
where \(S_c(\lambda)\) is the spectral sensitivity of channel \(c\) (e.g. red, green, blue, or a single luminance filter for grayscale). A pixel is therefore not a tiny coloured square — it is the value of one sample of the continuous image; the rendered square is just one possible reconstruction (see Alvy Ray Smith’s A Pixel Is Not A Little Square).
Two equivalent ways to think of an image:

As a matrix: I[i, j] indexed by row \(i\) (top → bottom) and column \(j\) (left → right). This is the convention NumPy / OpenCV / PyTorch tensors use.
As a function: \(I : \mathbb{Z}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^C\) mapping integer pixel positions to a \(C\)-vector of intensities.

Coordinate-system gotchas to memorise:









Library / API
Indexing
Origin
Notes




NumPy / PyTorch tensor [H, W]
img[y, x]
top-left
row first, column second


OpenCV cv2.imread
(H, W, 3) BGR
top-left
BGR not RGB by default


PIL Image
img.getpixel((x, y))
top-left
x first (column), y second (row)


Matplotlib imshow
displays [H, W]
top-left
y-axis points down


Scikit-image
(H, W, 3) RGB
top-left
float in [0, 1] for img_as_float





1.2 Channels, bit depth, and dynamic range
The number of bits per pixel per channel is the bit depth. Together with the number of channels it determines the storage and the dynamic range:

1 bit — binary image (e.g. fax, document scan).
8 bits / channel — most consumer images. Range \([0, 255]\). 24-bit RGB or 32-bit RGBA.
10 / 12 / 14 bits — raw camera sensor output, HDR video (HDR10, Dolby Vision uses up to 12-bit).
16 bits / channel — scientific imagery (medical CT/MRI, satellite), Adobe Photoshop “16-bit” mode.
32-bit float / channel — HDR formats (Radiance HDR, OpenEXR), linear-light pipelines, deep-learning tensors.

Dynamic range is the ratio of the brightest to the darkest representable value: \(DR = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_{\max}}{I_{\min}}\right)\) dB. The human visual system spans roughly \(10^{14}\) in radiance from starlight to sunlight (about 280 dB). 8-bit sRGB images cover only ~48 dB — hence the need for tone mapping when displaying HDR content.
LDR vs HDR:

LDR (Low Dynamic Range) — 8 bits / channel, gamma-encoded, e.g. JPEG/PNG. Optimised for display on standard monitors.
HDR (High Dynamic Range) — float pixels in linear light, e.g. EXR. Allows physically meaningful operations (white balance, exposure changes, compositing) without quantisation errors.



1.3 Gamma encoding and the sRGB transfer function
Display devices are non-linear; CRTs historically had a power-law luminance response \(L \propto V^{\gamma}\) with \(\gamma \approx 2.2\). To efficiently encode perceptually uniform brightness levels in 8 bits, images are stored gamma-encoded with the inverse curve:
\[
V_{\text{encoded}} = V_{\text{linear}}^{1/\gamma}
\]
For sRGB, the precise transfer function is a piecewise curve:
\[
V_{\text{sRGB}} = \begin{cases}
12.92 \, L & L \le 0.0031308 \\
1.055 \, L^{1/2.4} - 0.055 & L > 0.0031308
\end{cases}
\]
with \(L \in [0, 1]\) the linear luminance. The effective gamma is ≈ 2.2.
Why care? When you blend or filter images, the math is physically correct in linear light only. Averaging two gamma-encoded pixels of equal luminance gives a slightly darker result than averaging in linear. Most blur, resize, and alpha-blend operations in production pipelines should convert to linear, operate, then re-encode.
import numpy as np

def srgb_to_linear(v: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    return np.where(v <= 0.04045, v / 12.92, ((v + 0.055) / 1.055) ** 2.4)

def linear_to_srgb(L: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    return np.where(L <= 0.0031308, 12.92 * L, 1.055 * L ** (1 / 2.4) - 0.055)


1.4 Resolution
“Resolution” is overloaded — pin down which one you mean:

Spatial resolution — number of pixels (e.g. \(1920 \times 1080\)). Sometimes given as pixels per inch (PPI / DPI) when print size matters.
Intensity (tonal) resolution — bit depth per channel (8-bit, 10-bit, 16-bit).
Temporal resolution — frame rate (fps).
Spectral resolution — number and bandwidth of colour channels (grayscale, RGB, multispectral, hyperspectral).
Radiometric resolution — smallest measurable difference in radiance.

The useful spatial resolution is limited by the modulation transfer function (MTF) of the optical chain, not the pixel count alone. A 50 MP camera with a soft lens may resolve fewer line-pairs than a 24 MP camera with a sharp lens.


1.5 Pixel neighbourhoods and connectivity
For any pixel \((i, j)\) in a 2-D grid we define neighbourhoods:

4-connected (N4) — the four pixels sharing an edge: \((i\pm1, j)\), \((i, j\pm1)\).
8-connected (N8) — N4 plus the four diagonal pixels \((i\pm1, j\pm1)\).
6-connected — used in hexagonal grids and in 3-D voxel grids (six face-adjacent voxels).
18- and 26-connected — extensions in 3-D (face+edge and face+edge+corner respectively).

The Jordan curve paradox explains why we need to mix connectivities. If both foreground and background use 8-connectivity, then a 1-pixel-wide diagonal closed curve will fail to separate the background into inside and outside. The standard fix: use 8-connectivity for foreground and 4-connectivity for background (or vice versa).
def neighbours_4(i, j, H, W):
    return [(i+di, j+dj) for di, dj in [(-1,0),(1,0),(0,-1),(0,1)]
            if 0 <= i+di < H and 0 <= j+dj < W]

def neighbours_8(i, j, H, W):
    return [(i+di, j+dj) for di in (-1,0,1) for dj in (-1,0,1)
            if (di or dj) and 0 <= i+di < H and 0 <= j+dj < W]

title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 2: 2-D PRIMITIVES IN HOMOGENEOUS COORDINATES
A 2-D point \((x, y)\) has a homogeneous representation \((x, y, 1)^\top\) or, more generally, any \((\tilde x, \tilde y, \tilde w)^\top\) with \(\tilde w \ne 0\) representing the inhomogeneous point \((\tilde x/\tilde w, \tilde y/\tilde w)\). Points with \(\tilde w = 0\) are points at infinity — directions.
A line in 2-D is \(ax + by + c = 0\), written homogeneously as \(\mathbf{\ell} = (a, b, c)^\top\). A point lies on a line iff \(\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{\ell} = 0\).
Useful duality:

Line through two points: \(\mathbf{\ell} = \mathbf{x}_1 \times \mathbf{x}_2\).
Point at intersection of two lines: \(\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{\ell}_1 \times \mathbf{\ell}_2\).

Conics (ellipse, parabola, hyperbola) are \(\mathbf{x}^\top C \mathbf{x} = 0\) with \(C\) a symmetric \(3\times 3\) matrix.

title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 3: THE TRANSFORMATION HIERARCHY
Each transformation forms a group; higher-level groups contain the lower ones.









Class
DoF
Preserves
Matrix form (homogeneous)




Translation
2
orientation, lengths, angles, lines, parallelism
\(\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\)


Rigid (Euclidean)
3
lengths, angles, areas
\(\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & t_x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\)


Similarity
4
angles, ratios of lengths, parallelism
\(\begin{pmatrix} s\cos\theta & -s\sin\theta & t_x \\ s\sin\theta & s\cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\)


Affine
6
parallelism, ratios of areas, midpoints
\(\begin{pmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} & t_x \\ a_{21} & a_{22} & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\)


Projective (homography)
8
straight lines, cross-ratio
\(\begin{pmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} & h_{13} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} & h_{23} \\ h_{31} & h_{32} & h_{33} \end{pmatrix}\)



Composition is just matrix multiplication. Order matters: rotate-then-translate ≠ translate-then-rotate, because matrices don’t commute.

3.1 Translation, rotation, scaling, reflection
For a vector \(\mathbf{p} = (x, y, 1)^\top\):

Translation by \((t_x, t_y)\): \(T\mathbf{p}\) where \(T\) is the matrix above.
Rotation about origin by \(\theta\) (counter-clockwise): \(R\mathbf{p}\). About an arbitrary centre \((c_x, c_y)\): translate \(-c\), rotate, translate \(+c\) — i.e. \(T(c) R T(-c)\).
Uniform scaling by \(s\): diagonal \((s, s, 1)\). Non-uniform: \((s_x, s_y, 1)\).
Reflection: about \(x\)-axis \(\text{diag}(1, -1, 1)\); about \(y = x\) swap the matrix to \(\begin{pmatrix}0&1&0\\1&0&0\\0&0&1\end{pmatrix}\).
Shear (along \(x\)): \(\begin{pmatrix}1 & m & 0\\0 & 1 & 0\\0 & 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}\).



3.2 Affine transformation
Six DoF: a \(2\times 3\) matrix \[
\begin{pmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} t_x \\ t_y \end{pmatrix}.
\] Given three non-collinear point correspondences you can solve for the six unknowns.


3.3 Projective / homography
Eight DoF (the matrix is defined up to scale). The transform of a point is \[
x' = \frac{h_{11}x + h_{12}y + h_{13}}{h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33}},\quad y' = \frac{h_{21}x + h_{22}y + h_{23}}{h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33}}.
\] Four point correspondences suffice. Estimation is normally done with the DLT (Direct Linear Transform) algorithm and refined with non-linear least squares (Levenberg–Marquardt). Outliers are rejected with RANSAC (see §16).


3.4 Forward vs inverse warping
When you apply \(\mathbf{x}' = H\mathbf{x}\) to render a warped image, there are two strategies:

Forward mapping — for each source pixel, push it to the target. Problems: holes (target pixels nobody mapped to) and overlaps (target pixels mapped to multiple times). Use splatting to mitigate.
Inverse mapping — for each target pixel \(\mathbf{x}'\), compute \(\mathbf{x} = H^{-1}\mathbf{x}'\) and sample the source. This is the standard choice: every target pixel gets a value, and you can use a good interpolation kernel.



3.5 Interpolation kernels
When the back-projected source coordinate is fractional you must interpolate. Common kernels:

Nearest neighbour — pick the closest integer pixel. Cheap, blocky.
Bilinear — weighted average of the 4 nearest pixels, linear in \(x\) and \(y\). Smooth, slightly blurry, 2 mul + adds.
Bicubic — cubic convolution kernel over 16 neighbours; sharper than bilinear; standard for image resize.
Lanczos (\(a = 2\) or \(a = 3\)) — windowed sinc, \(L(x) = \text{sinc}(x)\,\text{sinc}(x/a)\) for \(|x| < a\), 0 otherwise. Best quality; can ring.
Area / box — average over the source area covered by each target pixel. Best for downsampling (preserves energy).

import cv2
img = cv2.imread("photo.jpg")
small = cv2.resize(img, None, fx=0.5, fy=0.5, interpolation=cv2.INTER_AREA)
large = cv2.resize(img, None, fx=2.0, fy=2.0, interpolation=cv2.INTER_CUBIC)
warped = cv2.warpAffine(img, M_2x3, (W, H), flags=cv2.INTER_LANCZOS4)

title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 4: COLOUR REPRESENTATION

4.1 Trichromacy and CIE XYZ
The human retina has three cone types with peak sensitivities around 420 nm (S), 530 nm (M), 560 nm (L). Trichromacy means any colour can be matched by mixing three suitably chosen primaries. CIE 1931 XYZ is a device-independent reference space in which \(Y\) corresponds to perceived luminance and any visible colour has non-negative coordinates.


4.2 RGB
R, G, B are device primaries. Multiple RGB spaces exist with different primaries and white points:

sRGB — most common (web, consumer). Primaries are HDTV (Rec. 709) phosphors; white point D65.
Adobe RGB (1998) — wider gamut, used in print.
DCI-P3 — modern phone/laptop displays; cinema standard.
Rec. 2020 / BT.2020 — UHDTV; very wide gamut.
ProPhoto RGB — even wider; used inside raw editors.



4.3 HSV / HSL
Cylindrical re-mappings of RGB that decouple chromaticity from brightness:

H (hue) — 0–360°; the dominant wavelength.
S (saturation) — 0–1; colourfulness vs gray.
V (value) / L (lightness) — brightness.

Why use HSV? Because objects of the same colour under different lighting span large RGB regions but cluster in a small H range, making HSV great for colour segmentation (e.g. picking out a red apple, a blue marker, a green plant) and for colour-based augmentation in training.
Conversion (RGB normalised to \([0, 1]\), \(C_{\max} = \max(R,G,B)\), \(C_{\min}=\min(R,G,B)\), \(\Delta = C_{\max} - C_{\min}\)):
\[
H = 60° \cdot \begin{cases} (G-B)/\Delta \mod 6 & C_{\max}=R \\ (B-R)/\Delta + 2 & C_{\max}=G \\ (R-G)/\Delta + 4 & C_{\max}=B \end{cases},\quad
S = \begin{cases} 0 & C_{\max}=0 \\ \Delta/C_{\max} & \text{else} \end{cases},\quad V = C_{\max}.
\]


4.4 YCbCr (luma + chroma)
Used in JPEG and video codecs because (a) the eye is much more sensitive to luma (Y) than to chroma (Cb, Cr), allowing chroma subsampling; and (b) Y can be displayed by itself as a grayscale image.
For ITU-R BT.601 (SDTV):
\[
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B \\
C_b &= -0.169 R - 0.331 G + 0.500 B + 128 \\
C_r &= 0.500 R - 0.419 G - 0.081 B + 128
\end{aligned}
\]
For BT.709 (HDTV) the luma coefficients are \((0.2126, 0.7152, 0.0722)\); for BT.2020 they are \((0.2627, 0.6780, 0.0593)\).
Chroma subsampling notations:

4:4:4 — no subsampling.
4:2:2 — chroma horizontally halved.
4:2:0 — chroma halved in both directions (4× fewer chroma samples). JPEG default.



4.5 CIE Lab (perceptually uniform)
L* (lightness 0–100), a* (green–red), b* (blue–yellow). Designed so that equal Euclidean distance ≈ equal perceptual difference. Often used for image-difference metrics (ΔE_76, ΔE_2000) and for the K-means palette extraction step in image quantisation.


4.6 CMYK
Subtractive primaries for print (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow + Key/black). Conversion from RGB is gamut-limited and printer-specific (ICC profiles).


4.7 White balance and chromatic adaptation
Illuminants ranging from candlelight (~1900 K) to overcast sky (~6500 K) shift the “white” of the scene. The brain performs colour constancy subconsciously; cameras must do it explicitly:

Gray-world assumption: average colour over the scene is gray; scale each channel to make that true.
White-patch / Max-RGB: find the brightest pixel, assume it should be white, scale.
Bradford / CAT02 chromatic adaptation transforms — map XYZ tristimulus values from one white point to another.
Learning-based: small CNN regresses illuminant chromaticity from raw image (e.g. Bianco et al., FC4).



4.8 Adjusting brightness, contrast, gamma
Three workhorse transforms applied to a normalised image \(I \in [0, 1]\):

Linear (gain & bias): \(I' = a \cdot I + b\). \(a\) is contrast (slope), \(b\) is brightness (offset).
Gamma / power: \(I' = I^{\gamma}\). \(\gamma < 1\) brightens shadows, \(\gamma > 1\) darkens them.
Log / inverse-log: \(I' = c \log(1 + I)\) compresses dynamic range; useful for FFT magnitude visualisation, dark scenes, X-ray.



4.9 Tone mapping (HDR → LDR)

Reinhard global: \(I' = \frac{I}{1 + I}\) — applied per pixel in luminance.
Reinhard local: as above but with a per-pixel adaptation luminance (so dark and bright regions are tone-mapped differently).
Drago logarithmic: \(L_d = \frac{\log(1 + L)}{\log(1 + L_{\max})}\) with adaptive base.
Filmic / ACES — industry-standard cinematic tone curve.
Mantiuk — perceptually motivated; preserves local contrast.

title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 5: SAMPLING THEORY

5.1 Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem
A continuous band-limited signal whose Fourier spectrum vanishes above \(f_{\max}\) can be perfectly reconstructed from samples taken at rate \(f_s \ge 2 f_{\max}\). The threshold \(2 f_{\max}\) is the Nyquist rate; sampling below it causes aliasing.
For 2-D images: the sensor samples a continuous luminance field at the pixel grid pitch \(\Delta\). Any spatial frequency above \(1/(2\Delta)\) cycles per pixel that exists in the optical image will alias to a lower apparent frequency in the sampled image.


5.2 Aliasing and Moiré
When high-frequency detail is undersampled it appears as Moiré patterns — false low-frequency structure such as the rainbow stripes on a digital photo of a striped shirt or the wagon-wheel effect in old films. The classic 1-D demonstration is sampling \(\sin(2\pi f t)\) with \(f > f_s/2\): the sampled values are indistinguishable from those of a lower-frequency sinusoid.


5.3 Anti-aliasing (prefiltering)
To avoid aliasing you must band-limit before sampling. Practical strategies:

Optical low-pass filter (OLPF / AA filter) in front of the sensor — slightly blurs the optical image so frequencies above Nyquist are attenuated.
Gaussian or box prefilter in software before downsampling: convolve, then drop samples. The Gaussian’s \(\sigma\) is chosen so its cutoff matches the new Nyquist rate (\(\sigma \approx \text{downscale\_factor} / 2\)).
In rendering: MSAA (multi-sample anti-aliasing, \(n\) depth/coverage samples per pixel), SSAA (super-sampling: render at higher resolution then downsample), FXAA / TAA (post-process).



5.4 Bayer pattern and demosaicing
Most colour sensors use a Bayer colour filter array (CFA) — one R, two G, one B per \(2\times 2\) tile (because the human eye is more sensitive to green):
G R G R
B G B G
G R G R
B G B G
Each pixel records only one colour; the other two are estimated by demosaicing (bilinear, Malvar–He–Cutler, gradient-based, DL-based). Demosaicing artefacts include zipper noise and false colours along high-frequency edges. Modern smartphones use Quad-Bayer / Nona-Bayer arrays with software re-mosaicing.


5.5 Quantisation
After sampling, each sample is rounded to a finite set of levels. Uniform quantisation with \(B\) bits gives \(2^B\) equally spaced levels; non-uniform schemes (logarithmic, \(\mu\)-law, perceptual) allocate more levels where the eye is more sensitive. Quantisation error is bounded by half a quantisation step; for natural images the PSNR ceiling at 8 bits is about 49 dB.
Visible artefacts of coarse quantisation:

Banding / posterisation — visible contours where slow gradients are quantised.
Contour noise — random dithering can mask banding.
False contours — especially in shadows; mitigated by ordered or error-diffusion dithering (Floyd–Steinberg).

title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 6: HISTOGRAMS AND POINT OPERATIONS

6.1 Image histogram
The histogram \(h[k]\) counts pixels with intensity \(k\) in \([0, L-1]\) (\(L = 256\) for 8-bit). The normalised histogram \(p[k] = h[k]/N\) is the empirical PDF of intensities. The CDF is \(c[k] = \sum_{j=0}^{k} p[j]\).
Inspect histograms to spot:

Under-exposure — mass piled on the left.
Over-exposure / clipping — mass piled on the right at \(L-1\).
Low contrast — mass concentrated in a narrow band.
Bimodality — likely two object classes (used by Otsu).



6.2 Contrast stretching (min–max normalisation)
\[
I'(x, y) = \frac{I(x, y) - I_{\min}}{I_{\max} - I_{\min}} \cdot (L - 1)
\]
Trim a small percentile from each end (e.g. 1 % and 99 %) so a handful of outliers don’t dominate \(I_{\min}/I_{\max}\).


6.3 Histogram equalisation
Goal: produce an image whose intensity histogram is approximately uniform → maximises contrast.
The transformation is the (scaled) CDF:
\[
T(k) = \text{round}\left( (L-1) \cdot c[k] \right) = \text{round}\left( (L-1) \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{k} \frac{h[j]}{N} \right).
\]
Why does this work? Treating \(I\) as a continuous random variable, applying \(T\) equal to its CDF yields a uniform distribution on \([0, 1]\). The discrete case is a piecewise-constant approximation.
import numpy as np

def hist_eq(img: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    h, _ = np.histogram(img.flatten(), 256, [0, 256])
    cdf = h.cumsum()
    cdf_m = np.ma.masked_equal(cdf, 0)
    cdf_m = (cdf_m - cdf_m.min()) * 255 / (cdf_m.max() - cdf_m.min())
    cdf = np.ma.filled(cdf_m, 0).astype("uint8")
    return cdf[img]


6.4 CLAHE — Contrast-Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalisation
Global equalisation can over-amplify noise. CLAHE equalises locally on small tiles (e.g. 8×8) and clips each tile’s histogram at a threshold before computing the CDF, then redistributes the clipped mass uniformly. Tiles are blended with bilinear interpolation to remove block boundaries.
clahe = cv2.createCLAHE(clipLimit=2.0, tileGridSize=(8, 8))
gray_eq = clahe.apply(gray)
CLAHE is the standard preprocessing step for medical imaging (X-ray, mammography) and astronomical images.


6.5 Histogram matching / specification
Make image \(A\)’s histogram resemble image \(B\)’s. Equalise both, then map: \(T = c_B^{-1} \circ c_A\). Used for photometric calibration, style transfer, and stitching so panorama tiles match in tone.


6.6 Otsu’s automatic threshold
Find threshold \(t\) that maximises between-class variance (equivalently minimises within-class variance). Derive:
Let \(\omega_0(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{t} p[k]\), \(\omega_1(t) = 1 - \omega_0(t)\), \(\mu_0(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{t} k p[k] / \omega_0\), \(\mu_1(t)\) analogous. The between-class variance is
\[
\sigma_B^2(t) = \omega_0(t)\,\omega_1(t)\,\bigl(\mu_0(t) - \mu_1(t)\bigr)^2.
\]
Iterate \(t = 0..L-1\), pick the argmax. \(O(L)\).
_, mask = cv2.threshold(gray, 0, 255, cv2.THRESH_BINARY + cv2.THRESH_OTSU)


6.7 Other thresholding strategies

Global: fixed value.
Adaptive mean / Gaussian — threshold = local mean (or Gaussian-weighted local mean) ± \(C\).
Niblack / Sauvola — local mean ± \(k\)·std; great for document binarisation.
Triangle method — geometric construction on the histogram.

title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 7: NOISE AND DENOISING

7.1 Noise models









Noise
Model
Source
Best filter




Gaussian
\(I' = I + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\)
thermal / read noise
Gaussian, NLM, BM3D, CNN


Salt-and-pepper (impulse)
with prob. \(p\), replace by 0 or \(L-1\)
bit errors, dead pixels
median


Poisson / shot
\(I' \sim \text{Poisson}(I)\)
photon counting (low light)
variance-stabilising transform + Gaussian, or specialised NLM


Speckle (multiplicative)
\(I' = I (1 + n)\), \(n \sim \mathcal{N}\)
ultrasound, SAR
Lee, Frost, Kuan filters


Quantisation / banding
rounding
low bit depth
dithering, bit-depth upscale + denoise


Compression
DCT-block artefacts
JPEG
deblocking filters


Periodic
sinusoidal
electromagnetic interference
notch filter in Fourier domain





7.2 Spatial filters
A linear shift-invariant filter is convolution \(I'[x, y] = \sum_{u, v} K[u, v]\, I[x-u, y-v]\).

Box / mean filter — \(K = \frac{1}{k^2}\mathbf{1}_{k\times k}\). Cheap, but blurs edges.
Gaussian filter — \(K[u, v] = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}e^{-(u^2+v^2)/(2\sigma^2)}\). Separable (\(K_{2D} = K_{1D}^x K_{1D}^y\)) → \(O(k)\) instead of \(O(k^2)\) per pixel. Optimal trade-off between spatial and frequency localisation.
Median filter — output = median of pixels in the window. Non-linear, edge-preserving, the canonical answer for salt-and-pepper noise.
Bilateral filter — Gaussian in both space and range: \[
I'(x) = \frac{1}{W(x)}\sum_{x'} G_{\sigma_s}(\|x' - x\|)\, G_{\sigma_r}(|I(x') - I(x)|)\, I(x').
\] Edge-preserving smoothing. Slow naive; fast approximations exist (joint bilateral upsampling, permutohedral lattice).
Non-local means (NLM) — replace each pixel by a weighted average of all pixels in the image (or a large search window) whose patch neighbourhood is similar. Massive denoising power; computationally heavy. Reference: Buades, Coll, Morel (2005).
BM3D — block-matching 3-D filtering. Groups similar patches into a 3-D stack, applies 3-D transform (DCT or wavelet) and shrinkage, then aggregates. State-of-the-art classical denoiser; baseline that learned methods must beat.
Wavelet denoising — DWT, soft-threshold detail coefficients (\(\hat w = \text{sign}(w) \max(0, |w| - \lambda)\)), inverse DWT.
Wiener filter — optimal linear filter for known noise PSD; assumes stationarity.
Total variation (ROF) — minimise \(\|I - I_0\|^2 + \lambda \|\nabla I\|_1\). Produces piecewise-constant (“cartoonish”) results.



7.3 Modern deep denoisers
DnCNN (Zhang et al. 2017), FFDNet, Noise2Noise (Lehtinen et al. 2018) — training without clean targets!, Noise2Void, Real-ESRGAN (real-world degradations). For the assessment know that median beats Gaussian for salt-and-pepper, bilateral / NLM / BM3D preserve edges, and deep learning is SOTA but expensive.
import cv2
g = cv2.GaussianBlur(img, (5, 5), 1.0)
m = cv2.medianBlur(img, 5)
b = cv2.bilateralFilter(img, d=9, sigmaColor=75, sigmaSpace=75)
n = cv2.fastNlMeansDenoisingColored(img, None, h=10, hColor=10,
                                    templateWindowSize=7, searchWindowSize=21)

title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 8: IMAGE RESIZING AND RESTORATION

8.1 Resampling and aliasing in resize

Down-sampling: must low-pass filter first (Gaussian or box) to avoid aliasing. cv2.INTER_AREA does this implicitly.
Up-sampling: choose a smooth interpolation (bicubic, Lanczos). For learned super-resolution use SRCNN, EDSR, ESRGAN, Real-ESRGAN, SwinIR.



8.2 Deblurring
If the blur is modelled as \(I' = I * h + n\) with known kernel \(h\) and noise \(n\):

Inverse filter — \(\hat I = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left(\mathcal{F}(I')/\mathcal{F}(h)\right)\). Hugely amplifies noise where \(\mathcal{F}(h) \approx 0\).
Wiener filter — \(\hat I = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left( \frac{\overline{\mathcal{F}(h)}}{|\mathcal{F}(h)|^2 + K} \cdot \mathcal{F}(I')\right)\). Regularised by noise-to-signal ratio \(K\).
Richardson–Lucy — iterative, non-negative, max-likelihood under Poisson noise. Used in astronomy and microscopy.

Blind deblurring estimates \(h\) and \(I\) jointly; deep learning (DeblurGAN, MPRNet, NAFNet) dominates today.


8.3 Inpainting
Fill holes / masked regions consistently with surroundings.

Telea / fast marching method (FMM) — propagates from boundary inward.
Navier–Stokes — solve a PDE that enforces continuation of isophote lines.
PatchMatch — exemplar-based, finds best matching patches elsewhere in the image.
Deep learning — Context Encoder, partial convolutions (NVIDIA), gated convolutions, LaMa (Fourier convolutions, ICCV 2022), and diffusion-based inpainting (Stable Diffusion).

mask = (img == 0).all(axis=2).astype(np.uint8) * 255
filled = cv2.inpaint(img, mask, inpaintRadius=3, flags=cv2.INPAINT_TELEA)


8.4 Super-resolution

Bicubic / Lanczos — classical baselines.
SRCNN (Dong et al. 2014) — first CNN super-resolution.
VDSR, DRCN, EDSR (Lim et al. 2017) — deeper residual networks.
SRGAN / ESRGAN / Real-ESRGAN — perceptual + adversarial losses, sharper results.
SwinIR, HAT — Transformer-based.
Diffusion-based — SR3, StableSR.

title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 9: MORPHOLOGICAL IMAGE PROCESSING
Morphology operates on binary or grayscale images using a structuring element (SE) \(B\) such as a 3×3 square, cross, or disk.

9.1 Fundamental binary operators

Erosion: \(I \ominus B = \{ z \mid B_z \subseteq I \}\). Shrinks foreground, removes small objects.
Dilation: \(I \oplus B = \{ z \mid (\hat B)_z \cap I \neq \varnothing \}\). Grows foreground, fills small holes.
Opening: \(I \circ B = (I \ominus B) \oplus B\). Removes small bright objects, smooths contours from outside.
Closing: \(I \bullet B = (I \oplus B) \ominus B\). Fills small dark holes, smooths from inside.
Top-hat: \(I - (I \circ B)\). Extracts small bright structures on a dark background.
Black-hat: \((I \bullet B) - I\). Small dark structures on a bright background.
Hit-or-miss: detects an exact local configuration via a paired SE \((B_1, B_2)\): \(I \otimes B = (I \ominus B_1) \cap (I^c \ominus B_2)\).

Grayscale generalisation: replace set operations with min (erosion) and max (dilation).


9.2 Skeletonisation and distance transform

Skeleton — thinnest centreline of a shape (medial axis). Computed by successive erosions or by the medial-axis transform = ridges of the distance transform.
Distance transform \(D(p) = \min_{q \in \partial \Omega} \|p - q\|\). Classical algorithms: chamfer (3-4 or 5-7-11 masks) and Felzenszwalb–Huttenlocher exact Euclidean (\(O(N)\)).



9.3 Connected components
Find maximal sets of connected pixels of a given label.

Two-pass scan-line algorithm: pass 1 assigns provisional labels and records equivalences in a union-find; pass 2 replaces each label by its root.
Flood fill (recursive or BFS / DFS) — region-grow from a seed.
cv2.connectedComponentsWithStats returns label image + per-component bbox, area, centroid.

n, labels, stats, centroids = cv2.connectedComponentsWithStats(bin_mask, connectivity=8)
Connectivity choice (4 vs 8) determines whether diagonal pixels belong to the same component — pick consistent with your background convention.

title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 10: IMAGE COMPRESSION

10.1 The bigger picture
Compression exploits two kinds of redundancy:

Statistical / coding redundancy — frequent symbols should get shorter codes (Huffman, arithmetic).
Psycho-visual redundancy — the eye doesn’t see all detail equally; high frequencies and chroma can be coarsely quantised.

Lossless schemes (PNG, GIF, lossless JPEG, FLIF) preserve every pixel; lossy schemes (JPEG, JPEG2000, WebP, HEIC, AVIF) discard some information for much smaller files.


10.2 JPEG pipeline (must know)

Colour transform: RGB → YCbCr.
Chroma subsampling (typically 4:2:0): downsample \(C_b, C_r\) by 2 in both axes.
Block split: 8×8 non-overlapping blocks of each channel.
DCT (Discrete Cosine Transform): each block \(f(x,y)\) → coefficients \(F(u, v)\) \[
F(u,v) = \tfrac{1}{4} C(u) C(v) \sum_{x=0}^{7}\sum_{y=0}^{7} f(x,y) \cos\!\tfrac{(2x+1)u\pi}{16} \cos\!\tfrac{(2y+1)v\pi}{16}
\] with \(C(0) = 1/\sqrt 2\), \(C(k>0)=1\).
Quantisation: divide each \(F(u,v)\) by a quantisation matrix \(Q(u,v)\) (with larger values at high frequencies). This is the lossy step.
Zig-zag scan orders coefficients by increasing frequency.
Run-length encode the long runs of zeros.
Huffman encode (arithmetic coding optionally).

JPEG quality slider \(Q \in [1, 100]\) scales the quantisation matrix. Visible artefacts: 8×8 blocking, mosquito noise around edges, chroma bleed from 4:2:0.


10.3 Other codecs









Codec
Year
Domain
Key idea




JPEG

photos
block DCT, lossy

wavelet (CDF 9/7), lossless or lossy

1996
screenshots, line art
DEFLATE (LZ77 + Huffman), lossless

1987
animation, 256-colour
LZW, palette

2010

VP8-derived, lossless+lossy, alpha

2015

2022
