Per-document ACLs — store ACLs in vector DB metadata (Pinecone namespaces, Weaviate tenants, Vertex AI Search ACL filters).
Embedding poisoning defense — detect anomalous embeddings before insertion.
Re-ranker filtering — re-rank with a model fine-tuned to ignore instruction-like text in documents.
Output citation — force the model to cite the chunk it used; surface to user.



15.7 Watermarking LLM outputs
Kirchenbauer et al. 2023 “A Watermark for Large Language Models” — at each step, partition the vocabulary into a green/red list based on a hash of the previous token (and a secret key); bias logits to favor green tokens by a small amount \(\delta\). Detection is a z-test over green-token frequency.

Robust to paraphrasing within limits; detectable with statistical significance after ~25 tokens.
Trade-off: detectability vs. quality.
Other approaches: SemStamp (semantic-level), Stanford “Undetectable Watermarks” (cryptographic).

title: Security in AI
text:
MODULE 16: MODEL THEFT AND EXTRACTION
Tramèr et al. 2016 “Stealing Machine Learning Models via Prediction APIs” — by querying a hosted model and learning from its predictions, an attacker can reconstruct an equivalent or near-equivalent model. Subsequent work: Knockoff Nets (Orekondy 2019), Cloud Leak (Yu 2020), MAZE / Data-Free Model Extraction (Truong 2021), Functionally Equivalent Extraction for ReLU networks (Jagielski 2020).

16.1 Attack techniques

Equation solving — for linear / logistic / softmax models with confidence outputs, solve a linear system to recover weights.
Path-finding — for ReLU networks, find critical points that reveal exact weights (Jagielski 2020) up to permutations and rescalings.
Distillation-style — query many inputs, train a student on (input, output) pairs.
Knockoff Nets — active learning + reinforcement learning to maximize information per query.
Generative extraction — generate inputs with a GAN to maximize student-teacher disagreement.

For LLMs:

Carlini et al. 2024 “Stealing Part of a Production Language Model” — recovered the final-layer projection of GPT-3.5 via API.
Prompt extraction — recovering hidden system prompts via clever follow-up prompts.



16.2 Defenses

Rate limiting and quotas (token bucket, IP/account-level).
Anomaly detection on query patterns — high entropy, near decision boundaries, atypical class distribution.
Output perturbation — return rounded probabilities, top-k labels, add small noise.
Watermarking the model itself:

Backdoor watermarks (Adi 2018) — secret trigger that produces a known wrong label; detect stolen models by probing.
Uchida 2017 — embed a fingerprint in the weights via regularization during training.
Fingerprinting — measure model behavior on a set of difficult examples; compare with suspected stolen model.

Cryptographic protections:

Encrypt model weights at rest with KMS.
Sign weights and verify on load.
Run inference inside a TEE; never expose plaintext weights to the host OS.

Differential privacy on outputs — limits information leakage per query.
Legal — Terms of Service prohibiting model extraction; trade-secret protection.

title: Security in AI
text:
MODULE 17: DoS AND SPONGE ATTACKS
Standard DDoS targets bandwidth or connections. AI systems have additional compute / latency vulnerabilities.

17.1 Sponge examples (Shumailov 2021)
Inputs crafted to maximize energy and latency of the model. For transformers, this means triggering worst-case attention (long sequences, many active heads), causing latency to spike and tokens-per-second to crater. Especially effective on autoregressive LLMs (request a very long generation; force many “thinking” tokens).


17.2 Resource exhaustion in LLM serving

Long context attacks — submit max-length context to drive up KV-cache memory.
Recursion attacks in agent loops — agents that call themselves indefinitely.
Tool-loop attacks — make the agent call a costly tool repeatedly.
Embedding flood — submit large batches to embedding endpoints to exhaust GPU memory.



17.3 Mitigations

Token / character limits per request (max_tokens, max_input_tokens).
Timeouts at every layer (gateway, model server, tool calls).
Rate limiting — token bucket and leaky bucket; per-IP, per-account, per-org.
Quotas — daily/monthly budgets with circuit breakers.
Autoscaling with cost caps — disable runaway spend.
WAF rules — block obvious abuse, e.g., bot signatures.
DDoS protection — AWS Shield Advanced, Cloudflare, GCP Cloud Armor, Akamai Prolexic.
CAPTCHA / proof-of-work for human-only endpoints.
Cost guards on agents — max tool calls per task, max reasoning tokens.
Anomaly detection on per-account compute consumption.

title: Security in AI
section: MODULE 18: GRADIENT MASKING — WHY IT FAILS
text:
MODULE 18: GRADIENT MASKING — WHY IT FAILS
Athalye, Carlini & Wagner 2018 “Obfuscated Gradients Give a False Sense of Security” identifies three failure modes that look like robustness but don’t survive adaptive attacks:








Failure mode
Mechanism
Counter-attack




Shattered gradients
Non-differentiable operations (rounding, JPEG, bit-depth reduction)
BPDA — Backward Pass Differentiable Approximation: replace the non-differentiable op with identity during the backward pass.


Stochastic gradients
Random input transforms, dropout at test time
EOT — Expectation over Transformations: average gradients over the randomness.


Exploding/vanishing gradients
Deep recurrent computations (e.g., PixelDefend’s PixelCNN)
Reparameterize; use BPDA.



Practical takeaway: never publish or trust a defense that hasn’t been evaluated with adaptive attacks (BPDA, EOT, AutoAttack). Tramer et al. 2020 “On Adaptive Attacks to Adversarial Example Defenses” defeated 13 defenses; Carlini & Tramer 2019 published the now-standard evaluation checklist.

18.1 The Carlini–Tramer evaluation checklist (paraphrased)

Define a clear threat model (norm, ε, query budget).
Evaluate with strong attacks (PGD with 100+ steps, multiple random restarts, AutoAttack).
Construct an adaptive attack that targets the specific defense.
Report robustness vs. budget curves, not single numbers.
Provide a working implementation; release pretrained checkpoints.
Don’t rely on obfuscated gradients, gradient masking, randomization without EOT.

title: Security in AI
text:
MODULE 19: RANDOMIZATION-BASED DEFENSES

19.1 Input-level randomization

Random resizing + padding (Xie 2018) — at inference, resize input to a random size, pad to original. Disrupts ε-budget adversarial perturbations.
Random crop / cutout / dropout-of-pixels at inference.
Bit-depth reduction with random offset.

Limitations: defeated by EOT. Provides a small gain only when combined with adversarial training.


19.2 Parameter-level randomization

Stochastic Activation Pruning (SAP) — randomly drop neurons during inference proportional to activation magnitude.
Random Self-Ensemble (RSE) — inject Gaussian noise into layers, average predictions over runs.
Parameter noise — add small Gaussian noise to weights at inference.



19.3 Randomized Smoothing (revisited)
Already covered in Module 7. The only randomization-based defense with formal guarantees. Combined with consistency training (Jeong 2020) it nearly matches empirical adversarial training for \(L_2\) robustness.


19.4 Ensemble defenses

Naive ensembles — average predictions of independently trained models. Modest robustness; adversarial examples often transfer.
Adaptive Diversity Promoting (ADP, Pang 2019) — encourage ensemble diversity in non-true classes via determinant regularization.
Diverse Architecture / Training Data ensembles — varied backbones, varied augmentation.
Boosted Adversarial Training — train ensemble jointly with adversarial training.

title: Security in AI
text:
MODULE 20: ROBUST OPTIMIZATION
The mathematical framework underpinning adversarial training and beyond.

20.1 Adversarial training as min-max
We saw \(\min_\theta \mathbb{E}[\max_{\delta\in\Delta} \mathcal{L}(\theta, x+\delta, y)]\). Inner max is solved approximately by PGD; outer min by SGD.


20.2 Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO)
Minimize worst-case loss over a distributional ambiguity set \(\mathcal{P}\):
\[
\min_\theta \;\sup_{Q \in \mathcal{P}} \;\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim Q}\bigl[\mathcal{L}(\theta, x, y)\bigr].
\]

Wasserstein DRO — \(\mathcal{P}\) is a Wasserstein ball around the empirical distribution; recovers adversarial training as a limit.
CVaR — minimize the conditional value at risk (average of worst-α fraction of losses).
f-divergence DRO — KL, χ², total variation balls.



20.3 Group DRO (Sagawa 2020)
Worst-case minimization over subgroups rather than individuals — protects fairness-relevant groups from majority-bias overfitting.
\[
\min_\theta \;\max_{g \in \mathcal{G}} \;\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim P_g}\bigl[\mathcal{L}(\theta, x, y)\bigr]
\]


20.4 Robustness–accuracy trade-off
Tsipras 2019; Raghunathan 2019. There is a fundamental tension: features that are useful for clean accuracy may be non-robust (Ilyas et al. 2019 “Adversarial Examples Are Not Bugs, They Are Features”). Adversarial training pushes the model to rely on robust features → loses some clean accuracy.
Recipe-level numbers to memorize (CIFAR-10, ResNet-50, \(\varepsilon = 8/255\), \(L_\infty\)):



Model
Clean acc
PGD-20 acc
AutoAttack acc




Standard
~95%
~0%
0%


Madry PGD-AT
~87%
~50%
~47%


TRADES
~85%
~55%
~52%


Recent SOTA (Wang 2023 + extra synthetic data)
~93%
~71%
~70%

title: Security in AI
section: MODULE 21: AI PENETRATION TESTING AND RED TEAMING
text:
MODULE 21: AI PENETRATION TESTING AND RED TEAMING
A traditional pentest looks for OS, web, network vulnerabilities. An AI pentest also probes the model:

Evasion under realistic threat model.
Membership / inversion / extraction.
Prompt injection (direct + indirect).
Data poisoning supply chain.
Tool / plugin abuse for LLM agents.
Bias and safety alignment failures.


21.1 Tools to know







Tool
What it tests




Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART) — IBM
Evasion, poisoning, inference, extraction (TF/PyTorch/JAX/sklearn)


CleverHans
Classic evasion attacks


Foolbox
Evasion (PyTorch/TF/JAX)


Torchattacks
PyTorch-native attacks


AutoAttack (Croce 2020)
Standardized evaluation ensemble


TextAttack
NLP attacks (synonym swaps, BERT-Attack)


Counterfit (Microsoft)
Automation framework for ML pentests


garak (NVIDIA)
LLM vuln scanner (jailbreak, prompt injection, etc.)


PyRIT (Microsoft)
LLM red-teaming with multi-turn orchestration


promptfoo
LLM regression + red-team evaluation


llm-guard, Rebuff, NeMo Guardrails
Runtime LLM filters





21.2 ART example (PGD on a Keras model)
from art.attacks.evasion import ProjectedGradientDescent
from art.estimators.classification import PyTorchClassifier
clf = PyTorchClassifier(model=model, loss=nn.CrossEntropyLoss(),
                       input_shape=(3,32,32), nb_classes=10)
atk = ProjectedGradientDescent(estimator=clf, eps=8/255, eps_step=2/255,
                               max_iter=20, norm=np.inf, random_eps=True)
x_adv = atk.generate(x=x_test)


21.3 Red-team exercises

Tabletop — paper-based attack walkthroughs with model owners.
Live red-team — purple-team engagement with defenders watching telemetry.
Bug bounty for AI — huntr.dev, ProtectAI Bounty, OpenAI’s Bug Bounty (incl. model safety scopes).
Capture-the-flag for ML — challenges like AI Village CTF at DEF CON, MLSec CTF.
Continuous red-teaming — automated nightly runs with garak / PyRIT on every new model checkpoint.



21.4 Documentation outputs

Model card (Mitchell 2019) — intended use, training data, evaluation, ethical considerations, and known security weaknesses.
Datasheet for datasets (Gebru 2018).
System card (OpenAI/Anthropic) — end-to-end deployment safety analysis.
Pentest report — executive summary, scope, threat model, findings (CVSS-AI scored), reproducibility, recommendations.

title: Security in AI
section: MODULE 22: THREAT MODELING FOR AI SYSTEMS
text:
MODULE 22: THREAT MODELING FOR AI SYSTEMS
Threat modeling is the structured practice of asking “what could go wrong?” before it does.

22.1 Classical methodologies

STRIDE (Microsoft) — Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege.
LINDDUN — privacy-focused: Linking, Identifying, Non-repudiation, Detecting, Data disclosure, Unawareness, Non-compliance.
PASTA — Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis (7-stage).
OCTAVE — risk-based, asset-centric.
TRIKE — requirements-driven.
kill-chain (Lockheed Martin) — reconnaissance → weaponization → delivery → exploitation → installation → C2 → actions on objectives.
MITRE ATT&CK — adversary tactics & techniques.



22.2 MITRE ATLAS — adversary tactics for AI
MITRE ATLAS (Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems) is the AI-specific counterpart to ATT&CK. Tactics include:



Tactic
AI-specific examples




Reconnaissance
Search public datasets, model cards, papers


Resource Development
Acquire surrogate models, datasets, accounts


Initial Access
Public API queries, supply-chain compromise


ML Model Access
Inference API, hosted weights


Execution
Adversarial input, prompt injection


Persistence
Backdoor in fine-tuned model


Defense Evasion
Adaptive attacks, gradient masking workarounds


Discovery
Model architecture inference, member inference


Collection
Extract training data, weights


ML Attack Staging
Train surrogate, craft adversarial examples


Impact
Misclassification, harmful generation, DoS





22.3 STRIDE applied to an LLM RAG system








Threat
Example
Mitigation




Spoofing
Attacker impersonates a privileged user to query private vectors
mTLS, OIDC, MFA


Tampering
Attacker writes injection content into a retrievable doc
Source ACLs, content scanning


Repudiation
Operator denies allowing a harmful generation
Signed audit log, model + prompt + output stored


Information disclosure
Model regurgitates PII from training
DP-SGD, output filter


DoS
Long-context prompt floods
max_input_tokens, rate limit


Elevation of privilege
Tool call escalates from read to write
Tool allowlist, capability tokens





22.4 LINDDUN applied to a face-recognition system








Privacy threat
Example
Mitigation




Linkability
Same face embedding across services
Embedding rotation per-tenant


Identifiability
Face → identity look-up
Restrict gallery, DP perturbations


Non-repudiation
User can’t deny a recognition match
Provide review process


Detectability
Attacker can confirm membership in gallery
Output perturbation, MIA defense


Disclosure of info
Gallery leak
Encryption, KMS, access control


Unawareness
Users don’t know they’re enrolled
Notice & consent


Non-compliance
Violates GDPR / Illinois BIPA
DPIA, opt-in





22.5 AI-specific attack surfaces

Training-data ingestion pipeline.
Training infrastructure (compute, code, dependencies).
Model artifacts (weights, optimizer states, tokenizer).
Inference API and gateway.
Prompt / system prompt store.
RAG vector DB and document corpora.
Plugins / tools / connectors.
Fine-tuning APIs accepting user data.
Telemetry / logs (often contain user prompts).
Human-in-the-loop annotation pipelines.

title: Security in AI
text:
MODULE 23: KEY REGULATIONS AND STANDARDS

23.1 EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689)
Came into force August 2024, applies in phases through 2026–2027. Risk-based tiers:








Tier
Examples
Obligations




Prohibited
Social scoring by public authorities, real-time biometric ID in public (with narrow exceptions), emotion recognition at workplace/school, untargeted facial-image scraping
Banned


High-risk (Annex III)
Recruitment, credit, critical infrastructure, education, law enforcement, biometric, employment
Conformity assessment, data governance, technical doc, transparency, human oversight, accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity, post-market monitoring, registration


Limited risk
Chatbots, emotion-recognition systems, deepfakes
Transparency labels


Minimal
Most consumer AI
No specific obligations


General-Purpose AI (GPAI)
LLMs, foundation models
Technical doc, training data summaries, copyright compliance; systemic-risk GPAI: model evaluation, adversarial testing, incident reporting, cybersecurity



Penalties up to €35M or 7% of worldwide turnover.


23.2 GDPR (EU 2016/679)
The privacy bedrock. Key concepts:

Lawful bases for processing: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, legitimate interest.
Data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure (“right to be forgotten”), portability, object, restrict, not be subject to solely automated decisions (Art. 22 — relevant to AI).
DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment) — required for high-risk processing including AI profiling.
Privacy by design and by default (Art. 25).
Cross-border transfer: Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), adequacy decisions.
DPO appointment for certain processors.
Maximum fine: €20M or 4% global turnover.



23.3 US frameworks

NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0, 2023) — voluntary; four functions:

Govern — culture, accountability, policies.
Map — context, intended use, risks identification.
Measure — testing, metrics, evaluation.
Manage — prioritization, mitigation, monitoring, response.
Plus an AI RMF Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1, 2024).

NIST SP 800-53 — security & privacy controls catalog (FedRAMP baseline).
NIST SP 800-61 — incident response.
NIST SP 800-218 / SSDF — secure software development.
CCPA / CPRA — California consumer privacy; opt-out of sale, right to know/delete, sensitive personal information.
HIPAA — health data privacy; technical safeguards, BAAs for vendors.
PCI-DSS — payment card data.
SOX — financial reporting controls; relevant for AI in finance.
Executive Order 14110 (Oct 2023, partially rescinded 2025) — defined safe/secure AI; replaced/updated by subsequent EOs.



23.4 International standards

ISO/IEC 27001 / 27002 — Information Security Management System (ISMS), Annex A controls.
ISO/IEC 42001 (2023) — AI Management System standard; the AI-specific counterpart to 27001.
ISO/IEC 23894 (2023) — AI risk management guidance.
ISO/IEC 22989 — AI concepts & terminology.
ISO/IEC 23053 — AI/ML framework.
ISO/IEC 5338 — AI system life cycle processes.
ISO/IEC 5259 series — data quality for AI.
SOC 2 (AICPA TSC) — Type I (design) / Type II (operating effectiveness); five trust services criteria: Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy.
FedRAMP — US gov cloud authorization; aligns with NIST 800-53.
NIS2 (EU 2022/2555) — cybersecurity directive for essential / important entities; incident reporting within 24h preliminary, 72h full.
DORA (EU 2022/2554) — Digital Operational Resilience Act for financial sector.
CSA CCM, CIS Controls, MITRE D3FEND.



23.5 Industry-specific

HIPAA Security Rule — administrative, physical, technical safeguards.
FFIEC — financial institutions.
FedRAMP High — government cloud.
ITAR / EAR — export controls (relevant for model weights — see US Commerce Bureau of Industry and Security 2024 IFR).
CCPA/CPRA, VCDPA, CPA, CTDPA — US state privacy laws.



23.6 Mapping AI security controls to standards







Control
Maps to




Encryption at rest
ISO 27001 A.8.24, NIST SP 800-53 SC-28, SOC 2 CC6.1, EU AI Act Art. 15


Access control
ISO 27001 A.5/A.8, NIST AC-1..AC-25, SOC 2 CC6


Incident response
ISO 27001 A.5.24-27, NIST IR family, SOC 2 CC7.3, NIS2 Art. 23


AI risk management
ISO 42001 6.1, NIST AI RMF Govern/Map/Measure/Manage


Logging & monitoring
ISO 27001 A.8.15, NIST AU family, SOC 2 CC7.2


Supply chain
ISO 27001 A.5.19-23, NIST SR family, SLSA

title: Security in AI
text:
MODULE 24: GOVERNANCE FRAMEWORKS

24.1 Roles and bodies

AI Risk Committee — cross-functional (security, legal, product, ML, ethics); approves high-risk use cases.
Responsible AI / AI Ethics Board — reviews societal/ethical implications.
Model Risk Management (MRM) — borrowed from finance (SR 11-7); independent validation of models.
CISO — ultimate accountability for AI security controls.
CDO / DPO — data governance & privacy.
AI Red Team — internal offensive testing function.
Model Owner — accountable for a specific model in production.



24.2 Inventory and lifecycle

Model inventory — every deployed model registered with metadata (owner, version, data lineage, evaluation, risk tier).
Datasheets for each dataset; Model Cards for each model.
Change management — versioned model artifacts, signed releases, approval gates for prod deploys.
Environment segregation — separate dev/staging/prod with strict data flow controls.
Third-party model approvals — risk review before adopting any external model (OpenAI, Anthropic, open-weights). Check license, training data disclosures, evaluation, jurisdiction.



24.3 SecMLOps / MLSecOps
Apply SecDevOps practices to ML:

Secure SDLC for ML — threat modeling at design, SAST/SCA on training code, code review, signed commits, branch protection.
CI/CD security — least-privileged runners, ephemeral creds, OIDC trust to cloud.
Model SBOM (MLBOM) — bill of materials covering datasets, base models, libraries, weights.
SLSA for ML — provenance attestations for training runs and model artifacts.
Signed models — sign weights with Sigstore/cosign; verify on load.
Vulnerability disclosure — huntr.dev for AI, internal VDP.
Continuous evaluation — nightly red-team runs, regression metrics, drift detection.



24.4 Audit logging
Log every privileged action: training run launched, dataset accessed, model deployed, prompt processed (with appropriate retention/redaction), tool call invoked, key used, IAM change made. Centralize to SIEM (Splunk, Sentinel, Chronicle, Elastic). Use immutable / append-only logs (write-once S3, Cloud Audit Logs).

title: Security in AI
text:
MODULE 25: INCIDENT RESPONSE FOR AI

25.1 NIST SP 800-61 phases (apply to AI incidents)

Preparation — playbooks, runbooks, IR team, contacts, tooling, tabletop exercises, model rollback procedures, known-good snapshots.
Detection & Analysis — monitoring (drift, accuracy drop, unusual outputs, safety filter triggers, anomalous query patterns), classification, prioritization.
Containment, Eradication, Recovery — isolate compromised endpoints, rotate keys, revert to last clean checkpoint, retrain from clean data, re-issue tokens, re-deploy.
Post-Incident Activity — blameless post-mortem, root-cause analysis, runbook updates, regulatory disclosures (GDPR 72h, NIS2 24h preliminary).



25.2 AI-specific incident categories and runbooks








Category
Example
First moves




Model poisoning
Backdoor discovered in prod model
Disable inference, switch to last-known-good model, audit training data, identify entry point


Data leak via outputs
LLM regurgitated PII
Add output filter rule, hotfix prompt, rotate any leaked secrets, notify DPO


Prompt-injection breach
Agent exfiltrated user data via indirect injection
Disable tool, audit conversation logs, rotate creds, push system-prompt hardening


Model theft
Stolen weights distributed externally
Engage legal, notify customers, prepare CVE-style disclosure, accelerate next-version release with fingerprint changes


Adversarial DoS
Sponge attack causing latency spike
Apply rate limit, deploy WAF rule, scale, monitor cost


Membership inference
Researcher publishes successful MIA
Evaluate severity, harden via DP fine-tune, notify data subjects if required


Bias incident
Public complaint of discrimination
Pause model, run fairness audit, comms response, model card update


Safety failure
Harmful content generated
Update Llama-Guard / guardrails, retrain safety filter, public statement





25.3 Communication and disclosure

Internal escalation matrix — sev levels (Sev-1: regulator notification likely; Sev-2: customer impact; Sev-3: contained internal).
Customer notice templates — what happened, what data, what we did, what they should do.
Regulator notice — GDPR Article 33 (72h), NIS2 (24h), state breach laws (varies), HIPAA breach notification (60d).
Public disclosure / CVE — coordinate via CERT/CC, MITRE, model-specific advisories.
Blameless post-mortem — describe the event, timeline, root cause, contributing factors, action items, owners, due dates.

title: Security in AI
text:
MODULE 26: OPERATIONAL RISK MANAGEMENT

26.1 Risk register
A living document listing all identified risks:







Field
Example




ID
RISK-AI-014


Description
Prompt-injection via uploaded PDFs in support agent


Asset
Customer Support LLM


Likelihood
High


Impact
High (data exfiltration, regulatory)


Risk score
9/10


Owner
AI Platform Team


Mitigation
Input sanitization + agent tool allowlist + sandbox URL fetch


Status
In progress


Residual risk
Medium


Review date
2026-Q2





26.2 Qualitative and quantitative methods

Qualitative: likelihood × impact matrix (5×5), risk heatmap.
Quantitative: FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk) — Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE), threat event frequency × loss magnitude.
Monte Carlo for tail-risk scenarios.
Bow-tie diagrams for cause/consequence analysis.



26.3 Strategy options
For each risk choose: Avoid, Mitigate, Transfer (insurance, contractual liability), Accept (with documented appetite). Document residual risk post-mitigation.


26.4 Continuous monitoring

KRIs — Key Risk Indicators (e.g., % requests blocked by safety filter, model drift score, time-to-patch, MTTR).
Trigger thresholds with auto-escalation.
Quarterly reviews with risk committee.



26.5 Risk appetite
A board-approved statement of the level of risk the organization will tolerate (e.g., “Zero tolerance for child-safety failures; medium tolerance for occasional hallucinations in summarization; high tolerance for experimental internal R&D”). Drives prioritization.

title: Security in AI
text:
MODULE 27: REFERENCE ARCHITECTURE
A secure-by-default AI/LLM platform looks like this:
                              ┌──────────────────────┐
                              │  Identity Provider   │
                              │  (Okta / Entra ID)   │
                              │   OIDC, SAML, MFA    │
                              └──────────┬───────────┘
                                         │ JWT
                                         ▼
   User ──TLS 1.3──▶ ┌───────────────────────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
                     │   API Gateway + WAF + DLP     │◀▶│   SIEM/SOC   │
                     │  rate-limit, auth, schema-    │  └──────────────┘
                     │  enforce, input scrub          │
                     └──────────────┬────────────────┘
                                    │ mTLS
                                    ▼
                     ┌───────────────────────────────┐
                     │   LLM Gateway                  │
                     │   - prompt-injection filter    │
                     │   - PII redaction              │
                     │   - per-tenant policy          │
                     │   - cost & quota guards        │
                     └────┬──────────────────────┬────┘
                          │ mTLS                 │ mTLS
                          ▼                      ▼
                ┌─────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────┐
                │ Model server     │       │  Vector store    │
                │  TEE (H100 CC)   │       │  per-doc ACLs    │
                │  signed weights  │       │  encrypted       │
                └────────┬─────────┘       └─────────────────┘
                         │ Llama-Guard / NeMo Guardrails
                         ▼
                 ┌─────────────────┐
                 │ Output filter   │
                 │  + PII scrub    │
                 │  + watermark    │
                 └─────────────────┘
Cross-cutting controls:

KMS-backed encryption at rest for every store, TLS 1.3 / mTLS everywhere in transit.
Zero-trust segmentation (BeyondCorp), short-lived workload identities (SPIFFE).
Centralized audit logs (immutable) to SIEM, alerting on anomalies.
Continuous red-teaming (garak + PyRIT in CI), AutoAttack for vision.
DP-SGD fine-tuning for any model trained on sensitive data.
Signed model artifacts (Sigstore/cosign), MLBOM, SLSA-L3 builds.
Per-tenant data isolation, per-model risk tier, model card + system card published.

title: Security in AI
section: MODULE 29: HIGH-VALUE FACTS AND CRISP DEFINITIONS
text:
MODULE 29: HIGH-VALUE FACTS AND CRISP DEFINITIONS
These are the kinds of definitions that show up verbatim in matching questions. Be able to recite them.
Evasion attack — adversarial input at inference time that causes incorrect prediction without modifying the model.
Poisoning attack — manipulation of training data or process so the deployed model behaves incorrectly on attacker-chosen inputs or overall.
Backdoor / Trojan — a poisoning attack that installs a hidden trigger: clean inputs behave normally, trigger inputs cause the attacker’s chosen behavior.
Model inversion — reconstructing training data attributes (or examples) from a trained model’s outputs.
Membership inference — determining whether a given record was used in training.
Model extraction / stealing — reconstructing a functionally equivalent model from query access.
Adversarial example — a (typically small) perturbation of an input that flips a model’s prediction.
Adversarial robustness — accuracy under a defined adversarial budget.
Adversarial training — training on adversarial examples generated on-the-fly.
Certified robustness — a mathematical guarantee of robustness within a ball.
Randomized smoothing — Gaussian-noise averaging that yields certified \(L_2\) robustness \(R = \sigma \Phi^{-1}(\underline{p_A})\).
Defensive distillation — train student on soft labels from a high-temperature teacher; not robust to adaptive attacks.
Gradient masking / obfuscation — defense that hides gradients without changing the decision boundary; broken by BPDA / EOT.
FGSM — single-step \(L_\infty\) adversarial attack: \(x' = x + \varepsilon\,\text{sign}(\nabla_x \mathcal{L})\).
PGD — iterative \(L_\infty\) attack with projection back into the ε-ball; canonical “strongest first-order attack”.
C&W — optimization-based attack with logit-margin loss and tanh box constraint; the gold standard for evaluation.
Differential privacy (DP) — randomized algorithm whose output distribution is nearly identical on neighboring datasets: \(\Pr[M(D)\in S]\le e^\varepsilon\Pr[M(D')\in S]+\delta\).
DP-SGD — DP training algorithm: clip per-example gradients, add Gaussian noise, average; track privacy via moments accountant / RDP.
Federated learning — train across decentralized devices/silos without centralizing raw data.
Secure aggregation — cryptographic protocol that lets a server compute the sum of client updates without seeing any single one.
Homomorphic encryption — encryption that allows computation on ciphertexts.
SMPC — secure multi-party computation; joint computation without revealing inputs.
TEE — Trusted Execution Environment; hardware enclave isolating compute and memory.
STRIDE — Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, DoS, Elevation of privilege.
MITRE ATLAS — Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems; the ATT&CK for AI.
OWASP Top 10 for LLMs — the standard LLM-application risk taxonomy.
Prompt injection — manipulating an LLM by including instructions in its input.
Indirect prompt injection — prompt injection delivered via content the LLM retrieves or ingests.
Jailbreak — bypass of an LLM’s safety training to elicit prohibited content.
GCG attack — gradient-guided suffix optimization (Zou 2023) that jailbreaks aligned LLMs and transfers across models.
Constitutional AI — Anthropic technique using a written constitution and RLAIF to align models without dense human feedback.
RLHF — Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback; PPO over a learned reward model.
DPO — Direct Preference Optimization; closed-form gradient over pairwise preferences, no separate reward model.
Llama Guard — open Meta classifier for unsafe inputs/outputs across taxonomy of harms.
MLBOM — Machine-Learning Bill of Materials; SBOM extended with data and model artifacts.
SLSA — Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts; provenance attestations.
Sigstore / cosign — keyless signing for artifacts (including model weights).
FAIR — Factor Analysis of Information Risk; quantitative risk method.
NIST AI RMF functions — Govern, Map, Measure, Manage.
EU AI Act risk tiers — Prohibited, High-risk, Limited (transparency), Minimal; plus GPAI duties.
ISO/IEC 42001 — AI management system standard (the AI counterpart of ISO 27001).
Zero Trust — never trust, always verify; every request authenticated and authorized regardless of network location.
Defense in depth — overlapping layers of controls so any single failure is non-catastrophic.
Least privilege — every identity has minimum necessary permissions.
Just-in-Time access — bounded-duration grant of elevated privileges.
Segregation of duties — sensitive actions require multiple actors.

title: Security in AI
section: MODULE 30: STUDY HABITS AND EXAM TACTICS
text:
MODULE 30: STUDY HABITS AND EXAM TACTICS

Read attack & defense in pairs: every attack you study, name its canonical defense before moving on.
Memorize the norms (\(L_0, L_2, L_\infty\)) and what attacks live in each.
Memorize the DP definition, FGSM/PGD equations, DP-SGD steps, randomized smoothing certificate.
Memorize the OWASP Top 10 for LLMs and NIST AI RMF four functions.
Memorize the EU AI Act tiers and the NIS2 / GDPR notification windows.
Practice writing the threat-model table (STRIDE/LINDDUN) for a sample AI system.
Re-derive the Madry minimax objective and explain it in words.
Recognize gradient masking failure modes (shattered, stochastic, vanishing/exploding) and the adaptive attack tools that defeat them (BPDA, EOT, AutoAttack).
For LLMs, be able to distinguish direct vs indirect prompt injection, GCG vs jailbreak, prompt extraction vs training-data extraction, model extraction vs theft of weights.
Be ready to map a real-world incident to its regulator notification timeline and the NIST SP 800-61 phase you are in.

title: Security in AI
text:
MODULE 31: SELF-TEST CHECKLIST
Tick each box. If you cannot answer a bullet in a sentence, return to the relevant module.

State (ε, δ)-DP formally and informally.
Write the FGSM update and explain why it works (linear hypothesis).
Write the PGD update and explain projection.
Explain C&W’s logit loss, box constraint, binary search on c.
Define randomized smoothing and its certificate \(R = \sigma\Phi^{-1}(\underline{p_A})\).
Explain DP-SGD’s clipping + noise + accounting.
List the three obfuscated-gradient failure modes and their counters (BPDA, EOT).
Distinguish white-box, gray-box, black-box; query-based, transfer-based, decision-based.
Name three poisoning defenses and the attack each targets.
Explain BadNets, clean-label backdoor, and Neural Cleanse.
Distinguish MIA (Shokri shadow models, loss thresh, LiRA) and their defenses.
Explain model inversion via gradient ascent and its defenses.
Explain model extraction and at least three defenses.
Recite the OWASP Top 10 for LLMs.
Distinguish direct vs indirect prompt injection with an example.
Explain the GCG attack at the level of one paragraph.
Explain Constitutional AI / RLHF / DPO / Llama Guard.
State the NIST AI RMF four functions.
State the EU AI Act risk tiers and the prohibited list.
State the GDPR breach window (72h) and NIS2 (24h preliminary).
Describe NIST SP 800-61 IR phases and one AI-specific runbook.
List five high-impact AI controls (KMS encryption, IAM least-privilege, output filter, rate-limit, audit logs).
Sketch a STRIDE table for an LLM RAG system.
Name three pentest tools for ML and three for LLMs.
Define MLBOM, SLSA for ML, signed weights, model card.
Distinguish anonymization, pseudonymization, k-anonymity, l-diversity, t-closeness.
Explain federated learning, secure aggregation, Byzantine-robust aggregation (Krum, Median, Bulyan, FLTrust).
Compare HE, SMPC, TEEs, DP — when to use each.

title: Vitals extraction from ICU monitors
text: As a member of the IIT Indore team that clinched gold at the Inter IIT Tech Meet 11.0, I’m excited to share our journey through the Vital Extraction Challenge. This competition, sponsored by Cloudphysician, pushed us to develop an innovative solution for extracting patient vitals from ICU monitor images. Our team’s dedication and ingenuity not only secured us the top spot but also resulted in a solution that was nearly five times faster than the runner-up, with a 2% edge in accuracy!

title: Vitals extraction from ICU monitors
section: The Challenge: Bridging the Gap in ICU Monitoring
text:
The Challenge: Bridging the Gap in ICU Monitoring
In an ideal world, ICUs would have a 1:6 nurse-to-patient ratio. However, the reality often falls short. Our challenge was to create a system that could extract vital signs from images of ICU monitors, effectively augmenting existing setups without requiring a complete overhaul of infrastructure.

title: Vitals extraction from ICU monitors
text:
Our Approach: A Three-Pronged Attack
We tackled this complex problem with a three-step pipeline:

Preprocessing
Vital Detection
Optical Character Recognition (OCR)

Let’s dive into each stage and explore the techniques that set our solution apart.

Preprocessing: Setting the Stage
The first hurdle was isolating the monitor screen from its surroundings. We employed the YOLO v8-n model for this task, achieving impressive results.After segmentation, we applied a perspective transformation to standardize the monitor’s orientation and size, crucial for the subsequent stages.


Vital Detection: The Heart of the Matter
This stage in the pipeline aims to detect the appropriate vitals in the cropped and transformed monitor screen. However, there was a lot of unlabelled data, and manually annotating all 9000 images was not an option. And it will never be a good solution in any real-world scenario. We identified two methods to deal with the vast amount of unlabelled data.
One approach was to use a customized Semi-Supervised Learning Model, which would learn from the small amount of labelled data and use it to generate pseudo labels and then true labels for the unlabelled data.
Another approach was to intelligently pick and manually annotate a small amount of unlabelled data and then add it to the training dataset in order to bring maximum diversity and representation to the training data without much effort. This approach was chosen because of its better results and lesser computational requirement.
For vital detection, we utilized the YOLO v8-s model, which delivered outstanding performance.


OCR: Deciphering the Digits
The final step was extracting the actual vital sign values. We opted for PaddleOCR, known for its accuracy and speed. However, this stage wasn’t without its challenges. We encountered issues like:

Misreading similar characters (e.g., “0” as “O”)
Detecting extraneous characters (like brackets)
Losing digits due to segmentation errors

To address these, we implemented a series of logic-based checks and corrections, ensuring the most probable correct value was returned.
##HR Graph Digitization Initially, we converted the HR graph segmented image into binary. In order to selectively obtain only the graph, the longest connected pixels row-wise were saved, and the rest discarded. The 2-D binary image was then projected into a 1-D Time Series. The plot was further rescaled in the x and y variables.

title: Vitals extraction from ICU monitors
section: Conclusion: Lessons Learned and Future Directions
text:
Conclusion: Lessons Learned and Future Directions
Our journey through the Vital Extraction Challenge was as enlightening as it was rewarding. We learned the importance of balancing innovation with practicality, and how sometimes the most straightforward approach, executed exceptionally well, can outperform more complex solutions. The potential impact of this technology extends far beyond a competition. By enabling more efficient monitoring of ICU patients, solutions like ours could help bridge the gap in healthcare resources, potentially saving lives in the process.
Find more about this project at : Github
