Are train/val/test splits time-aware where appropriate? Is there leakage?
Is the feature computation logic identical at train and serve time?
Are there fairness slices in the eval?
Are metrics reported with confidence intervals (bootstrap)?
Are model artifacts logged with a version and a config hash?
Are external dependencies (data tables, APIs) versioned/pinned?

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 5: REPRODUCIBILITY

5.1 The Five Pillars of Reproducibility
A run is reproducible only if all five are pinned:

Code — git SHA, plus dependency lock file.
Data — content hash of the exact training dataset (DVC, lakeFS, Delta Lake versioning).
Environment — Docker image digest (sha256:...), CUDA / cuDNN versions.
Configuration — full Hydra/YAML/json with overrides recorded.
Randomness — all seeds set deterministically; non-determinism explicitly disabled where possible.

Add a sixth in regulated contexts: hardware (GPU model, microcode), since some kernels are non-deterministic across architectures.


5.2 Seeding Everything
import os, random, numpy as np, torch

def seed_everything(seed: int = 42) -> None:
    os.environ["PYTHONHASHSEED"] = str(seed)
    random.seed(seed)
    np.random.seed(seed)
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(seed)
    torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True
    torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False
    # Strict determinism (PyTorch ≥ 1.8); requires CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG
    os.environ.setdefault("CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG", ":4096:8")
    torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True, warn_only=True)
Caveats: - Atomic reductions on GPUs are inherently non-deterministic in some ops. - Multi-GPU + NCCL all-reduce order may differ across runs. - TF32 on Ampere/Hopper changes precision; pin it (torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32).


5.3 Reproducible Data Splits

Use deterministic hash-based splits: hash(record_id) % 100 < 80 → train.
For time series: time-based (prefix train, suffix test); never random shuffle.
For grouped data (users, sessions): split by group to prevent leakage.
Record the exact split indices as an artifact (DVC params.yaml or MLflow artifact).

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 6: DATA VERSIONING

6.1 Why Version Data
Versioning code is necessary but not sufficient. A model’s behavior is a function of (code, data, config, env). Without data versioning, you cannot reproduce, audit, or rollback.


6.2 Tools — When to Use What









Tool
Best For
Mechanism
Notes




DVC (Data Version Control)
Files / directories alongside Git
Stores hashes in .dvc files; data in S3/GCS/Azure/local
Plays well with pipelines (dvc.yaml); ideal for training datasets and model artifacts


lakeFS
Data lake (S3) with Git semantics (branches, merges, commits)
Object-level versioning at the bucket level
Great for shared lakes, parallel experiments on the same physical data


Pachyderm
Containerized data pipelines with versioned commits
Kubernetes-native
Pipelines + versioning in one


Delta Lake
Lakehouse with ACID transactions on Parquet
Time-travel via version numbers / timestamps
First-class with Spark/Databricks


Apache Iceberg / Hudi
Open table formats for lakehouses
Snapshots, time-travel
Engine-agnostic (Spark, Trino, Flink)


Dolt
Git-for-tables (SQL)
Versioned database
Niche but powerful for slowly-changing reference data


Git-LFS
Large binaries in Git
Pointer files in Git, blobs in LFS server
Simple, but no pipeline semantics


Hugging Face Datasets / Hub
Open datasets + model artifacts
Git-LFS under the hood
Common in NLP/CV research





6.3 DVC Example
dvc init
dvc remote add -d storage s3://my-bucket/dvc-cache
dvc add data/raw/train.csv         # writes data/raw/train.csv.dvc
git add data/raw/train.csv.dvc .gitignore
git commit -m "data: track train.csv v1"
dvc push                           # uploads blob to S3
A dvc.yaml pipeline ties stages together:
stages:
  prepare:
    cmd: python -m my_project.data.prepare
    deps:
      - data/raw/train.csv
      - src/my_project/data/prepare.py
    outs:
      - data/processed/train.parquet
  train:
    cmd: python -m my_project.train
    deps:
      - data/processed/train.parquet
      - src/my_project/train.py
    params:
      - lr
      - batch_size
    outs:
      - models/model.pt
    metrics:
      - metrics.json:
          cache: false
dvc repro re-executes only stages whose deps changed (content-hash–based DAG).


6.4 Data Lineage and Catalogs

OpenLineage (spec) + Marquez (reference impl): emit lineage events from Airflow, Spark, dbt; visualize upstream/downstream impact.
Amundsen (Lyft), DataHub (LinkedIn), OpenMetadata: data catalogs with column-level lineage, ownership, freshness, popularity.
Why it matters: when a model misbehaves, lineage lets you trace back to the offending upstream change (“the partner team renamed user_age to age_years”). Lineage is also required for GDPR right-to-erasure and EU AI Act audit trails.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 7: MODEL VERSIONING AND REGISTRIES

7.1 What to Version
A model artifact is more than weights. The bundle typically includes:

Weights (model.pt, model.onnx, model.bin)
Tokenizer / preprocessing pipeline
Config (architecture hyperparams)
Metadata (training data hash, code SHA, metrics, training timestamp)
Model card (Mitchell et al., 2019)
Signature (input/output schema)
Optional: example payloads, baseline drift profile



7.2 Registries







Registry
Notes




MLflow Model Registry
Open-source; stages (None → Staging → Production → Archived); aliases; permissions


Weights & Biases Artifacts
Cloud-first, tightly tied to W&B runs


SageMaker Model Registry
AWS-native, integrates with Endpoint Config


Vertex AI Model Registry
GCP-native, integrates with Vertex Pipelines


Hugging Face Hub
De facto for transformer-era public models


BentoML Model Store / Yatai
Bento-bundles for serving


Neptune.ai / Comet / ClearML
Tracking + registry in one





7.3 Semantic Versioning for Models
A simple but effective scheme: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH-LABEL.

MAJOR: backwards-incompatible change (new feature schema, different output classes).
MINOR: new training data slice, architecture tweak.
PATCH: hyperparameter retune, bug fix in training.
LABEL: -shadow, -canary, -prod.



7.4 MLflow Snippet
import mlflow
import mlflow.pytorch

mlflow.set_tracking_uri("https://mlflow.mycorp.com")
mlflow.set_experiment("churn-v3")

with mlflow.start_run(run_name="run-2026-05-22") as run:
    mlflow.log_params({"lr": 3e-4, "bs": 256, "epochs": 30})
    mlflow.log_metric("val_auc", 0.872)
    mlflow.set_tag("git_sha", os.environ["GIT_SHA"])
    mlflow.set_tag("data_version", DATA_HASH)
    mlflow.pytorch.log_model(
        model,
        artifact_path="model",
        registered_model_name="churn-classifier",
    )

# Promote
client = mlflow.MlflowClient()
client.transition_model_version_stage(
    name="churn-classifier", version=12, stage="Staging",
)

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 8: EXPERIMENT TRACKING

8.1 What to Track (Eugene Yan’s “track everything”)
For every run, persist:







Category
Examples




Identity
Run ID, parent experiment, user, timestamp


Code
Git SHA, branch, diff


Data
Dataset hash/version, slice manifest


Config
All hyperparameters (including unused defaults)


Environment
Docker image digest, Python and library versions, CUDA


Metrics
Train/val/test, per epoch, per slice


Artifacts
Checkpoints, predictions on holdout, confusion matrices, calibration plots


Hardware
GPU type, count, RAM, utilization


Cost
Wall-clock time, $-cost estimate


Notes
Free-text observations and links





8.2 Experiment / Run / Trial Hierarchy

Project — the product or model family (e.g., “fraud-scoring”).
Experiment — a set of related runs sharing a hypothesis or codebase (“v3-architecture-sweep”).
Run — a single training execution.
Trial — within a run, a hyperparameter trial (in HPO frameworks like Optuna, Ray Tune).



8.3 Tools



Tool
Strengths




MLflow
OSS, simple API, easy self-host, registry built-in


Weights & Biases
Best UX, sweeps, reports, artifacts, alerts


Neptune.ai
Strong metadata/visualization, comparison


Comet
Similar to W&B, on-prem options


ClearML
Tracking + orchestration


TensorBoard
Local, in-training; pair with cloud tracker


DVC + dvclive
Git-native, no extra service required





8.4 Tracking Snippet (W&B)
import wandb

wandb.init(
    project="churn-v3",
    config={"lr": 3e-4, "bs": 256, "arch": "mlp-256-128"},
    tags=["baseline", "feature-set-v2"],
)
wandb.config.update({"git_sha": GIT_SHA, "data_version": DATA_HASH})

for epoch in range(EPOCHS):
    train_loss = train_one_epoch()
    val_auc    = evaluate()
    wandb.log({"epoch": epoch, "train_loss": train_loss, "val_auc": val_auc})

wandb.log_artifact("models/model.pt", name="churn-classifier", type="model")
wandb.finish()

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 9: WHAT “CI” MEANS FOR ML
In classical software CI, the unit is a commit. In ML CI, the units are {commit, data version, config}. A green CI must validate all three.

9.1 The ML CI Pipeline
A typical pipeline triggered by a PR:

Static checks — ruff, mypy, black, pre-commit.
Unit tests — pytest on src/.
Schema/data tests — Great Expectations / Pandera on a sample.
Small-batch training smoke test — train for 1 epoch on 1% of data; verify metrics on a frozen tiny holdout reach a sanity floor.
Model behavioral tests — invariance, directional, fairness slices.
Performance regression test — compare candidate vs current production model on a fixed eval set; block if regression > epsilon.
Container build — Docker image build & vulnerability scan (Trivy/Snyk).
Artifact promotion — push to registry with PR-tagged label.
Pull-request report — CML posts metrics, plots, drift report, diff vs main as a PR comment.



9.2 Great Expectations — Data Validation
import great_expectations as gx

ctx = gx.get_context()
suite = ctx.add_or_update_expectation_suite("training_data_suite")

batch = ctx.sources.pandas_default.read_csv("data/processed/train.csv")
validator = ctx.get_validator(batch_request=batch.batch_request, expectation_suite=suite)

validator.expect_column_to_exist("age")
validator.expect_column_values_to_be_between("age", min_value=0, max_value=120)
validator.expect_column_values_to_not_be_null("user_id")
validator.expect_column_proportion_of_unique_values_to_be_between("user_id", min_value=0.99, max_value=1.0)
validator.expect_column_kl_divergence_to_be_less_than(
    "income", partition_object=baseline_partition, threshold=0.1,
)
result = validator.validate()
assert result.success, result


9.3 Pandera — Lightweight DataFrame Schemas
import pandera as pa
from pandera.typing import DataFrame, Series

class TrainSchema(pa.DataFrameModel):
    user_id:  Series[str] = pa.Field(unique=True, nullable=False)
    age:      Series[int] = pa.Field(ge=0, le=120)
    income:   Series[float] = pa.Field(ge=0)
    country:  Series[str] = pa.Field(isin={"US","CA","UK","IN","DE","FR"})
    label:    Series[int] = pa.Field(isin={0,1})

@pa.check_types
def train(df: DataFrame[TrainSchema]) -> None:
    ...


9.4 TFDV (TensorFlow Data Validation)
For TFX shops:
import tensorflow_data_validation as tfdv

stats_train = tfdv.generate_statistics_from_csv("data/train.csv")
schema = tfdv.infer_schema(stats_train)

stats_serving = tfdv.generate_statistics_from_csv("data/serving.csv")
skew_anomalies = tfdv.validate_statistics(
    statistics=stats_serving, schema=schema, serving_statistics=stats_serving,
)


9.5 Model Behavioral Tests (CheckList, Ribeiro et al.)
Three categories:

Minimum Functionality Tests (MFT) — does the model handle the basics?
Invariance tests — perturbations that should not change the output (paraphrases, synonym swaps, capitalization).
Directional expectation tests — perturbations that should change the output in a known direction (negation flips sentiment; adding “not” should flip a binary classifier).

def test_negation_flips_sentiment():
    pos = model.predict("The food was great.")
    neg = model.predict("The food was not great.")
    assert (pos > 0.5) and (neg < 0.5)


9.6 Fairness Tests in CI
def test_disparate_impact():
    metrics = evaluate_by_group(model, holdout, group="gender")
    di = metrics["female"]["tpr"] / metrics["male"]["tpr"]
    assert 0.8 <= di <= 1.25, f"4/5ths rule violated: DI={di}"


9.7 Performance Regression Tests
def test_no_regression_against_current_prod():
    candidate_auc = evaluate(candidate_model, holdout)
    prod_auc      = evaluate(load_prod_model(), holdout)
    assert candidate_auc >= prod_auc - 0.005, (
        f"AUC regression: candidate={candidate_auc:.4f} prod={prod_auc:.4f}"
    )


9.8 GitHub Actions Skeleton
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: ML CI
on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.11" }
      - uses: snok/install-poetry@v1
      - run: poetry install --with dev
      - run: poetry run ruff check .
      - run: poetry run mypy src
      - run: poetry run pytest -q
      - name: Pull data with DVC
        env:
          AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
          AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
        run: poetry run dvc pull data/processed
      - name: Smoke train
        run: poetry run python -m my_project.train trainer.max_epochs=1 data.fraction=0.01
      - name: Behavioral & fairness tests
        run: poetry run pytest tests/behavior tests/fairness
      - name: Performance regression
        run: poetry run python tools/compare_to_prod.py --threshold 0.005
      - name: CML report
        uses: iterative/setup-cml@v2
      - run: |
          echo "## Model report" > report.md
          cat metrics.json >> report.md
          cml comment create report.md


9.9 Continuous Machine Learning (CML)
CML (by Iterative) integrates ML metrics & plots into PR conversations: post markdown reports, attach images, fail checks based on thresholds. Combines naturally with DVC.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 10: LEVELS 0, 1, 2
The Google MLOps whitepaper defines three levels:

Level 0 — Manual

Data and model preparation are manual.
Disconnect between data science and operations (researchers hand a model over).
Infrequent releases (months).
No CI, no CD, no active performance monitoring.
This is where most ML projects begin.



Level 1 — ML pipeline automation (CT)

The training pipeline is automated and reproducible.
Continuous Training (CT): retrain automatically based on triggers (new data, drift, schedule).
Components: feature store, metadata store, model registry, orchestrator.
The same pipeline runs in dev, staging, prod (with different inputs).
Still manual deployment of new pipelines themselves (no CI/CD on pipeline code).



Level 2 — CI/CD pipeline automation

The pipelines themselves are subject to CI/CD.
Source code → tests → build → deploy of pipeline components.
Automated end-to-end: a commit can trigger pipeline re-build, model re-train, evaluation, and deployment, all gated by quality checks.

Visualized:









Aspect
Level 0
Level 1
Level 2




Pipeline
Manual scripts
Automated training pipeline
Automated training pipeline + CI/CD on pipeline code


Retraining trigger
Manual
Drift / schedule / data
Drift / schedule / data


Registry
Optional
Yes
Yes


Feature store
Rare
Common
Standard


Monitoring
Ad-hoc
Live performance + drift
Live + automated rollback


Release cadence
Months
Days / weeks
Hours





10.1 Three Forms of CI/CD in ML








Term
What changes
Trigger




Code CI/CD
Application/training code
git push


Data CI/CD
Data schema, new ingestion
upstream change


Model CI/CD (CT)
Model weights
drift / performance / schedule



The three are intertwined: a data schema change may trigger model retrain; a code change may invalidate the registry; a model performance drop may trigger code investigations.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 11: CONTINUOUS TRAINING (CT) PIPELINES

11.1 Anatomy of a CT Pipeline
[trigger] → [data ingestion] → [data validation]
       → [feature engineering] → [training]
       → [model evaluation] → [validation gate]
       → [model registry] → [canary deploy]
       → [live evaluation] → [promote] / [rollback]
Each stage is idempotent, versioned, and retryable.


11.2 Orchestration Tools








Tool
Strengths
Weaknesses




Apache Airflow
Mature, huge community, many operators
Imperative DAGs (Python-as-config); historically heavy


Prefect (v2/v3)
Pythonic flows, dynamic DAGs, hybrid cloud
Smaller ecosystem


Dagster
Asset-oriented (data assets as first-class), built-in catalog
Newer; opinionated


Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP)
K8s-native, designed for ML
K8s ops overhead


Vertex AI Pipelines
KFP-compatible, GCP managed
GCP lock-in


SageMaker Pipelines
AWS-native, integrated with SM jobs
AWS lock-in


Argo Workflows
K8s-native, lightweight, language-agnostic
Lower-level


Metaflow (Netflix)
Pythonic; resume/scale across cloud
Less ML-specific UI


ZenML
Lightweight orchestrator-of-orchestrators
Newer


TFX (TensorFlow Extended)
End-to-end TF ecosystem
TF-centric


Flyte
K8s-native, strong typing
Steeper learning





11.3 Airflow DAG Example
from datetime import datetime
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.decorators import task
from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.operators.ecs import EcsRunTaskOperator

default_args = {"retries": 3, "retry_delay": 300}

with DAG(
    dag_id="churn_retrain",
    start_date=datetime(2024, 1, 1),
    schedule="@daily",
    default_args=default_args,
    catchup=False,
    max_active_runs=1,
    tags=["ml", "churn"],
) as dag:

    @task
    def check_drift() -> bool:
        from my_project.monitoring import psi_score
        return psi_score("income") > 0.2

    @task.short_circuit
    def gate(drifted: bool) -> bool:
        return drifted

    train = EcsRunTaskOperator(
        task_id="train",
        cluster="ml-cluster",
        task_definition="churn-train:42",
        launch_type="FARGATE",
    )

    @task
    def evaluate_and_register() -> None:
        ...

    drifted = check_drift()
    gate(drifted) >> train >> evaluate_and_register()


11.4 Kubeflow Pipeline Example
from kfp import dsl, compiler

@dsl.component(base_image="python:3.11")
def train(data_uri: str, lr: float) -> str:
    ...
    return "gs://bucket/models/run-123"

@dsl.component(base_image="python:3.11")
def evaluate(model_uri: str) -> float:
    ...

@dsl.pipeline(name="churn-pipeline")
def churn_pipeline(data_uri: str, lr: float = 3e-4):
    t = train(data_uri=data_uri, lr=lr)
    e = evaluate(model_uri=t.output)

compiler.Compiler().compile(churn_pipeline, "churn.yaml")


11.5 Idempotency, Retries, Backfills

Idempotent task: running it twice produces the same result. Achieved with deterministic outputs and write-then-rename to a content-hashed path.
Retries with exponential backoff for transient failures (network, spot eviction).
Backfills: ability to re-run historical runs against a fixed pipeline definition. Important for replaying after a bug fix.
SLAs: per-DAG / per-task deadlines; missed SLAs page on-call.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 12: DOCKER FOR ML

12.1 A Production Dockerfile
# ---- Stage 1: build ----
FROM python:3.11-slim AS builder
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 PIP_NO_CACHE_DIR=1
WORKDIR /app

RUN pip install --no-cache-dir poetry==1.8.3 \
 && poetry config virtualenvs.create false

COPY pyproject.toml poetry.lock ./
RUN poetry install --only main --no-root --no-interaction --no-ansi

COPY src ./src
RUN poetry install --only main --no-interaction --no-ansi

# ---- Stage 2: runtime ----
FROM python:3.11-slim AS runtime

RUN groupadd -g 10001 app && useradd -u 10001 -g app -s /sbin/nologin app
WORKDIR /app

COPY --from=builder /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages
COPY --from=builder /app/src /app/src

ENV PYTHONPATH=/app/src \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
    PORT=8080

USER app
EXPOSE 8080
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s CMD curl -fs http://localhost:8080/healthz || exit 1

ENTRYPOINT ["uvicorn", "my_project.serve:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8080"]
Best practices encoded above: - Multi-stage build to keep runtime image small (no build tools). - Slim base image (or distroless for minimum CVE surface). - Non-root user (UID 10001). - No secrets baked in (mounted at runtime). - Healthcheck for orchestrator readiness/liveness. - .dockerignore must exclude .git, .venv, data/, notebooks/, *.pyc.


12.2 BuildKit, Layer Caching, and CI
DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker buildx build \
  --platform linux/amd64 \
  --cache-from type=registry,ref=registry/myimg:cache \
  --cache-to   type=registry,ref=registry/myimg:cache,mode=max \
  --build-arg GIT_SHA=$GIT_SHA \
  -t registry/myimg:$GIT_SHA \
  -t registry/myimg:latest \
  --push .
Pin digests for base images in production:
FROM python:3.11-slim@sha256:abc123...


12.3 GPU Images
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-runtime-ubuntu22.04
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    python3.11 python3-pip && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
Run with: docker run --gpus all .... On Kubernetes, request GPUs with resources.limits["nvidia.com/gpu"].


12.4 Image Security

Trivy / Snyk / Grype scans for CVEs.
Cosign (sigstore) to sign images; verify at admission.
SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) — generate with Syft, embed in image labels.
SLSA provenance — record the build environment.
Distroless base (gcr.io/distroless/python3) for minimal attack surface.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 13: KUBERNETES FOR ML

13.1 Core Objects







Object
Purpose




Pod
Smallest unit; one or more containers


Deployment
Manages a ReplicaSet of stateless pods


StatefulSet
Stable identity and persistent volumes


Service
Stable IP/DNS for a set of pods (ClusterIP / NodePort / LoadBalancer)


Ingress
HTTP(S) routing


ConfigMap / Secret
Config and secret data injection


HPA / VPA
Horizontal / vertical pod autoscaling


Cluster Autoscaler
Node-level autoscaling


KEDA
Event-driven autoscaling (Kafka lag, Prometheus query, etc.)


DaemonSet
One pod per node (GPU operator, logging agent)


Job / CronJob
Run-to-completion batch / scheduled


CRD + Operator
Extend the API for domain-specific resources (e.g., KServe InferenceService)





13.2 Sample Deployment + Service
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: churn-classifier
  labels: { app: churn, version: "v1.4.0" }
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector: { matchLabels: { app: churn } }
  template:
    metadata: { labels: { app: churn, version: "v1.4.0" } }
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: churn-sa
      containers:
        - name: server
          image: registry/churn:v1.4.0@sha256:...
          ports: [{ containerPort: 8080 }]
          env:
            - name: MODEL_URI
              valueFrom:
                configMapKeyRef: { name: churn-config, key: model_uri }
            - name: DB_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef: { name: churn-secrets, key: db_password }
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet: { path: /healthz, port: 8080 }
            periodSeconds: 5
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet: { path: /livez, port: 8080 }
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
          resources:
            requests: { cpu: "500m", memory: "1Gi" }
            limits:   { cpu: "2",    memory: "4Gi", nvidia.com/gpu: "1" }
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata: { name: churn-svc }
spec:
  selector: { app: churn }
  ports: [{ port: 80, targetPort: 8080 }]
  type: ClusterIP


13.3 HPA (CPU and Custom Metrics)
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata: { name: churn-hpa }
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: churn-classifier
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 50
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target: { type: Utilization, averageUtilization: 70 }
    - type: Pods
      pods:
        metric: { name: requests_per_second }
        target: { type: AverageValue, averageValue: "100" }
  behavior:
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300
      policies: [{ type: Percent, value: 50, periodSeconds: 60 }]
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0
      policies: [{ type: Percent, value: 100, periodSeconds: 30 }]


13.4 GPU Scheduling
spec:
  nodeSelector: { cloud.google.com/gke-accelerator: "nvidia-h100-80gb" }
  tolerations:
    - key: "nvidia.com/gpu"
      operator: "Exists"
      effect: "NoSchedule"
  containers:
    - name: trainer
      image: registry/trainer:v1.0.0
      resources:
        limits: { nvidia.com/gpu: 4 }

NVIDIA GPU Operator installs drivers, device plugins, and MIG configuration.
MIG (Multi-Instance GPU) on A100/H100 partitions one GPU into up to 7 isolated slices — useful for cost-efficient inference.
Time-slicing / MPS for sharing without isolation (training side-cars, low-traffic inference).



13.5 Helm
Helm charts package Kubernetes manifests with templating. A values.yaml becomes the deployment config of record.
# values.yaml
image:
  repository: registry/churn
  tag: v1.4.0
replicas: 3
resources:
  limits: { cpu: "2", memory: "4Gi" }


13.6 RBAC & Network Policies
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata: { name: churn-reader, namespace: ml-prod }
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["configmaps", "secrets"]
    verbs: ["get", "list"]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata: { name: churn-only-from-gateway, namespace: ml-prod }
spec:
  podSelector: { matchLabels: { app: churn } }
  policyTypes: ["Ingress"]
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector: { matchLabels: { name: gateway } }
      ports:
        - { protocol: TCP, port: 8080 }

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 14: BATCH, REAL-TIME, STREAMING, EDGE

14.1 Decision Table










Pattern
Latency target
Throughput
Use cases
Tradeoffs




Batch (offline)
minutes–hours
very high
Nightly scoring, lead-gen, content tagging
Stale predictions; simplest infra


Real-time (online sync)
< 100 ms p99
medium
Fraud, ranking, recommendations
Strict latency budget, autoscaling required


Real-time (online async)
seconds
high
Background tasks, LLM long-running calls
Requires queue, retries, idempotency


Streaming
seconds
very high
IoT, fraud streams, telemetry
Stateful processing complexity (windows, late data)


Edge / on-device
tens of ms
device-bound
Mobile, AR/VR, robotics, privacy
Model size & energy constraints





14.2 Batch Inference

Compute embeddings/predictions for a slow-moving population once a day, write to a key-value store (Redis, DynamoDB), read at serve time.
Tools: Spark, Beam/Dataflow, Ray Data, AWS Batch, Vertex AI Batch Prediction.
Cost-efficient (large GPU bursts on spot/preemptible), but predictions are stale by definition.



14.3 Real-time Online Inference

A request comes in over HTTP/gRPC/GraphQL → load features → predict → return.
Common stack: FastAPI/BentoML/Triton behind a Kubernetes Deployment behind a load balancer.
Latency p99 < 100 ms typical; LLMs change the math (streaming tokens, SSE, sticky sessions).

# FastAPI service
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
import torch

app = FastAPI()
model = torch.jit.load("model.pt").eval()

class Req(BaseModel):
    user_id: str
    features: list[float]

@app.get("/healthz")
def healthz(): return {"ok": True}

@app.post("/predict")
def predict(req: Req):
    with torch.inference_mode():
        x = torch.tensor([req.features], dtype=torch.float32)
        y = float(model(x).sigmoid().item())
    return {"score": y, "model_version": MODEL_VERSION}


14.4 Streaming Inference

Kafka topic → Flink/Spark Structured Streaming/Beam → enrichment + prediction → output topic / sink.
Stateful windows for aggregations (e.g., features like “transactions in last 5 min”).
Watermarks and late-data handling are first-class concerns.



14.5 Edge Inference

Model runs on the device: phone (TFLite, CoreML, NNAPI), embedded (TensorRT on Jetson, OpenVINO on Intel), browser (ONNX Runtime Web, WebGPU).
Latency: tens of ms; offline; privacy-preserving (data stays local).
Constraints: model size, memory, energy, OTA update reliability.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
section: MODULE 15: RELEASE STRATEGIES (SHADOW, CANARY, BLUE/GREEN, A/B, BANDIT)
text:
MODULE 15: RELEASE STRATEGIES (SHADOW, CANARY, BLUE/GREEN, A/B, BANDIT)

15.1 Shadow Deployment (a.k.a. “Dark Launch”)

The new model receives production traffic in parallel with the current model.
Its predictions are logged but not returned to the user.
Purpose: validate latency, throughput, and prediction distribution under real load before any user impact.
Costs: 2× inference compute during shadow.

@app.post("/predict")
async def predict(req: Req):
    primary_task = asyncio.create_task(primary_model.predict(req))
    asyncio.create_task(log_shadow(shadow_model, req))  # fire and forget
    return {"score": await primary_task}


15.2 Canary Release

Route a small fraction (e.g., 1%) of traffic to the new version; gradually ramp (1% → 5% → 25% → 100%) while monitoring SLOs.
Automated rollback if error rate or KPI drops past a threshold.

# Argo Rollouts canary spec (excerpt)
strategy:
  canary:
    steps:
      - setWeight: 1
      - pause: { duration: 5m }
      - setWeight: 5
      - pause: { duration: 10m }
      - setWeight: 25
      - pause: { duration: 30m }
      - setWeight: 100
    analysis:
      templates: [{ templateName: success-rate }]


15.3 Blue/Green

Two full environments (blue=current, green=candidate). Swap a single router pointer to move traffic 100% in one step.
Fast rollback (flip back). Cost: ~2× while both are live.



15.4 A/B Testing

Randomly split users into A and B groups; measure a primary KPI; compute statistical significance.
Power analysis before launch:

\[
n \;\approx\; \frac{2\,(z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta})^2 \,\sigma^2}{\Delta^2}
\]
where \(\Delta\) is the minimum detectable effect, \(\sigma^2\) the variance, \(\alpha\) the significance level, \(1-\beta\) the power.

Stratified randomization for balance across segments.
Interleaving (especially for ranking): show items from A and B in the same list; less noisy than naive A/B.
Pitfalls: peeking, multiple comparisons, network effects (recommenders), Simpson’s paradox.



15.5 Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB)

Adaptive: route more traffic to better-performing variants in real time.
Algorithms: \(\varepsilon\)-greedy, UCB, Thompson sampling.
Tradeoff: faster regret minimization, but harder to interpret as a clean experiment; statistical inference is non-trivial.



15.6 Feature Flags

Decouple release from deployment.
LaunchDarkly, Unleash, Split, ConfigCat.
Use cases: dark launches, kill switches, targeted rollouts (premium users only), regional rollouts.

if flags.is_enabled("new-ranking-model", user=user_id):
    return new_model.predict(...)
return current_model.predict(...)

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 16: SCALING INFERENCE

16.1 Architectures








Architecture
Pros
Cons




Single-model service
Simple, isolated, easy to autoscale
Resource fragmentation for many models


Multi-model server (Triton, SageMaker MMS)
One server hosts N models, dynamic load/unload
Noisy neighbor, cold start cost


Model-as-a-function (serverless: Lambda, Cloud Run, KServe scale-to-zero)
Pay per request; great for spiky/low traffic
Cold starts; size limits


Embedded (model loaded in app process)
Lowest latency; no network hop
Couples release cadence; per-instance memory





16.2 Dynamic Batching
For GPU inference, batching is the single biggest throughput lever: more samples per kernel launch amortize compute overhead. Dynamic batching in Triton/TF Serving/TorchServe collects requests within a window (e.g., 5 ms) and runs them as a single batch.
Tradeoff: tail latency grows with batch wait time. Tune max_queue_delay_microseconds, preferred_batch_size.
# Triton config.pbtxt
name: "churn"
platform: "onnxruntime_onnx"
max_batch_size: 64
dynamic_batching {
  preferred_batch_size: [ 8, 16, 32 ]
  max_queue_delay_microseconds: 5000
}


16.3 Inference Servers Compared








Server
Best For
Notes




NVIDIA Triton Inference Server
High-throughput multi-model GPU serving (ONNX, TRT, TF, PyTorch, Python)
Dynamic batching, model ensembles, BLS scripting


TorchServe
PyTorch
Eager and TorchScript; lower ceiling than Triton


TensorFlow Serving
TF SavedModel
Mature, gRPC-first


BentoML
Python-first packaging, “Bentos”
Great DX; supports many backends


Ray Serve
Composable Python pipelines (pre/post + model graphs)
Native Ray scaling


Seldon Core
K8s-native, ensembles, explainers, outlier detection
Heavier


KServe (ex-KFServing)
K8s-native, model-mesh, scale-to-zero, V2 protocol
Becoming the K8s standard


ONNX Runtime Server
ONNX
Lightweight


vLLM / TensorRT-LLM / TGI
LLM inference (paged KV cache, continuous batching)
Specialized





16.4 KServe InferenceService
apiVersion: serving.kserve.io/v1beta1
kind: InferenceService
metadata: { name: churn }
spec:
  predictor:
    minReplicas: 1
    maxReplicas: 50
    scaleTarget: 80
    scaleMetric: rps
    sklearn:
      storageUri: gs://my-models/churn/v1.4.0
      resources:
        limits: { cpu: "2", memory: "4Gi" }


16.5 Feature Stores
A feature store is a centralized store for production-grade features with two flavors:

Offline store (warehouse: BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift, Parquet on S3) for training.
Online store (low-latency KV: Redis, DynamoDB, Bigtable, Cassandra) for serving.

Why: train/serve consistency (the same feature definition produces the same value at train and serve time, eliminating training-serving skew — Zinkevich’s Rule #29). Also: feature reuse across teams, point-in-time correctness, governance.
Tools: Feast (OSS), Tecton (managed Feast++), Hopsworks, Vertex AI Feature Store, SageMaker Feature Store, Databricks Feature Store.
# Feast snippet
from feast import FeatureStore
store = FeatureStore(repo_path=".")

features = store.get_online_features(
    features=["user_features:age", "user_features:tenure_days", "tx_features:amt_24h"],
    entity_rows=[{"user_id": uid}],
).to_dict()
Point-in-time correctness (“as-of join”): when building training data, fetch each feature’s value as it would have been at the time of the event, not the latest value — to prevent leakage.


16.6 Caching and CDN

Result caching (Redis) for deterministic functions of the request (e.g., embedding of a fixed item).
Feature caching in-process or near-process to reduce online-store roundtrips.
CDN for static model artifacts (Hugging Face, image embeddings).
Cache invalidation aligns with model version changes (key includes model version).



16.7 Cost / Latency / Quality Tradeoffs



Lever
Latency
Throughput
Cost
Quality




Quantize (FP16/INT8/INT4)
↓
↑
↓
slight ↓


Dynamic batching
↑ tail, ↓ avg
↑↑
↓
–


Distillation
↓
↑
↓
↓


Pruning
↓
↑
↓
small ↓


Spot/preemptible
–
–
↓
– (with retries)


Multi-model endpoints
–
↑ (consolidation)
↓
–


MIG slicing
–
↑
↓
–


Scale-to-zero
↑ (cold start)
–
↓↓
–

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 17: MODEL PERFORMANCE MONITORING

17.1 The Ground-Truth Lag Problem
You usually do not have labels at serve time. Examples: - Fraud: confirmation can take weeks (chargebacks). - Loan default: months. - Click prediction: minutes. - Medical diagnosis: never.
This is the ground-truth lag. It forces you to monitor proxies in the meantime:

Input drift: change in input distribution → model is operating out-of-distribution.
Prediction drift: shift in the model’s output distribution.
Action drift: change in downstream user behavior (CTR, conversion).
Calibration drift: mismatch between predicted probability and observed frequency.

When labels finally arrive, recompute true performance and reconcile.


17.2 Per-Slice Monitoring
A global metric can hide subgroup regressions (Simpson’s paradox). Monitor:

Per-country, per-language, per-device.
Per-customer-tier.
Per-protected-class (gender, age band, race) — required for fairness audits.
Per-time-of-day, per-day-of-week.

Set per-slice SLOs where appropriate.


17.3 Online Evaluation
When labels arrive quickly (clicks): - Real-time A/B reports: lift over control. - Counterfactual evaluation: off-policy estimators (IPS, doubly robust) for systems with bandit/RL learning. - Holdouts: 1% of traffic permanently shown the previous model; the honest baseline.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 18: DATA AND CONCEPT DRIFT

18.1 Definitions
Let \(X\) be inputs and \(Y\) the label. Decompose the joint \(P(X, Y) = P(Y \mid X) \, P(X)\).








Type
Formal
Practical




Covariate shift
\(P(X)\) changes, \(P(Y\mid X)\) does not
New geography sends more young users; the relationship age→churn is unchanged


Prior shift / Label shift
\(P(Y)\) changes, \(P(X\mid Y)\) does not
Fraud rate spikes seasonally


Concept drift
\(P(Y\mid X)\) changes
What “spam” looks like evolves; same features, new mapping


Train/serve skew
Training distribution ≠ serving distribution due to a pipeline bug, not the world changing
Feature computed differently at train vs serve time



Gama et al. (“A Survey on Concept Drift Adaptation,” 2014) classify drift as: - Sudden (abrupt change point) - Gradual (slow transition between regimes) - Incremental (continuous slow change) - Recurring (seasonality)


18.2 Detecting Drift: Statistical Tests
Population Stability Index (PSI) — for binned distributions \(p\) (reference) and \(q\) (current):
\[
\text{PSI}(p, q) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{B} \bigl(p_i - q_i\bigr) \,\ln\!\frac{p_i}{q_i}
\]
Rule of thumb: \(\text{PSI} < 0.1\) → no significant shift; \(0.1 \le \text{PSI} < 0.25\) → moderate; \(\ge 0.25\) → significant.
Kolmogorov–Smirnov (KS) test — for continuous 1D distributions:
\[
D \;=\; \sup_{x} \,\bigl| F_p(x) - F_q(x) \bigr|
\]
Two-sample p-value flags drift. Sensitive to large samples (everything is “significant”); pair with effect size.
Chi-square test — for categorical features:
\[
\chi^2 \;=\; \sum_i \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}
\]
where \(O_i\) are observed counts and \(E_i\) expected counts under the reference.
KL divergence:
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p \,\|\, q) \;=\; \sum_i p_i \,\ln\!\frac{p_i}{q_i}
\]
Asymmetric; undefined when \(q_i = 0\). Use Jensen–Shannon divergence (symmetric, bounded):
\[
\mathrm{JSD}(p, q) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p \,\|\, m) + \tfrac{1}{2} D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q \,\|\, m),\quad m=\tfrac{1}{2}(p+q)
\]
Wasserstein (Earth Mover’s) distance — for 1D:
\[
W_1(p, q) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \bigl|F_p(x) - F_q(x)\bigr|\,dx
\]
Captures distance even when supports do not overlap; robust to small bin counts.
Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) — kernel-based test for multivariate drift:
\[
\mathrm{MMD}^2(p, q) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{x,x'\sim p}\!\bigl[k(x,x')\bigr] + \mathbb{E}_{y,y'\sim q}\!\bigl[k(y,y')\bigr] - 2\,\mathbb{E}_{x\sim p, y\sim q}\!\bigl[k(x,y)\bigr]
\]
Page–Hinkley test / CUSUM — sequential change-point detection; flags when cumulative deviation from a baseline crosses a threshold.
Embedding drift: for unstructured data (text, images), monitor the distribution of embeddings (e.g., centroid distance, MMD over embeddings, classifier-based two-sample test).


18.3 Concept Drift Detectors

DDM (Drift Detection Method) (Gama et al., 2004): monitors classifier error rate; flags when it exceeds a threshold of \(\mu + 3\sigma\).
EDDM (Early DDM): monitors distance between misclassifications.
ADWIN (Adaptive Windowing) (Bifet & Gavaldà, 2007): adaptive sliding window with Hoeffding-bound statistical guarantees.
HDDM, Page–Hinkley, KSWIN are also common.



18.4 Toolkits







Tool
Strengths




EvidentlyAI
OSS dashboards, drift + data quality + model performance; CI-friendly reports


Arize
Production monitoring + tracing; UMAP for embedding drift


Whylabs
Profile-based (whylogs), low-overhead


Fiddler
Explainability + monitoring + fairness


Aporia
Customizable monitors, alerts


NannyML
Performance estimation without labels (CBPE, DLE) — clever and useful


Alibi Detect
OSS drift/outlier detectors (KS, MMD, LSDD, ChiSquare, learned kernel)





18.5 EvidentlyAI Snippet
from evidently.report import Report
from evidently.metric_preset import DataDriftPreset, DataQualityPreset

report = Report(metrics=[DataDriftPreset(), DataQualityPreset()])
report.run(reference_data=ref_df, current_data=cur_df)
report.save_html("drift_report.html")

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 19: OBSERVABILITY (LOGS, METRICS, TRACES)

19.1 The Three Pillars







Pillar
Stack




Metrics
Prometheus, OpenMetrics, StatsD; visualize in Grafana


Logs
Loki, Elasticsearch, CloudWatch, BigQuery; structured JSON


Traces
OpenTelemetry, Jaeger, Tempo, Honeycomb, Datadog APM



Add a fourth for ML: Predictions / Features (long-term storage, sampled, used for drift, replay, debugging).


19.2 SLI, SLO, SLA, Error Budgets

SLI (Service Level Indicator) — a metric you measure (request latency p99, error rate, prediction freshness).
SLO (Service Level Objective) — the target (“99.9% of requests respond in <200 ms over 30 days”).
SLA (Service Level Agreement) — the externally-promised version of an SLO (often with penalties).
Error budget — 1 − SLO. If 99.9% is the target, you may “burn” 0.1% of requests as errors per window. When the budget burns fast, freeze risky changes.



19.3 ML-Specific SLOs







SLI
Example SLO




p99 prediction latency
< 100 ms over rolling 30 min


Prediction error rate (5xx)
< 0.1%


Feature freshness
99% of features younger than 5 min


Drift PSI for top-10 features
< 0.2 over 24h


Model accuracy on delayed labels
within 2 pp of training holdout, 7-day rolling


Fairness disparity
within ±5% of group parity





19.4 Prometheus Alerts
groups:
  - name: ml-churn
    rules:
      - alert: ChurnHighLatency
        expr: histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{app="churn"}[5m])) > 0.2
        for: 10m
        labels: { severity: page }
        annotations:
          summary: "Churn p99 latency > 200ms"
      - alert: ChurnDriftPSI
        expr: ml_feature_psi{feature="income"} > 0.25
        for: 30m
        labels: { severity: ticket }


19.5 OpenTelemetry Tracing (Python)
from opentelemetry import trace
tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

@app.post("/predict")
def predict(req):
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("predict") as span:
        span.set_attribute("user.id", req.user_id)
        with tracer.start_as_current_span("fetch_features"):
            feats = feature_store.get(req.user_id)
        with tracer.start_as_current_span("model.infer"):
            y = model(feats)
        span.set_attribute("model.version", MODEL_VERSION)
        return {"score": y}
Traces let you see the breakdown (network → feature store → preprocess → model → postprocess) and detect bottlenecks per request.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 20: AUTOMATED RETRAINING

20.1 Triggers







Trigger
When




Schedule
Daily/weekly/monthly cron — simple, predictable


Drift threshold
PSI > 0.25 on any top-K feature for 24 h


Performance drop
Online metric falls below threshold


New labeled data
Volume threshold reached


Concept-change events
Holiday, new product launch, regulation change


Manual
One-click retrain in registry





20.2 Champion–Challenger

Champion: current production model.
Challenger: candidate; compete on a holdout and/or live shadow traffic.
Promote only if challenger wins by a margin with statistical significance over a sustained window.
“Stale champion” guardrails: even if no challenger wins, force retrains on a schedule to keep up with drift.



20.3 Automated Promotion Gates
Define a checklist that must pass before promotion:
def promotion_gate(report) -> bool:
    return all([
        report.holdout_auc >= prod_auc - 0.005,
        report.fairness_di >= 0.8 and report.fairness_di <= 1.25,
        report.latency_p99_ms < 100,
        report.calibration_ece < 0.05,
        report.drift_psi_max < 0.2,
        report.behavioral_tests_passed,
    ])
High-stakes domains (medical, financial) add a manual approval step (model risk management committee, regulator sign-off).


20.4 Pitfalls of Automated Retraining

Feedback loops: training on data shaped by your model’s actions biases the next model. Use exploration / holdouts to break the loop.
Label noise amplification: bad upstream labeling pipeline contaminates each retrain.
Catastrophic forgetting in online learning; consider replay buffers.
Schema changes that break the retrain pipeline silently (the model still trains, but on a different feature definition).

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 21: PRODUCTION DEBUGGING & INCIDENT RESPONSE

21.1 Train–Serve Skew
The #1 silent killer in ML production.
Causes: - Feature transform implemented twice (training in pandas, serving in Java) and they drift. - Time-of-day issues (training pulled latest snapshot; serving uses point-in-time). - Encoder/categorical maps differ. - Different default values for missing features.
Mitigations: - One feature transform library (importable from both training and serving). - Feature store as the canonical source. - Schemas with TFDV/Pandera/GE enforced both sides. - Skew detector: log a sample of serving features and compare distributions to training nightly.


21.2 Data Leakage
The model “knows” the future or target in training: - Target encoding leaking the label. - Future timestamps in train. - Identifiers correlated with label (e.g., record_id that increments with positives).
Detection: ablations, suspiciously high metrics, partial dependence plots showing implausible monotonicity.


21.3 Rollback Strategies

Immediate rollback: blue/green flip; feature flag off; service mesh route weight = 0.
Gradual rollback: reverse canary (100% → 25% → 0).
Model rollback: registry alias prod → previous version; redeploy.
Data rollback: when an upstream data bug corrupted training data, roll back the dataset version and rebuild from a known-good snapshot.
Schema rollback: if a schema change broke things, revert both schema and consumers.

Every model should have a runbook describing: - How to detect failure - How to roll back in <5 minutes - Who to page - How to communicate to stakeholders


21.4 Dark Launches and Ghost Evaluation

Dark launch: new feature/model receives traffic but its output is invisible to users; ghost rank/score.
Replay: stream historical traffic against the candidate to surface latency and prediction-distribution issues offline.



21.5 Incident Postmortems
Blameless postmortems for ML incidents — beyond the usual: - Root cause in {code, data, config, model, env, infra}. - Detection time (when did monitoring see it?), mitigation time, resolution time. - Affected slices and customers. - Followups as tickets (monitoring gaps, new tests, runbook entries). - Knowledge sharing: add learnings to the ML test suite.


21.6 Chaos Engineering for ML (“Gameday”)

Inject corrupted features (NaNs, swapped categories) — does the model fail closed or fail open?
Kill the feature store — does serving degrade gracefully?
Throttle the model endpoint — does the upstream fall back?
Replay an old data snapshot to test drift alerts.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 22: DEPLOYING TO THE EDGE

22.1 Targets and Frameworks







Target
Runtime




Android
TFLite, ONNX Runtime Mobile, MediaPipe; NNAPI / GPU delegate


iOS
CoreML, TFLite, ONNX RT Mobile; Apple Neural Engine


Embedded Linux
ONNX Runtime, TFLite, OpenVINO, TensorRT


NVIDIA Jetson (Nano/Orin)
TensorRT, DeepStream


Google Coral (Edge TPU)
TFLite with Edge TPU compiler


Intel Movidius / Myriad / NPU
OpenVINO


Hexagon DSP (Qualcomm)
SNPE, QNN


Browser
ONNX Runtime Web, TensorFlow.js, WebGPU





22.2 Conversion Pipeline
A typical flow:

Train in PyTorch/TF.
Export to ONNX (cross-framework intermediate).
Optimize with framework-specific compilers (TensorRT engine, TFLite converter, CoreML Tools).
Quantize (post-training INT8 with calibration data, or quantization-aware training).
Bench on the device (latency, energy, memory).
Package into a model bundle (versioned, signed).
OTA update with rollback.

# PyTorch -> ONNX -> TFLite snippet
import torch
torch.onnx.export(model, dummy_x, "model.onnx", opset_version=17, dynamic_axes={"x":{0:"batch"}})

# ONNX -> TFLite via onnx-tf and tf converter (one of many paths)


22.3 Quantization Recap (Deployment-Centric)

FP16 / BF16: half the memory; easy on most accelerators.
INT8 PTQ (post-training quantization) with a calibration dataset; works well for many models.
INT8 QAT (quantization-aware training) for accuracy-sensitive cases.
INT4 / NF4 for LLMs (bitsandbytes, AWQ, GPTQ).
Watch for quality degradation on long-tail classes and rare slices — re-evaluate.



22.4 Edge-Specific MLOps Constraints







Constraint
Handling




Heterogeneous hardware
Multiple model variants per SKU; A/B by device


OTA update reliability
Signed packages, staged rollouts, rollback to previous, delta updates


Limited monitoring bandwidth
Sampled telemetry; on-device aggregations; differential privacy if needed


Privacy
On-device inference; federated learning (Kairouz et al.); local DP


Energy / thermal
Throttle, budget; cap inference rate; on-demand wakeup


Offline operation
Cache last-known-good model; sync deltas opportunistically


Cold start
Pre-load on app start; mmap weights


Fallback to cloud
If on-device model uncertain, escalate to cloud model





22.5 Federated Learning Cameo
Train across devices without centralizing raw data: - FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017): each client updates locally; server averages weights. - Practical concerns: stragglers, secure aggregation, DP noise, communication efficiency (TensorFlow Federated, Flower).

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 23: REGULATED ENVIRONMENTS

23.1 Regulations Cheat Sheet








Regulation
Scope
ML-relevant requirements




GDPR (EU)
Personal data of EU residents
Lawful basis; data minimization; right to access/erasure; right to explanation for automated decisions (Art. 22)


HIPAA (US)
Protected health info
Encryption, audit logs, BAAs, access controls


SOC 2 (US)
Service org controls
Security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, privacy


ISO 27001
Information security mgmt
Risk assessments, control catalog


PCI DSS
Cardholder data
Strong segmentation, key mgmt, no PAN in logs


CCPA / CPRA (CA)
Personal info
Opt-out, deletion


EU AI Act (2024)
AI systems
Risk-tier classification (minimal, limited, high, unacceptable); for high-risk: risk management, data governance, technical documentation, transparency, human oversight, accuracy/robustness, post-market monitoring


NYC Local Law 144
AEDT in hiring
Independent bias audits, public reporting


FCRA / ECOA (US)
Credit decisions
Adverse action notices; reason codes





23.2 Model Cards (Mitchell et al., 2019)
A standardized doc per model. Sections: - Model details (architecture, version, owner) - Intended use (and out-of-scope uses) - Factors (relevant slices, performance variation) - Metrics (across slices) - Evaluation data - Training data - Quantitative analyses - Ethical considerations - Caveats and recommendations


23.3 Data Sheets for Datasets (Gebru et al., 2018)
Companion to model cards: collection process, composition, preprocessing, recommended uses, distribution constraints.


23.4 Governance Stack







Concern
Practice




Model risk management
Inventory of all models; risk tier; periodic review


Approval workflow
Multi-stakeholder gate for high-risk deployments


Lineage
End-to-end trace: data → features → training → model → deploy → predictions


Audit logs
Tamper-evident logs of training, promotion, rollback events


Access control
RBAC + ABAC; least privilege; separation of duties


Data residency
Region-pinned storage and compute


Encryption
At rest (KMS, customer-managed keys) + in transit (TLS 1.2+/1.3, mTLS)


PII handling
Tokenization, hashing, masking, differential privacy


Right-to-erasure
Deletion through training data and model retraining if necessary





23.5 Explainability for Compliance
For high-risk systems: - Global: feature importances, partial dependence per slice. - Local: SHAP/LIME-style explanations per decision. - Counterfactuals for adverse-action notices (“If income were $X higher, decision would change”). - Store reason codes alongside predictions for audit.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 24: SECURITY FOR ML SYSTEMS

24.1 Threat Model







Category
Example




Confidentiality
Model stealing via query, training data extraction


Integrity
Data poisoning, adversarial examples, backdoor triggers


Availability
Inference flooding, prompt injection storms (for LLMs)


Supply chain
Compromised dependency, model weight tampering


Insider
Exfiltration via notebooks, dataset leaks





24.2 Secret Management

HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Azure Key Vault, SOPS.
Inject secrets at runtime (env vars, files), never bake into images.
Rotate; audit access.



24.3 Identity, Network, Image

IAM: least privilege; service identities (workload identity / IRSA on EKS); short-lived tokens; no static keys.
Network: VPC + private endpoints; NetworkPolicies; mTLS between services; PrivateLink for SaaS dependencies.
Images: signed (cosign), pinned by digest, scanned (Trivy), SBOM (Syft), provenance (SLSA), admission control (Kyverno/OPA/Gatekeeper).



24.4 Reproducible Builds and Supply-Chain

Use lock files everywhere (Poetry, npm-shrinkwrap).
Build in hermetic environments (Bazel, Nix) where possible.
Mirror upstream package indexes to a private repo to defend against typosquatting.
Verify dependency provenance (Sigstore).



24.5 ML-Specific Attacks and Defenses







Attack
Defense




Data poisoning
Anomaly detection on training data; robust aggregation in federated learning; provenance of training data


Adversarial examples
Adversarial training; certified defenses; input validation


Model inversion / membership inference
Differential privacy; reduce overfitting; limit query rates


Model stealing
Rate limiting; output truncation; watermarking


Backdoor / trojan
Activation clustering; spectral signatures; trusted training data only


Prompt injection (LLMs)
Input sanitization; structured outputs; tool/agent permissioning; allow-listing





24.6 Disaster Recovery & Reliability

Backups: data lake snapshots, registry exports, config in git.
RPO/RTO targets: e.g., RPO 1 h, RTO 4 h.
Multi-region: active-active or active-passive replication.
Failover drills quarterly.
Chaos testing: kill pods, throttle networks, simulate region loss.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
MODULE 25: COST OPTIMIZATION

25.1 Compute

Spot / preemptible for training and (carefully) for inference behind a queue.
Fractional GPUs / MIG for low-utilization workloads.
Right-sizing: profile actual GPU util (DCGM / nvidia-smi), CPU, memory — eliminate waste.
Autoscale to zero for low-traffic endpoints (KServe, Cloud Run).
Batch where possible: schedule heavy compute in off-peak.



25.2 Inference

Quantize (INT8/FP16) and distill for production.
Multi-model endpoints to consolidate idle capacity.
Dynamic batching to maximize GPU utilization.
Cache embeddings/features/responses for hot paths.



25.3 Storage

Tiered storage (hot → warm → cold/glacier) for data lakes.
TTL on logs, predictions, and experiment artifacts.
Deduplicate datasets (content-hash storage in DVC/lakeFS).
Compress (zstd) and columnize (Parquet) — orders of magnitude wins.



25.4 FinOps for ML

Tag every resource (team, model, env).
Per-model cost dashboards: GPU-hours, GBs, $/1k predictions.
Set budgets and alerts; chargeback / showback to product teams.
Make engineers cost-aware via PR comments (“this image is 12 GB; smaller base?”).

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
APPENDIX A: GLOSSARY







Term
Definition




CT
Continuous Training — automated retraining triggered by code/data/model events


CD
Continuous Delivery / Deployment — automated release of code and models


CACE
Changing Anything Changes Everything — entanglement in ML systems


Drift
Change in statistical properties of inputs or input-output relationship over time


Train–Serve Skew
Discrepancy between training and serving feature distributions/values


Feature Store
Central repository for features with offline (training) and online (serving) stores


Model Registry
Catalog of trained model versions with stages and metadata


Shadow
New model receives prod traffic but its predictions are not used


Canary
Gradual rollout of new version to a small fraction first


Blue/Green
Two parallel environments with router-level switchover


SLO / SLI / SLA
Objective / Indicator / Agreement for service quality


PSI
Population Stability Index — drift metric


KS
Kolmogorov–Smirnov — drift test for continuous features


MMD
Maximum Mean Discrepancy — multivariate drift test


DDM / ADWIN
Concept drift detectors


Model Card
Standardized documentation of a model


Data Sheet
Standardized documentation of a dataset


MIG
Multi-Instance GPU partitioning


KServe
Kubernetes-native model serving (was KFServing)


Triton
NVIDIA inference server


BentoML
Python-first model packaging and serving


Feast / Tecton / Hopsworks
Feature stores


DVC / lakeFS / Delta
Data versioning systems


TFX
TensorFlow Extended — Google’s MLOps platform


MLflow
OSS tracking, registry, deployment


OpenLineage / Marquez
Lineage standard and reference impl


CML
Continuous Machine Learning (Iterative) — CI reports for ML

Pipeline orchestrators


OTel
OpenTelemetry — vendor-neutral telemetry


GE / Pandera / TFDV
Data validation tools


EvidentlyAI / Arize / Whylabs / Fiddler / NannyML
Production monitoring tools


PII / PHI / PCI
Sensitive data types (personal / health / payment)


Right-to-erasure
GDPR right to have personal data deleted


EU AI Act risk tiers
Unacceptable / High / Limited / Minimal risk categories

title: MLOps Fundamentals
section: APPENDIX B: ML TEST SCORE CHECKLIST (BRECK ET AL.)
text:
APPENDIX B: ML TEST SCORE CHECKLIST (BRECK ET AL.)
Score each item 0/0.5/1; sum the score across four categories.
Tests for features and data: 1. Feature expectations are captured in a schema. 2. All features are beneficial (ablation tested). 3. No feature is too expensive vs its benefit. 4. Features comply with meta-level requirements (privacy, regulatory). 5. The data pipeline has appropriate privacy controls. 6. New features can be added quickly. 7. All input feature code is tested.
Tests for model development: 1. Every model spec is reviewed and checked in. 2. Offline and online metrics correlate. 3. All hyperparameters are tuned. 4. Effect of model staleness is known. 5. A simpler baseline is not better. 6. Model quality is sufficient on important data slices. 7. The model is tested for inclusion (fairness).
Tests for ML infrastructure: 1. Training is reproducible. 2. Model specs are unit-tested. 3. ML pipeline is integration-tested. 4. Model quality is validated before serving. 5. The model is debuggable. 6. Models can be quickly rolled back.
Monitoring tests: 1. Dependency changes result in notification. 2. Data invariants hold in inputs. 3. Training and serving features compute the same values. 4. Models are not too stale. 5. The model is numerically stable. 6. Computing performance has not regressed. 7. Prediction quality has not regressed.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
section: APPENDIX D: A REFERENCE LEVEL-2 STACK (EXAMPLE)
text:
APPENDIX D: A REFERENCE LEVEL-2 STACK (EXAMPLE)
A coherent, opinionated Level-2 stack you could ship today:

Source: GitHub + monorepo (or polyrepo with shared libs).
Code CI: GitHub Actions; ruff, mypy, pytest, pre-commit.
Data versioning: DVC backed by S3; data tests via Great Expectations.
Pipeline orchestration: Prefect (or Airflow); KFP for K8s-native training.
Training compute: K8s + GPU operator + Karpenter for spot autoscaling.
Experiment tracking + registry: MLflow (self-hosted on K8s) + Postgres + S3.
Feature store: Feast (offline: BigQuery; online: Redis).
Serving: KServe with Triton runtime; LLMs on vLLM.
CI for models: GitHub Actions + CML; performance + behavioral + fairness tests.
Release: Argo Rollouts (canary + blue/green) with auto-rollback on Prometheus SLOs.
Monitoring: Prometheus + Grafana for infra; EvidentlyAI + NannyML for drift/performance; Arize for embedding drift; OpenTelemetry + Tempo for traces; Loki for logs.
Alerting: PagerDuty; runbooks in Backstage; incident retros in linear/Jira.
Governance: Model cards, data sheets, model registry approvals; OpenLineage to DataHub.
Security: Vault for secrets; cosign + Kyverno for image admission; Trivy for scans; Workload Identity for IAM.
Cost: Karpenter spot; KEDA + KServe scale-to-zero; per-team cost tags; FinOps dashboard.

title: MLOps Fundamentals
section: APPENDIX E: RUNBOOK TEMPLATE FOR AN ML MODEL

title: MLOps Fundamentals
text:
APPENDIX F: KEY EQUATIONS (REVIEW SHEET)

PSI: \(\text{PSI} = \sum_i (p_i - q_i)\,\ln(p_i/q_i)\)
KS statistic: \(D = \sup_x |F_p(x) - F_q(x)|\)
Chi-square: \(\chi^2 = \sum_i (O_i - E_i)^2 / E_i\)
KL: \(D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p\,\|\,q) = \sum_i p_i \ln(p_i/q_i)\)
JS: \(\mathrm{JSD}(p,q) = \tfrac{1}{2}D_{\mathrm{KL}}(p\,\|\,m) + \tfrac{1}{2}D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q\,\|\,m), \; m=\tfrac{1}{2}(p+q)\)
Wasserstein-1 (1D): \(W_1(p,q) = \int |F_p(x) - F_q(x)|\,dx\)
MMD\(^2\): \(\mathbb{E}_p[k(x,x')] + \mathbb{E}_q[k(y,y')] - 2\mathbb{E}_{p,q}[k(x,y)]\)
A/B sample size: \(n \approx 2(z_{1-\alpha/2}+z_{1-\beta})^2 \sigma^2 / \Delta^2\)
Expected Calibration Error: \(\mathrm{ECE} = \sum_{b=1}^{B} \frac{|S_b|}{N}\,\bigl|\mathrm{acc}(S_b) - \mathrm{conf}(S_b)\bigr|\)
Brier score: \(\mathrm{BS} = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(p_i - y_i)^2\)
Drift score (NannyML CBPE estimate of accuracy without labels) — see NannyML docs.
Error budget: \(\text{EB} = 1 - \text{SLO}\); burn rate \(= (\text{observed error rate}) / (\text{error budget})\).

title: MLOps Fundamentals
section: APPENDIX H: SELF-TEST PROMPTS (FOR YOUR OWN REVIEW)
text:
APPENDIX H: SELF-TEST PROMPTS (FOR YOUR OWN REVIEW)
Open-ended prompts to confirm you can teach the topic without notes:

Explain the difference between covariate shift, label shift, and concept drift, with a concrete production example of each.
Walk through the end-to-end CT pipeline for a churn model triggered by drift. What guards prevent a bad model from reaching prod?
Why does Google define Level 0/1/2 of MLOps maturity? What does moving from Level 1 to Level 2 actually change?
Compare PSI vs KS vs Wasserstein vs JS — when do you reach for each?
Describe train–serve skew, give three causes, and three mitigations.
How do you roll back a model in ≤5 minutes? What systems need to know?
What goes into a model card, and why does the EU AI Act make them de-facto mandatory for high-risk systems?
Explain dynamic batching in Triton: the parameters, the tradeoffs, and how it interacts with p99 latency.
Why is a feature store the canonical answer to Rule #29?
Sketch a Kubernetes deployment for a GPU inference service with HPA, readiness/liveness probes, secrets, and resource limits.
What is the ML Test Score, and which categories tend to be weakest in early-stage teams?
Describe the canary release procedure for a high-stakes model: traffic steps, observation windows, rollback criteria.
Discuss edge-specific MLOps constraints for a phone-deployed vision model and the OTA update strategy.
Differentiate DVC, lakeFS, Delta Lake, and Pachyderm; pick one for a 100 TB image dataset and justify.
Explain hidden feedback loops with a recommender example, and how exploration/holdouts mitigate them.
Give a Prometheus alert for drift and another for latency; what is an error budget burn rate?
Outline the incident response runbook for a production fraud model whose recall drops 5 pp overnight.
Why and how do you seed everything in PyTorch, and what residual sources of non-determinism remain?
Explain CACE and three architectural patterns that mitigate it.
Compare MLflow Model Registry and W&B Artifacts; when would you pick one over the other?

If you can answer all twenty cleanly with examples, you are well above the bar for an expert MLOps assessment.

End of MLOps Fundamentals study guide.

title: Neural Accelerator Technology
section: MODULE 1: NUMBER FORMATS, WHY WE QUANTIZE, AND THE ARITHMETIC OF LOW PRECISION
text:
MODULE 1: NUMBER FORMATS, WHY WE QUANTIZE, AND THE ARITHMETIC OF LOW PRECISION

1.1 Why quantize at all?
Deep models are trained in 32-bit floating point (FP32). Each weight occupies 4 bytes, each activation 4 bytes, every multiply-accumulate (MAC) is an FP32 operation. For a 7-billion-parameter LLM that means 28 GB just for weights, before you even consider activations, KV-cache, optimizer state, gradient buffers, or batch dimension. On an edge SoC with 4 GB of LPDDR4 and a 50 mW power envelope this is hopeless.
Quantization replaces FP32 numbers by integers (typically INT8, INT4 or even INT2) or by lower-precision floating point (FP16, BF16, FP8, FP4). The objective function of quantization is roughly:
\[
\min_{Q} \; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}}\big[\, \mathcal{L}(f_{Q}(x), y) - \mathcal{L}(f_{FP32}(x), y) \,\big] \quad \text{subject to memory, latency, and energy budgets.}
\]
Quantization touches four simultaneous benefits, and you should be able to enumerate all of them on demand:

Memory footprint — INT8 is 4× smaller than FP32; INT4 is 8× smaller. This matters both for storage (on-disk model size) and for runtime DRAM/SRAM occupancy.
Memory bandwidth — modern inference is memory-bound. If you can stream half as many bytes from HBM/DRAM to compute, you double effective throughput. Roofline analysis: more arithmetic intensity per byte loaded.
Compute throughput — INT8 tensor cores on NVIDIA Ampere give 4× the TOPS of FP16; Hopper FP8 Tensor Cores give another 2×. Edge accelerators (Edge TPU, Hexagon HVX, Apple Neural Engine) are typically integer-only — FP simply does not execute.
Energy per inference — energy is dominated by data movement. A 32-bit DRAM access costs roughly 640 pJ while a 32-bit FP MAC costs ~3.7 pJ and an 8-bit INT MAC ~0.2 pJ (Horowitz, ISSCC 2014). Quantization is the single biggest lever on battery life.


Reference: Jay Alammar’s “A Visual Guide to Quantization” and Lilian Weng’s “Large Transformer Model Inference Optimization” lay out these motivations beautifully — read them.
