title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 10.3: Instruction Tuning & SFT
Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on (instruction, response) pairs teaches a base LM to follow instructions.
Public datasets:

FLAN / FLAN-T5 / FLAN-Collection — instruction templates over hundreds of NLP tasks.
Self-Instruct (Wang et al. 2022) — bootstrap instructions from a teacher LLM.
Alpaca (52k examples generated by text-davinci-003).
Dolly, OpenAssistant (OASST), UltraChat, WizardLM, Orca.

Templates: ChatML (<|im_start|>system ... <|im_end|>), LLaMA-2 chat ([INST] ... [/INST]), Alpaca (### Instruction: ... ### Response:).

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 10.4: RLHF — Ouyang et al. (InstructGPT) 2022
Three stages:

SFT — fine-tune base on human demonstrations.
Reward model — train \(r_\phi(x, y)\) on pairwise human preferences with the Bradley-Terry loss:

\[
\mathcal{L}_{\text{RM}} = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l) \sim D}\!\left[ \log \sigma\big( r_\phi(x, y_w) - r_\phi(x, y_l) \big) \right].
\]

PPO (Schulman et al. 2017) optimizes the policy \(\pi_\theta\) against the RM, with a per-token KL penalty from the SFT reference \(\pi_\text{ref}\) to prevent reward hacking:

\[
\max_\theta \mathbb{E}_{x \sim D, y \sim \pi_\theta}\!\left[ r_\phi(x, y) - \beta \, \mathrm{KL}\!\big[\pi_\theta(\cdot|x) \,\|\, \pi_\text{ref}(\cdot|x)\big] \right].
\]
PPO uses the clipped surrogate objective
\[
\mathcal{L}^{\text{CLIP}} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \min\!\big(r_t(\theta) \hat{A}_t,\; \text{clip}(r_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) \hat{A}_t \big) \right],
\]
with \(r_t(\theta) = \pi_\theta(a_t|s_t) / \pi_{\theta_\text{old}}(a_t|s_t)\) and advantage \(\hat{A}_t\).
RLAIF (Bai et al. 2022, “Constitutional AI”) replaces human preferences with AI-generated ones.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 10.5: DPO — Rafailov et al. 2023
Direct Preference Optimization avoids the RM and PPO entirely. Algebraic trick: the closed-form optimum of the RLHF objective is
\[
\pi^*(y|x) \propto \pi_\text{ref}(y|x) \exp\!\left( \frac{1}{\beta} r(x, y) \right),
\]
so \(r(x, y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y|x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y|x)} + \text{const}(x)\). Plug into Bradley-Terry:
\[
\mathcal{L}_\text{DPO} = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l)}\!\left[ \log \sigma\!\left( \beta \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_w|x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_w|x)} - \beta \log\frac{\pi_\theta(y_l|x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_l|x)} \right) \right].
\]
A simple cross-entropy on log-prob differences; no rollouts. Variants:

IPO (Azar et al.) — fixes DPO’s tendency to overfit pure preferences.
KTO (Ethayarajh) — uses Kahneman-Tversky utility; needs only binary good/bad signal.
ORPO (Hong et al.) — combines SFT and odds-ratio preference in one stage; no reference model.
SimPO — reference-free, length-normalized.
GRPO (DeepSeekMath / R1) — group-relative policy optimization for math/code RL.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 11.1: Decoding Algorithms

11.1.1 Greedy
\(y_t = \arg\max_w p(w | y_{<t}, x)\). Cheap, deterministic, locally optimal. Tends to be repetitive and bland.


11.1.2 Beam Search
Maintain top-\(k\) partial hypotheses by cumulative log-prob. Standard for translation and summarization. Pitfalls: bland, repetitive (“the the the”), and biased toward shorter sequences.

Length penalty (Wu et al. 2016): divide by \(((5 + |y|)/6)^\alpha\) with \(\alpha \in [0.6, 1.0]\).
Coverage penalty to discourage repeating same source positions in MT.
No-repeat-n-gram blocking: forbid generating any n-gram already produced.
Diverse beam search (Vijayakumar et al.): split beam into groups with a diversity penalty.



11.1.3 Sampling

Pure sampling: \(w \sim p(\cdot | y_{<t})\). High diversity, low coherence.
Temperature: \(p_T(w) \propto p(w)^{1/T}\). \(T < 1\) sharpens (more greedy-like), \(T > 1\) flattens, \(T = 0\) is greedy.
Top-k (Fan et al. 2018): renormalize over the \(k\) most probable tokens.
Top-p / nucleus (Holtzman et al. 2019): smallest set whose probabilities sum to \(\ge p\); renormalize. Adapts the set size to entropy of the distribution.
Typical sampling (Meister et al.): sample tokens with probability close to expected information content.
Min-p sampling: only tokens with probability above \(p \cdot \max p\) are kept.
Repetition penalty (Keskar et al. 2019 — CTRL): divide the logit of recently seen tokens by \(\rho > 1\).



11.1.4 Constrained Decoding

Beam search with constraints (forbidden / required tokens).
Grammar-constrained decoding (e.g., llama.cpp JSON grammar, outlines).
Logit biasing (OpenAI API logit_bias).
Guided decoding with regex / DFA.



11.1.5 Speculative Decoding
Run a small draft model to propose \(k\) tokens; the target model verifies them in a single forward pass and accepts a prefix. 2–3× speedup with no quality loss (Leviathan et al. 2023; Chen et al. 2023). Variants: Medusa, EAGLE, lookahead decoding.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 12.1: Classification & Tagging Metrics
Already covered: accuracy, precision, recall, F1, macro/micro/weighted, MCC, ROC-AUC, PR-AUC. For sequence tagging, evaluate at the span/entity level with seqeval.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 12.2: Machine Translation — BLEU
BLEU (Papineni et al. 2002) — modified n-gram precision with brevity penalty.

Modified n-gram precision: count clipped matches (each reference n-gram contributes at most its reference count).

\[
p_n = \frac{\sum_{C} \sum_{\text{n-gram} \in C} \text{Count}_\text{clip}(\text{n-gram})}{\sum_{C} \sum_{\text{n-gram} \in C} \text{Count}(\text{n-gram})}.
\]

Brevity Penalty to discourage too-short outputs:

\[
\text{BP} = \begin{cases} 1 & c > r \\ \exp(1 - r/c) & c \le r \end{cases}
\]
with \(c\) = candidate length, \(r\) = closest-reference length.

BLEU-N (usually \(N=4\)):

\[
\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\left( \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n \right), \quad w_n = 1/N.
\]
Important caveats:

BLEU is computed on a corpus, not single sentences (sentence-BLEU is noisy).
BLEU between systems is comparable only with the same tokenizer; sacreBLEU standardizes this.
BLEU correlates weakly with human judgment for high-quality MT; modern metrics (COMET, chrF) are preferred.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 12.3: Summarization — ROUGE (Lin 2004)

ROUGE-N: n-gram recall of reference n-grams found in the candidate.
ROUGE-L: longest common subsequence (LCS) based F-measure.
ROUGE-Lsum: applies LCS per sentence, sums and normalizes.
ROUGE-W: weighted LCS, favors consecutive matches.

\[
\text{ROUGE-N}_\text{recall} = \frac{\sum_{S \in R} \sum_{g_n \in S} \text{Count}_\text{match}(g_n)}{\sum_{S \in R} \sum_{g_n \in S} \text{Count}(g_n)}.
\]
By default ROUGE is recall-oriented; F-measure variants combine recall and precision.
from datasets import load_metric
rouge = load_metric("rouge")
rouge.compute(predictions=["the cat sat"], references=["a cat sat on the mat"])

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 12.4: Other Generation Metrics

METEOR (Banerjee & Lavie) — unigram F-mean with stemming, synonymy (WordNet), word order penalty. Better human correlation than BLEU.
chrF / chrF++ (Popović) — character n-gram F-score; robust across languages, no tokenizer dependency.
TER — Translation Edit Rate (insertions + deletions + substitutions + shifts).
BERTScore (Zhang et al. 2020) — token-level cosine similarity between candidate and reference BERT embeddings; precision/recall/F1 over best matches.
MoverScore — earth-mover’s distance over BERT embeddings.
BLEURT (Sellam et al.) — learned BLEU; BERT fine-tuned on synthetic data + human ratings.
COMET (Rei et al. 2020) — cross-lingual reference-based; trained on Direct Assessment.
GEMBA / G-Eval / LLM-as-a-judge — use GPT-4 etc. to rate outputs (faster than humans, must be calibrated against humans).

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 12.5: Language Modeling — Perplexity
\[
\text{PPL}(W) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(w_i | w_{<i}) \right) = \exp(\text{cross-entropy}).
\]
Lower = better. Caveats:

Comparable only when tokenization is the same.
Drops automatically when vocabulary shrinks (use bits-per-byte instead for cross-tokenizer comparison).
Token-level PPL does not predict downstream task performance perfectly.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 12.6: Retrieval & Ranking

MRR — Mean Reciprocal Rank: \(\frac{1}{Q} \sum_i \frac{1}{\text{rank}_i}\).
Recall@k — fraction of queries whose relevant doc is in top-k.
Precision@k, MAP@k — averaged precision.
NDCG@k — Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain:

\[
\text{DCG@k} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{2^{\text{rel}_i} - 1}{\log_2(i + 1)},\quad \text{NDCG@k} = \text{DCG@k} / \text{IDCG@k}.
\]

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 12.7: QA, Reasoning, Code

Exact Match (EM) and token-level F1 (SQuAD).
HumanEval / MBPP: pass@k = expected probability that at least one of \(k\) samples passes all unit tests. Unbiased estimator: \(\text{pass@k} = 1 - \binom{n - c}{k} / \binom{n}{k}\) with \(n\) samples, \(c\) correct.
MMLU / BBH / ARC / GSM8K / MATH — accuracy on multi-choice or numeric answers.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 12.8: Error Analysis & Behavioral Testing

Confusion matrix dive — which class pairs collapse?
Slice analysis — break F1 by entity length, domain, capitalization, sequence length.
SliceLine / DOMINO — automated slice discovery.
CheckList (Ribeiro et al. 2020) — behavioral tests:

MFT (Minimum Functionality Test) — simple sanity (negation flips sentiment).
INV (Invariance) — paraphrase shouldn’t change label.
DIR (Directional Expectation) — adding “not” should flip sentiment.

Contrast sets (Gardner et al. 2020) — manually perturbed test examples.
Adversarial sets — ANLI, AdvGLUE, HANS — diagnostic for shortcut learning.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 13.1: Attention Visualization
Attention weights \(\alpha_{ij}\) are often displayed as heatmaps or token-to-token arcs (BertViz). They show what the model attends to.
Important debate:

“Attention is not Explanation” (Jain & Wallace 2019) — attention weights are not unique; alternate attentions can produce the same prediction. Therefore attention is not a faithful explanation.
“Attention is not not Explanation” (Wiegreffe & Pinter 2019) — counter-argument: alternate attentions are hard to find without breaking the model, so the original attention does explain the model as trained.

Practical advice: treat attention as a hypothesis-generating view, not a faithful explanation. Validate with perturbation, ablation, or attribution.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 13.2: Gradient-Based Attribution

Vanilla gradient \(\partial f(x) / \partial x_i\). Noisy.
Input × Gradient \(x_i \cdot \partial f / \partial x_i\). A first-order Taylor expansion at zero.
SmoothGrad — average gradients over Gaussian-perturbed inputs.
Integrated Gradients (Sundararajan et al. 2017):

\[
\text{IG}_i(x) = (x_i - x'_i) \int_0^1 \frac{\partial f(x' + \alpha (x - x'))}{\partial x_i} d\alpha,
\]
approximated by Riemann sum over \(\sim 50\) steps. Axioms: completeness (\(\sum_i \text{IG}_i = f(x) - f(x')\)), sensitivity, implementation invariance. Choosing a baseline \(x'\) (e.g., all-zero embeddings or a [PAD] sequence) matters.

Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP) — propagate relevance backward through the network using conservation rules.

from captum.attr import IntegratedGradients
ig = IntegratedGradients(model)
attributions, delta = ig.attribute(inputs=input_ids, target=label,
                                   baselines=baseline_ids, return_convergence_delta=True)

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 13.3: Perturbation Methods

LIME (Ribeiro et al. 2016) — locally fit a sparse linear model on perturbed inputs (words turned on/off). Easy to use, sometimes unstable.
SHAP for text (KernelSHAP, PartitionSHAP) — Shapley values over tokens or token groups.
Occlusion — drop or mask each token, measure change in output.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 13.4: Probing
Train a small classifier (a probe) on frozen representations to test what they encode (POS, dependency, NER, syntax tree depth, factual knowledge). Hewitt & Manning’s structural probe recovers dependency trees from BERT hidden states. Control tasks (Hewitt & Liang 2019) check that the probe isn’t learning the task itself.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 13.5: Mechanistic Interpretability

Circuit analysis (Anthropic Transformer Circuits) — find subnetworks responsible for specific behaviors (induction heads, indirect object identification).
Activation patching / causal tracing (Meng et al. ROME) — patch activations from a “clean” run into a “corrupt” run to localize where information lives.
Logit lens (nostalgebraist) — apply the unembedding directly to intermediate hidden states; visualize what the model “would say” at each layer.
Sparse autoencoders (Anthropic, OpenAI) — decompose activations into mono-semantic features.

title: Natural Language Processing
section: MODULE 14.1: Monitoring NLP Systems in Production
text:
MODULE 14.1: Monitoring NLP Systems in Production
Four kinds of drift to track:

Data / input drift — the distribution of inputs changes. Detect with:

Population Stability Index (PSI) on summary stats (length, vocab coverage, language ID).
Kolmogorov–Smirnov / Anderson–Darling on continuous features.
Chi-square on categorical features.
Embedding drift — mean and covariance of input embeddings; MMD (Maximum Mean Discrepancy) on samples.

Concept drift — the relationship \(P(y | x)\) changes (e.g., slang shifts, new product names, new entities). Detect via performance monitoring on labeled samples or proxy metrics (e.g., user thumbs-down rates).
Output / prediction drift — distribution of predictions changes. Detect with histograms, KL divergence between current and reference output distributions.
Latency / cost drift — token-per-second, p50/p95/p99, GPU utilization.

PSI:
\[
\text{PSI} = \sum_i (p_i - q_i) \ln(p_i / q_i),
\]
over \(i\) bins of feature distribution. Rule of thumb: \(<0.1\) stable, \(0.1\)–\(0.25\) slight shift, \(>0.25\) major shift.

14.1.1 Detection Toolkits

Evidently AI, Arize, WhyLabs, Fiddler, NannyML — purpose-built model monitoring.
Alibi-Detect — drift, outlier, adversarial detection.
Prometheus + Grafana for metrics; OpenTelemetry for traces.



14.1.2 Retraining Strategies

Periodic retraining (weekly / monthly) on rolling windows.
Triggered retraining when drift exceeds a threshold or performance drops.
Online / incremental — partial fits on streaming data (river, vw).
Continual / lifelong learning — beware catastrophic forgetting; use rehearsal, EWC, LwF, or replay buffers.



14.1.3 Deployment Patterns

Shadow deployment — new model runs alongside old; outputs logged but not served.
Canary release — small % of traffic routed to new model; ramp up if healthy.
A/B testing — split traffic, measure metrics, statistical significance.
Blue-green — two identical envs, swap pointer.
Multi-armed bandits — adaptively route to best-performing model.
Feature flags for instant rollback.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 14.2: Privacy & Security

14.2.1 PII Redaction

Microsoft Presidio — pluggable analyzers (regex, spaCy NER, custom recognizers) + anonymizers (redact, replace, mask, hash, encrypt).
AWS Comprehend / Google DLP — managed services.
Roll-your-own with regex for emails, phones, SSNs + an NER for names.

from presidio_analyzer import AnalyzerEngine
from presidio_anonymizer import AnonymizerEngine
text = "John lives at 5th Ave, his email is john@example.com."
analyzer = AnalyzerEngine()
results = analyzer.analyze(text=text, language="en")
print(AnonymizerEngine().anonymize(text=text, analyzer_results=results).text)


14.2.2 Differential Privacy
A randomized algorithm \(\mathcal{A}\) is \((\varepsilon, \delta)\)-DP if for any neighboring datasets \(D, D'\) differing in one record, and any output set \(S\):
\[
P[\mathcal{A}(D) \in S] \le e^\varepsilon \cdot P[\mathcal{A}(D') \in S] + \delta.
\]
In ML, DP-SGD (Abadi et al. 2016) clips per-example gradients to \(C\) and adds Gaussian noise \(\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 C^2 I)\). Smaller \(\varepsilon\) = more privacy = more noise = worse utility. Track privacy budget with Rényi DP / moments accountant (Opacus, TensorFlow Privacy).


14.2.3 Federated Learning
Clients train locally on private data; only model updates are sent to a server. FedAvg (McMahan et al. 2017) averages updates. Combine with secure aggregation and DP for stronger guarantees.


14.2.4 Model & Data Security

Encryption — TLS in transit; KMS-managed keys at rest.
Access control — IAM, least privilege, audit logs.
Secret scanning — trufflehog, gitleaks in CI.
Prompt-injection defenses — input filtering, output filtering, defensive prompts, allowlists, tool-use sandboxing.
Jailbreak detection — LlamaGuard, Prompt Guard, output classifiers.
Model extraction defenses — rate limiting, output watermarking, query-cost-aware throttling.
Membership inference — DP and regularization reduce leakage.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 15.1: Where Bias Enters







Stage
Mechanism




Data collection
Over-/under-representation of demographics


Annotation
Annotator demographics, prescriptive vs descriptive labels


Preprocessing
Stripping dialectal features, name normalization


Modeling
Capacity differences across groups, shortcut learning


Decoding
Mode collapse to majority


Evaluation
Benchmarks underrepresenting minorities


Deployment
Feedback loops

title: Natural Language Processing
section: MODULE 15.2: Diagnosing Bias in Embeddings & Representations
text:
MODULE 15.2: Diagnosing Bias in Embeddings & Representations

Analogy probing (Bolukbasi 2016): “man : programmer :: woman : ?” — uncovered “homemaker” in word2vec.
WEAT (Word Embedding Association Test) (Caliskan et al. 2017) — IAT analog: compute mean cosine of target words (“programmer”, “engineer”) with attribute words (“man”, “he”) vs (“woman”, “she”). Permutation test for significance.
SEAT (May et al. 2019) — sentence-level WEAT.
StereoSet (Nadeem et al.) and CrowS-Pairs (Nangia et al.) — pairs designed to reveal stereotype preferences.
WinoBias / WinoGender (Zhao et al. 2018) — coreference resolution test for gender bias around occupations.
BBQ (Parrish et al.) — bias benchmark for QA.
Distributional fairness — measure performance gaps across demographic slices (gender, race, dialect, country).

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 15.3: Bias Mitigation

15.3.1 Pre-processing

Balanced sampling / reweighing to equalize representation.
Counterfactual Data Augmentation (CDA) — swap gendered words (“he/she”, “him/her”, “doctor/nurse”) and re-train.
Data cleaning — remove or rewrite biased sources; document-level filtering.
Synthetic data — generate examples to fill demographic gaps.



15.3.2 In-processing

Hard debiasing (Bolukbasi 2016) — identify a “gender direction” via PCA on gendered pairs, project gender-neutral words orthogonal to it. Caveat: Gonen & Goldberg (2019) show bias persists in clustering.
Soft debiasing — minimize gender direction component while preserving similarity.
Iterative Null-space Projection (INLP) (Ravfogel et al. 2020) — train a probe to predict the protected attribute; project representations onto its null-space; repeat until probes fail.
Adversarial debiasing (Zhang et al. 2018) — predictor + adversary trying to recover protected attribute; encoder trained to fool adversary.
Fair regularization — add penalty for performance gap across groups.



15.3.3 Post-processing

Calibrated equalized odds — adjust thresholds per group.
Reject option classification — defer to human when near decision boundary for protected group.
Output rewriting — paraphraser to neutralize biased generations.



15.3.4 Fairness Metrics

Demographic parity (statistical parity): \(P(\hat{Y} = 1 | A = 0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 | A = 1)\).
Equalized odds: \(P(\hat{Y} = 1 | Y = y, A = 0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 | Y = y, A = 1)\) for \(y \in \{0, 1\}\).
Equal opportunity: equalized odds restricted to \(y = 1\).
Predictive parity / calibration: \(P(Y = 1 | \hat{Y} = 1, A = a)\) equal across groups.
Counterfactual fairness (Kusner et al.) — predictions same under counterfactual change of protected attribute.
Impossibility theorem (Chouldechova 2017; Kleinberg et al. 2016): except in degenerate cases, calibration, equal FPR, and equal FNR cannot all hold simultaneously across groups.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
MODULE 15.4: LLM-Specific Harms & Mitigations

Toxicity / hate — RealToxicityPrompts, Perspective API, ToxiGen. Mitigations: data filtering, RLHF, post-hoc classifier, system prompts.
Hallucination — retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), uncertainty calibration, “I don’t know” training, citations, self-consistency.
Misinformation — RAG, fact-checking, watermarking, content provenance (C2PA).
Privacy leakage — Carlini et al. show LMs memorize training data; mitigate with deduplication, DP-SGD, filtering, output classifier.
Adversarial attacks — see Lilian Weng’s “Adversarial Attacks on LLMs”. Categories:

Jailbreaks — Do-Anything-Now, persona injection, role-play.
Prompt injection — direct (user input) and indirect (poisoned tool output / RAG corpus).
Token smuggling / encoding attacks — base64, leet-speak, low-resource languages.
Multi-turn escalation.
Defenses — output safety classifier, dual-LLM (privileged + unprivileged), structured outputs, tool sandboxing, “spotlighting” (mark untrusted content), allowlists, content filters.

Provenance & accountability — model cards (Mitchell et al. 2019), system cards, third-party audits, red-teaming.

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
D.1 Fine-Tuning DistilBERT on SST-2 (Sentiment)
from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import (AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification,
                          TrainingArguments, Trainer, DataCollatorWithPadding)
import evaluate, numpy as np

ds = load_dataset("glue", "sst2")
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("distilbert-base-uncased")
def prep(b): return tok(b["sentence"], truncation=True)
ds = ds.map(prep, batched=True)

model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    "distilbert-base-uncased", num_labels=2)

acc = evaluate.load("accuracy")
f1 = evaluate.load("f1")
def metrics(p):
    preds = np.argmax(p.predictions, axis=1)
    return {**acc.compute(predictions=preds, references=p.label_ids),
            **f1.compute(predictions=preds, references=p.label_ids, average="macro")}

args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="sst2-distilbert", learning_rate=2e-5, per_device_train_batch_size=32,
    per_device_eval_batch_size=64, num_train_epochs=3, weight_decay=0.01,
    eval_strategy="epoch", save_strategy="epoch", load_best_model_at_end=True,
    metric_for_best_model="accuracy")

trainer = Trainer(model=model, args=args, train_dataset=ds["train"],
                  eval_dataset=ds["validation"], tokenizer=tok,
                  data_collator=DataCollatorWithPadding(tok), compute_metrics=metrics)
trainer.train()
trainer.evaluate()

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
D.2 NER with transformers + seqeval
from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import (AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForTokenClassification,
                          TrainingArguments, Trainer, DataCollatorForTokenClassification)
from seqeval.metrics import f1_score, classification_report
import numpy as np

ds = load_dataset("conll2003")
labels = ds["train"].features["ner_tags"].feature.names
id2label = dict(enumerate(labels)); label2id = {l:i for i,l in id2label.items()}

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-cased")
def align(batch):
    enc = tok(batch["tokens"], truncation=True, is_split_into_words=True)
    new_labels = []
    for i, lab in enumerate(batch["ner_tags"]):
        word_ids = enc.word_ids(batch_index=i)
        ids = []
        prev = None
        for wid in word_ids:
            if wid is None: ids.append(-100)
            elif wid != prev: ids.append(lab[wid])
            else: ids.append(lab[wid] if id2label[lab[wid]].startswith("I-") else -100)
            prev = wid
        new_labels.append(ids)
    enc["labels"] = new_labels
    return enc

ds = ds.map(align, batched=True, remove_columns=ds["train"].column_names)

model = AutoModelForTokenClassification.from_pretrained(
    "bert-base-cased", num_labels=len(labels), id2label=id2label, label2id=label2id)

def metrics(p):
    preds = np.argmax(p.predictions, axis=2)
    true_labels = [[id2label[l] for l in lab if l != -100] for lab in p.label_ids]
    true_preds = [[id2label[pi] for pi, li in zip(pl, ll) if li != -100]
                  for pl, ll in zip(preds, p.label_ids)]
    return {"f1": f1_score(true_labels, true_preds)}

args = TrainingArguments(output_dir="ner-bert", learning_rate=3e-5, num_train_epochs=3,
                         per_device_train_batch_size=16, eval_strategy="epoch")
trainer = Trainer(model=model, args=args, train_dataset=ds["train"],
                  eval_dataset=ds["validation"], tokenizer=tok,
                  data_collator=DataCollatorForTokenClassification(tok),
                  compute_metrics=metrics)
trainer.train()

title: Natural Language Processing
section: D.3 LoRA Fine-Tune of a 7B Causal LM with QLoRA
text:
D.3 LoRA Fine-Tune of a 7B Causal LM with QLoRA
import torch
from transformers import (AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM,
                          BitsAndBytesConfig, TrainingArguments)
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, prepare_model_for_kbit_training
from trl import SFTTrainer
from datasets import load_dataset

bnb = BitsAndBytesConfig(load_in_4bit=True, bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
                        bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
                        bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16)

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf")
tok.pad_token = tok.eos_token

base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf", quantization_config=bnb, device_map="auto")
base = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(base)

cfg = LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=32, lora_dropout=0.05, bias="none",
                 task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
                 target_modules=["q_proj","k_proj","v_proj","o_proj",
                                 "gate_proj","up_proj","down_proj"])
model = get_peft_model(base, cfg)
model.print_trainable_parameters()

ds = load_dataset("yahma/alpaca-cleaned", split="train")
def fmt(ex):
    p = (f"### Instruction:\n{ex['instruction']}\n\n"
         + (f"### Input:\n{ex['input']}\n\n" if ex['input'] else "")
         + f"### Response:\n{ex['output']}")
    return {"text": p}
ds = ds.map(fmt)

args = TrainingArguments(output_dir="llama2-7b-alpaca-qlora",
                         per_device_train_batch_size=4, gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
                         num_train_epochs=1, learning_rate=2e-4,
                         bf16=True, logging_steps=20, save_strategy="epoch",
                         optim="paged_adamw_8bit")
trainer = SFTTrainer(model=model, train_dataset=ds, args=args,
                     tokenizer=tok, dataset_text_field="text", max_seq_length=1024)
trainer.train()

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
D.4 Sentence Embeddings + Cosine Retrieval
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util
import numpy as np

corpus = [
    "Cats are small carnivorous mammals.",
    "Python is a popular programming language.",
    "The Eiffel Tower is in Paris, France.",
    "Transformers revolutionized natural language processing.",
]
model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5")
emb_corpus = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True)
query = "Where can I find the Eiffel Tower?"
emb_q = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True)
scores = util.cos_sim(emb_q, emb_corpus)[0]
top = np.argsort(-scores)
for i in top[:3]:
    print(f"{scores[i]:.3f}  {corpus[i]}")

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
D.5 BLEU and ROUGE Quickref
import evaluate
bleu = evaluate.load("sacrebleu")
rouge = evaluate.load("rouge")

preds = ["the cat sat on the mat"]
refs  = [["the cat is sitting on the mat", "a cat sat on the mat"]]
print(bleu.compute(predictions=preds, references=refs)["score"])

preds_s = ["the cat sat on the mat"]
refs_s  = ["a cat sat on the mat"]
print(rouge.compute(predictions=preds_s, references=refs_s))

title: Natural Language Processing
text:
D.6 Drift Monitoring Skeleton
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import ks_2samp

def psi(reference, current, bins=10):
    breakpoints = np.linspace(0, 1, bins + 1)
    breakpoints[0], breakpoints[-1] = -np.inf, np.inf
    ref_hist, _ = np.histogram(reference, breakpoints)
    cur_hist, _ = np.histogram(current, breakpoints)
    ref_pct = ref_hist / max(ref_hist.sum(), 1)
    cur_pct = cur_hist / max(cur_hist.sum(), 1)
    eps = 1e-6
    return np.sum((cur_pct - ref_pct) * np.log((cur_pct + eps) / (ref_pct + eps)))

ref_lengths = np.random.normal(120, 30, 5000)        # training input lengths
prod_lengths = np.random.normal(140, 40, 1000)       # production input lengths
print("PSI:", psi(ref_lengths / 300, prod_lengths / 300))
print("KS p-value:", ks_2samp(ref_lengths, prod_lengths).pvalue)

title: LLMs for Engineers
text:
This document is a single, self-contained reference for the Large Language Models for Engineers domain. It is organized into six Parts that mirror the syllabus tree:

Preparing and Augmenting Data — where the tokens come from, how they are validated, structured, augmented, and tokenized.
Designing and Building LLM Architectures — attention, multi-head attention and its variants, transformer blocks, GPT-style decoders.
Adapting and Tuning Models — full fine-tuning, instruction tuning, parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA, QLoRA, DoRA, IA3, prefix / prompt / P-tuning, BitFit, adapters), RLHF, DPO, IPO, KTO, RLAIF, Constitutional AI, transfer learning.
Optimizing and Scaling Model Efficiency — quantization (PTQ, QAT, GPTQ, AWQ, SmoothQuant, NF4, FP8, bitsandbytes, GGUF), knowledge distillation, mixed precision, ZeRO, FSDP, tensor / pipeline / sequence / context / expert parallelism, FlashAttention, KV cache, paged attention, speculative decoding, continuous batching, vLLM, TensorRT-LLM.
Controlling and Generating Outputs — beam search, top-k, top-p (nucleus), temperature, typical sampling, min-p, repetition penalty, contrastive decoding, zero-shot / few-shot prompting, CoT, Self-Consistency, Tree-of-Thoughts, ReAct, Reflexion, program-of-thought, prompt chaining, function calling, tool use.
Evaluating and Interpreting Models — perplexity, BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR, BERTScore, exact match, F1, pass@k, MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval, MT-Bench, Arena Elo, judge-LLM evaluation, attention visualization, probing classifiers, induction heads, circuits, logit lens, tuned lens, sparse autoencoders.

Each Module contains:

A precise definition and motivation.
The full mechanism / algorithm explained step by step.
A clean mathematical formulation.
Concrete worked examples with small numbers.
Minimal-but-complete Python / PyTorch / HuggingFace code snippets the reader could rewrite from scratch.
Comparison tables.
Practical engineering considerations: memory, latency, hyperparameter ranges, failure modes, debugging tips.
“Common assessment traps” sidebars where vocabulary distinctions matter.

Three appendices at the back provide a glossary, a curated reading list of canonical papers and influential blog posts, and a 50-question self-test bank.
The goal is not breadth alone but the connections: tokenization choices propagate to data preparation, to vocabulary sizing, to embedding tables, to memory at inference time, to which decoding strategies even apply. Each part repeatedly references the others.

title: LLMs for Engineers
text:
MODULE 1 — Collecting and Validating Data

1.1 Scrape and Aggregate Cross-Domain Text Data
Definition and motivation. “Cross-domain text data” means a deliberate mixture of sources spanning the breadth of human language and knowledge — the open web, encyclopedic prose, books, scientific papers, code, dialogue, government data, and curated mid-size collections. LLMs generalize because their training corpus did. A model fed only news articles cannot reason about code, cannot answer math questions, cannot follow legal arguments. The first job of the data team is to assemble a balanced, deduplicated, quality-filtered corpus at the trillions-of-tokens scale.
The canonical public corpora to know by name:









Corpus
Size (tokens)
Source
Notes




Common Crawl
hundreds of TB raw, ~petabyte-scale archived
Open web crawl
Raw, very noisy; the starting point for everything web-based


C4 (Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus)
~750 GB / ~150 B tokens
Filtered Common Crawl
Used to train T5 (Raffel et al., 2020)


The Pile (EleutherAI)
825 GB / ~300 B tokens
22 curated subsets
Books, ArXiv, PubMed, GitHub, Stack Exchange, etc.


RedPajama
1.2 T tokens
Reproduces LLaMA 1 mix
Open replication of Meta’s mix


RefinedWeb
5 T tokens
Filtered Common Crawl
Used for Falcon


SlimPajama
627 B tokens
Deduplicated RedPajama
Aggressive cleaning


FineWeb / FineWeb-Edu
15 T / 1.3 T
Filtered Common Crawl
Used by HuggingFace; FineWeb-Edu filters for educational quality with a classifier


Dolma (AI2)
3 T tokens
Multi-source open
Transparent recipe used for OLMo


The Stack
6 TB code
GitHub permissively licensed
Used for StarCoder


CulturaX / mC4 / Multilingual C4
trillions
Multilingual
Hundreds of languages


arXiv, PubMed, S2ORC, USPTO
hundreds of GB each
Scientific / patents
Domain depth


Project Gutenberg, Books3 (retracted)
10–100 B
Books
Legally sensitive


Wikipedia dumps
~25 GB / ~5 B tokens per language
Encyclopedic
High quality reference


StackExchange, Reddit, HN dumps
tens of GB
Q&A / discussion
Conversational training value



Pipeline overview (end-to-end). Assembling a pretraining corpus is a series of stages, each of which can reject most of its input.
raw sources  →  text extraction  →  language ID  →  quality filtering  →  deduplication  →  PII / toxicity filtering  →  decontamination  →  domain mixing  →  tokenization  →  shuffling + sharding  →  training-ready tokens
Every arrow above is a 5–50× data reduction stage. A typical web-scale pipeline turns 10 PB of raw HTML into a few TB of clean text.
Stage-by-stage detail.
(a) Text extraction. HTML is converted to text. Tools: trafilatura, boilerpy3, jusText, resiliparse. The goal is to strip navigation, ads, footers, scripts, while keeping the main article body. Bad extraction is the most common source of “garbage in, garbage out”.
(b) Language identification. A fastText or cld3 classifier labels each document’s language; you can keep only the languages you want, or weight them. Modern multilingual models keep 100+ languages.
(c) Quality filtering. Removes documents that are clearly low-value. Common heuristics:

Length thresholds (drop <50 words or >100k words).
Symbol-to-word ratio limits.
Stop-word presence (real prose has lots of common words).
Repetition filters (drop pages where the top n-gram covers >20% of the text).
Perplexity filtering with a small reference LM (drop documents the LM finds bizarre — typically machine-generated SEO spam, OCR errors, encoding glitches).
Classifier-based filters (e.g., FineWeb-Edu trains a classifier on “is this educational?” and applies a threshold).

(d) Deduplication. Two flavors:

Exact deduplication — remove identical documents (often via MD5 / SHA hashes), and identical paragraphs / line stretches.
Near-duplicate (fuzzy) deduplication — typically MinHash + LSH over shingles, or SimHash. Removes documents whose Jaccard similarity over \(k\)-gram shingles is above a threshold (commonly 0.8). Lee et al. (2022) (“Deduplicating Training Data Makes Language Models Better”) showed substantial perplexity improvements and reduced memorization from aggressive dedup. Frontier corpora are deduplicated at multiple granularities — document, paragraph, even line.

(e) PII filtering. Regex and ML-based scrubbers remove emails, phone numbers, SSNs, credit cards, IPs. Some pipelines also redact named entities like people’s addresses.
(f) Toxicity / NSFW filtering. Classifiers (e.g., Detoxify, Perspective API-style models) score documents; the highest-toxicity tail is dropped or down-weighted.
(g) Decontamination. Removes any text that appears in the evaluation benchmarks (MMLU questions, GSM8K problems, HumanEval prompts). This is non-negotiable; benchmarks leak into the web crawl and would otherwise inflate scores artificially. Methods range from exact-string substring search to n-gram overlap detection.
(h) Domain mixing. Each subset is sampled with a weight \(w_d\) chosen by the data team. Books may be upsampled, low-quality web downsampled. Mix weights are tuned experimentally — small “scaling proxy” runs at 1–7 B parameters explore the space, then the chosen mix is applied to the production model.
(i) Tokenization, shuffling, sharding. Final documents are tokenized (Section 2.3), concatenated with end-of-document tokens, shuffled, and packed into binary shards (e.g., .bin files with offsets). At training time these shards stream into a DataLoader that yields fixed-length sequences.
Mathematical view of the mixture. Let domains \(d \in \{1,\dots,D\}\) each have a deduplicated token count \(N_d\) and an assigned mixture weight \(w_d\). The expected number of times each token in domain \(d\) is seen during a \(T\)-token training run is
\[
\text{epochs}_d = \frac{T \cdot w_d}{N_d}
\]
If \(\text{epochs}_d > 1\) the domain is upsampled; if \(<1\) it is downsampled. The Chinchilla recipe argues for roughly one epoch across the mixture; some recent work (Muennighoff et al. 2023) shows up to four epochs can be safe for high-quality subsets.
Worked example. Suppose your corpus has Web (1 T tokens), Books (50 B), Code (200 B), Math (10 B). You decide to train on \(T=300\) B tokens with mix weights \(\{0.5, 0.15, 0.25, 0.10\}\). Then per-domain epoch counts are:
\[
\text{Web: } \frac{0.5 \cdot 300}{1000} = 0.15, \quad \text{Books: } \frac{0.15 \cdot 300}{50} = 0.9
\] \[
\text{Code: } \frac{0.25 \cdot 300}{200} = 0.375, \quad \text{Math: } \frac{0.10 \cdot 300}{10} = 3.0
\]
Math is seen three times; Web is seen 15% of the way through. The team would either grow the math corpus, accept the repetition (often fine if quality is high), or rebalance.
Practical engineering considerations.

Don’t crawl yourself unless you must. The Common Crawl Foundation has been doing it for a decade; reuse the WARC files.
Streaming everywhere. A pipeline that materializes the full corpus to disk multiple times is too slow. Use streaming map-reduce (Spark, Ray Data, Hadoop, datatrove).
Idempotent stages. Each stage should be re-runnable; store outputs in immutable shards keyed by content hash.
Profile early. Before scaling to 10 PB, run the full pipeline on 1% of the data; measure dropout rate at each stage.
Provenance. For every token in the final corpus, you should be able to trace back to the source URL or document ID. Auditing, takedown requests, and decontamination demand it.

Common assessment trap. Scraping ≠ training-ready data. Raw scrape is the input; the training corpus is the output of a long filtering pipeline. The bullet point “scrape and aggregate cross-domain text data” includes everything above.
Minimal Python sketch — text extraction + language ID + length filter.
import trafilatura
import fasttext

lid = fasttext.load_model("lid.176.bin")  # language identifier

def extract_and_filter(html: str, min_words: int = 50) -> str | None:
    text = trafilatura.extract(html, favor_recall=False)
    if not text:
        return None
    if len(text.split()) < min_words:
        return None
    lang, conf = lid.predict(text.replace("\n", " ")[:512])
    if lang[0] != "__label__en" or conf[0] < 0.65:
        return None
    return text
Minimal Python sketch — MinHash deduplication.
from datasketch import MinHash, MinHashLSH

def shingles(text: str, k: int = 5) -> set[str]:
    tokens = text.split()
    return {" ".join(tokens[i:i+k]) for i in range(len(tokens) - k + 1)}

def minhash_of(text: str, num_perm: int = 128) -> MinHash:
    m = MinHash(num_perm=num_perm)
    for sh in shingles(text):
        m.update(sh.encode("utf-8"))
    return m

lsh = MinHashLSH(threshold=0.8, num_perm=128)

def add_or_skip(doc_id: str, text: str) -> bool:
    m = minhash_of(text)
    if lsh.query(m):       # near-duplicate of something already kept
        return False
    lsh.insert(doc_id, m)
    return True



1.2 Compliance with Licensing and Copyright in LLM Data Collection
Definition and motivation. LLM training corpora are dominated by text written by other people. Most of that text is copyrighted by default the moment it is fixed in tangible form. Whether you may train on it, and what rights the resulting model carries, is a legal question — but it is also an engineering question because compliance must be designed into the pipeline. Failures here have produced lawsuits (NYT v. OpenAI, Authors Guild v. OpenAI, Getty v. Stability) and forced model retractions.
The legal categories you need to recognize.








Category
What it means
Examples




Public domain
No copyright (expired, never had one, dedicated).
Pre-1928 US works, government documents, CC0-dedicated works


Permissive open license
Reuse allowed with conditions (often attribution).
MIT, Apache-2.0, BSD, CC-BY


Copyleft / share-alike
Derivatives must carry a similar license.
GPL, AGPL, CC-BY-SA


Non-commercial
Commercial use forbidden.
CC-BY-NC, research-only


No-derivatives
Verbatim only, no modification.
CC-BY-ND


All rights reserved
Default for any unlicensed work.
Most websites, books, news articles


Fair use / fair dealing
Doctrine (US / UK / Canada) allowing limited use without permission.
Quotation, criticism, parody; transformative use


Robots / TDM exceptions
Some jurisdictions (EU TDM exception under DSM Directive) allow text and data mining unless rightsholders opt out.
EU, Japan, Singapore


Privacy regimes (GDPR, CCPA)
Personal data has separate protections orthogonal to copyright.
Names, emails, location


Special data classes
Health (HIPAA), children (COPPA), biometrics.
Subject to specific rules



Sources of obligations to track.

The site’s robots.txt — a crawler-etiquette signal; ignoring it is poor practice and increasingly cited in litigation.
ai.txt / LLMs.txt — emerging conventions for sites to declare AI-training opt-outs.
Terms of Service — many sites’ ToS forbid automated scraping or training.
Licensing metadata — Creative Commons license tags, SPDX identifiers in source code, package metadata.
Country of operation — EU TDM exception, Japan’s broad ML exception, US fair-use doctrine, UK exception. The legal posture is not uniform.

Engineering controls inside the pipeline.

Source whitelisting. Maintain an allowlist of source domains with vetted licenses. Anything outside the allowlist is staged for legal review or excluded.
License classification. Tag each document with its license at ingestion; do not strip license metadata. For code corpora, parse LICENSE, package.json, pyproject.toml, SPDX headers. For Common Crawl, use the page’s CC license tags if present.
Robots.txt cache. Snapshot the relevant robots.txt for each crawl; record what the policy was at the moment of the crawl.
PII redaction. Required under GDPR / CCPA for personal data — regardless of copyright. Mask emails, phone numbers, addresses, government IDs.
Opt-out registries. Companies like Spawning maintain Have I Been Trained? registries; honor takedown requests by removing documents from future runs and (where possible) by retraining.
Decontamination of evaluation sets. Distinct from compliance, but mentioned here because it is a similar “what should not be in the corpus?” engineering control.
Attribution preservation. For attribution-required licenses (CC-BY, many code licenses), keep author + URL metadata; some labs publish “Datasheet for the Dataset” disclosures with this information.

Doctrine: training as fair use. US labs commonly argue that ingesting copyrighted text to train a model is transformative and falls under fair use. Counterarguments: the model can reproduce verbatim training data (“regurgitation”); the model competes commercially with the works it was trained on; the use is not de minimis. Courts have not fully resolved this. Engineering responses to mitigate risk:

Memorization mitigation. Aggressive dedup reduces memorization (Lee et al., 2022). Training-data extraction attacks (Carlini et al., 2021, 2023) are the empirical measurement: if your model can be prompted to reproduce a passage verbatim, it has memorized it.
Output filtering. A second model or rule-based filter catches near-verbatim regurgitation in outputs.
“Clean room” datasets. Some labs (e.g., Adobe Firefly’s stock-photo corpus, BigCode’s permissively-licensed-only The Stack) deliberately constrain to permissive licenses to side-step the question.

The Books3 cautionary tale. Books3 was a corpus of ~196k pirated books used in The Pile and (allegedly) in early LLaMA training. After legal complaints, it was retracted from public hosting; new pretraining mixes (RedPajama, Dolma) explicitly exclude it. Engineering lesson: provenance documentation is a defensive asset.
Comparison: license classes and training implications.








License class
Use for training?
Conditions




Public domain / CC0
Yes
None


CC-BY, MIT, Apache-2.0, BSD
Yes
Preserve attribution metadata; consider attributing in model card


CC-BY-SA, GPL
Risky
Output may inherit license obligations (debated)


CC-BY-NC
Forbidden for commercial models
OK for research-only


CC-BY-ND
Risky
Training likely creates derivative work


All rights reserved, no robots opt-out
Disputed — depends on jurisdiction and fair-use argument
Increasingly conservative labs exclude


All rights reserved with opt-out signal
Exclude
Robots.txt / ai.txt / DSM opt-out should be honored


Personal data (GDPR scope)
Requires lawful basis
Pseudonymize / minimize / honor erasure



Operational checklist.

Catalog every source with a license tag and a snapshot of its robots/ToS state at crawl time.
Maintain a denylist for known opt-outs and takedowns.
Run periodic memorization probes against your trained model; if a copyrighted passage can be extracted with a short prompt, consider retraining without that source.
Publish a model card and dataset card disclosing categories and sizes of training data, in line with Bender et al. (2018, “Datasheets for Datasets”) and Mitchell et al. (2019, “Model Cards”).
Honor takedown requests promptly; record them; remove the relevant documents before the next training run.

Common assessment trap. “Compliance” is not a single rule — it is a stack: copyright (per work), licensing (per work), site terms (per source), robots/ai.txt (per source), privacy laws (per data subject), jurisdiction (per company location). All four must clear before a document is “safe to train on” by a conservative standard.

title: LLMs for Engineers
section: MODULE 2 — Enhancing and Structuring Training Inputs
text:
MODULE 2 — Enhancing and Structuring Training Inputs

2.1 Preprocess and Integrate Multimodal Data for LLM Training
Definition and motivation. A multimodal model accepts inputs from more than one modality — text, images, audio, video, code, tabular, structured documents — and predicts targets in one or more modalities. “Multimodal LLM” usually means a model whose backbone is a language model, augmented to accept non-text inputs by embedding them into the same token space as text. Examples: CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), Flamingo (Alayrac et al., 2022), BLIP-2 (Li et al., 2023), LLaVA (Liu et al., 2023), GPT-4V, Gemini, Llama 3.2 Vision, Pixtral, Qwen2-VL, Whisper (audio encoder), AudioPaLM, Florence, Idefics, Molmo.
The unifying recipe. Almost all modern multimodal LLMs follow the same three-component pattern.
modality input  →  modality-specific encoder  →  projector  →  token sequence  →  shared transformer (LLM)  →  text out

A modality-specific encoder turns raw signal into a feature sequence (vision: ViT or ConvNeXt; audio: Whisper or wav2vec; video: spatiotemporal ViT).
A projector (a small MLP or linear layer, sometimes a Q-Former) maps those features into the LLM’s embedding dimension and (often) compresses the sequence length.
The resulting “soft” tokens are concatenated with text token embeddings and fed through the LLM as if they were text.

Image preprocessing pipeline (detailed).

Decode (PNG/JPEG → tensor) and convert to RGB.
Resize. Most ViTs expect a fixed input size (e.g., \(224\times224\), \(336\times336\), \(448\times448\)). Some models (Qwen2-VL, Pixtral) support native dynamic resolution by patchifying at arbitrary aspect ratios and using 2D RoPE.
Normalize. Subtract the dataset mean, divide by the standard deviation (e.g., ImageNet: mean \((0.485,0.456,0.406)\), std \((0.229,0.224,0.225)\)).
Patchify. Cut the image into non-overlapping patches (typically \(14\times14\) or \(16\times16\)). A \(336\times336\) image with \(14\times14\) patches yields \(24\times24=576\) patches.
Encode. A ViT produces a \(576 \times d_v\) feature sequence (plus a CLS token).
Project. A 2-layer MLP maps each \(d_v\)-vector to \(d_{\text{model}}\), the LLM’s hidden size. The result is a sequence of “image tokens” that look, to the LLM, like ordinary token embeddings.
Pool / compress (optional). A Q-Former (BLIP-2) or perceiver resampler (Flamingo) compresses the 576 image tokens to, say, 32 or 64 — saving downstream context length.

Image–text data format. Training examples are typically chat templates with <image> placeholders:
USER:
<image>
What is happening in this picture?

ASSISTANT:
A black-and-white dog is catching a frisbee in a park.
During tokenization, the <image> placeholder is replaced by the projected image-token sequence. The text loss is computed only on the assistant response tokens (image tokens are not predicted).
Audio preprocessing pipeline.

Resample to a target rate (Whisper uses 16 kHz).
Compute log-mel spectrogram (e.g., 80 mel bins, 25 ms window, 10 ms hop) over 30-second chunks. Output shape: \((80, 3000)\).
Encode with a transformer encoder (Whisper) → sequence of audio features.
Project to LLM embedding dim and prepend / interleave with text tokens.

For audio-out models (AudioPaLM, MusicLM), the target is also a token stream — typically discrete audio tokens from a neural codec (SoundStream, Encodec).
Video preprocessing. Frames are sampled (e.g., 1–8 fps), each is patchified, and the temporal dimension is handled either by stacking frame tokens (and using a temporal positional encoding) or by a spatiotemporal ViT with 3D patches. Long videos require aggressive compression — a 1-hour video at 8 fps is 28,800 frames; even at 32 tokens per frame that is ~1 M tokens.
Tabular / structured data. Two approaches:

Serialize to text (“Column A is 5, Column B is 7…”) — works because the LLM was trained on text; loses some inductive bias.
Custom encoder. TabTransformer, FT-Transformer, TaBERT — train an encoder that emits embeddings consumed by the LLM.

Code as a modality. Code is technically text, but it is often handled specially: a code-aware tokenizer (often byte-level BPE trained jointly on prose + code, e.g., StarCoder, CodeLlama), file-level context windows with <fim_*> (“fill-in-the-middle”) tokens for code infilling tasks.
Alignment objectives.

Contrastive (CLIP-style). \(\mathcal{L}=-\log \frac{\exp(\langle f(x_i), g(y_i)\rangle/\tau)}{\sum_j \exp(\langle f(x_i), g(y_j)\rangle/\tau)}\). Trains the image encoder and text encoder to land each (image, caption) pair near each other in a shared space. Used for retrieval, zero-shot classification, and to initialize the projector that ports image features into an LLM.
Image captioning / VQA loss. Cross-entropy on the assistant tokens, conditioned on image features. Used in LLaVA-style supervised fine-tuning.
Visual instruction tuning. Chat datasets where each instruction is paired with one or more images.

Memory and compute considerations.

Image tokens inflate context length. A \(448\times448\) image at \(14\times14\) patches contributes 1,024 tokens before projector compression.
Vision encoders typically run in fp16/bf16; their KV cache contributes to GPU memory.
For long video, expect to need temporal sub-sampling, projection compression, and/or aggressive sequence pruning to fit context windows.

Common assessment traps.

“Multimodal” does not require multiple modalities at output — many models are vision-in, text-out.
A “vision encoder” is not the same as a “projector”; the encoder is large (ViT), the projector is a small MLP whose job is dimensional and (sometimes) sequence-length adaptation.
Image tokens are continuous embeddings, not discrete IDs (unlike text tokens). They never go through an embedding-lookup table inside the LLM.

Minimal LLaVA-style sketch.
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from transformers import CLIPVisionModel, AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

class MiniMultiModalLM(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, llm_name="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"):
        super().__init__()
        self.vision = CLIPVisionModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-large-patch14-336")
        d_v = self.vision.config.hidden_size               # 1024
        self.llm = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(llm_name)
        d_t = self.llm.config.hidden_size                  # 4096
        self.proj = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_v, d_t),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(d_t, d_t),
        )

    def encode_image(self, pixel_values):
        # pixel_values: (B, 3, 336, 336)
        v = self.vision(pixel_values=pixel_values).last_hidden_state[:, 1:]  # drop CLS -> (B, 576, 1024)
        return self.proj(v)                                                  # (B, 576, 4096)

    def forward(self, input_ids, pixel_values, attention_mask, labels):
        img_tokens = self.encode_image(pixel_values)                         # (B, 576, d_t)
        text_emb = self.llm.get_input_embeddings()(input_ids)                # (B, L, d_t)
        # Splice img_tokens at the position of <image> in text_emb (omitted for brevity)
        # ...
        out = self.llm(inputs_embeds=spliced, attention_mask=attention_mask, labels=labels)
        return out.loss



2.2 Synthetic Data Generation Techniques for LLMs
Definition and motivation. Synthetic data is training (or evaluation) data generated by a model or a procedure rather than scraped from human-produced sources. It has become a cornerstone of modern LLM development for three reasons:

Public web is finite and noisy. Frontier teams have already scraped most of it; further data must come from new sources or be created.
Targeted capability gain. Synthetic data lets you control distribution. Want better math? Generate millions of math problems. Want a particular response style? Synthesize chats with that style.
Privacy and licensing safety. Synthetic data is owned by the generator; it can substitute for sensitive real data.

Synthetic data appears in every stage of training: pretraining (rare), instruction tuning (universal), preference data (universal), evaluation (common).
Major techniques and where they apply.








Technique
Where used
Idea




Self-instruct (Wang et al., 2022)
Instruction tuning
Seed with a few human-written instructions; have an LLM generate many more, then generate responses


Evol-Instruct (WizardLM; Xu et al., 2023)
Instruction tuning
Take an instruction; “evolve” it to be harder/deeper using LLM rewrite operators (constraints, complications, multiple steps)


Distillation
SFT / continued pretraining
Use a stronger teacher LLM to produce labeled data; train a smaller student


Self-play / debate
Reasoning fine-tuning
Two LLM copies critique each other’s outputs; keep the wins


Bootstrapped reasoning (STaR, ReST, V-STaR)
Reasoning fine-tuning
Generate reasoning chains; keep only those whose final answer is correct; fine-tune on the kept set


Rejection sampling
RLHF / SFT
Generate \(n\) responses, keep the best by a reward model


Constitutional AI / RLAIF (Bai et al., 2022)
Preference tuning
Use AI feedback (a rubric + LLM judge) to label preference pairs instead of humans


Templated generation
Math, code, structured
Generators (SymPy for math, fuzzers for code) emit deterministic problems with verified answers


Back-translation / paraphrasing
Multilingual / robustness
Translate to language \(B\) and back, or paraphrase, to enlarge the dataset


Persona-driven generation
Diversity
Condition the generator on persona, role, demographic, locale to expand coverage


Tool-use traces
Agentic fine-tuning
Run an LLM in a sandbox with tools; record successful trajectories; train on them


Synthetic preferences
DPO / IPO data
Score \(n\) generations with a reward model; pair best vs. worst



Self-Instruct algorithm (Wang et al., 2022) — step by step.

Start with a seed pool of ~175 hand-written (instruction, input, output) triples spanning many tasks.
Sample 6 seeds from the pool and prompt the LLM: “Generate 8 new instructions in the style of the following examples.”
Filter generated instructions: drop near-duplicates of existing ones (ROUGE-L > 0.7), drop those whose first word looks like a “classification” task already overrepresented.
Generate inputs and outputs for each new instruction, again by prompting the LLM.
Add the surviving (instruction, input, output) triples to the pool.
Repeat until you have ~50k–100k examples.

The Stanford Alpaca dataset (Taori et al., 2023) is the canonical example: 175 seeds → 52k Alpaca instructions generated by text-davinci-003.
Evol-Instruct (WizardLM). Defines a set of “evolution operators”:

In-depth evolving — add constraints, deepen, concretize.
In-breadth evolving — generate a completely different but related instruction.

Each round, an instruction is rewritten with a randomly chosen operator. A discriminator filters degenerate evolutions (too short, off-topic, no progress). The result is a corpus that is more diverse and harder than the seed.
Rejection sampling (a.k.a. Best-of-\(n\)). Given a prompt \(x\) and a reward model \(r(x,y)\):
for prompt x in dataset:
    candidates = [policy.sample(x) for _ in range(n)]
    y* = argmax_y r(x, y)
    keep (x, y*) for SFT
Used as both a training-data generator (LLaMA 2 used it heavily for SFT data) and as a cheap inference-time alternative to RL (output the best-of-\(n\) at serving).
STaR (Self-Taught Reasoner; Zelikman et al., 2022).

Generate a chain-of-thought + answer for each problem.
Keep \((x, \text{cot}, y)\) tuples where \(y\) matches the gold answer.
For problems the model gets wrong, rationalize: provide the model with the gold answer and ask it to generate a CoT that arrives at it; keep that.
Fine-tune on the resulting (problem, CoT, answer) corpus.
Iterate.

Synthetic preference data. Given a base model and a reward model:

Sample \(n\) completions per prompt.
Score them with the reward model.
For each prompt, pick the top-1 (chosen) and bottom-1 (rejected) as a preference pair \((x, y_w, y_l)\).
Train via DPO (see Section 6.1.4).

Quality-control mechanisms.

Diversity filters. Embed each new sample and reject if cosine similarity > threshold to anything already in the set.
Length filters. Drop one-line outputs or pathologically long ones.
Format filters. For code, require parseability. For math, require an integer / boxed answer.
Critic LLM. A separate LLM judges whether the response is helpful, harmless, and on-instruction; low-scoring examples are dropped.
Verifier-based filtering (V-STaR, math-shepherd). Use a programmatic verifier (unit tests for code, symbolic equality for math) to keep only verifiably correct examples.
Manual spot checks. Always sample 100 examples by hand; if 10 are bad, your pipeline has a leak.

Risks and failure modes.

Mode collapse. The generator imitates its own style; the dataset’s effective diversity is much smaller than its surface count.
Hallucination amplification. If the generator hallucinates 5% of facts and you scale to a million examples, you have 50k confidently wrong examples. Always pair with a verifier when possible.
Distribution drift. Synthetic data may not match the real query distribution; the model overfits to the generator’s quirks.
Bias inheritance. The generator’s biases (verbose, sycophantic, formatted with bullet lists) become the student’s biases.
License contamination. If the generator is a commercial API, its terms-of-service may forbid using the outputs to train competing models. Check before you build.
Reward hacking (when filtering with a reward model). The kept examples cluster around the reward model’s blind spots.

Numerical scale.

Alpaca: 52k instructions (~$500 of API cost in 2023).
Tulu-3 / OpenHermes / Capybara: 100k–1M instructions, mixed sources.
Phi-3 (Microsoft) trained on a mostly-synthetic curriculum of “textbook-quality” tokens (Gunasekar et al., 2023).
Llama 3.1 405B SFT used 25M synthetic examples (Meta’s report).

Sketch — self-instruct loop.
import random, json
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()
pool: list[dict] = json.load(open("seed_tasks.json"))   # 175 (instruction, input, output) triples

def generate_batch(pool):
    seeds = random.sample(pool, 6)
    prompt = (
        "You are generating diverse instruction-following data.\n"
        "Examples:\n"
        + "\n".join(f"- {s['instruction']}" for s in seeds)
        + "\n\nWrite 8 NEW instructions in the same style."
    )
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        temperature=1.0,
    )
    return parse_instructions(resp.choices[0].message.content)

def materialize(instr: str) -> dict | None:
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": instr}],
        temperature=0.7,
    )
    return {"instruction": instr, "input": "", "output": resp.choices[0].message.content}

while len(pool) < 52_000:
    for instr in generate_batch(pool):
        if is_duplicate(instr, pool):       # ROUGE-L threshold
            continue
        item = materialize(instr)
        if item and quality_ok(item):
            pool.append(item)
Common assessment trap. Synthetic data is not the same as data augmentation in the traditional sense (random cropping, paraphrasing). Modern LLM synthetic data is generated by other LLMs, often more capable than the trainee; the data path is teacher → student in everything but name.



2.3 Tokenization for Language Models
Definition and motivation. Tokenization is the function that maps a raw string \(s\) to a sequence of integer IDs \(\mathbf{t} = (t_1,\dots,t_L)\) drawn from a fixed vocabulary \(V\). The reverse function (the decoder) maps IDs back to a string. Tokenizers are not learned during pretraining — they are fitted once on a sample of the training corpus, then frozen.
Why it matters:

Every byte of text must become a token. The vocabulary controls how many tokens the text becomes, which directly determines context length, training cost, inference cost, and KV-cache memory.
Bad tokenization handicaps the model on multilingual text, code, numbers, and rare words.
Tokenizer choice constrains everything downstream: the embedding table size, the output softmax, the maximum effective vocabulary.

Three families of tokenizers used in modern LLMs.

Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) — used by GPT-2/3/4, Llama family, Mistral, Mixtral, Falcon, Qwen, GPT-NeoX.
WordPiece — used by BERT, DistilBERT, MobileBERT.
Unigram language-model tokenization (and its wrapper SentencePiece) — used by T5, mT5, ALBERT, XLNet, mBART; also the SentencePiece library can be configured to train BPE.

Several refinements layer on top:

Byte-level BPE — operates on bytes rather than Unicode characters. Used by GPT-2/3/4, RoBERTa, Llama 3.
tiktoken — OpenAI’s fast byte-level BPE library; ships the vocabularies cl100k_base (GPT-3.5/4) and o200k_base (GPT-4o / o1).
SentencePiece — a library wrapping BPE and Unigram, supporting whitespace-as-character and language-independent tokenization.


(A) Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE).
Originally a data-compression algorithm (Gage, 1994); adopted for NMT by Sennrich, Haddow, Birch (2016) — “Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units.”
Training (vocabulary construction).

Initialize the vocabulary as the set of all characters (or bytes) appearing in the corpus.
Pre-tokenize the corpus into words (or pre-tokenizer chunks) and represent each word as a sequence of symbols, with end-of-word markers if desired (e.g., </w> for original BPE; word boundaries via leading Ġ in GPT-style byte-level BPE).
Count all adjacent symbol pairs across the corpus.
Merge the most frequent pair into a new symbol; add the merged symbol to the vocabulary; record the merge rule.
Repeat step 3–4 until the vocabulary reaches the desired size (e.g., 32k, 50k, 100k, 200k).

Encoding (tokenizing a new string at inference).

Pre-tokenize the string into words/chunks.
For each chunk, split into characters/bytes.
Apply the merge rules in the order they were learned until no more merges apply.
Look up the integer ID of each remaining symbol.

Worked example. Suppose the corpus is low low low low low lower newer newer newer newer newer wider wider wider. With end-of-word markers:
l o w </w> : 5
l o w e r </w> : 1
n e w e r </w> : 5
w i d e r </w> : 3
Initial pair counts (some shown): (l,o)=6, (o,w)=6, (w,</w>)=5, (w,e)=1, (e,r)=9, (r,</w>)=9, …
Top pair: (e,r) with count 9. Merge → new symbol er. Update:
l o w </w> : 5
l o w er </w> : 1     (was l o w e r </w>)
n e w er </w> : 5     (was n e w e r </w>)
w i d er </w> : 3     (was w i d e r </w>)
Recount; (er, </w>) now leads with 9. Merge → er</w>. And so on. After a few iterations you accumulate er, er</w>, low, newer</w>, etc.
Encoding a new word lower (also with </w>): start l o w e r </w>. Apply merges in order: (e,r) → l o w er </w>. Then (er, </w>) → l o w er</w>. Then if you learned (l,o) → lo, etc. Final tokens depend on which merges you completed.
Byte-level BPE. Modern systems (GPT-2 onward, Llama 3) operate on bytes rather than characters. The initial vocabulary has 256 entries — one per byte — plus a few special tokens. Advantages:

Every Unicode string is representable; no <UNK> token ever needed.
A small initial alphabet means more merges spent on real subwords.
Multibyte characters (CJK, emoji) become 2–4 byte tokens before merging.

GPT-2 pre-tokenizes with a regex that splits punctuation, contractions, digits, and (crucially) prefixes leading whitespace into the next word so " the" becomes a single byte sequence starting with Ġ (a renamed space). This avoids learning a separate token for every word “with and without a leading space.”
Properties of BPE.

Deterministic. Given a tokenizer, every string has exactly one token sequence.
No <UNK> at byte level.
Greedy left-to-right. Some implementations (Llama uses the SentencePiece BPE variant) do leftmost longest match instead of strict merge-order replay. Both give similar but not identical splits.
Sensitive to pretokenization. Differences in whitespace and punctuation handling change tokens drastically.


(B) WordPiece.
Used by BERT, DistilBERT, ELECTRA. Conceptually similar to BPE but with a likelihood-based merge criterion.
Training.

Initialize vocabulary with all characters.
At each step, for each candidate merge of symbols \(a, b \to ab\), compute the score

\[
\text{score}(a, b) = \frac{\text{freq}(ab)}{\text{freq}(a) \cdot \text{freq}(b)}
\]

Merge the highest-scoring pair (rather than the most frequent raw pair, as in BPE).
Repeat until the vocabulary size is reached.

The score \(\propto\) the pointwise mutual information; it penalizes merges where the constituents are already common (so the + space would not merge even though their joint count is huge).
Encoding. Greedy longest-match from left to right. If the longest prefix doesn’t match, fall back to a one-character token; tokens that are not word-initial are prefixed with ##. Example: "unaffable" → ["un", "##aff", "##able"].
Pros. Cleaner morphological breaks; ##-prefix makes word-boundary explicit. Cons. Cannot represent arbitrary bytes (needs an <UNK> for out-of-vocabulary characters unless using byte-level WordPiece).

(C) Unigram language-model tokenization.
Proposed by Kudo (2018), “Subword Regularization.” Used by T5, ALBERT, XLNet, mBART.
Training.

Start with a large initial vocabulary (e.g., all substrings up to length \(k\) that pass a frequency cutoff).
Treat tokenization probabilistically: \(p(s) = \prod_i p(t_i)\) for a segmentation \(\mathbf{t}\).
Fit unigram probabilities \(p(t)\) by EM, maximizing the marginal likelihood \(\sum_\text{segmentations} \prod p(t_i)\) over the corpus.
Prune. For each token \(t\), estimate the loss in likelihood if \(t\) were removed. Remove the bottom \(X\%\) of tokens by loss.
Repeat 3–4 until the vocabulary reaches the target size.

Encoding. Tokenization is the Viterbi-best segmentation under the unigram model — viterbi over the dynamic-programming lattice of all segmentations.
Sampling. Because the model is probabilistic, you can sample alternative segmentations at training time as data augmentation (“subword regularization”). BPE-dropout (Provilkov et al., 2020) emulates this for BPE by randomly dropping merges with some probability \(p\).
Pros.

Probabilistic; multiple valid segmentations; data augmentation comes for free.
Often produces more linguistically coherent units.
Default in SentencePiece; supports a tokenization stochasticity knob.

Cons. Slightly slower to train; harder to reason about deterministically.

(D) SentencePiece.
A library (Kudo & Richardson, 2018) rather than a new algorithm. Two important properties:

It treats whitespace as a regular character (commonly the Unicode lower-eighth-block ▁, U+2581). This means it does not need a language-specific pre-tokenizer — works the same on Japanese (no spaces) as on English.
It supports both BPE and Unigram backends.

A SentencePiece tokenization of "Hello world" looks like ["▁Hello", "▁world"]. The ▁ is part of the token; decoding strips it back to whitespace.
Used by T5, mT5, ALBERT, XLNet, mBART, LLaMA 1/2 (BPE backend), Mistral, Mixtral, Gemma.

(E) tiktoken.
OpenAI’s fast byte-level BPE library, used by GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4o, and o1. Ships several named vocabularies:








Name
Vocab size
Models




r50k_base
50,257
GPT-2, GPT-3 davinci/curie/babbage/ada


p50k_base
50,281
code-davinci, text-davinci-002/003


cl100k_base
100,277
GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4, text-embedding-ada-002


o200k_base
200,019
GPT-4o, o1, GPT-4o-mini



Larger vocabularies tokenize natural language and code into fewer tokens — meaning lower API costs and longer effective contexts. o200k_base typically yields 10–15% fewer tokens than cl100k_base on English prose, and even more on code and non-English text.
Compute / cost implication. Going from a 32k to a 200k vocab adds embeddings \((200{,}000-32{,}000) \cdot d_\text{model}\) parameters, plus the same in the output head if untied. For \(d_\text{model}=4096\), that is ~688 M extra parameters. Frontier labs accept that cost because it pays for itself in tokenization efficiency.

Comparison table — the four/five major tokenizer schemes.











Property
BPE
Byte-level BPE
WordPiece
Unigram (SentencePiece)
tiktoken




Algorithm
Frequency-based pair merge
BPE on bytes
Likelihood-scored pair merge
Probabilistic vocabulary pruning
Byte-level BPE


Encoding
Apply merges in order
Same
Greedy longest-match
Viterbi
Apply merges (Rust impl)


Initial alphabet
Characters
256 bytes
Characters
All frequent substrings
256 bytes


<UNK> possible?
Yes (char-level)
No
Yes
No (if all chars covered)
No


Whitespace handling
Pre-tokenized
Pre-tokenized + Ġ prefix
Pre-tokenized + ## prefix
▁ as part of token
Pre-tokenized + Ġ prefix


Multilingual
OK with proper pre-tok
Excellent
Limited
Excellent
Excellent


Subword regularization
BPE-dropout
BPE-dropout
No

No


Used by
GPT-2 (loose), early NMT
GPT-2/3/4, Llama 3, Falcon, Qwen
BERT, DistilBERT
T5, ALBERT, mBART, Llama 1/2, Mistral, Gemma
GPT-3.5/4/4o


Library
many
many
tokenizers, original BERT
SentencePiece
tiktoken




Worked numeric example — vocab size, embedding memory, sequence cost.
Suppose a model has hidden size \(d=4096\) and you choose:

Vocab 32,000: embedding params \(32\text{k}\cdot 4096 = 131\) M; output head (if untied) +131 M.
Vocab 128,256 (Llama 3): \(\approx 525\) M; if untied, +525 M.
Vocab 200,019 (o200k_base): \(\approx 819\) M; +819 M if untied.

Tokenization efficiency on a 1,000-character English passage:

32k BPE (e.g., Llama 2): ~250 tokens.
cl100k_base: ~225 tokens.
o200k_base: ~200 tokens.

Compute per training step scales with tokens; over a 15 T-token run, a 10% reduction in tokens-per-character is genuine money.

Numbers, code, and the “tokenization tax”.

Numbers. Most BPE/WordPiece tokenizers split numbers into digit chunks; "1729" may become ["172", "9"] or ["1", "7", "2", "9"]. Some models force digit-level tokenization to improve arithmetic (Llama, PaLM 2 with digit splitting).
Code. Tokenization affects code drastically — indentation, brace handling, identifier splits. Code-trained tokenizers (CodeLlama, StarCoder) have merges biased toward common code patterns (def, return, ).
Whitespace. A tokenizer that doesn’t fold leading whitespace into the next word will double its vocabulary for common words.
Multilingual. Languages without spaces (Chinese, Japanese, Thai) require byte-level or SentencePiece-style handling; otherwise the vocabulary explodes.


Common assessment traps.

BPE vs. WordPiece vs. Unigram — the merge criterion is the distinguishing characteristic (frequency vs. likelihood-score vs. EM-pruned).
SentencePiece is a library, not an algorithm. It can run BPE or Unigram inside.
Byte-level BPE has no <UNK> token. This is a frequent multiple-choice gotcha.
WordPiece’s ## marks continuation, not start. ["un", "##aff", "##able"] is one word; ["aff"] alone would be a standalone word.


Hands-on snippets.
Train a BPE tokenizer with HuggingFace tokenizers.
from tokenizers import Tokenizer
from tokenizers.models import BPE
from tokenizers.trainers import BpeTrainer
from tokenizers.pre_tokenizers import ByteLevel

tok = Tokenizer(BPE(unk_token=None))
tok.pre_tokenizer = ByteLevel(add_prefix_space=True)
trainer = BpeTrainer(vocab_size=32_000, special_tokens=["<|endoftext|>"])
tok.train(files=["corpus.txt"], trainer=trainer)
tok.save("bpe-tokenizer.json")

ids = tok.encode("Hello, world!").ids
text = tok.decode(ids)
Use tiktoken.
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
ids = enc.encode("The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.")
print(len(ids), ids)         # 9 tokens (for cl100k_base)
print(enc.decode(ids))
Use SentencePiece in Unigram mode.
import sentencepiece as spm
spm.SentencePieceTrainer.train(
    input="corpus.txt",
    model_prefix="ug",
    vocab_size=32000,
    model_type="unigram",
    character_coverage=0.9995,
)
sp = spm.SentencePieceProcessor(model_file="ug.model")
print(sp.encode("Hello world", out_type=str))   # ['▁Hello', '▁world']
Sample alternative segmentations (subword regularization).
print(sp.encode("Hello world", out_type=str, enable_sampling=True, nbest_size=-1, alpha=0.1))

Design checklist for choosing a tokenizer.

Coverage: byte-level if you want any string to be representable.
Domain: include code, math, and target languages in the training data.
Vocab size: 32k is comfortable for English-only; 100k–256k for multilingual + code.
Special tokens: BOS/EOS, padding, role tokens for chat (<|system|>, <|user|>, <|assistant|>), tool tokens, <image> placeholders for multimodal.
Numbers and digits: consider explicit digit splitting if the model must do arithmetic.
Reproducibility: ship the tokenizer file alongside model weights; never silently switch.

title: LLMs for Engineers
text:
MODULE 3 — Implementing Attention Mechanisms

3.1 Self-Attention in LLM Architectures
Definition and motivation. Self-attention is a function that maps a sequence of vectors \(X \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}\) to another sequence \(Y \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d}\) of the same length, where each output vector \(Y_i\) is a weighted sum of all input vectors, with weights learned dynamically as a function of the inputs themselves. The mechanism allows every position in the sequence to attend to (gather information from) every other position, in \(O(1)\) depth — replacing the \(O(L)\) recurrence of RNNs and the \(O(\log L)\) depth of convolutions with stacking.
The defining innovation is that the mixing weights are content-dependent: \(y_i\)’s weighting of \(x_j\) depends on a learned similarity between \(x_i\) and \(x_j\), not on a fixed positional pattern.
Full derivation — scaled dot-product attention.
Given an input sequence \(X \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d_\text{model}}\):

Project to three matrices — Queries, Keys, Values:

\[
Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V
\]
where \(W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_\text{model} \times d_k}\) and \(W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_\text{model} \times d_v}\).

Compute attention scores as scaled dot products:

\[
S = \frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times L}
\]
The \(\sqrt{d_k}\) scaling keeps the variance of the dot products around \(1\) regardless of \(d_k\) — without it, softmax saturates and gradients vanish for large \(d_k\).

Apply a mask if needed (causal mask for decoder-only LLMs; padding mask for variable-length batches):

\[
S_\text{masked}[i, j] = \begin{cases} S[i, j] & \text{if allowed} \\ -\infty & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\]

Normalize each row with softmax to obtain the attention weights:

\[
A_{ij} = \frac{\exp(S_\text{masked}[i, j])}{\sum_{k=1}^{L} \exp(S_\text{masked}[i, k])}
\]

Compute the output as the weighted average of values:

\[
Y = A V \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times d_v}
\]
In one line:
\[
\boxed{\;\operatorname{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \operatorname{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right) V\;}
\tag{1}\]
where \(M\) is the additive mask (0 where attention is allowed, \(-\infty\) where it is forbidden).
Why this formula works — intuition.

\(Q\) asks a question about the current token; \(K\) advertises what each token has to offer; \(V\) is the content delivered if the question matches the advertisement.
\(QK^\top\) is the cosine-similarity-like compatibility matrix.
Softmax converts compatibilities to a probability distribution; each row sums to 1.
\(AV\) is the weighted retrieval of values according to those probabilities.

Worked \(2\times2\) numeric example. Let \(X = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0\\ 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}\) (two tokens, \(d_\text{model}=2\)). Let \(W^Q = W^K = W^V = I_2\). Then \(Q=K=V=X\). Compute:
\[
QK^\top = \begin{pmatrix}1\cdot 1 + 0\cdot 0 & 1\cdot 0 + 0\cdot 1\\ 0\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 0 & 0\cdot 0 + 1\cdot 1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0\\ 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}
\]
With \(d_k=2\), \(\sqrt{d_k}\approx 1.41\):
\[
S = \begin{pmatrix}0.707 & 0\\ 0 & 0.707\end{pmatrix}
\]
Apply row-wise softmax. Row 0: \(\frac{e^{0.707}}{e^{0.707}+e^0} \approx \frac{2.028}{2.028+1} \approx 0.670\); off-diagonal \(\approx 0.330\). By symmetry, Row 1 is the mirror.
\[
A \approx \begin{pmatrix}0.670 & 0.330\\ 0.330 & 0.670\end{pmatrix}
\]
\[
Y = AV = AX = \begin{pmatrix}0.670 & 0.330\\ 0.330 & 0.670\end{pmatrix}
\]
Each output token is a blend dominated by itself (0.67) with a contribution from the other (0.33).
