Problem. Naïve KV cache reserves contiguous memory for the max prompt length per request. This wastes memory (under-utilized blocks) and forces request-level reservation.
Solution. Treat KV cache like virtual memory: split into fixed-size blocks (e.g., 16 tokens). Each request’s logical KV sequence maps to physical blocks via a block table — like OS paging. Three benefits:

No internal fragmentation. Memory wasted per request reduces to <1 block.
Sharing. Prefixes (system prompts) can be shared across requests by mapping their blocks.
Throughput. Higher batch sizes possible because the same memory hosts more requests.

vLLM (the serving system that introduced this) is the de facto standard for high-throughput LLM serving today.

Speculative decoding. Leviathan, Kalman & Matias (2022) “Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding”; Chen et al. (2023) “Accelerating Large Language Model Decoding with Speculative Sampling.”
Idea. Run a small, cheap “draft” model to propose \(k\) candidate tokens at once. Then run the large “verifier” model on the proposed sequence in a single forward pass (cheap because it’s the same prompt + \(k\) tokens). For each draft token, accept or reject based on the ratio of verifier vs. draft probabilities. On rejection, sample the next token from a corrected distribution. Statistically equivalent to sampling from the large model.

Speedup. 2–4× wall-clock for typical models when the draft has a ~70% accept rate.
No quality loss — the output distribution is exactly the large model’s.
Draft model. Either a small dedicated model (TinyLlama for Llama, or a Llama-3-8B drafting for Llama-3-70B), or a model “self-drafted” via Medusa heads, Lookahead Decoding, or n-gram skipping (Eagle, ReDrafter).


Continuous batching (vLLM, TGI). Traditional batching waits for the slowest request in a batch to finish before processing the next batch. Continuous batching processes each token step in parallel across requests, removing finished requests and adding new ones every step. Combined with paged attention, this dramatically improves GPU utilization.

vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, llama.cpp, SGLang — production serving.








Tool
Strengths
Use case




vLLM
PagedAttention, continuous batching, broad model support
High-throughput open-source serving


TensorRT-LLM
NVIDIA-optimized kernels, FP8, in-flight batching
Top throughput on NVIDIA GPUs


llama.cpp
CPU + Apple Silicon + GGUF
Local / edge deployment


SGLang
RadixAttention prefix caching, fast structured generation
Agentic / structured workflows


HuggingFace TGI
Production-grade, good for ecosystem
Hosted Inference Endpoints




Putting it together — recipes.
Training a 7B model from scratch on 64 H100 GPUs. TP=1, PP=1, DP=64, ZeRO-1 (shard optimizer), BF16, FlashAttention, gradient checkpointing for long sequences, AdamW optimizer.
Training a 405B model on 16,000 H100 GPUs (Llama 3.1). TP=8, PP=16, DP=128, FSDP, BF16 with FP8 in some layers, FlashAttention-3, sequence parallel for the activation copies in TP, gradient checkpointing per block.
Inference for a 70B model on a single 8×H100 node. TP=8, FlashAttention, paged attention, continuous batching with vLLM, optional INT4 quantization (AWQ or GPTQ).
Local inference of a 13B model on a Mac laptop. GGUF Q4_K_M, llama.cpp with Metal backend, 4–8 GB RAM.

Common assessment traps.

ZeRO and FSDP are equivalent at stage 3. ZeRO is DeepSpeed’s API; FSDP is PyTorch’s.
Tensor parallel splits weights inside a layer; pipeline parallel splits layers across stages.
Gradient checkpointing trades compute for memory (~33% more compute, much less activation memory).
Speculative decoding is exact — it does not approximate the large model’s output distribution.
Paged attention is about KV-cache memory layout, not about attention math.

title: LLMs for Engineers
text:
MODULE 9 — Applying Decoding Strategies

9.1 Beam Search for Sequence Generation
Definition and motivation. Beam search is a deterministic, breadth-limited search over the space of output sequences. At each step it keeps the \(B\) highest-scoring partial sequences (the beams) and expands each by appending every possible next token, scoring the resulting \(B \cdot |V|\) candidates and keeping the top-\(B\) for the next step. Continues until all beams hit an end-of-sequence token (or a maximum length).
Beam search is the natural choice for tasks with a clearly correct answer: translation, summarization, code completion. It is rarely used for open-ended generation (chat, creative writing) because it produces overly safe, repetitive text and lacks diversity.
Greedy decoding is the special case \(B=1\) — always pick the single highest-probability next token. Fast but quality is brittle.
Algorithm.
beams = [(empty_sequence, score=0)]
while not all beams ended:
    new_beams = []
    for (seq, score) in beams:
        if seq ends with <eos>: keep seq; continue
        logits = model(seq)[-1]                 # next-token logits
        log_probs = log_softmax(logits)
        for token, lp in top_B(log_probs):
            new_beams.append((seq + [token], score + lp))
    beams = top_B(new_beams)
return beams[0]
Scoring. The natural score is the joint log-likelihood:
\[
\log p(y_1, \dots, y_T \mid x) = \sum_{t=1}^T \log p(y_t \mid x, y_{<t})
\]
Length bias. Joint log-likelihood is monotonically decreasing in length — every appended token has \(\log p < 0\). Beam search systematically prefers shorter sequences. Length normalization divides by length raised to a hyperparameter \(\alpha\) (Wu et al., 2016, GNMT):
\[
\text{score} = \frac{\log p(y \mid x)}{|y|^\alpha},\quad \alpha \in [0.6, 1.0]
\]
Repetition. Beam search produces repetitive output (“text degeneration”, Holtzman et al., 2019). Common mitigations:

N-gram blocking. Forbid any beam from generating an \(n\)-gram (typically \(n=3\)) it has already emitted.
Repetition penalty. Divide the logit of any already-emitted token by \(r > 1\) (commonly 1.1–1.3).
Diverse Beam Search (Vijayakumar et al., 2018). Run \(G\) groups of beams in parallel; each group is penalized for resembling the others.

Worked example. Imagine a tiny model and beam width \(B=2\):

Step 1: logits → top-2 tokens “I” (lp = -0.2) and “The” (lp = -0.3). Beams: [("I", -0.2), ("The", -0.3)].
Step 2: from “I”: top-2 are “love” (-0.1), “see” (-0.4) → scores -0.3, -0.6. From “The”: top-2 are “cat” (-0.2), “dog” (-0.3) → scores -0.5, -0.6. Keep top-2 overall: [("I love", -0.3), ("The cat", -0.5)].
Continue until <eos>.

Computational cost. Per step: \(B\) forward passes (one per beam), or one batched forward of size \(B\). Memory: \(B\) KV caches. Total: \(\sim B\) × greedy cost.
Common assessment traps.

Beam search is not sampling — it is deterministic given the model.
Greedy decoding equals beam search with \(B=1\).
Beam search maximizes likelihood, but maximum-likelihood text is often poor quality for open-ended generation (Holtzman et al.).
Diverse beam search is a structural change (groups + penalty), not a hyperparameter.




9.2 Sampling Techniques
Why sample at all? For open-ended generation (chat, story-writing, creative tasks), the maximum-likelihood sequence is often bland or repetitive. Sampling introduces randomness, producing diverse outputs that match the LLM’s learned distribution.
The basic operation. At each step, draw the next token from a distribution derived from the model’s logits. The exact distribution is controlled by temperature and various truncation schemes.

(A) Temperature.
Apply \(T > 0\) to the logits before softmax:
\[
p_i(T) = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j / T)}
\]

\(T = 1\): model’s natural distribution.
\(T < 1\): sharpens — high-probability tokens get more probability, low ones less. The limit \(T \to 0\) is greedy.
\(T > 1\): flattens — increases diversity but risks low-quality samples.

Typical chat models use \(T = 0.7\)–\(1.0\). Code models often use \(T = 0.2\)–\(0.4\) for deterministic-ish output.

(B) Top-k sampling. Fan et al., 2018, “Hierarchical Neural Story Generation.”
Keep only the top \(k\) logits; zero out the rest; renormalize; sample.
def top_k(logits, k):
    v, _ = torch.topk(logits, k)
    logits = logits.clone()
    logits[logits < v[..., [-1]]] = -float("inf")
    return logits

\(k = 1\): greedy.
\(k = 50\): typical chat default.
Large \(k\): closer to unrestricted sampling.

Problem. Fixed \(k\) ignores the shape of the distribution. If the model is confident (one option much higher than all others), top-\(k\) with large \(k\) admits noise. If the model is uncertain (uniform-ish), top-\(k\) with small \(k\) chops off plausible options.

(C) Top-p (nucleus) sampling. Holtzman et al., 2019, “The Curious Case of Neural Text Degeneration.”
Adaptive: keep the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds \(p\).
def top_p(logits, p):
    sorted_logits, sorted_idx = logits.sort(descending=True)
    cum = sorted_logits.softmax(-1).cumsum(-1)
    keep = cum < p
    keep[..., 1:] = keep[..., :-1].clone()
    keep[..., 0]  = True                              # always keep top-1
    mask = keep.scatter(-1, sorted_idx, keep)
    return logits.masked_fill(~mask, -float("inf"))

\(p = 0.95\): typical chat default.
\(p = 0.9\): tighter, more focused.
\(p = 1.0\): no truncation.

Top-\(p\) adapts: in confident distributions only a few tokens make the cut; in flat distributions many do.

(D) Combining temperature, top-k, top-p. Order matters: temperature, then top-k truncation, then top-p truncation, then sample. HuggingFace’s generate() does exactly this.

(E) Min-p sampling. Nguyen et al., 2024.
Keep tokens whose probability exceeds \(\min_p \cdot p_\max\), where \(p_\max\) is the most-probable token. Adaptive like top-p but scales with the maximum probability — more robust at high temperatures.
threshold = min_p * max(probs)
keep = probs >= threshold
Typical \(\min_p \in [0.05, 0.1]\).

(F) Typical sampling. Meister et al., 2022, “Locally Typical Sampling.”
Sample from the set of tokens whose log-probability is near the conditional entropy of the distribution (rather than the highest log-probs). Motivation: human text uses tokens of “typical” information content, not always the most likely ones. Hyperparameter \(\tau \in (0, 1)\) — the probability mass to retain.

(G) Repetition penalty. Keskar et al., 2019, “CTRL.”
Divide the logit of any token that has appeared in the generated context (within a window) by \(r > 1\):
\[
z_i \leftarrow z_i / r \quad \text{if } i \in \text{generated}
\]
(For negative logits, the formula is \(z_i \cdot r\) — i.e., scale logits toward zero either way.)
Common \(r \in [1.1, 1.3]\). Reduces but doesn’t eliminate repetition. The newer frequency penalty (subtract \(\alpha \cdot \text{count}(i)\)) and presence penalty (subtract \(\beta\) if seen once) refine the idea.

(H) Contrastive decoding. Li et al., 2022, “Contrastive Decoding.”
Compare a large expert model with a small amateur model; reward tokens the expert finds plausible but the amateur does not:
\[
\text{score}(y) = \log p_\text{expert}(y \mid x) - \log p_\text{amateur}(y \mid x)
\]
(with constraints to keep \(p_\text{expert}\) above a threshold). Pushes the output toward what is distinctively “good model” behavior. Best for analytical / factual writing; less for creative tasks.

(I) Contrastive search. Su et al., 2022.
Combine model probability with a penalty for similarity to previously generated tokens. At each step, candidate token \(v\) is scored:
\[
\text{score}(v) = (1-\alpha) p(v|x,y_{<t}) - \alpha \max_{j < t} \text{sim}(h_v, h_{y_j})
\]
Reduces degeneration without the harshness of repetition penalties.

(J) DRY sampling. “Don’t Repeat Yourself” — recent (2024) repetition-suppression scheme that penalizes any token starting a previously-seen sequence of length \(\geq 2\).

Comparison table — decoding strategies.










Method
Determinism
Diversity
Quality
Best for




Greedy
Yes
None
Brittle
Structured outputs, JSON


Beam search
Yes
Low
Strong on likelihood
Translation, summarization


Diverse beam search
Yes
Medium
Strong
Diverse summarization


Sampling (T only)
No
Tunable
Variable
Quick chat


Top-k
No
Bounded
Decent
Older chat default


Top-p (nucleus)
No
Adaptive
Strong
Modern chat default


Min-p
No
Adaptive
Robust at high \(T\)
Creative writing


Typical sampling
No
Informational
Strong
Human-like text


Contrastive decoding
Mostly
Low
Strong on factual
Analytical / factual


Contrastive search
No
Medium
Strong
Open-ended generation




Putting it together — modern defaults.







Use case
Decoding




Chat
Temperature 0.7, top-p 0.9 (or 0.95)


Code
Temperature 0.2, top-p 0.95


Translation
Beam 4–8, length norm \(\alpha \approx 0.7\)


Summarization
Beam 4 + n-gram blocking


JSON / function call
Greedy, with grammar / regex constraints


Reasoning
Greedy or temperature 0 (often with self-consistency at \(T=0.6\)+)




Constrained decoding. Force the output into a grammar, regex, JSON schema, or finite-state machine by masking out logits that would violate the constraint at each step. Tools: outlines, guidance, lm-format-enforcer, vLLM grammar mode, OpenAI structured outputs.

Worked example — temperature math. Suppose logits are \(z = (2, 1, 0)\).

\(T=1\): \(p \propto (e^2, e^1, e^0) = (7.39, 2.72, 1.00)\); normalize → \((0.665, 0.244, 0.090)\).
\(T=0.5\): \(z/T = (4, 2, 0)\); \(p \propto (54.6, 7.39, 1)\) → \((0.867, 0.117, 0.016)\).
\(T=2.0\): \(z/T = (1, 0.5, 0)\); \(p \propto (2.72, 1.65, 1.00)\) → \((0.506, 0.307, 0.187)\).

Smaller \(T\) sharpens, larger \(T\) flattens — exactly as expected.

Common assessment traps.

Top-k vs top-p. Top-k truncates by count; top-p truncates by cumulative probability. Top-p is adaptive; top-k is not.
Temperature applies to logits before softmax. Many implementations apply it before truncation.
Beam search ≠ “sampling with beams”; beam search is deterministic.
Greedy is just argmax; it has no temperature, no top-p.
Repetition penalty is applied to logits, not to probabilities — it works for both positive and negative logits via division by \(r\).

title: LLMs for Engineers
text:
MODULE 10 — Designing Prompt-Based Behaviors

10.1 Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Prompting for Instruction Following
Definition and motivation. A prompt is the input text the LLM is conditioned on. The art and engineering of prompting is choosing inputs that elicit the desired behavior without modifying the model’s weights.

Zero-shot prompt. No examples; just an instruction and the task input.
Few-shot prompt (in-context learning, ICL; Brown et al., 2020). The prompt contains \(k\) examples of the task before the actual query. The model infers the pattern from the examples.

Zero-shot.
Translate to French: "I love language models."
Few-shot.
Translate to French.

English: "Hello, how are you?"
French:  "Bonjour, comment allez-vous?"

English: "The weather is nice."
French:  "Le temps est agréable."

English: "I love language models."
French:
Why few-shot works. Brown et al. (2020, GPT-3) showed that as model scale grew, in-context learning emerged: the model behaves as if it had been fine-tuned on the examples in its prompt, despite no weight updates. The mechanism is not fully understood (induction heads / Bayesian view both contribute) but empirically robust.
Prompt-engineering principles (cumulative).

Be specific. Vague instructions yield vague outputs.
Specify the format. “Reply as a JSON object with fields name, age.” LLMs often follow format directives literally.
Decompose. Long, multi-part tasks benefit from numbered sub-instructions.
Provide a persona. “You are a senior code reviewer…” — sets style.
Show, don’t tell. Few-shot examples often outperform verbose explanations.
Constrain. “Answer in at most two sentences.” “Do not use the word X.”
Ground. Provide context. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is a prompt technique scaled to corpora.
Plan, then act. Asking the model to “think step-by-step” (CoT, Section 10.2) before answering improves complex reasoning.
Use delimiters. Triple backticks, XML-like tags (<context>...</context>), or fenced examples help the model recognize structural boundaries.
Specify the output schema. Most modern LLMs support structured output (JSON mode, grammar-constrained decoding).

System prompts. Many chat-tuned LLMs accept a system role with model-wide instructions. It’s a strong knob for persona and behavior shaping, but can be overridden by sufficiently emphatic user text (prompt injection risk).
Selecting few-shot examples. Several heuristics, ranked by effort:

Random examples from a training set — works, but suboptimal.
Semantic-similarity retrieval. Embed the query, retrieve the \(k\) most similar examples. Often a meaningful improvement.
Diversity-aware selection. Combine similarity with diversity to avoid redundant examples.
Class-balanced selection (for classification).
Adaptive / data-curated few-shot — pre-curate per-cluster exemplars; pick the cluster matching the query.

Number of shots. Diminishing returns past 3–10 examples for most tasks. Very long contexts may admit 50+ examples (“many-shot ICL”; Agarwal et al., 2024).
Example sensitivity. Example order, prompt phrasing, and even punctuation can move accuracy by several points (Lu et al., 2022, “Fantastically Ordered Prompts”). Best practice: sweep small variations on a held-out set; don’t claim performance from a single prompt.
Common assessment traps.

In-context learning is not training. Weights do not change.
Few-shot is a form of prompting, not fine-tuning.
Instruction tuning enables zero-shot performance; without it the base model often needs few-shot examples to behave.
Prompts are part of the contract — when you switch model versions, re-test your prompts.




10.2 Chaining Prompts and Reasoning Strategies
A single prompt can do only so much. Modern LLM applications chain prompts and tool calls into pipelines: each step’s output feeds the next; verifiers gate progress; tools are invoked. This section covers the canonical chaining and reasoning strategies.

(A) Chain-of-Thought (CoT). Wei et al., 2022, “Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models.”
Ask the model to show its reasoning before answering. Two variants:

Few-shot CoT. Include examples with explicit reasoning chains.

Q: Alice has 5 apples. She gives 2 to Bob. How many does she have?
A: Alice starts with 5 apples. She gives 2 away. 5 - 2 = 3. The answer is 3.

Q: Tom has $20. He spends $7. How much is left?
A:

Zero-shot CoT (Kojima et al., 2022). Append “Let’s think step by step.” to the prompt; the model produces a reasoning chain even without examples.

CoT typically improves performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks by 10–40 points at the 100B+ scale. Smaller models benefit less or not at all (“emergent” capability).

(B) Self-Consistency. Wang et al., 2022, “Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models.”
Sample \(N\) CoT chains (temperature > 0), extract each chain’s final answer, majority-vote. The intuition: many reasoning paths can lead to the same correct answer; few wrong answers are reached by many independent paths.
chains = [model.generate(prompt, temperature=0.7) for _ in range(40)]
answers = [extract_answer(c) for c in chains]
final   = mode(answers)
Lifts GSM8K accuracy by 10–20 points on top of CoT. Costs \(N\times\) inference.

(C) Tree of Thoughts (ToT). Yao et al., 2023, “Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models.”
Generalize CoT into a tree of partial solutions; at each step the LLM proposes several next steps; a verifier (LLM-as-judge or programmatic) scores them; expand the most promising branches; backtrack when needed.
Three components:

Thought decomposition. Split the task into discrete reasoning steps.
Thought generator. Propose \(k\) candidate continuations per state.
State evaluator. Score states (“sure / maybe / impossible”).
Search algorithm. BFS, DFS, or beam over the tree.

Effective on combinatorial puzzles (Game of 24), creative writing with constraints, crosswords. Much more compute per query than CoT.

(D) ReAct (Reason + Act). Yao et al., 2022, “ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models.”
Interleaves reasoning (think) and acting (tool-call) tokens in a single trace:
Thought: I should look up the population of Hyderabad.
Action: search("population of Hyderabad")
Observation: Hyderabad has approximately 10 million people.
Thought: The question asks for the population in 2010. Let me search more specifically.
Action: search("Hyderabad population 2010")
Observation: 7.75 million in 2010.
Thought: Now I have the answer.
Answer: 7.75 million.
Standard pattern for tool-using agents. The model emits an action; the orchestrator parses it, runs the tool, returns an observation; the model continues.

(E) Reflexion. Shinn et al., 2023, “Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning.”
After a failed attempt, the agent generates a verbal critique (a “reflection”) and retries. The critique is stored in a memory buffer; subsequent attempts see prior critiques. Effectively self-improvement at inference time, without weight updates. Works well for code generation (use unit-test failures as the signal) and HotpotQA.

(F) Program of Thoughts (PoT) / PAL. Chen et al., 2022 (PoT); Gao et al., 2022 (PAL).
Instead of doing arithmetic / logical reasoning in natural language, the model writes a Python program that, when executed, produces the answer. The program’s output is the answer.
Q: If a train travels 60 mph for 3.5 hours, how far does it go?

Program:
speed = 60
time = 3.5
distance = speed * time
print(distance)

Output: 210
Offloads arithmetic to the Python interpreter, where errors are impossible.

(G) Self-Refine. Madaan et al., 2023, “Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback.”
A loop: generate → critique → refine. The same LLM plays generator, critic, and refiner. Iterate until the critique says “good” or budget is exhausted. Useful for writing tasks.

(H) Plan-and-Solve. Wang et al., 2023.
Two-stage: ask the model to write a high-level plan, then execute the plan step-by-step. Encourages decomposition before reasoning.

(I) Function calling / Tool use. Modern LLMs (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, Llama 3.1) are trained to emit structured function calls when given a tool schema:
[
  {"name": "search", "description": "Web search", "parameters": {"q": {"type": "string"}}},
  {"name": "calc",   "description": "Eval math",  "parameters": {"expr": {"type": "string"}}}
]
The model decides whether to call a tool; emits a JSON object naming the function and arguments; the runtime executes; the result is fed back as a tool-response message; the model continues.
Both ReAct and modern structured tool calling describe the same idea — structured tool calling is the more reliable, schema-enforced form.

(J) Prompt chaining (a.k.a. pipelines).
A chain is a directed graph of LLM calls and tool calls. Each node consumes outputs of upstream nodes and produces inputs for downstream nodes.
Q --> [classify intent] --> route
                              ├── (research)
                              │   └── [search] -> [summarize] -> [answer]
                              ├── (compute)
                              │   └── [program] -> [execute] -> [answer]
                              └── (chat)
                                  └── [answer]
Frameworks: LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack, DSPy (Stanford NLP — programmatic + auto-optimizing prompts).

(K) Agents. A loop of LLM calls + tools + memory + (sometimes) planning. The LLM decides what to do next based on the goal, prior actions, and observations. The recent surge in agentic systems (AutoGPT, BabyAGI, OpenAI Assistants, Anthropic computer-use, OpenAI o1’s deliberative reasoning) builds on the techniques above.

(L) Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Covered exhaustively in the companion RAG study guide; here it is “use a retriever to fetch context before the LLM call.” The most widely deployed prompt-chaining pattern.

Comparison table — reasoning strategies.









Method
Compute multiplier
Improves on
Best at




Direct prompting
1×
n/a
Simple questions


CoT
1–2×
Reasoning tasks
Math, commonsense


Self-Consistency
\(N\)× (typ. 40×)
CoT
Math, multi-step QA


ToT
many×
Combinatorial search
Puzzles, planning


ReAct
\(T\) × (T = tool calls)
Knowledge-intensive QA
Web QA, HotpotQA


Reflexion
iterative
Code, multi-step
Self-correcting tasks


PoT / PAL
1× + interpreter
Arithmetic / symbolic
Math word problems


Self-Refine
iterative
Writing
Drafting, editing


Plan-and-Solve
2×
Multi-step problems
Decomposable tasks


Function calling
T ×
Anything tool-friendly
Agents, APIs




Common assessment traps.

CoT is a prompt; it is not a model. Any sufficiently capable LLM can be CoT-prompted.
Self-Consistency operates on CoT samples — it requires temperature > 0.
ToT is a search procedure, not a single prompt.
ReAct interleaves reasoning and tool calls in one trace; tool calling (the OpenAI/Anthropic spec) is a structured form of the same idea.
Reflexion adds verbal self-critique to memory between attempts; it does not update weights.

title: LLMs for Engineers
text:
MODULE 11 — Measuring Language Model Performance

11.1 Task-Specific Evaluation Metrics
This subsection enumerates the metrics named in the syllabus (perplexity, BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR, BERTScore, exact match, F1, pass@k, MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval, MT-Bench, Arena Elo, judge-LLM evals) and the standards-of-practice around using them.

(A) Perplexity.
The exponential of the cross-entropy. Measures how surprised a language model is by held-out text.
\[
\operatorname{PPL}(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\!\Big(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \log p_\theta(x_i \mid x_{<i})\Big) = \exp(\mathrm{H})
\]
where \(\mathrm{H}\) is the average cross-entropy per token in nats. With natural log, the formula is \(\exp\); with log base 2, it is \(2^H\) — both are perplexity, just in different “units.”
Properties.

\(\operatorname{PPL} \geq 1\). A perfect model has \(\operatorname{PPL} = 1\); a uniform model over \(V\) tokens has \(\operatorname{PPL} = V\).
Tokenizer-dependent: a model with a smaller vocabulary produces more tokens per word and tends to have lower perplexity per token. Cross-tokenizer comparisons are misleading.
Length-normalized: dividing by \(N\) removes the document-length bias.

Worked numeric example. A model assigns the held-out sentence “The cat sat” probabilities \(p_1=0.5, p_2=0.4, p_3=0.6\). Cross-entropy \(= -(\ln 0.5 + \ln 0.4 + \ln 0.6)/3 \approx 0.671\). \(\operatorname{PPL} = e^{0.671} \approx 1.96\).

(B) BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy). Papineni et al., 2002.
Measures n-gram overlap between a generated text and one or more reference translations.

For each \(n\) (typically \(n=1,\dots,4\)), compute the clipped modified \(n\)-gram precision \(p_n\): fraction of generated \(n\)-grams that appear in the reference, clipped per \(n\)-gram to the maximum count in the reference.
Geometric-mean precisions: \(\prod_n p_n^{w_n}\), with \(w_n = 1/4\) typically.
Brevity penalty to discourage too-short outputs:

\[
\text{BP} = \begin{cases} 1 & c > r \\ e^{1 - r/c} & c \le r \end{cases}
\]
where \(c\) = candidate length, \(r\) = reference length.

BLEU \(= \text{BP} \cdot \exp\!\sum_n w_n \log p_n\).

Range: \([0, 1]\) (often reported as \([0, 100]\)). Higher is better.
Drawbacks. Precision-only; ignores synonyms; rewards literal phrases; uncorrelated with quality on creative tasks.

(C) ROUGE. Lin, 2004.
Recall-oriented metrics for summarization. Variants:

ROUGE-N — \(n\)-gram recall against references.
ROUGE-L — longest-common-subsequence (LCS) based F-measure.
ROUGE-S — skip-bigram.

For summarization, ROUGE-1 (unigram recall) and ROUGE-2 (bigram recall) are reported alongside ROUGE-L.

(D) METEOR. Banerjee & Lavie, 2005.
Includes stemming and synonym matching (via WordNet). Weighted F-mean of precision and recall, with a fragmentation penalty for word-order. Better correlation with human judgments than BLEU for short outputs.

(E) BERTScore. Zhang et al., 2019.
Embed each candidate and reference token with a contextual encoder (BERT, RoBERTa); compute cosine similarities; greedy alignment; report precision, recall, F1 over aligned similarities. Captures semantic similarity that BLEU misses.

(F) Exact Match (EM). A binary metric: did the model’s output exactly equal the gold answer? Used in SQuAD-style QA (after normalizing whitespace, punctuation, articles).

(G) F1 (token-overlap). For span-prediction QA: the F1 of the predicted token set against the gold token set. SQuAD reports both EM and F1.

(H) pass@k (code). Chen et al., 2021, “Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code” (Codex paper).
Probability that at least one of \(k\) generated samples passes the unit tests. Estimated unbiasedly via:
\[
\text{pass}@k = \mathbb{E}_\text{problems}\!\left[1 - \binom{n - c}{k} / \binom{n}{k}\right]
\]
where \(n\) is samples generated, \(c\) is the number that pass, \(k\) is the budget. Commonly reported at \(k = 1, 10, 100\).

(I) MMLU. Hendrycks et al., 2020, “Measuring Massive Multitask Language Understanding.”
57 subjects (math, physics, law, medicine, history, …), 4-way multiple choice, ~14k questions. Tests broad knowledge. Reported as overall accuracy and per-subject accuracy.

(J) GSM8K. Cobbe et al., 2021, “Training Verifiers to Solve Math Word Problems.”
8.5k grade-school math word problems with integer answers and detailed solutions. Standard benchmark for arithmetic reasoning. Reported as accuracy (exact-match on the final integer).

(K) HumanEval. Chen et al., 2021.
164 hand-written Python coding problems with unit tests. Reported as pass@k.

(L) MT-Bench. Zheng et al., 2023, “Judging LLM-as-a-Judge.”
80 multi-turn chat queries spanning 8 categories. Each response scored 1–10 by an LLM judge (GPT-4). Reports mean score; sometimes per-category breakdown.

(M) Chatbot Arena Elo. Zheng et al., 2023; lmsys.org.
Crowdsourced pairwise preference: users blind-test two anonymous chatbots; the winning model accumulates Elo points. Reflects head-to-head human preference rather than any single benchmark.

(N) LLM-as-judge. Use a strong LLM (GPT-4, Claude) to grade responses with a rubric. Cheaper than human labels; biased (verbosity bias, position bias, self-preference). Mitigations: random position, calibration prompts, multiple judges.
Position bias. LLM judges prefer the first option in pairwise comparison. Mitigation: present each pair twice in both orders and average.
Verbosity bias. Judges prefer longer responses regardless of quality.
Self-bias. A judge LLM prefers responses generated by the same family. Use a different family as the judge when possible.

Comparison table — when to use what.







Task
Metric




Pretraining quality
Perplexity (on a fixed test set, same tokenizer)


Machine translation
BLEU, chrF, COMET


Summarization
ROUGE-1/2/L, BERTScore


Extractive QA
Exact match, token-F1


Code generation
pass@k


Multiple-choice QA
Accuracy (MMLU, ARC, HellaSwag, BoolQ)


Math reasoning
Exact match on final answer (GSM8K, MATH)


Chat quality
MT-Bench, Arena Elo, LLM-as-judge


Instruction following
IFEval, AlpacaEval, MT-Bench


Harmlessness
TruthfulQA, HHH, ToxiGen


Long context
RULER, Needle-in-Haystack, LongBench




Common assessment traps.

Perplexity is tokenizer-dependent — cannot compare across tokenizers.
BLEU = precision; ROUGE = recall; both ignore semantics.
BERTScore captures semantics; correlated with human judgments.
pass@1 and pass@k are different settings of the same metric.
MT-Bench uses GPT-4 as judge; results are not the same as Elo (human preference).
Decontamination of training data is required before reporting MMLU / GSM8K / HumanEval — otherwise the model has memorized the test set.




11.2 Zero-Shot Evaluation
Definition. Zero-shot evaluation measures how well a model performs a task without task-specific fine-tuning and without in-context examples. Just the instruction (or, for log-likelihood evaluation, no instruction at all).
Two formulations.

Generative zero-shot. Prompt the model with the question; sample a response; score (exact match, regex, BLEU, judge).

Q: What is the capital of France?
A:

Log-likelihood zero-shot. For multiple-choice tasks, compute the log-probability the model assigns to each candidate answer (with the same prefix), pick the argmax.

P(A | Q): "The capital of France is Paris."   → log p_a
P(B | Q): "The capital of France is London."  → log p_b
...
choose argmax
Log-likelihood evaluation is deterministic and tokenization-stable, but only applies to fixed-option tasks. Generative evaluation is more realistic but introduces decoding variance.
Lengt normalization. For LL evaluation, longer candidates have lower joint probability. Common fix: divide log-prob by the number of tokens (or characters, or bytes). EleutherAI’s lm-evaluation-harness exposes acc (raw) and acc_norm (length-normalized) for many tasks.
Why zero-shot matters.

Measures the model’s generalization to unseen task formats.
Allows fair comparison: every model is evaluated on the same prompt.
Required for reasoning about emergent capabilities — abilities that appear without explicit training.

Tooling. The de facto standard is EleutherAI’s lm-evaluation-harness. Supports MMLU, ARC, HellaSwag, TruthfulQA, GSM8K (CoT), HumanEval, and hundreds more, with consistent log-likelihood and generative scoring.
lm_eval --model hf \
        --model_args pretrained=meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B \
        --tasks mmlu,arc_challenge,hellaswag,gsm8k \
        --num_fewshot 0 \
        --batch_size 8
Few-shot harness. Same setup but with \(k\) in-context examples drawn from the dataset’s dev split. Few-shot accuracy is usually 2–10 points higher than zero-shot.

Common assessment traps.

Zero-shot evaluation does not necessarily prompt the model to “answer the question.” With base models, an empty zero-shot prompt may continue rather than answer. Use log-likelihood evaluation for base models on multiple-choice tasks; use instruction-tuned models for generative zero-shot.
Decontamination is critical. Any model that has seen the test set will score artificially high.
Zero-shot ≠ unsupervised — the model was trained on a vast supervised-ish corpus; “zero-shot” refers only to the task presentation.

title: LLMs for Engineers
text:
MODULE 12 — Visualizing and Explaining Behavior

12.1 Visualizing Attention Weights
Definition and motivation. Attention weights \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{L\times L}\) assign each query position a probability distribution over key positions. Visualizing them tells you which tokens each token attended to — a coarse but informative view of how the model is routing information.
Tools.

BertViz (Vig, 2019, “A Multiscale Visualization of Attention in the Transformer Model”). Browser-based, supports HuggingFace models. Three views:

Head view: attention from each token to each other token, per head, per layer.
Model view: small-multiples grid of all heads across all layers.
Neuron view: dot-product attention with Q/K vectors shown.

exBERT (Hoover et al., 2019). Browser app for BERT-family models with cluster analysis of attention patterns.
TransformerLens / nnsight / Pyvene (more advanced; for mechanistic interpretability).

Reading an attention map.

Diagonal-dominant pattern: token attends mostly to itself.
Off-diagonal stripes: token attends to specific relative positions.
“Look-back” heads: attend to specific previous occurrences of the same token (induction heads).
“Global” heads: attend to special tokens like [CLS] or BOS.

Caveats.

A high attention weight is not a causal explanation. Attention \(\neq\) saliency. Two papers (Jain & Wallace 2019; Wiegreffe & Pinter 2019) debated whether attention is “explanation”; consensus: attention shows where information can flow, not where it must.
Multi-head attention diffuses interpretation; one head’s pattern may be canceled by another.
Visualization helps generate hypotheses; rigorous attribution requires causal interventions (Module 12.2).

Code — visualizing attention in HuggingFace.
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
m = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased", output_attentions=True)
inputs = tok("The cat sat on the mat.", return_tensors="pt")
out = m(**inputs)
attentions = out.attentions     # tuple of (B, n_heads, L, L) per layer

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
attn_l0_h0 = attentions[0][0, 0].detach().numpy()
plt.imshow(attn_l0_h0, cmap="viridis")
plt.xticks(range(len(inputs.input_ids[0])), tok.convert_ids_to_tokens(inputs.input_ids[0]), rotation=90)
plt.yticks(range(len(inputs.input_ids[0])), tok.convert_ids_to_tokens(inputs.input_ids[0]))



12.2 Interpreting Attention Maps and Mechanistic Interpretability
This subsection goes beyond visualization into the mechanistic interpretability program: reverse-engineering what specific components of a transformer do.
The residual-stream view. Elhage et al. (2021), “A Mathematical Framework for Transformer Circuits.” Treat the transformer as components that read from and write to a \(d\)-dimensional residual stream. Each attention head and MLP is a function read(residual) -> write(residual). Interpretability ≈ identifying which directions each component reads and writes.

(A) Induction heads. Olsson et al. (2022), “In-context Learning and Induction Heads.”
A specific two-attention-head circuit that implements pattern-matching in-context: given a sequence ... A B ... A, the model predicts B. The first head identifies the previous occurrence of token A; the second copies the token that followed it.
Induction heads are present in essentially every transformer LLM beyond a few hundred parameters and explain a substantial fraction of in-context learning. They emerge during training at a specific compute threshold (“phase transition”).

(B) Circuits. Olah, Cammarata et al. (2020, “Zoom In: An Introduction to Circuits”) and the transformer-circuits.pub series. A circuit is a sub-graph of model components (heads, MLPs, neurons) jointly implementing a behavior — e.g., indirect-object identification (Wang et al. 2022).
Methods to discover circuits:

Activation patching. Replace one component’s activation with a different prompt’s activation; see how outputs change. Identifies which components contribute causally.
Path patching. Trace specific routes through the graph.
Ablation. Zero out or mean-ablate components; measure performance drop.


(C) Probing classifiers. Conneau et al. (2018); Tenney et al. (2019).
Train a linear (or shallow MLP) probe on top of frozen model activations to predict a linguistic property (POS tag, dependency relation, named-entity boundary, factual claim). If the probe scores high, the property is linearly decodable from those activations.
Caveat: a high-accuracy probe shows the information is present, not that the model uses it. Causal probes (Elazar et al., 2021 “amnesic probing”) test usage by removing the information and remeasuring.

(D) Logit lens. nostalgebraist (2020 blog post).
Project each layer’s residual-stream output through the final LM head to inspect the next-token distribution as if generation stopped at that layer. Reveals how predictions evolve across depth — early layers often have shallow guesses; later layers refine.
final_norm = model.transformer.ln_f
lm_head    = model.lm_head
for i, h in enumerate(hidden_states):       # one per layer
    logits = lm_head(final_norm(h))
    print(i, top_k(logits[:, -1]))

(E) Tuned lens. Belrose et al., 2023, “Eliciting Latent Predictions from Transformers with the Tuned Lens.”
Improves logit lens by training a small affine probe per layer to better project residual streams into logit space. Smoother, more informative depth-curves of predictions.

(F) Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs). Cunningham et al., 2023, “Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features in Language Models”; Anthropic’s “Scaling Monosemanticity” (2024).
Train an autoencoder on a transformer layer’s activations with an L1 sparsity penalty:
\[
h \to \text{encoder} \to z \quad \text{(sparse code)} \quad \to \text{decoder} \to \hat{h}
\]
Loss: \(\|h - \hat{h}\|^2 + \lambda \|z\|_1\).
The dictionary \(z\) has many more features than \(h\)’s dimension. Each \(z\) feature, when interpreted, often corresponds to a monosemantic concept (“the Golden Gate Bridge”, “Python list comprehensions”, “anger about traffic”). Anthropic’s 2024 work scaled SAEs to frontier models, recovering tens of millions of features.
SAEs are now a primary tool for circuit-finding, feature steering (“amplify this feature, suppress that one”), and safety auditing.

(G) Activation steering / representation engineering. Turner et al., 2023; Zou et al., 2023, “Representation Engineering.”
Add a learned (or hand-crafted) direction to the residual stream during inference to bias the model toward a behavior (truthfulness, helpfulness, refusal). Cheap and effective for some behaviors; less reliable than fine-tuning.

(H) Causal tracing. Meng et al., 2022, “Locating and Editing Factual Associations in GPT” (ROME). Identify which layer / token positions store a specific fact by patching activations from a corrupted run into a clean run.

Tools for interpretability.







Tool
What it does




TransformerLens (Neel Nanda)
Hooks for attention/MLP activations, ablation, patching


nnsight
Remote-execution interpretability (works on 70B+ via NDIF)


SAELens
Train and analyze sparse autoencoders


pyvene
Declarative interventions on PyTorch models


BertViz / exBERT
Attention visualization


Captum
Saliency, integrated gradients, layer attributions




Common assessment traps.

Attention weights are not explanations — they show where information could flow, not which information actually shaped the output.
Probing shows decodability, not causal use.
Logit lens projects intermediate residual streams through the final unembedding; tuned lens learns a per-layer projection.
Sparse autoencoders find features, not full circuits; circuits are wired-together features.
Mechanistic interpretability is the program of reverse-engineering transformers, distinct from post-hoc explainability (SHAP, saliency).

title: LLMs for Engineers
text:
Foundational architecture

Vaswani, Shazeer, Parmar, Uszkoreit, Jones, Gomez, Kaiser, Polosukhin (2017). Attention Is All You Need. The original transformer. arXiv:1706.03762.
Radford, Narasimhan, Salimans, Sutskever (2018). Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training. GPT-1.
Radford, Wu, Child, Luan, Amodei, Sutskever (2019). Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners. GPT-2.
Brown et al. (2020). Language Models are Few-Shot Learners. GPT-3. arXiv:2005.14165.
Devlin, Chang, Lee, Toutanova (2018). BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding. arXiv:1810.04805.
Raffel et al. (2020). Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer. T5. JMLR.
Chowdhery et al. (2022). PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways.
Touvron et al. (2023). LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models. arXiv:2302.13971.
Touvron et al. (2023). Llama 2: Open Foundation and Fine-Tuned Chat Models. arXiv:2307.09288.
Grattafiori et al. (Meta) (2024). The Llama 3 Herd of Models. arXiv:2407.21783.
Jiang et al. (2023). Mistral 7B.
Jiang et al. (2024). Mixtral of Experts. arXiv:2401.04088.
Hoffmann et al. (2022). Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models. Chinchilla. arXiv:2203.15556.

title: LLMs for Engineers
text:
Attention and architecture variants

Shazeer (2019). Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need. MQA. arXiv:1911.02150.

title: Deep Generative Models
text:
This document is a single, self-contained reference on deep generative models, written as a book chapter that takes a reader from zero to expert. It is organized to mirror the assessment syllabus exactly so that every named skill has a dedicated section, but the content is presented as continuous study material — equations, derivations, code, pseudocode, comparisons, and worked numerical exercises sit side by side.
A few orientation notes before diving in:

Notation. \(x \in \mathbb{R}^d\) is the data point (image, audio, token sequence). \(z\) is a latent variable. \(\theta\) is a generic parameter vector for a neural network. \(p_{\text{data}}(x)\) is the true (unknown) data distribution; \(p_\theta(x)\) is the model’s distribution. \(q\) usually denotes a variational or forward-noise distribution. \(\mathbb{E}_{x\sim p}[f(x)]\) is an expectation. \(\mathrm{KL}(q\Vert p) = \mathbb{E}_q[\log q - \log p]\) is the Kullback–Leibler divergence. \(\mathcal{N}(\mu,\Sigma)\) is a Gaussian.
Math rendering. All math uses $...$ (inline) or $$...$$ (block) for Quarto/MathJax. Derivations are shown step by step, not just stated.
Code. PyTorch-style snippets are minimal but complete in the sense that the tensor shapes, loss expressions, and update rules can be read like a recipe. Tensor shapes are written as comments where they matter.
Pseudocode. Algorithm 1 / Algorithm 2 boxes follow the DDPM paper’s convention: a training loop and a sampling loop, side by side.
Comparison tables. Whenever multiple methods share a problem (e.g., DDPM vs DDIM, VAE vs GAN vs flow vs diffusion, FID vs IS vs Precision/Recall), a table makes the differences explicit.
“Assessment traps” sidebars highlight vocabulary distinctions that are easy to confuse: generative vs discriminative, ε-prediction vs v-prediction, classifier vs classifier-free guidance, mode collapse vs mode covering.
Worked exercises appear inline. They use small numbers (e.g., \(T=4\) timesteps, scalar latents) so you can carry the arithmetic in your head and verify each formula.

Read the parts in order on a first pass. On revisits, you can jump to a specific Module via the table of contents — every Module is self-contained enough that it stands alone after the foundational definitions in Part 1.

title: Deep Generative Models
section: MODULE 1: Comparing Generative and Discriminative Approaches
text:
MODULE 1: Comparing Generative and Discriminative Approaches

1.1 Distinguishing Generative from Discriminative Models in Data Contexts
The cleanest definition. Given data \((x, y)\) where \(x\) is an input (e.g., an image) and \(y\) is a label,

a discriminative model learns the conditional distribution \(p(y \mid x)\) directly. It answers “given this input, which class / value of \(y\) is likely?”.
a generative model learns the joint distribution \(p(x, y)\), or in the unsupervised case just \(p(x)\). It answers “how is the data itself distributed?” and can be sampled to produce new \(x\).

If you know the joint \(p(x,y)\), you can derive the discriminative answer via Bayes’ rule:
\[
p(y \mid x) \;=\; \frac{p(x \mid y)\, p(y)}{p(x)} \;=\; \frac{p(x, y)}{\sum_{y'} p(x, y')}.
\]
So generative models contain discriminative ones as a special inference query, but the reverse is not true: a model trained only to output \(p(y \mid x)\) cannot synthesize new \(x\).
Generative vs discriminative — side by side.








Aspect
Discriminative \(p(y\mid x)\)
Generative \(p(x, y)\) or \(p(x)\)




What is modeled
Decision boundary between classes
Distribution of the data itself


Typical objective
Cross-entropy / hinge / MSE on labels
Maximum likelihood, ELBO, adversarial, score matching


Can synthesize new \(x\)?
No
Yes


Handles missing inputs?
Poorly
Naturally (marginalize over the missing dimensions)


Robust to label noise / class imbalance?
Sensitive
Often more robust because \(p(x)\) is shared


Inference query supported
\(\arg\max_y p(y\mid x)\)
\(\arg\max_y p(y\mid x)\), \(p(x)\), sampling, anomaly score, imputation


Data efficiency on small labels
Often better with enough labels
Useful when labels are scarce and unlabeled \(x\) is plentiful


Examples
Logistic regression, SVM, CNN classifier, BERT for classification
Naive Bayes, GMM, HMM, VAE, GAN, normalizing flow, diffusion model, GPT (autoregressive over \(x\))



Mental picture. A discriminative model draws a boundary through the input space. A generative model paints the density of the input space itself; the boundary is then a side-effect of comparing how much each class explains a given \(x\).
Bayes-rule sanity check. A Gaussian discriminant analysis classifier fits one Gaussian per class and combines them through Bayes’ rule:
\[
p(y=k \mid x) \;=\; \frac{\pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x;\mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j} \pi_j \, \mathcal{N}(x;\mu_j, \Sigma_j)}.
\]
It is generative because you can sample: pick a class \(k\) with probability \(\pi_k\), then draw \(x \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_k,\Sigma_k)\). Logistic regression, by contrast, parametrizes \(p(y\mid x) = \sigma(w^\top x + b)\) directly without modeling \(p(x)\) — purely discriminative.
Examples table (named architectures).








Model
Models
Generative or discriminative




Logistic regression
\(p(y\mid x)\)
Discriminative


SVM
sign of \(w^\top x + b\)
Discriminative (no probability at all)


Standard CNN classifier (ResNet for ImageNet)
\(p(y\mid x)\) via softmax
Discriminative


Naive Bayes
\(p(x\mid y)\,p(y)\)
Generative


HMM
\(p(x_{1:T}, y_{1:T})\)
Generative


GPT (decoder LM)
\(p(x_{1:T})\) as \(\prod_t p(x_t \mid x_{<t})\)
Generative (autoregressive over tokens)


BERT (encoder, masked LM)
\(p(x_{\text{mask}} \mid x_{\text{visible}})\)
Often called “discriminative-style pretraining” (the model is used downstream as a feature extractor / classifier); but the masked-language objective is conditional generative over masked tokens


VAE
\(p(x)\) via latent \(z\)
Generative


GAN
implicit \(p(x)\) (sample-only)
Generative


Normalizing flow
\(p(x)\) exactly
Generative


DDPM / score-based diffusion
\(p(x)\)
Generative



Common assessment trap — vocabulary.

Generative is about modeling the distribution; discriminative is about modeling the boundary.
“Generative AI” colloquially means “AI that makes new content”; technically, generative in the statistical sense is broader (anything that models \(p(x)\) or \(p(x,y)\)) but in this study guide the two senses coincide because we focus on models that do generate samples.
A GAN models \(p(x)\) but does not give you a tractable likelihood; it is still generative because you can sample from it.
A masked language model (BERT) is sometimes described as discriminative because its main downstream use is classification, but the pretext task itself is a (conditional) generative one.




1.2 Applications of Generative Models in Synthesis and Augmentation
Generative models are interesting not because they trace pretty contours of \(p(x)\) but because of the things you can do once you can sample from \(p(x)\) or \(p(x \mid c)\) for some condition \(c\). The applications below are the textbook list — each is worth knowing by name because the assessment loves “which of these is a generative-model application?” items.
Image synthesis. Unconditional: sample faces (StyleGAN), animals (BigGAN), arbitrary images. Conditional: text-to-image (Stable Diffusion, Imagen, DALL·E, Midjourney), class-conditional ImageNet (BigGAN, ADM, EDM), sketch-to-image, segmentation-to-image (Pix2Pix), pose-to-image (ControlNet).
Audio synthesis. Text-to-speech (Tacotron + WaveNet/HiFi-GAN vocoders, VALL-E, Bark), music generation (MusicLM, Jukebox, MusicGen, AudioLDM), sound effects, neural codecs (SoundStream, Encodec) that combine a generative decoder with a learned discrete representation.
Text generation. Autoregressive language models (GPT family, Llama, Claude, Gemini) are generative models of token sequences. Open-ended dialogue, summarization, translation, code generation are all sampling problems from \(p(x_{1:T} \mid \text{prompt})\).
Video generation and editing. Sora, Veo, Runway Gen-3, Stable Video Diffusion. Same diffusion or transformer toolkit, but on space-time tokens / latents.
Molecule and protein generation. Drug discovery (generate novel molecules with desired properties, e.g., MolGAN, EDM-for-molecules, RFdiffusion for protein backbones); de novo protein design (RFdiffusion, Chroma, ProteinMPNN). The data distribution lives on graphs or 3-D point clouds with equivariance constraints, but the underlying technology is still diffusion / flow / autoregressive.
Data augmentation. Synthesize extra training samples to expand or rebalance a dataset — especially powerful for rare classes (medical lesions, fraud examples), or for unsupervised representation learning where the augmentations themselves are the supervision signal (SimCLR, MoCo treat augmentations as positive pairs).
Simulation. World models for reinforcement learning (Dreamer, DayDreamer, GameNGen), simulators for self-driving (driving scenarios), physics surrogates that replace expensive PDE solvers with a learned generative model conditioned on parameters.
Anomaly detection. A generative model trained on “normal” data assigns low likelihood (or high reconstruction error) to anomalies. VAEs, flows, and diffusion models can all be used this way. The key signal is some scalar that estimates how far \(x\) is from the data manifold — exact likelihood, ELBO, score norm, or reconstruction loss.
Super-resolution. Upscale a low-resolution image to a high-resolution one. ESRGAN (GAN-based), SR3 (diffusion-based), Real-ESRGAN. The model learns \(p(x_{\text{HR}} \mid x_{\text{LR}})\).
Inpainting and editing. Fill in masked regions of an image: Pix2Pix (paired translation), LaMa, Stable Diffusion inpainting, RePaint (DDPM inpainting via masked diffusion). Same idea for audio (gap filling) and video (object removal).
Translation / domain transfer. CycleGAN (horses ↔︎ zebras, summer ↔︎ winter), Pix2Pix (paired sketch ↔︎ photo), neural machine translation, code translation. Conditional generative models with a strong cycle-consistency or paired-supervision signal.
Compression. Neural compression (Ballé et al., HiFiC) uses a learned generative decoder so that a small bitstream + decoder generates a perceptually faithful reconstruction.
Likelihood-based scientific modeling. Cosmology (generative models of galaxy images), particle physics (fast detector simulators replacing GEANT4), neuroscience (generative models of neural activity).
Application table — what each modality usually uses today.







Domain
Dominant modern generative families




Images
Diffusion (latent diffusion / Stable Diffusion, DALL·E 3, Imagen), GANs still strong for faces (StyleGAN), autoregressive transformers on image tokens (Parti, Muse)


Video
Diffusion (Sora, Veo, SVD), autoregressive token models


Audio (TTS)
Diffusion + neural vocoder, or autoregressive token models (VALL-E) on neural-codec tokens


Music
Autoregressive token models on codec tokens (MusicGen), latent diffusion (AudioLDM)


Text
Autoregressive transformer LMs (GPT family)


Molecules / proteins
Diffusion / flow on graphs and 3-D structures; equivariant networks


Tabular
VAEs, normalizing flows, conditional diffusion, autoregressive (TabPFN-style)


Anomaly detection
VAE / flow / diffusion likelihoods; reconstruction error



Why “data augmentation” is on the list. For supervised learning with very few labels per class, training a class-conditional generative model on the unlabeled pool and then sampling per class is a way to manufacture new training data. The catch: a generative model that has already learned the dataset cannot, in principle, add genuinely new information; but as a regularizer it often helps. The most successful modern variant of this idea is augmentation by generation (e.g., synthetic medical images by class-conditional diffusion).



1.3 Unsupervised Learning Principles for Generative Modeling
Generative modeling is the canonical unsupervised-learning problem. The principles below recur in every architecture later in the guide.
Principle 1 — Density estimation. The fundamental task is to estimate \(p_{\text{data}}(x)\). Given i.i.d. samples \(x^{(1)}, \dots, x^{(N)} \sim p_{\text{data}}\), learn \(p_\theta\) that is close to \(p_{\text{data}}\). “Close” is usually measured by KL divergence:
\[
\mathrm{KL}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\Vert\, p_\theta\right) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}\!\left[\log p_{\text{data}}(x) - \log p_\theta(x)\right].
\]
Since \(\log p_{\text{data}}(x)\) doesn’t depend on \(\theta\), minimizing this KL is equivalent to maximizing \(\mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log p_\theta(x)]\), i.e., maximum likelihood. With \(N\) samples, this is the average log-likelihood
\[
\mathcal{L}_{\text{MLE}}(\theta) \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_\theta(x^{(i)}).
\]
Three deep generative families optimize this (or a bound on it) directly: autoregressive models, normalizing flows, and VAEs (via ELBO). GANs optimize a different objective (Jensen–Shannon-like adversarial). Diffusion models optimize an ELBO that, in practice, is reduced to a denoising regression loss equivalent to score matching.
Principle 2 — Latent variable models. A latent-variable model writes the data distribution as a marginal:
\[
p_\theta(x) \;=\; \int p_\theta(x \mid z)\, p(z)\, dz,
\]
where \(z\) is an unobserved “explanation” and \(p(z)\) is a prior (often \(\mathcal{N}(0, I)\)). This decomposition is conceptually powerful for three reasons:

Sampling is easy. Draw \(z \sim p(z)\), then \(x \sim p_\theta(x\mid z)\).
A simple prior + a complex decoder can model a very complex data distribution, because \(z\) encodes the slow modes of variation (pose, identity, content) and the decoder paints in details.
Inference \(p_\theta(z \mid x)\) gives a useful representation that can be used for downstream tasks (clustering, retrieval, interpolation).

Examples: VAE (continuous \(z\), parametric decoder, learned approximate posterior); GAN (implicit decoder, no explicit likelihood); normalizing flow (decoder is invertible, so we can compute \(z = f^{-1}(x)\) exactly).
Principle 3 — The manifold hypothesis. Natural data — images, audio, language — does not fill \(\mathbb{R}^d\); it concentrates on a much lower-dimensional manifold \(\mathcal{M} \subset \mathbb{R}^d\). Two consequences:

Most random vectors in pixel space are noise; only a thin set are valid images. The job of a generative model is to learn that set.
A useful latent space \(z \in \mathbb{R}^{d'}\) with \(d' \ll d\) should give a chart of that manifold; “moving” in \(z\) should correspond to traversing the manifold smoothly (smile \(\to\) frown, day \(\to\) night).

Principle 4 — The ELBO (Evidence Lower Bound). For latent-variable models, the marginal likelihood \(p_\theta(x) = \int p_\theta(x,z)\,dz\) is intractable. The variational trick introduces a tractable approximate posterior \(q_\phi(z\mid x)\) and writes a lower bound on \(\log p_\theta(x)\).
Derivation. For any distribution \(q_\phi(z\mid x)\),
\[
\log p_\theta(x)
= \log \int p_\theta(x, z)\, dz
= \log \int q_\phi(z\mid x) \frac{p_\theta(x,z)}{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\, dz.
\]
Applying Jensen’s inequality (because \(\log\) is concave):
\[
\log p_\theta(x) \;\ge\; \int q_\phi(z\mid x)\, \log \frac{p_\theta(x,z)}{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\, dz \;\equiv\; \mathrm{ELBO}(x;\theta,\phi).
\]
Rearranging,
\[
\mathrm{ELBO}(x;\theta,\phi)
= \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\!\left[\log p_\theta(x\mid z)\right]}_{\text{reconstruction}} \;-\; \underbrace{\mathrm{KL}\!\left(q_\phi(z\mid x) \,\Vert\, p(z)\right)}_{\text{regularization}}.
\]
The gap between \(\log p_\theta(x)\) and the ELBO is exactly \(\mathrm{KL}(q_\phi(z\mid x)\Vert p_\theta(z\mid x))\) — when the approximate posterior matches the true posterior, the bound is tight. The VAE optimizes the ELBO. Diffusion models also use an ELBO whose KL terms reduce to denoising losses (Module 3).
Principle 5 — Contrastive learning (an alternative unsupervised signal). Contrastive methods learn a representation by pulling together views of the same data and pushing apart views of different data. The InfoNCE objective is
\[
\mathcal{L}_{\text{InfoNCE}} \;=\; -\,\mathbb{E}\!\left[\log \frac{\exp(\mathrm{sim}(f(x), f(x^+))/\tau)}{\sum_{x^-} \exp(\mathrm{sim}(f(x), f(x^-))/\tau)}\right].
\]
Contrastive learning is not, strictly speaking, generative — it does not give you \(p(x)\) — but it is closely related: it is mutual-information maximization between views, and it produces representations that often work as drop-in features for downstream tasks. CLIP combines contrastive learning over (image, text) pairs to produce embeddings used in diffusion-model conditioning. The connection to generative modeling is the energy-based view below.
Principle 6 — Energy-based view. An energy-based model (EBM) writes the density as a normalized exponential:
\[
p_\theta(x) \;=\; \frac{\exp(-E_\theta(x))}{Z(\theta)}, \qquad Z(\theta) = \int \exp(-E_\theta(x'))\, dx'.
\]
\(E_\theta : \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}\) is a freely parametrized neural net (no normalization constraint). The headache is that \(Z(\theta)\) is intractable. Training is done with surrogates: contrastive divergence, score matching (avoid \(Z\) entirely by working with \(\nabla_x \log p_\theta\)), noise contrastive estimation, or by sampling via Langevin dynamics. Score-based diffusion models are formally a particular kind of energy-based model whose score is parametrized directly — that is why “denoising score matching” and “diffusion models” turn out to be the same thing.
Worked toy — MLE on a 1-D Gaussian. Suppose \(p_{\text{data}} = \mathcal{N}(\mu_*, 1)\), and the model is \(p_\theta = \mathcal{N}(\theta, 1)\). With \(N\) samples \(x^{(i)}\),
\[
\log p_\theta(x^{(i)}) = -\tfrac{1}{2}(x^{(i)} - \theta)^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}\log(2\pi).
\]
Setting the derivative of the sum to zero,
\[
\sum_i (x^{(i)} - \theta) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \hat\theta_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i x^{(i)}.
\]
This sample mean is the canonical MLE result. The same logic, with vastly more parameters, drives every likelihood-based deep generative model.
Common assessment trap — vocabulary.

Density estimation and generative modeling are nearly synonyms in this guide; “estimation” emphasizes the statistical view, “modeling” the engineering view.
Likelihood and evidence both mean \(p_\theta(x)\); the ELBO is a lower bound on \(\log p_\theta(x)\).
Latent variable \(z\) is unobserved by definition; a feature or embedding is similar in spirit but not necessarily probabilistic.
Unsupervised learning has no labels; self-supervised learning constructs pseudo-labels from the data itself (e.g., next-token prediction). Generative pretraining (GPT) is unsupervised in the classical sense and self-supervised in the modern sense.

title: Deep Generative Models
text:
MODULE 2: Introducing Main Generative Architectures

2.1 Core Types of Deep Generative Models
This is the menagerie. Each family has a defining trick — a way of writing \(p(x)\) or sampling from it that side-steps the intractable normalization constant. We’ll see each in much more depth later; here is the inventory, with the one-line idea you must keep straight on the assessment.
Autoregressive models (AR). Factorize \(p(x) = \prod_t p(x_t \mid x_{<t})\) and learn each conditional with a neural net. Tractable likelihood, slow sequential sampling. Examples: PixelRNN/PixelCNN (images, pixel-by-pixel), WaveNet (audio, sample-by-sample), GPT-style decoders (text, token-by-token), ImageGPT, Parti, Muse, MaskGIT (image tokens), MusicGen, VALL-E (audio codec tokens).
Variational Autoencoders (VAE). Latent-variable model with \(z\sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)\), decoder \(p_\theta(x\mid z)\), encoder \(q_\phi(z\mid x)\). Optimize the ELBO. Reparameterization trick makes gradients flow through stochastic \(z\). Closed-form Gaussian-Gaussian KL. Variants: β-VAE (disentanglement), VQ-VAE / VQ-VAE-2 (discrete latents, the workhorse for image tokenizers feeding latent diffusion / autoregressive transformers), NVAE, VDVAE. Strengths: fast sampling, interpretable latents, decent likelihoods, stable training. Weaknesses: samples tend to be blurry because the Gaussian decoder averages plausible reconstructions.
Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN). Train a generator \(G\) that maps noise \(z\) to \(G(z)\) and a discriminator \(D\) that distinguishes real from generated. Minimax game:
\[
\min_G \max_D \; \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}}\!\left[\log D(x)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{z\sim p(z)}\!\left[\log(1 - D(G(z)))\right].
\]
No explicit likelihood, but sharp samples. Variants: DCGAN (deep conv), WGAN/WGAN-GP (Wasserstein, gradient penalty), Progressive GAN, StyleGAN1/2/3 (state-of-the-art faces), BigGAN (class-conditional ImageNet), CycleGAN/Pix2Pix (image translation), ESRGAN (super-resolution), HiFi-GAN (audio), VQ-GAN (used as a tokenizer for autoregressive image models).
Normalizing flows. Define an invertible neural net \(f_\theta : z \to x\) with \(z\sim p(z)\). By the change-of-variables formula,
\[
\log p_\theta(x) \;=\; \log p\!\left(f_\theta^{-1}(x)\right) + \log\left|\det \frac{\partial f_\theta^{-1}}{\partial x}\right|.
\]
Exact likelihood, exact inversion, free sampling. The art is in designing layers that are invertible and have tractable Jacobian determinants. Examples: NICE (additive coupling), RealNVP (affine coupling), Glow (1×1 invertible conv + affine coupling), MAF/IAF (autoregressive flows), FFJORD / Neural ODE (continuous-time flow), CTM/CNF (continuous normalizing flow).
Diffusion / score-based models. Define a forward process that gradually adds Gaussian noise to a data point over \(T\) steps, and learn the reverse process that denoises. The objective reduces to predicting the noise (or the data, or the velocity) from a noisy sample at a random step. The key papers fall into two equivalent formulations:

DDPM (Ho et al., 2020) — discrete-time, ELBO derivation, \(\epsilon\)-prediction.
NCSN (Song & Ermon, 2019) and score-based SDE (Song et al., 2021) — continuous-time stochastic differential equation; train a score network \(s_\theta(x,t) \approx \nabla_x \log p_t(x)\).

Variants: Improved DDPM (cosine schedule, learned variance, importance-weighted loss), ADM / Guided Diffusion (UNet improvements, classifier guidance), Latent Diffusion / Stable Diffusion (run diffusion in a VAE’s latent space), DDIM, DPM-Solver, EDM (fast and deterministic samplers), Imagen (text-to-image with T5), DiT (diffusion transformer), Cascaded Diffusion (low-res → super-res chain).
Energy-based models (EBM). \(p_\theta(x) = \exp(-E_\theta(x))/Z\) with intractable \(Z\). Trained by contrastive divergence (MCMC + sample comparison), score matching, or noise-contrastive estimation. Examples: classical Restricted Boltzmann Machines, modern deep EBMs (Du & Mordatch, “Implicit Generation and Modeling with Energy-Based Models”). EBMs are conceptually general but harder to train at scale than diffusion; nevertheless the score-based formulation has rehabilitated them — a diffusion model is, mathematically, a denoising score-matching EBM.
Consistency models. Introduced by Song et al., 2023 (“Consistency Models”). Learn a function \(f_\theta(x_t, t)\) that maps any noisy point on a probability-flow ODE trajectory back to the same starting point. The result is a model that can generate samples in one step (or a small number of steps) while remaining trainable from scratch or distilled from a diffusion teacher. Variants: Latent Consistency Models (LCM), CM++, Stable Diffusion XL Turbo (distilled consistency-like models for one-step text-to-image).
Flow matching / rectified flow. Learn a velocity field \(v_\theta(x_t, t)\) that transports samples from a simple prior \(p_0\) (Gaussian) to the data distribution \(p_1 = p_{\text{data}}\) along an ODE \[
\frac{dx}{dt} = v_\theta(x, t).
\] Flow Matching (Lipman et al., 2022) trains \(v_\theta\) by regressing it on a closed-form conditional velocity along straight-line paths between samples and Gaussians. Rectified Flow (Liu et al., 2022) does the same with straighter trajectories that allow few-step sampling. Stable Diffusion 3 and many recent image models are rectified-flow models, not “diffusion” in the original DDPM sense — but the practical machinery (UNet/DiT, samplers, CFG) is essentially the same.
Family inventory — at a glance.

2.2 Key Use Cases for Each Generative Model Type
This subsection answers the recurring assessment question “which model family fits this use case?” It restates the families from 2.1 in terms of where each one is the standard choice today.
Autoregressive models — when to reach for them.

Discrete tokens (text, code, image tokens, audio codec tokens). The factorization is natural; no need for a continuous Gaussian likelihood.
Tasks where you want exact likelihood and likelihood-based decisions (compression, anomaly detection on sequences).
Long context, sequential streaming generation where you can pay the per-step cost (LLM chat).
Image and audio at low resolution / token-level (PixelCNN, WaveNet) when latency budgets are generous.
Not the first choice for high-resolution images and video at modern quality — diffusion / latent diffusion / flow matching dominate there because their parallel score predictions are much faster than per-pixel autoregression.

VAEs — when to reach for them.

Representation learning with a smooth latent space (interpolation, attribute manipulation, β-VAE for disentanglement).
Fast, stable, latent-variable models for moderate-fidelity generation (faces at low resolution, tabular synthesis, anomaly detection with a likelihood proxy).
As a tokenizer / latent codec for other generative models (VQ-VAE → autoregressive tokens for DALL·E, Parti; KL-VAE → latent diffusion for Stable Diffusion). This is, in 2026 practice, by far the biggest “use case” for VAE-style autoencoders: they compress images into a smaller latent space where the real generative model (diffusion, autoregressive transformer) operates.
Conditional VAEs (CVAE) for one-to-many prediction tasks (motion forecasting, missing-data imputation).

GANs — when to reach for them.

High-resolution face and portrait synthesis (StyleGAN family is still extremely strong here at modest compute).
Domain translation with cycle consistency (CycleGAN: horse↔︎zebra; UNIT/MUNIT).
Paired image-to-image translation (Pix2Pix: edges→shoes, segmentation→photo).
Super-resolution with sharp outputs (ESRGAN, Real-ESRGAN).
Audio vocoders (HiFi-GAN, MelGAN) where a few-millisecond inference and sharp waveforms matter.
As a tokenizer (VQ-GAN) feeding autoregressive transformers (Taming Transformers / Parti / MaskGIT pipeline).
Anywhere you need a 1-step generator with sharp output, you don’t need likelihood, and you can engineer around training instability.
Less competitive in 2026 as a general text-to-image model — diffusion / flow matching are state-of-the-art there.

Normalizing flows — when to reach for them.

Exact likelihood requirements (scientific applications, physics simulation surrogates, density estimation with calibrated uncertainty).
Invertible representations for normalizing-flow posteriors in Bayesian inference (variational inference with flows).
Lossless compression (bits-back coding with flow likelihoods).
Tabular density estimation, particle physics, cosmology — anywhere \(p(x)\) itself is the deliverable.
Continuous-time flows / Neural ODEs for time-series generative modeling.
Less competitive for natural images at high resolution — flow architectures struggle to match diffusion’s quality at the same compute, although flow matching (which is not exactly the same as a classical normalizing flow but shares the change-of-variables flavor) has now closed much of the gap.

Diffusion and score-based models — when to reach for them.

Text-to-image, text-to-video, image-to-image at modern quality (Stable Diffusion, Imagen, DALL·E 3, Sora).
Conditional image generation with rich control (ControlNet, T2I-Adapter, IP-Adapter, inpainting, SDEdit).
Audio diffusion for TTS, music, sound effects (AudioLDM, NaturalSpeech 2/3, Diff-TTS).
3-D and protein structure generation (RFdiffusion, Chroma, EDM-for-molecules).
Solving inverse problems (super-resolution, inpainting, MRI reconstruction) via conditional samplers.
Anywhere mode coverage matters and you can pay 20–50 denoising steps, or you can distill to consistency / few-step models for latency.

Energy-based models — when to reach for them.

Composable conditioning (combine multiple energy terms additively to enforce constraints).
Theoretical / principled modeling when you want \(\nabla_x \log p(x)\) for sampling (Langevin) or downstream optimization (test-time guidance).
As the conceptual foundation for score-based diffusion: every diffusion model is “an energy-based model parametrized by its score”.
Less common as a standalone deployed system in 2026 because diffusion + consistency + flow matching cover the same theoretical ground with better optimization properties.

Consistency models — when to reach for them.

One-step or few-step image generation with quality close to a 20–50-step diffusion teacher (LCM, SDXL Turbo).
Real-time interactive generation (drawing apps, live preview).
Latency-critical text-to-image in mobile and edge deployments.

Flow matching / rectified flow — when to reach for them.

Modern image / video generative models (Stable Diffusion 3, Movie Gen, parts of Sora’s family).
Few-step sampling with straight-line probability paths.
Whenever you would have reached for diffusion but want simpler training (no noise schedule tuning) and faster inference.




2.3 Advantages and Drawbacks of Major Generative Modeling Paradigms
A condensed but exhaustive comparison along the axes that the assessment cares about: tractability of likelihood, sample quality, mode coverage, sampling speed, training stability.
Tractability of likelihood.

Family

Autoregressive

VAE
